topics.title: Fluid Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fluid Pressure?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Fluid Pressure (p)** is defined as the *normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area* on a real or imaginary surface. Mathematically,
> $$p = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF_n}{dA}$$
> where $\Delta F_n$ is the normal component of the force acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$. The SI unit is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**, and the CGS unit is **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)**.

### 1.1.1 Key Properties of Fluid Pressure at a Point
1. **Pressure is a scalar quantity** — it has magnitude but no specific direction (although the *force* it produces is always normal to the surface).
2. **Pressure acts normal (perpendicular) to any surface** with which it is in contact. It cannot sustain shear stress in a static fluid.
3. **Pressure at a point in a static fluid is the same in all directions** (Pascal's principle — proved later).
4. **Pressure always acts inward, compressing the fluid** (compressive normal stress).

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inflated Balloon" Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine inflating a balloon with air. You squeeze the pump inward, and the air molecules push *outward* against the rubber from *every* direction equally. Now poke a tiny hole — air rushes out perpendicular to the surface at that spot. This is exactly how fluid pressure behaves: it is the molecules' continuous bombardment of any surface they touch, always pushing **at right angles** to that surface, and the same push occurs whether you measure it from the top, side, or bottom of a fluid parcel. This is why a scuba diver at 10 m depth feels the same crushing pressure on their chest, ears, and mask — *the fluid does not "prefer" any direction*.

---

## 1.2 Pressure Variation with Depth (The Hydrostatic Principle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrostatic Pressure Equation:** For a static, incompressible fluid under gravity,
> $$p = p_0 + \rho g h$$
> where:
> - $p$ = absolute pressure at depth $h$ (**Pa**)
> - $p_0$ = pressure acting on the free surface (**Pa**)
> - $\rho$ = density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
> - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
> - $h$ = vertical depth below the free surface (**m**)

### 1.2.1 Intuition — The "Stacked Books" Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a tall stack of books on a table. The bottom book experiences the weight of *all* the books above it, plus the weight of the table pressing down. A fluid works identically: each layer of fluid bears the weight of every layer above it. The deeper you go, the more fluid is "stacked" on top, and the more weight presses down — so pressure **increases linearly with depth**. This is why dam walls are built thicker at the base than at the top.

### 1.2.2 Atmospheric Pressure and the Concept of a "Free Surface"
**Atmospheric pressure ($p_{atm}$)** is the pressure exerted by the weight of the Earth's atmosphere on any surface exposed to it.
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $p_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1 \text{ atm}$
- Equivalent to a column of mercury $760 \text{ mm}$ high (this is the **barometric height**).

---

## 1.3 Absolute, Gauge, and Vacuum Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference-Based Pressure Definitions:**
> 1. **Absolute Pressure ($p_{abs}$):** Pressure measured from a *perfect vacuum* (zero pressure) reference.
> 2. **Gauge Pressure ($p_{gauge}$):** Pressure measured *above* local atmospheric pressure. This is what most instruments (tyre gauge, Bourdon gauge) read.
> 3. **Vacuum Pressure ($p_{vac}$):** The amount by which local atmospheric pressure *exceeds* absolute pressure (used when $p_{abs} < p_{atm}$).

The relationships are:
$$p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$$
$$p_{vac} = p_{atm} - p_{abs}$$

**Why does this matter?** A flat tyre reads **0 bar gauge** but actually has **~1 bar absolute** inside it. A perfect vacuum reads **−1 bar gauge**.

---

## 1.4 Pascal's Law — The Foundation of Hydraulics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *A change in pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.*

This is the principle behind **hydraulic jacks, hydraulic brakes, hydraulic lifts, and hydraulic presses**.

### 1.4.1 Intuition — The "Squeezed Toothpaste Tube" Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Squeeze a sealed, water-filled plastic pouch at one point. The water inside has nowhere to go, so the squeeze is felt *instantly* and *equally* at every point on the pouch's wall. This is Pascal's Law in action — pressure changes propagate through an incompressible fluid as if the fluid were a rigid "hydraulic link".

---

## 1.5 Manometry — Measuring Fluid Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manometry** is the science of measuring fluid pressure using columns of liquid (manometric fluids). Devices include **piezometers, U-tube manometers, and inverted U-tube manometers**.

A **manometric fluid** is chosen to be *immiscible* with the process fluid and ideally *denser* (e.g., mercury for water systems, or oil for gas systems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a static fluid element of cross-sectional area $A$ and vertical height $h$, immersed in a fluid of density $\rho$:

1. **Forces acting on the element:**
   - Pressure $p_1$ pushing *down* on the top face → force $= p_1 A$ (downward)
   - Pressure $p_2$ pushing *up* on the bottom face → force $= p_2 A$ (upward)
   - Weight of the element acting downward → $W = mg = (\rho \cdot A \cdot h) \cdot g$

2. **Equilibrium condition** (since the fluid is static, $\sum F = 0$):
$$p_2 A - p_1 A - \rho g h A = 0$$

3. **Solving for the pressure difference:**
$$\boxed{\Delta p = p_2 - p_1 = \rho g h}$$

4. **If the top surface is open to atmosphere** ($p_1 = p_{atm}$):
$$p_2 = p_{atm} + \rho g h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This linear increase of pressure with depth is why **submarine hulls** must be cylindrical (uniform stress), **dam walls** taper with depth, and **deep-sea submersibles** use spherical pressure hulls — the geometry that distributes hydrostatic stress most evenly.

---

## 2.2 The Hydrostatic Paradox

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement of the Paradox:** The pressure at the bottom of a fluid column depends *only* on the **vertical height** of the fluid and its **density** — not on the shape of the container or the total volume of fluid.

A famous demonstration: three differently shaped vessels (narrow, wide, slanted) all connected at the base produce the **same pressure** at the base level, even though they contain very different *masses* of fluid. This is because the **slanted walls** of an irregular vessel carry part of the fluid's weight, transferring it to the side supports rather than to the base.

---

## 2.3 Manometer Equation (U-Tube Manometer)

For a U-tube manometer connecting points A and B (with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ and process fluid of density $\rho_f$):

$$p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m - \rho_f g (z_A - z_B)$$

For a **simple differential manometer** (both sides process fluid, manometric fluid only in the U-bend):
$$p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

where $h$ is the **manometric deflection** (difference in manometric fluid levels in the two limbs).

### 2.3.1 Special Case: Piezometer (Open to Atmosphere)
A piezometer is an open vertical tube tapped into a pipe. It measures the *static pressure head* directly:
$$h = \frac{p}{\rho g}$$

---

## 2.4 Force on a Submerged Plane Surface

The total hydrostatic force on a submerged plane surface of area $A$ is:
$$F = p_c \cdot A = \rho g \bar{h} \cdot A$$

where:
- $p_c$ = pressure at the **centroid** of the submerged surface
- $\bar{h}$ = vertical depth of the centroid below the free surface
- $A$ = area of the submerged surface

**Location of the Centre of Pressure** (where the resultant force effectively acts):
$$h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$$

where:
- $I_{xc}$ = second moment of area of the surface about its *centroidal axis* parallel to the free surface
- $\theta$ = angle the surface makes with the horizontal
- $h_{cp}$ = depth of the centre of pressure below the free surface

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is used to design **sluice gates, tank walls, lock gates, and ship hull plates**. The centre of pressure is *always below* the centroid for a vertical (or inclined) submerged surface — except when the surface coincides with the free surface, in which case they coincide.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas, units, and conditions commonly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for **Fluid Pressure** are summarized below.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | SI Units | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | $p = \dfrac{dF_n}{dA}$ | Definition of pressure | Pa ($\text{N/m}^2$) | General |
| 2 | $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa | Static, incompressible fluid |
| 3 | $\Delta p = \rho g h$ | Pressure difference between two points in a static fluid | Pa | Vertical separation $h$ |
| 4 | $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ | Absolute = Gauge + Atmospheric | Pa | $p_{atm} \approx 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ |
| 5 | $p_{vac} = p_{atm} - p_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure relation | Pa | When $p_{abs} < p_{atm}$ |
| 6 | $F = p_c \cdot A = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | Force on a submerged plane surface | N | Plane surface, static fluid |
| 7 | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ | Depth of centre of pressure | m | Inclined plane surface |
| 8 | $p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Differential U-tube manometer | Pa | Same elevation, A and B |
| 9 | $p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m - \rho_f g(z_A - z_B)$ | General manometer equation | Pa | Points A and B at different heights |
| 10 | $h = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (piezometer reading) | m | Open piezometer |
| 11 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Pascal's Law (hydraulic lever) | — | Enclosed, incompressible fluid |
| 12 | $p_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg}$ | Barometric height equation | Pa | Mercury barometer |

### 2.5.1 Standard Reference Values (Frequently Required in KTU Problems)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| Density of water | $\rho_{water}$ | $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg}$ | $13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of air (at STP) | $\rho_{air}$ | $1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | $1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure (other) | $p_{atm}$ | $101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ |
| Barometric height of mercury | $h_{Hg}$ | $760 \text{ mm} = 0.76 \text{ m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Application:** The hydraulic brake system of a car uses Pascal's Law. The driver's foot applies a small force on a small piston; this pressure is transmitted through brake fluid to a larger piston at each wheel, multiplying the force. A force of just **50 N** on a piston of area **5 cm²** produces **2000 N** at a wheel cylinder of area **200 cm²** — a 40× mechanical advantage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a small static fluid element shaped like a thin slab of horizontal cross-section $A$ and vertical thickness $dh$, submerged in a fluid of density $\rho$. The element is in static equilibrium.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Fluid Element
- Top face (at depth $h$): atmospheric/process pressure $p$ pushing **down** with force $p \cdot A$
- Bottom face (at depth $h + dh$): pressure $p + dp$ pushing **up** with force $(p + dp) \cdot A$
- Body force: weight $W = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh \cdot g$ acting **downward**

### 3.1.3 Equilibrium Equation
Since the fluid is static, the **net vertical force must be zero**:
$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$
$$(p + dp) A - p A - \rho g A \, dh = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Simplification
Expanding the first term and cancelling $pA$ from both sides:
$$pA + dp \cdot A - pA - \rho g A \, dh = 0$$
$$dp \cdot A = \rho g A \, dh$$

The cross-sectional area $A$ cancels from both sides (it is arbitrary — this is the beauty of the continuum assumption):
$$dp = \rho g \, dh$$

### 3.1.5 Integration with Boundary Conditions
Integrate from the free surface (where $h = 0$, $p = p_0$) to an arbitrary depth $h$ (where pressure is $p$):
$$\int_{p_0}^{p} dp = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

The left-hand side integrates to $(p - p_0)$. The right-hand side integrates to $\rho g h$ (assuming $\rho$ and $g$ are constant):
$$p - p_0 = \rho g h$$

### 3.1.6 Final Result
$$\boxed{p = p_0 + \rho g h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Conditions:** This derivation assumes (i) the fluid is *incompressible* ($\rho = $ constant), (ii) the fluid is *static* (no motion), and (iii) $g$ is uniform. For compressible fluids (e.g., the atmosphere), $p = p_0 \exp(-Mgh/RT)$ (isothermal lapse) or a more complex polytropic relation is used.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: General Manometer Equation (Connecting Two Points A and B)

### 3.2.1 Setup
A U-tube manometer connects two points A and B in a piping system. The manometer contains a heavy, immiscible manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$. The process fluid (between A and B) has density $\rho_f$. Point A is at elevation $z_A$ and point B is at elevation $z_B$, with a manometric fluid level difference $h_m$ (height of the heavier fluid in the right limb above the level in the left limb).

### 3.2.2 Principle Used
The **hydrostatic paradox** (pressure depends only on vertical height in a connected static fluid) is the foundation. We apply the equation $p + \rho g z = $ constant for any continuous column of the *same* static fluid.

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Pressure Tracing
Start at point A and trace the pressure upward through the manometer to the manometric fluid interface, then back down to point B. We will *add* $\rho g z$ when going **down**, and *subtract* when going **up**.

Let the left-hand manometric fluid interface be at elevation $z_L$ and the right-hand interface at elevation $z_R$, with $h_m = z_R - z_L$.

**Step 1 — Go from A down (or up) to the left interface (level $z_L$), through process fluid:**
$$p_L = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_L)$$
*(Adding $\rho g z$ as we descend by $(z_A - z_L)$.)*

**Step 2 — Cross the manometric fluid interface:**
At a *horizontal level* within a connected static fluid, the pressure is the same. So just below the manometric fluid surface on the left, the pressure is $p_L$. Just above it, on the process-fluid side, the pressure is also $p_L$.

**Step 3 — Go from the left interface, down through mercury, to the level of the right interface ($z_R$):**
The pressure at the right interface level (in manometric fluid) is:
$$p_{R,\text{left col}} = p_L + \rho_m g (z_R - z_L) = p_L + \rho_m g h_m$$

**Step 4 — At the right interface, jump to the process fluid side:**
The pressure just above the manometric fluid (on the process fluid side of the right limb) is also $p_{R,\text{left col}}$:
$$p_{R,\text{right col}} = p_L + \rho_m g h_m$$

**Step 5 — Go from the right interface up to point B through process fluid:**
$$p_B = p_{R,\text{right col}} - \rho_f g (z_B - z_R) = p_L + \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g (z_B - z_R)$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting and Rearranging
Replace $p_L$ from Step 1:
$$p_B = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_L) + \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g (z_B - z_R)$$

Note that $h_m = z_R - z_L$, so $z_R = z_L + h_m$. Substituting:
$$p_B = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_L) + \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g (z_B - z_L - h_m)$$
$$p_B = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_L) + \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g (z_B - z_L) + \rho_f g h_m$$

Grouping the $\rho_f g h_m$ terms:
$$p_B = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_B) + (\rho_m + \rho_f) g h_m - \rho_f g h_m$$
$$p_B = p_A + \rho_f g (z_A - z_B) + (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m$$

### 3.2.5 Final Manometer Equation
$$\boxed{p_A - p_B = \rho_f g (z_B - z_A) + (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m}$$

Or equivalently, in the standard textbook form:
$$p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m + \rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Watch:** Many KTU students lose marks by confusing the sign of $h_m$. The rule: **the manometric fluid is *higher* on the side connected to the *lower-pressure* point**. So $h_m$ is positive when measured on the limb nearer to point B (if $p_A > p_B$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Centre of Pressure for a Submerged Plane Surface

### 3.3.1 Setup
Consider a flat plate of area $A$ submerged in a static liquid with its plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface. Let the free surface be the $x$-axis. The plate is submerged with its **upper edge** at depth $h_1$ and **lower edge** at depth $h_2$. Let the centroid of the plate be at depth $\bar{h}$.

### 3.3.2 Pressure Distribution
The pressure at depth $h$ is:
$$p(h) = p_0 + \rho g h$$

If we ignore atmospheric pressure $p_0$ (it acts on both sides of the plate in a typical submerged-gate problem and so cancels out from the net force), the **gauge pressure** distribution along the plate is:
$$p(h) = \rho g h$$

### 3.3.3 Total Force on the Plate
Consider a differential strip of the plate at depth $h$, of width $b(h)$ and height $dh'$ (measured *along* the plate's plane). The differential area is $dA = b(h) \, dh'$.

The differential force on this strip:
$$dF = p(h) \, dA = \rho g h \cdot b(h) \, dh'$$

Using the geometric relationship $h = \bar{y} \sin\theta$ where $\bar{y}$ is measured along the plate from the free surface, and $dh = dh' \sin\theta$:

The **total force** is:
$$F = \int_A \rho g h \, dA = \rho g \int_A h \, dA$$

Since $h$ is the depth of $dA$, and the centroid depth is $\bar{h} = (1/A) \int_A h \, dA$:
$$F = \rho g A \bar{h}$$

### 3.3.4 Location of the Centre of Pressure
The total moment of the pressure force about the **free surface line** (the $x$-axis, where $h = 0$) is:
$$M = \int_A h \cdot dF = \int_A h \cdot (\rho g h) \, dA = \rho g \int_A h^2 \, dA$$

The second moment of area about the free surface (the $x$-axis) is:
$$I_x = \int_A h^2 \, dA$$

The moment of the resultant force $F$ about the same axis is $F \cdot h_{cp}$, so:
$$F \cdot h_{cp} = \rho g I_x$$
$$\rho g A \bar{h} \cdot h_{cp} = \rho g I_x$$
$$h_{cp} = \frac{I_x}{A \bar{h}}$$

### 3.3.5 Applying the Parallel Axis Theorem
$I_x$ is the second moment about the *free surface* line. By the parallel axis theorem:
$$I_x = I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2$$

where $I_{xc}$ is the second moment about the **centroidal axis** of the plate (parallel to the free surface).

Substituting:
$$h_{cp} = \frac{I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2}{A \bar{h}} = \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$$

### 3.3.6 Final Result
$$\boxed{h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Since $I_{xc} > 0$ and $\bar{h} > 0$ for a submerged surface, we always have $h_{cp} > \bar{h}$. The **centre of pressure is always below the centroid** for a vertically (or inclined) submerged plane surface — meaning the resultant hydrostatic thrust acts *lower* than the geometric centre of the surface.

### 3.3.7 Analytical Matrix — $I_{xc}$ for Standard Geometries (Frequently Tested)

| Geometry | Centroidal Second Moment of Area ($I_{xc}$) | Where the axis is the one parallel to the free surface |
|----------|----------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Rectangle (width $b$, height $a$) | $\dfrac{b a^3}{12}$ | Through centroid, parallel to $b$ (i.e., horizontal) |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $a$, apex up) | $\dfrac{b a^3}{36}$ | Parallel to base, through centroid |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $a$, apex down) | $\dfrac{b a^3}{36}$ | Parallel to base, through centroid |
| Circle (radius $R$) | $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ | Any diameter through the centre |
| Semicircle (radius $R$, flat side up) | $0.1098 R^4 \approx \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8} \cdot \left(\dfrac{4}{3\pi}\right)$ | Through centroid, parallel to diameter |
| Trapezoid (parallel sides $a$ and $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{h^3 (a^2 + 4ab + b^2)}{36(a+b)}$ | Parallel to parallel sides, through centroid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU Students:** Memorize the rectangle and triangle values — they are the most frequently tested. The circle is also common for sluice-gate and orifice problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fluid Pressure Concepts

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **conceptual hierarchy** of Fluid Pressure as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. It traces the logical flow from fundamental definitions to engineering applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PRESSURE<br/>PCPET303 Module 1]:::root
    A --> B[1. Pressure at a Point<br/>p = dF/dA]:::level1
    A --> C[2. Hydrostatic Pressure<br/>p = p0 + rho g h]:::level1
    A --> D[3. Pressure Measurement<br/>Manometry]:::level1
    A --> E[4. Hydrostatic Forces<br/>on Submerged Surfaces]:::level1
    A --> F[5. Pascal's Law<br/>Hydraulic Systems]:::level1

    B --> B1[Scalar Quantity]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Acts Normal to Surface]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Same in All Directions<br/>at a Point]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Incompressible Fluid]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Linear Variation with Depth]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Hydrostatic Paradox]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Absolute / Gauge / Vacuum]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Piezometer<br/>Open Tube]:::leaf
    D --> D2[U-Tube Manometer<br/>Differential Pressure]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Inverted U-Tube<br/>for Light Fluids]:::leaf
    D --> D4[Mercury Barometer<br/>Atmospheric Pressure]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Force on Plane Surface<br/>F = rho g h_bar A]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Centre of Pressure<br/>Depth h_cp]:::leaf
    E --> E3[Vertical / Inclined<br/>Curved Surfaces]:::leaf

    F --> F1[Pressure Transmission<br/>Undiminished]:::leaf
    F --> F2[Hydraulic Press<br/>F1/A1 = F2/A2]:::leaf
    F --> F3[Hydraulic Jack<br/>Vehicle Brakes]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef level1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** The root node `A` is the topic itself. The five level-1 nodes (`B`–`F`) are the **core sub-topics** of KTU Module 1. The leaf nodes enumerate the **concepts, formulas, and devices** that examiners frequently test. A student preparing for the KTU University Exam should be able to explain every leaf node without referring to this diagram.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: U-Tube Manometer Pressure-Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pipe at Point A<br/>Pressure pA]:::sys --> A2[Process Fluid<br/>Density rho_f]:::fluid
    A2 --> A3[Left Limb<br/>of U-Tube]:::tube
    A3 --> A4[Manometric Fluid<br/>Mercury rho_m]:::manofluid
    A4 --> A5[Right Limb<br/>of U-Tube]:::tube
    A5 --> A6[Process Fluid<br/>Density rho_f]:::fluid
    A6 --> A7[Pipe at Point B<br/>Pressure pB]:::sys

    A4 -.-> A8[Manometric Deflection<br/>h_m]:::meas
    A8 --> A9[Apply Manometer Equation<br/>pA - pB = rho_m g h_m]:::calc
    A9 --> A10[Result: Differential<br/>Pressure Reading]:::result

    classDef sys fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c55a11,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef fluid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef tube fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#7030a0,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef manofluid fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef meas fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef result fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A pressure difference between two points A and B in a piping system is *physically manifested* as a height difference $h_m$ of the heavy manometric fluid in the U-tube. The right-hand side of the diagram (dotted arrow) shows the *mathematical* workflow: from the deflection $h_m$, the student applies the manometer equation to compute the differential pressure $p_A - p_B$.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pascal's Law — Hydraulic Press Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Piston<br/>Area A1]:::input --> F1[Input Force F1]:::force
    F1 --> P2[Enclosed<br/>Incompressible<br/>Fluid]:::medium
    P2 --> P3[Pressure p = F1/A1<br/>Transmitted Undiminished<br/>Pascal's Law]:::law
    P3 --> P4[Output Piston<br/>Area A2 > A1]:::output
    P4 --> F2[Output Force F2<br/>F2 = F1 x A2/A1]:::force

    classDef input fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef force fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef medium fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef law fill:#e2efda,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** A small input force $F_1$ on a small piston $A_1$ creates a pressure $p = F_1 / A_1$. Pascal's Law guarantees this pressure is transmitted undiminished through the fluid to the larger piston $A_2$. The output force $F_2 = p \cdot A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1)$, achieving mechanical advantage equal to the area ratio. This is the operating principle of **hydraulic jacks, hydraulic lifts, hydraulic presses, and automotive brake systems**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hydrostatic Force Resolution Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1<br/>Identify Submerged Surface<br/>Geometry, Area, Orientation]:::step
    S1 --> S2[Step 2<br/>Locate Centroid<br/>Depth h_bar]:::step
    S2 --> S3[Step 3<br/>Compute Hydrostatic Force<br/>F = rho g h_bar A]:::step
    S3 --> S4[Step 4<br/>Find Centroidal<br/>Second Moment I_xc]:::step
    S4 --> S5[Step 5<br/>Compute Centre of<br/>Pressure Depth h_cp]:::step
    S5 --> S6[Step 6<br/>Draw Free-Body Diagram<br/>Show F at h_cp]:::step
    S6 --> S7[Result<br/>Design Gate / Wall /<br/>Hull Plate Thickness]:::final

    classDef step fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions are short-answer questions typically requiring a definition, a statement, or a brief derivation. They appear in **University Exam (ESE)** as well as **Internal Assessments**. Each carries **3 marks**.

---

### Question 1: Define Fluid Pressure. State and explain Pascal's Law.
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Pressure** is the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a real or imaginary surface:
> $$p = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A}$$
> The SI unit is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**, and other common units are **bar, psi, mm Hg, and atm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *A change in pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel.*
> This is the operating principle of **hydraulic presses, jacks, and brakes**. For a hydraulic system with input piston of area $A_1$ and output piston of area $A_2$:
> $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \implies F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$
> (3 marks: definition = 1, statement = 1, application = 1)

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Absolute Pressure, Gauge Pressure, and Vacuum Pressure.
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | Reference | Relation | When used |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Absolute Pressure ($p_{abs}$)** | Zero (perfect vacuum) | $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ | Thermodynamics, gas laws |
| **Gauge Pressure ($p_{gauge}$)** | Local atmospheric pressure | $p_{gauge} = p_{abs} - p_{atm}$ | Most engineering instruments (tyres, boilers) |
| **Vacuum Pressure ($p_{vac}$)** | Local atmospheric pressure | $p_{vac} = p_{atm} - p_{abs}$ | When $p_{abs} < p_{atm}$ (condensers, suction lines) |

> [!NOTE]
> A flat tyre reads **0 kPa gauge** but contains **~100 kPa absolute**. A condenser operating at **−80 kPa gauge** has only **21 kPa absolute** inside it.
> (3 marks: definitions = 2, relations + example = 1)

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have *internal choice* (you must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B). Each part-question (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Full-length derivations, numerical solutions, and clear free-body diagrams are expected.

---

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1]`**

**Q.A)** A U-tube mercury manometer is connected between two points A and B in a pipeline conveying water. Point A is at an elevation of **1.5 m** above the centreline of the manometer, and point B is at an elevation of **0.8 m** above the centreline. The mercury level in the limb connected to A is **0.25 m** below the centreline, and the mercury level in the limb connected to B is **0.55 m** above the centreline. Determine the pressure difference $p_A - p_B$ in kPa. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(b)** A vertical rectangular gate **2 m wide** and **3 m high** is installed in a dam. The top edge of the gate is **2 m** below the water surface. Determine:
1. The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
2. The location of the centre of pressure measured from the water surface.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify and set up the manometer equation.**

We use the general manometer equation:
$$p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m + \rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$$

**Step 2: Determine $h_m$, the manometric deflection.**

The manometric deflection is the difference in mercury levels between the two limbs:
$$h_m = (\text{Hg level in B-limb}) - (\text{Hg level in A-limb})$$
$$h_m = 0.55 - (-0.25) = 0.55 + 0.25 = 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Determine $z_A$ and $z_B$.**

Both are given as elevations above the manometer centreline:
$$z_A = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad z_B = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the manometer equation.**

$$p_A - p_B = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.80 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.8 - 1.5)$$

Calculate each term separately:

**Term 1** (manometric term):
$$(13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.80 = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.80 = 98884.8 \text{ Pa}$$

**Term 2** (elevation term):
$$1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.8 - 1.5) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (-0.7) = -6867 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5: Combine.**

$$p_A - p_B = 98884.8 - 6867 = 92017.8 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kPa.**

$$\boxed{p_A - p_B = 92.02 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(approximately)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Sign:** Since $p_A - p_B > 0$, point A is at a higher pressure. This is consistent with the fact that the mercury is depressed on the A-side and raised on the B-side — the heavier fluid is pushed *away* from the higher-pressure side. ✓

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify geometry.**
- Width of gate: $b = 2$ m
- Height of gate: $a = 3$ m
- Top edge depth: $h_1 = 2$ m
- Bottom edge depth: $h_2 = h_1 + a = 2 + 3 = 5$ m

**Step 2: Find the centroid depth.**
The centroid of the rectangular gate is at the geometric centre:
$$\bar{h} = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2} = \frac{2 + 5}{2} = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the area.**
$$A = b \times a = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Total hydrostatic thrust.**
$$F = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 6$$
$$\boxed{F = 206{,}010 \text{ N} \approx 206.01 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the centroidal second moment of area.**
For a rectangle of width $b$ and height $a$ (with the centroidal axis parallel to the width $b$, i.e., horizontal):
$$I_{xc} = \frac{b a^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 27}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6: Centre of pressure depth.**
$$h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}} = 3.5 + \frac{4.5}{6 \times 3.5}$$
$$h_{cp} = 3.5 + \frac{4.5}{21} = 3.5 + 0.2143$$
$$\boxed{h_{cp} = 3.7143 \text{ m} \text{ (below the water surface)}}$$

**Step 7: Distance from top edge of the gate.**
$$\text{Distance from top edge} = h_{cp} - h_1 = 3.7143 - 2 = 1.7143 \text{ m below the top edge}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The centre of pressure (3.7143 m) is *below* the centroid (3.5 m), as theoretically expected. ✓

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1]`**

**Q.B)** A hydraulic press has a ram of diameter **30 cm** and a plunger of diameter **5 cm**. An effort of **400 N** is applied on the plunger. Find:
1. The pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid.
2. The load that can be lifted by the hydraulic press.

**(b)** Derive an expression for the total hydrostatic force and the centre of pressure on a submerged vertical plane surface. State the assumptions clearly.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Find the area of the plunger (small piston).**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Find the area of the ram (large piston).**
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.09 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Pascal's Law to find the pressure.**

By Pascal's Law, the pressure generated by the plunger is transmitted undiminished through the fluid:
$$p = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{400}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$\boxed{p = 203{,}718 \text{ Pa} \approx 203.72 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 4: Find the load that can be lifted.**

The same pressure acts on the ram, so:
$$F_2 = p \cdot A_2 = 203{,}718 \times 0.07069$$
$$\boxed{F_2 = 14{,}400 \text{ N} \approx 14.4 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5: Verify the mechanical advantage.**
$$\text{MA} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{0.07069}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 36$$
$$F_2 = F_1 \times \text{MA} = 400 \times 36 = 14{,}400 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A modest 400 N effort (roughly the weight of a 40 kg person) lifts a 14,400 N load (over 1.4 tonnes) — a 36× mechanical advantage. This is the magic of hydraulic systems, and the reason they are used in car jacks, lift platforms, and forging presses.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of Total Hydrostatic Force and Centre of Pressure on a Submerged Vertical Plane Surface**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is **static** (no motion, no shear stress).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$).
3. The free surface is **open to atmosphere** or at uniform pressure $p_0$.
4. The plane surface is **flat** and **vertical** (so $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$).

**Setup:**
Consider a vertical plane surface submerged in a liquid of density $\rho$, with the free surface at the top. The surface extends from depth $h_1$ (top edge) to depth $h_2$ (bottom edge), where $h_2 > h_1$. The width of the surface at depth $h$ is $b(h)$.

**Step 1: Pressure at a point at depth $h$.**
$$p(h) = p_0 + \rho g h$$
If we work in *gauge* pressure, $p_0 = 0$ and $p(h) = \rho g h$.

**Step 2: Force on an infinitesimal strip at depth $h$.**
A horizontal strip of height $dh$ and width $b(h)$ has area $dA = b(h) \, dh$. The force on this strip is:
$$dF = p(h) \, dA = \rho g h \cdot b(h) \, dh$$

**Step 3: Total force by integration.**
$$F = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \rho g h \cdot b(h) \, dh = \rho g \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot b(h) \, dh$$

Using the definition of the centroid of area, $\bar{h} A = \int h \, dA$:
$$\boxed{F = \rho g \bar{h} A}$$
where $\bar{h}$ is the depth of the **centroid** of the submerged surface and $A$ is its area.

**Step 4: Location of the centre of pressure.**

The moment of the pressure forces about the **free surface line** (where $h = 0$):
$$M_{\text{FS}} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot dF = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot \rho g h \cdot b(h) \, dh = \rho g \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h^2 \, b(h) \, dh$$

The second moment of area about the free surface line is:
$$I_x = \int h^2 \, dA = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h^2 \, b(h) \, dh$$

So:
$$M_{\text{FS}} = \rho g I_x$$

Equating this to the moment of the resultant force $F$ about the same axis:
$$F \cdot h_{cp} = \rho g I_x$$
$$\rho g \bar{h} A \cdot h_{cp} = \rho g I_x$$
$$h_{cp} = \frac{I_x}{A \bar{h}}$$

**Step 5: Apply the parallel axis theorem.**

By the parallel axis theorem (shifting the axis from the free surface to the centroidal axis):
$$I_x = I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2$$

Substituting:
$$h_{cp} = \frac{I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2}{A \bar{h}} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}}$$

$$\boxed{h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The centre of pressure is **always below the centroid** of a submerged vertical plane surface (since $I_{xc} > 0$). The deeper the surface is submerged, the closer $h_{cp}$ approaches $\bar{h}$. For a horizontal surface flush with the free surface, $h_{cp} = \bar{h}$ exactly.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Fluid Pressure Problems (observed in KTU valuation):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Manometer Equation:** Students frequently get the sign of $h_m$ reversed. **Rule:** The manometric fluid is *higher* on the side connected to the *lower-pressure* point. Always define $h_m$ explicitly and consistently with this rule. **(−2 to −3 marks typical)**
> 2. **Confusing Gauge with Absolute:** Some students add $p_{atm}$ when the problem statement already gives absolute pressures, or vice versa. **Always re-read the problem** to identify the pressure reference. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting Units Conversion:** $g = 9.81$ (not $9.8$ or $10$), and Pa must be converted to kPa if the answer requires it. Density must be in **kg/m³** (not g/cm³) and depth in **m** (not cm or mm). **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Wrong Centroidal $I_{xc}$ for Inclined Plates:** When a rectangular plate is *inclined*, students sometimes use $ba^3/12$ for the wrong axis. **Check the orientation**: $I_{xc}$ is about the axis *parallel to the free surface* (i.e., parallel to the *top* of the inclined plate). **(−2 to −3 marks)**
> 5. **Skipping Free-Body Diagrams:** KTU evaluators expect a clearly labelled FBD or sketch for any force-related question. Omitting it can cost **1 mark** even if the calculation is correct.
> 6. **Not Stating Pascal's Law Verbatim:** Part A questions on Pascal's Law require the *exact statement* (especially "transmitted **undiminished**"). Marks are reserved for keyword matches. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 7. **Forgetting the Hydrostatic Paradox Mention:** When asked to "explain hydrostatic pressure", mentioning the hydrostatic paradox and the *non-dependence on container shape* fetches bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluid Pressure (KTU PCPET303 Module 1)**

### A. Core Definitions
- [ ] **Pressure** = normal force per unit area; SI unit = Pa (N/m²); scalar; acts normal to any surface.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$: $p = p_0 + \rho g h$.
- [ ] **Pascal's Law**: pressure change in an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted *undiminished* to all parts.
- [ ] **Absolute pressure** = reference is *zero* (perfect vacuum).
- [ ] **Gauge pressure** = reference is *local atmospheric*.
- [ ] **Vacuum pressure** = amount by which atmospheric pressure exceeds absolute pressure.

### B. Essential Formulas (memorize verbatim)
- [ ] $p = \rho g h$ — pressure difference between two points in a connected static fluid
- [ ] $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ — pressure reference conversion
- [ ] $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ — total hydrostatic force on a *plane* surface
- [ ] $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}}$ — centre of pressure depth
- [ ] $p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m + \rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$ — general manometer equation
- [ ] $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ — Pascal's Law applied to hydraulics

### C. Standard Reference Values
- [ ] $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- [ ] $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- [ ] $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$

### D. Centroidal Second Moments of Area (top 3 most tested)
- [ ] **Rectangle** ($b \times a$): $I_{xc} = \dfrac{b a^3}{12}$ (axis parallel to $b$)
- [ ] **Triangle** (base $b$, height $a$): $I_{xc} = \dfrac{b a^3}{36}$ (axis parallel to base)
- [ ] **Circle** (radius $R$): $I_{xc} = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ (any diameter)

### E. Common KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] **U-tube manometer with elevation difference** — apply the *general* manometer equation (with $\rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$ term).
- [ ] **Inverted U-tube manometer for gas pressure** — use the *air/oil* column, not mercury.
- [ ] **Vertical/inclined gate in a dam** — use $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ and find $h_{cp}$.
- [ ] **Hydraulic press / jack** — use $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1)$ and $p = F/A$.
- [ ] **Pressure on a curved surface** — resolve into horizontal and vertical components using *projected areas* and *weight of fluid above* — covered in advanced sections.

### F. High-Yield Conceptual Points (for 3-mark questions)
- [ ] Hydrostatic paradox: pressure at the base depends only on **vertical height**, not on container shape or volume.
- [ ] Centre of pressure is *below* the centroid for a vertical/inclined submerged surface.
- [ ] Pascal's Law requires an *enclosed, incompressible, static* fluid.
- [ ] Gauge pressure is what a Bourdon gauge reads; absolute pressure is what enters thermodynamic equations.
- [ ] Manometric fluids must be **immiscible** with the process fluid and **denser** for a standard U-tube.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces on Solid Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forces on Solid Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Fluid Statics**, the topic *Forces on Solid Surfaces* deals with the determination of the magnitude, direction, and point of application of the resultant force exerted by a **static fluid** (liquid or gas) on a surface that is either **fully submerged**, **partially submerged**, or in **contact with a fluid surface**. These forces arise due to the **hydrostatic pressure distribution**, which varies linearly with depth according to the hydrostatic pressure equation.

Mathematically, the governing principle is derived from:

$$P = \rho g h$$

where the pressure at depth $h$ produces a distributed load on the surface, and the resultant force is obtained by integrating this pressure over the wetted area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Forces on solid surfaces* encompasses the analysis of hydrostatic thrust on **plane submerged surfaces** (horizontal, vertical, inclined), **curved submerged surfaces**, the principle of **buoyancy and flotation**, and the **stability of floating and submerged bodies** including **metacentric height** calculations.

## 1.2 Sub-Topics Covered (Aligned to KTU Module 1 Outcomes)

| Sub-Topic | KTU CO Mapping | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surfaces | CO1 | Apply / Analyze |
| Center of Pressure | CO1 | Apply / Analyze |
| Hydrostatic Force on Curved Surfaces | CO1 | Analyze |
| Buoyancy & Archimedes' Principle | CO1 | Apply |
| Stability of Floating Bodies (Metacentre) | CO1, CO2 | Evaluate |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> 
> Imagine standing at the **edge of a swimming pool** and looking at the **vertical side wall**. You will notice that the water does not push the wall uniformly — it pushes **very little at the surface** but pushes **enormously at the bottom**. This is because the deeper you go, the **more water weight** is sitting above you, and that weight creates pressure. 
> 
> Now imagine a **submarine door** at the bottom of the pool. The water pushes on it with a huge force. Engineers must know **exactly** how much force and **where** it acts so they can design the hinges and latches properly.
> 
> - A **dam wall** is thicker at the base because the force grows with depth.
> - A **ship floats** because the water pushes it **up** with a force equal to the weight of the water it displaces.
> - A **balloon** filled with helium rises because the surrounding air exerts a net upward buoyant force.
> 
> All of these are "Forces on Solid Surfaces" — the core idea is to replace a **distributed pressure load** with a **single equivalent force** and locate its line of action.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are used throughout this module:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of water (at 4 °C):** $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = \rho_w g = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$
- **Density of mercury:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of mercury:** $\gamma_{Hg} = 133416 \; \text{N/m}^3$
- **Atmospheric pressure (standard):** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} \approx 101.3 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Gauge pressure:** $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$
- **Absolute pressure:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU problems involving submerged surfaces, **gauge pressure** is used (i.e., atmospheric pressure is neglected on the free surface) unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Force on a Plane Submerged Surface

### 2.1.1 The Governing Idea

A flat surface of area $A$ is submerged in a static fluid. At every point, the fluid exerts a pressure $P = \rho g h$, where $h$ is the vertical depth of that point below the free surface. Because pressure increases linearly with depth, the **pressure distribution is a trapezoid** (uniform component + triangular component).

The resultant force is the integral of pressure over the area:

$$F_R = \int_A P \, dA = \int_A \rho g h \, dA$$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the centroid** $G$ of the submerged plane area — its depth is $\bar{h}$.
2. **Compute the pressure at the centroid** using $P_G = \rho g \bar{h}$.
3. **The resultant force** equals this centroidal pressure multiplied by the total area: $F_R = P_G \cdot A = \rho g \bar{h} A$.
4. **Locate the point of action** — this is the **center of pressure** $h^*$, which is always **below** the centroid for a vertical/inclined surface with a single free surface above.

### 2.1.3 Inclined Plane Surface — Geometry Setup

For an inclined surface making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal (or with the free surface), we resolve depths along the inclined direction:

$$h = y \sin\theta$$

where $y$ is the slant distance measured along the inclined plane from the free surface (where the plane intersects it).

## 2.2 Center of Pressure

The **center of pressure** $h^*$ (or $y^*$) is the point on the submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic force is considered to act. For a plane surface, it is located at:

$$y^* = \bar{y} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{\bar{y} A} = \frac{I_{xx,o}}{\bar{y} A}$$

where:
- $y^*$ = slant distance of center of pressure from the free surface
- $\bar{y}$ = slant distance of the centroid from the free surface
- $I_{xx,c}$ = second moment of area about the centroidal axis (parallel to the free surface)
- $I_{xx,o}$ = second moment of area about the axis where the plane meets the free surface ($I_{xx,o} = I_{xx,c} + A\bar{y}^2$ by parallel axis theorem)

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Force on a Curved Submerged Surface

For a **curved surface**, the resultant force is **not** perpendicular to the surface. We resolve it into:

- **Horizontal component ($F_H$):** Equal to the force on the **vertical projection** of the curved surface.
- **Vertical component ($F_V$):** Equal to the **weight of the fluid** vertically above (real or imaginary) the curved surface up to the free surface.

$$F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_V A_V$$

$$F_V = \rho g \cdot V_{\text{fluid above}}$$

The magnitude of the resultant:

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$$

The direction (angle with horizontal):

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{F_V}{F_H}\right)$$

## 2.4 Buoyancy and Archimedes' Principle

**Archimedes' Principle:** *Any body fully or partially immersed in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.*

$$F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}$$

- If the buoyant force exceeds the weight → body **floats** (partially submerged).
- If the buoyant force equals the weight → body is in **neutral equilibrium** (fully submerged at constant depth).
- If the buoyant force is less than the weight → body **sinks**.

## 2.5 Stability of Floating Bodies — Metacentre

For a floating body, the **metacentre** $M$ is the point about which the body oscillates (rotates) when slightly tilted. The **metacentric height** $GM$ determines stability:

$$GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}} - BG$$

where:
- $BM$ = distance from centre of buoyancy $B$ to metacentre $M$
- $BG$ = distance from centre of gravity $G$ to centre of buoyancy $B$
- $I$ = second moment of area of the **waterline plane** about the axis of rotation
- $V_{\text{submerged}}$ = volume of fluid displaced

**Stability Criterion:**
- $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (body returns to upright)
- $GM = 0$ → **Neutral** (body remains at new tilt)
- $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (body capsizes)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Force on plane surface (inclined) | $F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | $\text{N}$, $\bar{h}$ is vertical depth of centroid |
| 3 | Force on horizontal surface | $F_R = \rho g h A$ | $h$ is uniform depth |
| 4 | Force on vertical surface | $F_R = \rho g \bar{h}_c A$ | $\bar{h}_c = h/2$ for rectangular gate (top edge at free surface) |
| 5 | Center of pressure (depth) | $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ | $I_G$ about centroidal axis |
| 6 | Center of pressure (slant) | $y^* = \dfrac{I_{xx,o}}{\bar{y} A}$ | $I_{xx,o}$ about free surface axis |
| 7 | Horizontal force (curved) | $F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_V A_V$ | $A_V$ = vertical projection of curved area |
| 8 | Vertical force (curved) | $F_V = \rho g V_{\text{fluid}}$ | $V_{\text{fluid}}$ = weight of fluid column above curve |
| 9 | Resultant (curved) | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ | Magnitude of total force |
| 10 | Buoyant force | $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}$ | $V_{\text{disp}}$ in $\text{m}^3$ |
| 11 | Weight of floating body | $W = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}$ | For floating equilibrium |
| 12 | Metacentric radius | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{sub}}}$ | $I$ = second moment of waterline area |
| 13 | Metacentric height | $GM = BM - BG$ | Stability criterion uses $GM$ |
| 14 | Moment of inertia of waterline | $I = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$ (rectangle) | $B$ = breadth in tilting direction |
| 15 | Oscillatory time period | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | $k$ = radius of gyration of floating body |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always check whether the problem gives **gauge** or **absolute** pressure, whether the surface is **fully or partially** submerged, and whether the free surface is **above** or **below** the surface. Drawing a clear free-body diagram (FBD) showing the surface, fluid column, and centroid will fetch easy partial marks even if the final numerical answer has an arithmetic error.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of "Forces on Solid Surfaces" |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Civil — Dams** | Designing thickness profile of gravity dams, buttress dams, and spillway gates. |
| **Civil — Tanks & Silos** | Determining wall thickness of water tanks, fuel storage tanks, and grain silos. |
| **Marine — Ship Design** | Computing draft, freeboard, and metacentric height for ship stability. |
| **Aerospace — Buoyancy** | Design of stratospheric balloons, airships, and submersibles. |
| **Mechanical — Hydraulic Gates** | Sluice gates, lock gates, and turbine inlet valves. |
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Spillways, weirs, and pressure on tunnel linings. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Resultant Force on a Plane Submerged Surface

### Setup

Consider a flat surface of area $A$ submerged in a static liquid of density $\rho$. The surface is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface. Let us set up an inclined coordinate system:
- $x$-axis: along the inclined surface.
- $y$-axis: perpendicular to the inclined surface (going into the liquid).
- Origin: at the point where the inclined surface (extended) meets the free liquid surface.

A small elemental strip at slant distance $y$ from the origin has:
- Area: $dA = b(y) \, dy$, where $b(y)$ is the width of the strip at distance $y$.
- Vertical depth: $h = y \sin\theta$.

### Derivation

Pressure at the elemental strip:

$$P = \rho g h = \rho g y \sin\theta$$

Force on the elemental strip:

$$dF = P \, dA = \rho g y \sin\theta \, b(y) \, dy$$

Total resultant force (integrate from $y = y_1$ to $y = y_2$):

$$F_R = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \rho g y \sin\theta \, b(y) \, dy = \rho g \sin\theta \int_{y_1}^{y_2} y \, b(y) \, dy$$

Recognize that the integral $\int y \, b(y) \, dy$ over the area equals $\bar{y} \cdot A$, where $\bar{y}$ is the slant distance of the centroid of the area from the origin:

$$\int_{y_1}^{y_2} y \, b(y) \, dy = \bar{y} A$$

Substitute back:

$$F_R = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{y} \cdot A = \rho g (\bar{y} \sin\theta) \cdot A$$

But $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$ is the vertical depth of the centroid below the free surface. Therefore:

$$\boxed{F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A}$$

**Physical meaning:** The resultant hydrostatic force on a plane submerged surface equals the pressure at the centroid multiplied by the total area.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Location of Center of Pressure

The center of pressure $y^*$ is defined as the point where the moment of the resultant force equals the moment of the distributed pressure force about the origin (or free surface line).

Moment of the elemental force about the origin:

$$dM = y \, dF = y \cdot (\rho g y \sin\theta) \cdot b(y) \, dy = \rho g \sin\theta \, y^2 \, b(y) \, dy$$

Total moment about origin:

$$M = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \rho g \sin\theta \, y^2 \, b(y) \, dy = \rho g \sin\theta \int y^2 \, b(y) \, dy$$

Recognize the integral $\int y^2 \, b(y) \, dy$ as the **second moment of area** about the axis where the plane meets the free surface, i.e., $I_{xx,o}$:

$$\int y^2 \, b(y) \, dy = I_{xx,o}$$

Therefore:

$$M = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_{xx,o}$$

By the principle of moments, $M = F_R \cdot y^*$. So:

$$F_R \cdot y^* = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_{xx,o}$$

$$y^* = \frac{\rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_{xx,o}}{F_R} = \frac{\rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_{xx,o}}{\rho g \bar{h} A} = \frac{\rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_{xx,o}}{\rho g (\bar{y} \sin\theta) A}$$

$$\boxed{y^* = \frac{I_{xx,o}}{\bar{y} A}}$$

Using the parallel axis theorem: $I_{xx,o} = I_{xx,c} + A\bar{y}^2$, we get the alternative form:

$$y^* = \bar{y} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{\bar{y} A}$$

The vertical depth of the center of pressure:

$$h^* = y^* \sin\theta = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c} \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** For a surface that has its **top edge at the free surface** (such as a vertical rectangular gate hinged at the top), the center of pressure is located at $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ from the top, i.e., at $\dfrac{1}{3}$ from the bottom — it is always **below** the centroid.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Hydrostatic Force on a Curved Surface (Horizontal & Vertical Components)

### Setup

Consider a curved surface $AB$ submerged in a liquid. The free surface is at the top. We divide the curved surface into infinitesimal horizontal strips.

### Horizontal Component ($F_H$)

Consider the vertical projection of the curved surface (i.e., the area seen from the side). The horizontal force on the curved surface equals the horizontal force on this vertical projection (by the principle that pressure forces in the horizontal direction balance on either side of any vertical line through the fluid).

$$F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_V A_V$$

where $\bar{h}_V$ is the vertical depth of the centroid of the **vertical projection** and $A_V$ is the area of the vertical projection.

### Vertical Component ($F_V$)

The vertical force on the curved surface equals the **weight of the fluid column** vertically above the curved surface (real or imaginary), extending from the surface up to the free liquid surface.

$$F_V = \rho g \cdot V_{\text{fluid column}}$$

If the curved surface has fluid **above** it (concave-up), $F_V$ acts **downward**. If fluid is **below** it (concave-down), $F_V$ acts **upward**.

### Resultant

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$$

The line of action of $F_R$ passes through the intersection of the lines of action of $F_H$ and $F_V$.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)

Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape with volume $V$ in a fluid of density $\rho$.

Consider a small vertical column of cross-section $dA$ at depth $h$. The pressure at the top of the column is $P_{\text{top}} = P_0 + \rho g h_1$ (where $P_0$ is pressure at the free surface and $h_1$ is the depth of the top of the column). The pressure at the bottom of the column is $P_{\text{bot}} = P_0 + \rho g h_2$.

Net upward force on the column:

$$dF_{\text{up}} = (P_{\text{bot}} - P_{\text{top}}) dA = \rho g (h_2 - h_1) dA = \rho g \, dV$$

where $dV$ is the volume of the elemental column. Integrating over the entire body:

$$F_B = \int_V \rho g \, dV = \rho g V$$

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho g V_{\text{displaced}}}$$

This is the buoyant force — equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

## 3.5 Derivation V: Metacentric Height

A floating body is in equilibrium when the weight $W$ acts downward through $G$, and the buoyant force $F_B$ acts upward through $B$ (centre of buoyancy). When the body tilts by a small angle $\theta$:

1. The centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$ (the new centroid of the submerged volume).
2. The line of action of the new buoyant force passes through $B_1$ vertically upward.
3. The metacentre $M$ is defined as the point where the **new** line of action of $F_B$ intersects the **original** vertical line through $B$ (and $G$).

The metacentric radius:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline cross-section about the longitudinal axis of rotation.

The metacentric height:

$$\boxed{GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}} - BG}$$

For a **rectangular** floating body of length $L$ and breadth $B$ (with $B$ being the dimension perpendicular to the tilt axis):

$$I = \frac{L B^3}{12} \quad \text{or} \quad I = \frac{B L^3}{12}$$

depending on which axis tilting occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Sign convention for $BG$ — $B$ is **below** $G$ in most floating bodies (because the heavier material is concentrated low). If the problem provides $GB$ (which is positive when $B$ is below $G$), then $BG = GB$ and $GM = BM - GB$. If the problem gives $GB$ with $G$ below $B$ (rare, e.g., a cork), use $GM = BM + GB$.

## 3.6 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A circular plate of diameter $1.5 \; \text{m}$ is placed vertically in water with its top edge at the free water surface. Find (a) the total hydrostatic force on the plate, and (b) the depth of the center of pressure. Take $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

### Part (a): Resultant Force

Area of circular plate:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.5)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 2.25 = 1.767 \; \text{m}^2$$

The top edge is at the free surface, so the centroid is at depth:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \; \text{m}$$

Total hydrostatic force:

$$F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.75 \times 1.767$$

$$F_R = 9810 \times 0.75 \times 1.767 = 9810 \times 1.3253 = 13000.97 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_R \approx 13.001 \; \text{kN}}$$

### Part (b): Depth of Center of Pressure

For a circle, the second moment of area about the centroidal axis (horizontal diameter) is:

$$I_G = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (1.5)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = 0.2485 \; \text{m}^4$$

The surface is vertical, so $\theta = 90°$ and $\sin^2\theta = 1$. Depth of center of pressure:

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} = 0.75 + \frac{0.2485 \times 1}{0.75 \times 1.767}$$

$$h^* = 0.75 + \frac{0.2485}{1.3253} = 0.75 + 0.1875 = 0.9375 \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h^* = 0.9375 \; \text{m} = \frac{5d}{8}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by direct geometry:** For a circular plate with top edge at the free surface, the center of pressure is at $\dfrac{5d}{8}$ from the top. This matches the formula derivation — confirming the calculation is correct.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of "Forces on Solid Surfaces"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Forces on Solid Surfaces"] --> B0["Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surfaces"]
    A0 --> C0["Hydrostatic Force on Curved Surfaces"]
    A0 --> D0["Buoyancy and Flotation"]
    A0 --> E0["Stability of Floating Bodies"]

    B0 --> B1["Vertical Surface"]
    B0 --> B2["Horizontal Surface"]
    B0 --> B3["Inclined Surface"]
    B0 --> B4["Center of Pressure Calculation"]

    C0 --> C1["Horizontal Force Component FH"]
    C0 --> C2["Vertical Force Component FV"]
    C0 --> C3["Resultant Force FR"]

    D0 --> D1["Archimedes Principle"]
    D0 --> D2["Weight of Fluid Displaced"]
    D0 --> D3["Floating Equilibrium"]

    E0 --> E1["Centre of Buoyancy B"]
    E0 --> E2["Centre of Gravity G"]
    E0 --> E3["Metacentre M"]
    E0 --> E4["Metacentric Height GM"]

    B1 --> K1["F = rho g h_c A"]
    B4 --> K2["y star equals I_o over y bar A"]
    C3 --> K3["F_R equals sqrt FH squared plus FV squared"]
    D3 --> K4["W equals rho g V_disp"]
    E4 --> K5["GM equals I over V_sub minus BG"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Algorithm for Solving Plane-Surface Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Plane submerged surface problem"] --> S2["Read geometry: shape, size, depth, angle"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute area A and centroidal depth h_bar"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute pressure at centroid: P_G = rho g h_bar"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute resultant force: F_R = P_G times A"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute second moment of area: I_G about centroidal axis"]
    S6 --> S7["Compute slant distance of centroid: y_bar = h_bar over sin theta"]
    S7 --> S8["Compute center of pressure: y_star = I_G plus A y_bar squared over y_bar A"]
    S8 --> S9["Convert to vertical depth: h_star = y_star sin theta"]
    S9 --> S10["State F_R magnitude, direction, and h_star"]
    S10 --> S11["End: Solution complete"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Curved Surface Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CURV["Curved Submerged Surface"] --> PROJ["Vertical Projection AV"]
    CURV --> COL["Fluid Column Above Curve"]

    PROJ --> FH["Horizontal Force FH equals rho g h_V A_V"]
    COL --> FV["Vertical Force FV equals rho g V_fluid"]

    FH --> RES["Resultant FR equals sqrt FH squared plus FV squared"]
    FV --> RES

    RES --> ANG["Angle theta equals arctan FV over FH"]
    RES --> LOC["Line of action passes through intersection of FH and FV lines"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Floating Body Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FB["Floating Body Given"] --> WM["Weight W and Submerged Volume V_sub"]
    WM --> CG["Locate Centre of Gravity G"]
    WM --> CB["Locate Centre of Buoyancy B"]
    CB --> GB["Compute BG = distance between G and B"]
    CB --> WL["Compute second moment I of waterline area"]
    WL --> BM["Compute BM = I over V_sub"]
    BM --> GM["Compute GM = BM minus BG"]
    GM --> STAB{"Is GM greater than 0?"}
    STAB -->|Yes| SS["STABLE: Body returns to upright"]
    STAB -->|Zero| SN["NEUTRAL: Body stays at new tilt"]
    STAB -->|No| SU["UNSTABLE: Body capsizes"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram instead of a sketch?** Mermaid cannot render a faithful free-body diagram of a dam or ship. The functional architecture flows above preserve the **logical structure** of each concept, which is the high-yield takeaway for KTU exams — examiners reward method clarity as much as numerical answers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2-Mark/3-Mark Pattern]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — Conceptual

**Q: State Archimedes' Principle. A solid body of volume $0.02 \; \text{m}^3$ is fully immersed in a liquid of specific gravity $0.85$. Calculate the buoyant force acting on the body. Take $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Archimedes' Principle:** *When a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.*

Density of the liquid: $\rho = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$.

Buoyant force: $F_B = \rho g V = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 = 166.77 \; \text{N}$.

$$\boxed{F_B \approx 166.77 \; \text{N}}$$

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — Definition / Short Derivation

**Q: Define "center of pressure" for a plane submerged surface. Show that for a vertical rectangular surface of height $h$ and width $b$ with its top edge at the free surface, the center of pressure lies at a depth of $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ from the top.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *The center of pressure is the point on a submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic thrust is considered to act.*

For a vertical rectangular surface ($b \times h$) with top at free surface:
- $\bar{h} = h/2$, $A = b h$.
- $I_G = bh^3 / 12$.

Depth of center of pressure:

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{bh^3/12}{(h/2)(bh)} = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{h}{6} = \frac{3h + h}{6} = \frac{4h}{6} = \frac{2h}{3}$$

$$\boxed{h^* = \frac{2h}{3}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Pattern, Module 1]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**A vertical rectangular gate $1.5 \; \text{m}$ wide and $2 \; \text{m}$ high** is installed in the side wall of a water tank. The **top of the gate is $1 \; \text{m}$ below the free water surface**.

(a) **Calculate the magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force** on the gate. (7 marks)
(b) **Determine the location of the center of pressure** measured from the free water surface. Also, find the depth of the center of pressure **below the centroid**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
- Width of gate: $b = 1.5 \; \text{m}$
- Height of gate: $h = 2 \; \text{m}$
- Depth of top edge below free surface: $h_1 = 1 \; \text{m}$
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Part (a): Resultant Force**

Area of gate:

$$A = b \times h = 1.5 \times 2 = 3 \; \text{m}^2$$

Depth of centroid (mid-height of the gate):

$$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{h}{2} = 1 + \frac{2}{2} = 1 + 1 = 2 \; \text{m}$$

Resultant hydrostatic force:

$$F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 3 = 58860 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_R = 58.86 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Part (b): Center of Pressure**

Second moment of area of the rectangle about its centroidal horizontal axis:

$$I_G = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times (2)^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8}{12} = \frac{12}{12} = 1 \; \text{m}^4$$

Since the gate is vertical, $\theta = 90°$ and $\sin^2\theta = 1$. Depth of center of pressure from free surface:

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} = 2 + \frac{1 \times 1}{2 \times 3} = 2 + \frac{1}{6}$$

$$h^* = 2 + 0.1667 = 2.1667 \; \text{m}$$

Depth of center of pressure below the centroid:

$$h^* - \bar{h} = 2.1667 - 2 = 0.1667 \; \text{m} = \frac{1}{6} \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h^* = 2.1667 \; \text{m} \quad \text{and} \quad h^* - \bar{h} = 0.1667 \; \text{m below centroid}}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**A circular gate of diameter $2 \; \text{m}$** is provided in an inclined surface making an angle of **60° with the horizontal**. The **top edge of the gate is at a depth of $2 \; \text{m}$** below the free water surface.

(a) **Determine the magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force** acting on the gate. (7 marks)
(b) **Locate the position of the center of pressure** along the inclined surface measured from the free surface intersection. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
- Diameter: $d = 2 \; \text{m}$, radius $r = 1 \; \text{m}$
- Angle with horizontal: $\theta = 60°$
- Depth of top edge: $h_1 = 2 \; \text{m}$
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Part (a): Resultant Force**

Area of circular gate:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (2)^2}{4} = \pi \; \text{m}^2 = 3.1416 \; \text{m}^2$$

Depth of centroid (at the center of the gate, which is $d/2 = 1 \; \text{m}$ below the top edge):

$$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{d}{2} = 2 + 1 = 3 \; \text{m}$$

Resultant force:

$$F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3 \times 3.1416 = 92470.6 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_R \approx 92.47 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Part (b): Center of Pressure**

Second moment of area of a circle about its centroidal diameter:

$$I_G = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (2)^4}{64} = \frac{16\pi}{64} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 \; \text{m}^4$$

Slant distance of centroid from free surface (along the plane):

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\bar{h}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{3}{\sin 60°} = \frac{3}{0.8660} = 3.4641 \; \text{m}$$

Slant distance of center of pressure:

$$y^* = \frac{I_G + A \bar{y}^2}{\bar{y} A} = \frac{I_G}{\bar{y} A} + \bar{y}$$

$$y^* = \frac{0.7854}{3.4641 \times 3.1416} + 3.4641 = \frac{0.7854}{10.882} + 3.4641$$

$$y^* = 0.0722 + 3.4641 = 3.5363 \; \text{m}$$

Vertical depth of center of pressure:

$$h^* = y^* \sin\theta = 3.5363 \times 0.8660 = 3.0625 \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{y^* = 3.5363 \; \text{m along the plane}, \quad h^* = 3.0625 \; \text{m below free surface}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the angle conversion:** Many students compute $\bar{y}$ as $\bar{h}$ itself, forgetting to divide by $\sin\theta$. This gives the wrong location of the center of pressure.
> 2. **Confusing second moment of area:** For a circle, the second moment about a **diameter** is $\pi d^4 / 64$, not $\pi d^4 / 32$ (which is the polar moment of inertia).
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Always keep $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $g$ in $\text{m/s}^2$, and dimensions in $\text{m}$ to get force in Newtons.
> 4. **Sign error in $GM$ formula:** $GM = BM - BG$ (not $GM = BM + BG$). Use the **parallel axis theorem** correctly: $I_{xx,o} = I_G + A\bar{y}^2$, not $I_G - A\bar{y}^2$.
> 5. **Curved surface error:** Students often write the vertical force as "weight of fluid below" — it is the weight of fluid **above** the curved surface up to the free surface.
> 6. **For atmospheric pressure:** In KTU problems, use **gauge pressure** unless explicitly stated. Do not add $P_{atm}$ to the force calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):**

- **Hydrostatic pressure** varies **linearly** with depth: $P = \rho g h$.
- **Resultant force on a plane surface**: $F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A$. The pressure at the centroid times the area.
- **Center of pressure** is **always below the centroid** (unless the surface is horizontal, in which case they coincide).
- **Center of pressure formula (slant):** $y^* = \dfrac{I_{xx,o}}{\bar{y} A} = \dfrac{I_{xx,c} + A\bar{y}^2}{\bar{y} A}$.
- **Center of pressure formula (vertical):** $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$.
- **For a top-edge-at-surface vertical rectangle:** $F_R = \dfrac{\rho g b h^2}{2}$ acting at $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ from the top.
- **For a fully submerged vertical rectangle (top at depth $h_1$):** $F_R = \rho g (h_1 + h/2) (b h)$ acting at $h_1 + \dfrac{2h^3 + 6h_1 h^2}{6h(h_1 + h/2)}$... or simply use the universal formula.
- **Curved surface:** Split into $F_H$ (on vertical projection) and $F_V$ (weight of fluid column above).
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}$.
- **Floating condition:** Weight of body = Weight of fluid displaced.
- **Metacentric height:** $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}} - BG$.
- **Stability:** $GM > 0$ stable, $GM = 0$ neutral, $GM < 0$ unstable.
- **Time period of oscillation:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$, where $k$ is the radius of gyration of the floating body.
- **Always use $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$** unless the problem specifies a different value.
- **Draw a free-body diagram** showing the surface, fluid column, centroid, and center of pressure for full marks.
- **For KTU Module 1, the metacentre concept is in CO1/CO2** — practice at least one numerical involving $BM$, $BG$, and $GM$.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1: Forces on Solid Surfaces**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyant Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyant Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buoyant Force (Hydrostatic Upthrust):** When a body is either fully or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a net upward vertical force on the body equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. This upward force is called the **Buoyant Force** or **Upthrust**, and it acts through the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**, which coincides with the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

### Archimedes' Principle (The Governing Law)

> [!NOTE]
> **Archimedes' Principle (287–212 BC):** *"Any body fully or partially submerged in a fluid at rest is acted upon by an upward buoyant force, the magnitude of which is exactly equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

This principle is the foundation of **fluid statics** and is empirically verified for all Newtonian fluids under standard gravitational conditions ($g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing a **cork** under water in a bucket. The instant you release it, the cork shoots back up to the surface. Why?

The water *below* the cork is pushing it **upward**, while the water *above* it is pushing it **downward**. Because fluid pressure increases with depth (Pascal's law: $P = \rho g h$), the upward push from the bottom of the cork is **stronger** than the downward push from the top. The *net* of all these tiny pressure forces over the entire wetted surface is what we call the **buoyant force**.

Think of it this way:

| Real-World Phenomenon | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Cork shooting up in water | Submerged body experiences net upward force |
| Heavy steel ship floating in the ocean | Average density of ship < density of seawater |
| Submerging a balloon pulls it down more in air | Buoyant force in air = weight of air displaced |
| Hydrometer floating higher in denser liquid | Principle used to measure specific gravity |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Buoyancy is *not* a new fundamental force — it is simply the **resultant of hydrostatic pressure acting on a curved submerged surface**. You are essentially performing a **pressure integration** and getting a single equivalent force vector.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are commonly used in KTU board numericals:

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- Density of water at 4 °C: $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$
- Density of mercury: $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Density of standard air at 15 °C: $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

---

## 1.4 Centre of Buoyancy vs Centre of Gravity

Two distinct points govern submerged and floating bodies:

- **Centre of Gravity (G):** Point through which the body's *total weight* acts. Determined by mass distribution of the solid.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** Point through which the *buoyant force* acts. Always equal to the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a homogeneous body fully submerged in a uniform fluid, G and B *coincide* only if the body is also of constant cross-section (like a vertical cylinder). In general, they are *separate points* and their relative location determines **stability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Buoyancy — The Pressure-Integration View

Consider an arbitrarily shaped body fully submerged in a static fluid. Pick any elemental area $dA$ on the submerged surface. The fluid exerts a pressure force:

$$dF = P \cdot dA = \rho_f g h \cdot dA$$

directed *normal* (perpendicular) to the surface at that point.

The horizontal components of these pressure forces cancel pairwise (by symmetry of the pressure field on opposite sides of the body). The **vertical components add up** because pressure increases with depth. The resultant of all vertical components is the **buoyant force**, and the point through which it acts is the **centroid of the displaced volume**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Solving Buoyancy Problems

Step 1 → Identify whether the body is **fully submerged** or **partially submerged (floating)**.

Step 2 → Calculate the **displaced volume** $V_d$:
- For fully submerged: $V_d$ = total body volume $V$
- For floating: $V_d$ = submerged portion of the body

Step 3 → Compute the buoyant force using:
$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d = \gamma_f \cdot V_d$$

Step 4 → Compare $F_B$ with the **actual weight** $W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V$:

| Condition | Result |
|---|---|
| $F_B > W$ | Body **rises** (floats up to surface) |
