| $F_B = W$ | Body is in **neutral equilibrium** (floats fully submerged) |
| $F_B < W$ | Body **sinks** to the bottom |

Step 5 → The **apparent weight** in fluid is:
$$W_{app} = W - F_B = (\rho_b - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot V$$

---

## 2.3 Stability of Floating Bodies — Metacentre Theory

When a floating body is slightly tilted by an external disturbance (e.g., wave action), the centre of buoyancy **shifts** from its original position $B$ to a new position $B'$. The vertical line through $B'$ intersects the original vertical axis (centre line of the body) at a point called the **Metacentre (M)**.

### Metacentric Height ($GM$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentric Height ($GM$):** The distance between the centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M) along the body's vertical axis. It is the single most important parameter in determining the rotational stability of a floating vessel.

- If $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (M lies above G)
- If $GM = 0$ → **Neutral equilibrium**
- If $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (M lies below G)

### Components of Metacentric Height

The metacentric height can be decomposed into three measurable distances:

$$GM = BM - BG = BM + KB - KG$$

Where each term is rigorously defined below.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** you must memorize for KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units (SI) | Governing Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant Force (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$ | $\text{N}$ | Any submerged body |
| 2 | Buoyant Force (using $\gamma$) | $F_B = \gamma_f \cdot V_d$ | $\text{N}$ | Any submerged body |
| 3 | Apparent Weight | $W_{app} = W - F_B$ | $\text{N}$ | Fully submerged |
| 4 | Fractional Submergence (Floating) | $\dfrac{V_d}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$ | dimensionless | Floating equilibrium |
| 5 | Specific Gravity (Hydrometer logic) | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{body}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | Reference = water at 4 °C |
| 6 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d}$ | $\text{m}$ | Small tilt angles ($\le 15°$) |
| 7 | Metacentric Height | $GM = BM - BG$ | $\text{m}$ | Stability check |
| 8 | Metacentric Height (expanded) | $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG$ | $\text{m}$ | Practical application |
| 9 | Oscillation Period of Floating Body | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | $\text{s}$ | Rolling motion |
| 10 | Time Period of Rolling | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_V}{W \cdot GM}}$ | $\text{s}$ | Where $I_V$ = mass moment of inertia |
| 11 | Stability of Submerged Body | Stable if G is **below** B | — | Fully submerged |
| 12 | Condition for Floating | $\rho_{body} < \rho_{fluid}$ | — | Equilibrium floatation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never confuse $BG$ with $GB$.** The convention in KTU textbooks (and in the standard fluid mechanics convention) is $BG = \overline{BG}$ measured along the centre line, **always taken as positive when G lies above B**. The formula $GM = BM - BG$ holds with $BG$ positive in that case.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Buoyant force analysis is foundational in:

- **Naval Architecture**: Ship hull design, ballast tank operation, metacentric height regulation.
- **Hydrometer Design**: Measuring specific gravity of battery acid, milk, alcohol, and petroleum products.
- **Submarine Engineering**: Variable ballast for controlled diving and surfacing.
- **Hydraulic Engineering**: Dredging, caissons for underwater construction, buoyancy-assisted pipelines.
- **Aerospace**: Hot-air balloons, lighter-than-air dirigibles, buoyancy-driven underwater gliders (AUVs).
- **Civil Engineering**: Cofferdams, floating docks, pontoon bridges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buoyant Force from Pressure Integration

### Setup
Consider a fully submerged, irregularly shaped body in a static fluid of density $\rho_f$. Let an elemental horizontal strip at depth $h$ below the free surface have:
- Top horizontal area: $dA_t$
- Bottom horizontal area: $dA_b$
- Vertical thickness: $dh$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Pressure at the top of the strip (depth $h$):
$$P_t = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Downward force on the top face:
$$dF_{down} = P_t \cdot dA_t = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA_t$$

**Step 3:** Pressure at the bottom of the strip (depth $h + dh$):
$$P_b = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + dh)$$

**Step 4:** Upward force on the bottom face:
$$dF_{up} = P_b \cdot dA_b = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + dh) \cdot dA_b$$

**Step 5:** Net upward force on the strip:
$$dF_B = dF_{up} - dF_{down}$$

**Step 6:** The body is bounded laterally by a vertical projection. By the principle of equal projected areas (for a closed surface, projected area on any horizontal plane equals the cross-sectional area of the body at that depth), we can write:
$$dA_t = dA_b = dA$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back:
$$dF_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + dh) \cdot dA - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA$$

**Step 8:** Simplifying:
$$dF_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot dh \cdot dA$$

**Step 9:** Recognize that $dh \cdot dA$ is the differential volume $dV$ of the strip. Integrating over the entire body volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= \int_V \rho_f \cdot g \, dV \\
&= \rho_f \cdot g \int_V dV \\
&= \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10:** Final boxed result (Archimedes' Principle proven):

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The buoyant force depends *only* on the **fluid density** and **displaced volume** — *not* on the material, weight, shape, or surface texture of the submerged body.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Metacentric Height ($GM$) Formula

### Setup
A floating body is given a small angular tilt $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis. The metacentre $M$ is defined as the intersection of:
1. The original vertical line through the centre of buoyancy (at the undisturbed float position).
2. The new vertical line through the *tilted* centre of buoyancy $B'$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Original displaced volume = $V_d$. After tilt, displaced volume is still $V_d$ (incompressible fluid, body still floating). However, the *shape* of the displaced volume changes — the wedge on one side enters the water, and the wedge on the other side emerges.

**Step 2:** Consider a small angular tilt $\theta$. The horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy is:
$$\Delta B = \frac{\int x \, dV}{V_d} = \frac{\text{First Moment of Wedge Volume}}{V_d}$$

**Step 3:** The wedge that has *entered* the water has volume:
$$dV_{wedge} = \int_0^L (x \cdot \theta) \, dx \cdot dA_{strip} = \theta \int x \, dA_{strip}$$

This integral is the **second moment of area** $I$ of the waterline plane cross-section about the longitudinal axis of tilting.

$$dV_{wedge} = \theta \cdot I$$

**Step 4:** The shift of the centre of buoyancy is:
$$BB' = \frac{\theta \cdot I}{V_d}$$

**Step 5:** The metacentre $M$ lies on the original vertical, and on the new vertical through $B'$. Therefore, in the small-angle limit:

$$\sin \theta \approx \theta = \frac{BB'}{BM}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $BM$:
$$BM = \frac{BB'}{\theta} = \frac{I}{V_d}$$

**Step 7:** From the geometry along the centreline, the distance from $B$ to $G$ is $BG$ (positive if $G$ is above $B$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM &= BM - BG \\
&= \frac{I}{V_d} - BG
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Final boxed result:

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V_d} - BG \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad GM = \frac{I}{V_d} + KB - KG}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** The body is **stable** when $GM > 0$. The body is **unstable** when $GM < 0$ (capsizes). For passenger ships, regulatory bodies (IMO) require $GM \ge 0.15 \, \text{m}$ as a minimum.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stability Summary Table

| Body State | Condition | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Fully submerged, $\rho_b < \rho_f$ | Body rises | Stable only if G lies **below** B |
| Fully submerged, $\rho_b = \rho_f$ | Neutral suspension | Neutral regardless of G position |
| Fully submerged, $\rho_b > \rho_f$ | Body sinks | Unstable |
| Floating, $\rho_{avg} < \rho_f$ | Equilibrium float | Stable iff $GM > 0$ |
| Floating, tilting by small $\theta$ | Oscillates about M | Rolling time period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{gGM}}$ |

Where:
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation
- $V_d$ = Displaced volume
- $k$ = Radius of gyration of the body about its longitudinal axis

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cubical block of side $0.5 \, \text{m}$ and density $800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ is fully submerged in water. Determine:
(a) The buoyant force on the block.
(b) The apparent weight of the block in water.
(c) Whether the block will float or sink when released.

**Solution:**

**(a) Buoyant Force:**
Volume of block: $V = (0.5)^3 = 0.125 \, \text{m}^3$
Since fully submerged: $V_d = V = 0.125 \, \text{m}^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_d \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.125 \\
&= 1226.25 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Actual Weight and Apparent Weight:**
Actual weight: $W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V = 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.125 = 981 \, \text{N}$

Apparent weight:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{app} &= W - F_B \\
&= 981 - 1226.25 \\
&= -245.25 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative** apparent weight confirms the block is **buoyed upward**.

**(c) Float or Sink?**
Since $\rho_b = 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3 < \rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, the block will **float**.

Depth of submergence at equilibrium:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_w} \times L \\
&= \frac{800}{1000} \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.4 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $0.4 \, \text{m}$ of the block will be below the waterline, and $0.1 \, \text{m}$ will protrude above.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Buoyancy Analysis Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Buoyancy Problem]) --> A{Is body fully submerged or floating?}
    A -- Fully Submerged --> B[Compute Volume V of Body]
    A -- Floating --> C[Assume floating equilibrium: FB = W]
    B --> D[Compute FB = rho_f × g × V]
    C --> E[Find Submerged Depth h]
    E --> F[Compute V_d from h]
    F --> G[Verify FB equals W]
    D --> H{Compare FB vs W}
    G --> H
    H -- FB greater than W --> I[Body Accelerates Upward]
    H -- FB equals W --> J[Equilibrium: Floating or Suspended]
    H -- FB less than W --> K[Body Sinks to Bottom]
    I --> End([End])
    J --> End
    K --> End
```

## 4.2 Stability of Floating Body — Metacentre Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FloatingBody["Floating Vessel Cross-Section"]
        M[Metacentre M]:::stableNode
        G[Centre of Gravity G]:::neutralNode
        B[Centre of Buoyancy B]:::neutralNode
        WL[Waterline Plane]:::waterNode
        K[Keel Point K]:::neutralNode
    end

    M -- Distance BM --> B
    B -- Distance KB --> K
    K -- Distance KG --> G
    G -- Distance GM --> M

    classDef stableNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef neutralNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef waterNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#191970,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The metacentre $M$ is located above the centre of gravity $G$. The vertical distance $GM$ (the metacentric height) is the stability indicator. For a stable vessel, $GM > 0$.

## 4.3 Submerged Body — Pressure Force Balance Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PressureTop[Pressure on Top Face: P_top × A] -->|Acts Downward| NetForce[Net Vertical Force]
    PressureSide[Pressure on Side Walls: Horizontal Components] -->|Cancel Pairwise| NetForce
    PressureBottom[Pressure on Bottom Face: P_bottom × A] -->|Acts Upward| NetForce
    NetForce --> BuoyancyResult[Buoyant Force FB upward]
    BuoyancyResult --> Centroid[Acts at Centroid of Displaced Volume = Centre of Buoyancy B]
```

## 4.4 Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Floating or Fully Submerged?} -->|Floating| Q2{Compute GM = I/V_d minus BG}
    Q1 -->|Fully Submerged| Q3{Is G below B?}
    Q2 -->|GM greater than 0| R1[Stable]
    Q2 -->|GM equals 0| R2[Neutral]
    Q2 -->|GM less than 0| R3[Unstable: Capsize]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R4[Stable]
    Q3 -->|No| R5[Unstable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State and explain Archimedes' Principle. A solid metal block weighing $150 \, \text{N}$ in air appears to weigh $110 \, \text{N}$ when fully immersed in water. Determine: (i) the buoyant force, and (ii) the specific gravity of the metal.*

**Model Answer:**

Archimedes' Principle states that when a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

(i) Buoyant force:
$$F_B = W_{air} - W_{water} = 150 - 110 = 40 \, \text{N}$$

(ii) Volume of metal (since $F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$):
$$V = \frac{F_B}{\rho_w \cdot g} = \frac{40}{1000 \times 9.81} = 4.077 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$$

Density of metal:
$$\rho_m = \frac{W_{air}}{g \cdot V} = \frac{150}{9.81 \times 4.077 \times 10^{-3}} = 3758.4 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

Specific gravity:
$$\boxed{SG = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} = \frac{3758.4}{1000} = 3.76}$$

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define: (a) Centre of Buoyancy, (b) Metacentre, and (c) Metacentric Height. Explain how the metacentric height governs the stability of a floating body.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The point on a submerged or floating body through which the resultant buoyant force acts. It is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

**(b) Metacentre (M):** The point on the vertical line through the original centre of buoyancy where the new vertical line (after a small angular tilt of the body) intersects it.

**(c) Metacentric Height (GM):** The vertical distance between the centre of gravity $G$ and the metacentre $M$. It is given by:
$$GM = \frac{I}{V_d} - BG$$

**Stability criterion:**
- If $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (restoring couple exists on tilting).
- If $GM = 0$ → **Neutral** (no restoring couple).
- If $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (capsizing couple exists).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — most common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign convention errors** in $BG$ — remember $BG$ is **positive when G lies above B**.
> 3. **Confusing fully submerged vs floating** — displaced volume is *not* always equal to total body volume.
> 4. **Not stating the stability conclusion** explicitly — examiners want a clear "stable/unstable" verdict.
> 5. **Skipping the free-body diagram** for floating body problems — this alone can cost you 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — Question A [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A wooden cylinder of diameter $0.4 \, \text{m}$ and height $0.6 \, \text{m}$ has a specific gravity of $0.75$. It is floating in water with its axis vertical.

**(a)** Determine the depth of immersion and the position of the centre of buoyancy relative to the base of the cylinder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the cylinder is tilted by a small angle about its axis, determine the metacentric height and comment on the stability of the cylinder. Take the mass moment of inertia of the cylinder about its longitudinal axis as $I_V = m k^2$ where $k = 0.058 \, \text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Diameter $D = 0.4 \, \text{m}$, so radius $r = 0.2 \, \text{m}$
- Height $H = 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- Specific gravity $SG = 0.75$, so $\rho_b = 750 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $k = 0.058 \, \text{m}$

**Part (a):** Depth of immersion

Volume of cylinder:
$$V = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times (0.2)^2 \times 0.6 = 0.0754 \, \text{m}^3$$

Weight of cylinder:
$$W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V = 750 \times 9.81 \times 0.0754 = 555.1 \, \text{N}$$

For floating equilibrium, $F_B = W$:
$$\rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_d = W$$
$$V_d = \frac{W}{\rho_w \cdot g} = \frac{555.1}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.0566 \, \text{m}^3$$

Since the cylinder floats with axis vertical, the immersed portion is also cylindrical. Let $h$ be the immersion depth:
$$\pi r^2 \cdot h = 0.0566$$
$$h = \frac{0.0566}{\pi \times 0.04} = 0.45 \, \text{m}$$

The centre of buoyancy is at the centroid of the immersed volume, i.e., at $h/2$ from the base:
$$\boxed{h = 0.45 \, \text{m}, \quad \overline{KB} = \frac{h}{2} = 0.225 \, \text{m}}$$

**Part (b):** Metacentric height and stability

Second moment of area of waterline cross-section (circle) about a diameter:
$$I = \frac{\pi r^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^4}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^4$$

Metacentric radius:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V_d} = \frac{1.257 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0566} = 0.0222 \, \text{m}$$

Total height of cylinder $H = 0.6 \, \text{m}$, so the centre of gravity $G$ lies at $H/2 = 0.3 \, \text{m}$ from the base (assuming uniform density).
$$KB = 0.225 \, \text{m}, \quad KG = 0.3 \, \text{m}$$
$$BG = KG - KB = 0.3 - 0.225 = 0.075 \, \text{m}$$

Metacentric height:
$$
\begin{aligned}
GM &= BM - BG \\
&= 0.0222 - 0.075 \\
&= -0.0528 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $GM < 0$, the cylinder is **UNSTABLE** in this orientation (it will capsize).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A key teaching point is that floating cylinders are often *more stable when floated on their side*, because the waterline area (and hence $I$) increases dramatically. Try the calculation with the cylinder lying horizontally to see the contrast.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Question B [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A rectangular pontoon of length $L = 6 \, \text{m}$, breadth $B = 4 \, \text{m}$, and height $H = 2 \, \text{m}$ weighs $W = 200 \, \text{kN}$ when empty. It floats in seawater of density $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(a)** Determine the depth of immersion when floating at rest, and the location of the centre of buoyancy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the metacentric height and the time period of oscillation (rolling) if the pontoon is given a small angular displacement. Assume the radius of gyration $k = 1.8 \, \text{m}$ about the longitudinal axis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $L = 6 \, \text{m}, \quad B = 4 \, \text{m}, \quad H = 2 \, \text{m}$
- $W = 200 \, \text{kN} = 200{,}000 \, \text{N}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $k = 1.8 \, \text{m}$

**Part (a):** Depth of immersion

For floating equilibrium:
$$F_B = W$$
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot h = W$$
$$h = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{200{,}000}{1025 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 4}$$

$$h = \frac{200{,}000}{241{,}308} = 0.829 \, \text{m}$$

Centre of buoyancy (above keel):
$$\boxed{KB = \frac{h}{2} = 0.4145 \, \text{m}}$$

Displaced volume:
$$V_d = L \cdot B \cdot h = 6 \times 4 \times 0.829 = 19.896 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Part (b):** Metacentric height

Second moment of area of waterline plane (rectangle $L \times B$) about the longitudinal axis (length $L$):
$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = 32 \, \text{m}^4$$

Metacentric radius:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V_d} = \frac{32}{19.896} = 1.608 \, \text{m}$$

Assuming the pontoon has uniform weight distribution, its CG lies at $H/2 = 1 \, \text{m}$ from the base.
$$KG = 1 \, \text{m}, \quad KB = 0.4145 \, \text{m}$$
$$BG = KG - KB = 1 - 0.4145 = 0.5855 \, \text{m}$$

Metacentric height:
$$
\begin{aligned}
GM &= BM - BG \\
&= 1.608 - 0.5855 \\
&= 1.0225 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $GM > 0$, the pontoon is **STABLE**.

Time period of rolling:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(1.8)^2}{9.81 \times 1.0225}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{3.24}{10.031}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{0.323} \\
&= 2\pi \times 0.568 \\
&= 3.57 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{GM = 1.0225 \, \text{m} \quad \text{(Stable)}, \quad T = 3.57 \, \text{s}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it handy before your KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Archimedes' Principle** is the governing law of buoyancy — state it word-for-word if asked for 2–3 marks.
- ✅ **Buoyant force**: $F_B = \rho_f g V_d$ — independent of body material or shape.
- ✅ **Apparent weight**: $W_{app} = W - F_B$ — negative means body is buoyant.
- ✅ **Floating condition**: $F_B = W$ at equilibrium; depth of submergence is found by equating these.
- ✅ **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** is the *centroid of displaced fluid*, NOT the centroid of the body.
- ✅ **Centre of Gravity (G)** is the centroid of the body's *mass*.
- ✅ **Metacentre (M)** is defined only for *floating* bodies under small angular tilt.
- ✅ **Metacentric Height**: $GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG$.
- ✅ **Stability criterion**: $GM > 0$ → stable; $GM = 0$ → neutral; $GM < 0$ → unstable.
- ✅ **Stability of fully submerged body** depends on whether G lies below B (stable) or above B (unstable).
- ✅ **$I$ in the formula** is the second moment of area of the *waterline plane* about the axis of tilting.
- ✅ **Rolling time period**: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ — higher $GM$ means *faster*, more "stiff" rolling.
- ✅ **Use SI units consistently**: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, densities in $\text{kg/m}^3$, volumes in $\text{m}^3$, forces in $\text{N}$.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** showing $W$ (down through $G$) and $F_B$ (up through $B$) for KTU full-mark answers.
- ✅ **Hydrometer relation**: $\rho_{liquid} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of hydrometer}}{\text{Submerged volume}}$ — useful for SG measurement problems.
- ✅ **Air buoyancy correction**: $W_{true} = W_{measured} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{air}}{\rho_{body}}\right)^{-1}$ — occasionally asked in numericals involving weighing in air.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematics of Fluid Flow: Equations for Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematics of Fluid Flow: Equations for Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the study of **Fluid Kinematics**, we describe fluid motion without necessarily considering the forces that cause it. The *acceleration* of a fluid particle is not simply the time derivative of velocity at a fixed point, because a fluid particle moving through space experiences velocity changes both *temporal* and *spatial*. 

The **Equation for Acceleration** in fluid flow, more formally known as the **Material Derivative** (also called the **Substantial Derivative** or **Total Derivative** following a fluid particle**), is expressed as:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local Acceleration}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective Acceleration}}$$

This is the single most fundamental kinematic identity in continuum mechanics. It decomposes the acceleration of a fluid element into two physically distinct contributions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology Alert:** Examiners expect the term *Material Derivative* (symbol **D/Dt**) in the answer key. Writing only "total derivative" or "d/dt" without specifying it follows a fluid particle will cost marks. Always use the notation $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ for the material derivative.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a river holding a leaf in the water:

- **Local Acceleration** = How fast the *velocity of the water at the spot where the leaf is right now* is changing with time. If a dam releases water upstream, the velocity at your spot will increase over time — even if the leaf is not moving. This is $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$.

- **Convective Acceleration** = How fast the velocity changes as the leaf *drifts downstream* into faster or slower water. Even in a perfectly steady flow, a particle that moves from a slow region into a fast region accelerates. This is $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$.

- **Material (Total) Acceleration** = The actual acceleration experienced by the leaf — the sum of both effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In solid mechanics, a particle's acceleration is simply $d\vec{V}/dt$ because we track *individual particles*. In fluid mechanics, we prefer to describe the flow as a *field* $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ at fixed points, so we need the **Material Derivative** to bridge the two perspectives.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The velocity field $\vec{V}$ has units of **m/s** in SI and **ft/s** in BG units.
- Acceleration has units of **m/s²**.
- The gradient operator $\nabla$ has units of **m⁻¹** in SI.
- The convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ therefore has units of $(\text{m/s})(\text{m}^{-1})(\text{m/s}) = \text{m/s}^2$ — dimensionally consistent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Reference Frames: Lagrangian vs Eulerian

Before deriving the acceleration equation, it is critical to understand the two ways of describing fluid motion:

| Feature | Lagrangian Description | Eulerian Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Observer** | Follows an *individual fluid particle* | Fixed at a *point in space* |
| **Independent Variables** | Time $t$ and initial position $(a, b, c)$ of the particle | Time $t$ and spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ |
| **Field Function** | $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a, b, c, t)$ | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ |
| **Acceleration** | Direct: $\vec{a} = \frac{d^2 \vec{r}}{dt^2}$ | Requires Material Derivative |
| **Used in** | Solid mechanics, particle tracking | CFD, experimental fluid mechanics, KTU board problems |

The Material Derivative is the *mathematical bridge* that converts an Eulerian field description into the Lagrangian acceleration of a fluid particle.

### 2.2 Component-wise Breakdown of the Acceleration

#### (A) Local Acceleration: $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$

- This term is **non-zero only in unsteady flow**.
- It represents the rate of change of velocity at a *fixed spatial point*.
- **Physical meaning:** Unsteadiness of the flow at that location.
- **Example:** Water hammer in a pipe after valve closure; pulsatile blood flow in arteries.

#### (B) Convective Acceleration: $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$

- This term is **non-zero whenever the velocity field is spatially non-uniform**, even in *steady* flow.
- It represents the acceleration a fluid particle experiences as it moves through regions of differing velocity.
- **Physical meaning:** Particle's motion (convection) into a velocity gradient.
- **Example:** Flow in a contracting pipe (converging nozzle) — fluid speeds up simply because the cross-section decreases, even when $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** "Give an example of steady flow in which a fluid particle accelerates." The board's expected answer is *converging/diverging duct flow* — the local term is zero, but the convective term is non-zero.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Cartesian Expansion | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ | Material (total) acceleration following a fluid particle | — | Conceptual |
| 2 | $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Decomposition into local + convective | — | Core identity |
| 3 | $a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ | x-component of acceleration | Full 3-D Cartesian | Most common in KTU |
| 4 | $a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}$ | y-component of acceleration | Full 3-D Cartesian | Most common in KTU |
| 5 | $a_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$ | z-component of acceleration | Full 3-D Cartesian | Most common in KTU |
| 6 | $a_r = \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} - \frac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ | Radial acceleration (cylindrical) | Polar/Cylindrical | Rotating machinery |
| 7 | $a_\theta = \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ | Tangential acceleration (cylindrical) | Polar/Cylindrical | Vortex flow |
| 8 | $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | Material derivative operator | Cartesian | Apply to scalars too (T, p, ρ) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The Material Derivative is *not* exclusive to velocity. It applies to **any** field property — temperature, pressure, density — that is advected with the flow. KTU may ask: "Write the material derivative of temperature." The answer is $\frac{DT}{Dt} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial T}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Navier–Stokes Equations:** The acceleration field $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ is the inertial term that appears on the left-hand side of the Navier–Stokes momentum equation. The Material Derivative is therefore the *foundation* of all viscous flow analysis.
- **Aerodynamics:** Calculating pressure distribution on an airfoil requires evaluating the convective acceleration of streamlines over the curved surface.
- **Turbomachinery:** In a centrifugal pump, fluid particles experience enormous convective acceleration as they move radially outward through the impeller — a textbook case where the local term is zero but acceleration is non-zero.
- **Weather Prediction:** Atmospheric acceleration involves both local (diurnal heating) and convective (advection of air masses) components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Material Derivative in 3-D Cartesian Coordinates

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 1. Examiners have repeatedly asked students to "derive the expression for acceleration of a fluid particle" — typically worth 5–7 marks.

**Starting Point (Eulerian Field):** 
The velocity field is known as a function of position and time:
$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u(x, y, z, t)\hat{i} + v(x, y, z, t)\hat{j} + w(x, y, z, t)\hat{k}$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Lagrangian Perspective:**
A specific fluid particle is identified by its position vector $\vec{r}_p(t) = x_p(t)\hat{i} + y_p(t)\hat{j} + z_p(t)\hat{k}$, which is *time-varying* because the particle moves.

**Step 2 — Define Particle Velocity:**
By definition, the velocity of the particle is the time rate of change of its position:
$$\vec{V}_p = \frac{d\vec{r}_p}{dt} = \frac{dx_p}{dt}\hat{i} + \frac{dy_p}{dt}\hat{j} + \frac{dz_p}{dt}\hat{k} = u_p\hat{i} + v_p\hat{j} + w_p\hat{k}$$

where the subscript $p$ reminds us that the velocity is evaluated *on the trajectory of the particle*.

**Step 3 — Define Particle Acceleration:**
The acceleration of the fluid particle is:
$$\vec{a}_p = \frac{d\vec{V}_p}{dt} = \frac{du_p}{dt}\hat{i} + \frac{dv_p}{dt}\hat{j} + \frac{dw_p}{dt}\hat{k}$$

This is the Lagrangian definition. The challenge: $u_p, v_p, w_p$ are *implicit* functions of time (through $x_p(t), y_p(t), z_p(t)$).

**Step 4 — Apply the Chain Rule of Partial Differentiation:**
The x-component of velocity evaluated on the particle is $u_p(t) = u(x_p(t), y_p(t), z_p(t), t)$. Differentiating with respect to time using the multivariable chain rule:

$$\frac{du_p}{dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\cdot\frac{\partial t}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\cdot\frac{dx_p}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\cdot\frac{dy_p}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{dz_p}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Particle Kinematics:**
Since $\frac{dx_p}{dt} = u_p$, $\frac{dy_p}{dt} = v_p$, $\frac{dz_p}{dt} = w_p$, and $\frac{\partial t}{\partial t} = 1$:

$$\frac{du_p}{dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u_p\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v_p\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w_p\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

**Step 6 — Drop the Subscript (Reinterpret on the Particle Trajectory):**
At the location of the particle, the field velocity equals the particle velocity, so the subscript $p$ is conventionally dropped. We obtain the x-component of material acceleration:

$$a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

**Step 7 — Repeat for y and z Components:**
By identical reasoning (cyclic permutation of $u \to v \to w$ and $x \to y \to z$):

$$a_y = \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}$$

$$a_z = \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

**Step 8 — Combine in Vector Form:**
The total acceleration vector of a fluid particle is:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 9 — Recognize the Compact Form:**
Using the gradient operator $\nabla = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{k}$ and the scalar product $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla = u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$:

$$\boxed{\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Identity Used by Examiners:** It is sometimes useful to rewrite the convective term using the identity $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\frac{\vert \vec{V} \vert^2}{2}\right) + (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \times \vec{V}$. This separation into *vorticity* and *Bernoulli-like* parts is occasionally tested in higher-mark KTU problems involving rotational flow.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (2-D, Steady Flow)

**Problem:** Given the velocity field $\vec{V} = (3x + 2t)\hat{i} + (5y)\hat{j}$ m/s, find the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(2, 1)$ at time $t = 3$ s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Components:**
$u = 3x + 2t$, $v = 5y$, $w = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute Local Acceleration (x-component):**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivatives for Convective Terms:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 5$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Velocity at the Point:**
At $(x, y, t) = (2, 1, 3)$:
$$u = 3(2) + 2(3) = 12 \text{ m/s}, \quad v = 5(1) = 5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Convective Terms:**
$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 5 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 12 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 5 \times 5 = 25 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Assemble Total Acceleration:**
$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2 + 36 + 0 = 38 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 + 0 + 25 = 25 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer (Vector Form):**
$$\vec{a} = 38\hat{i} + 25\hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{38^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{1444 + 625} = \sqrt{2069} \approx 45.49 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology Note for KTU Board:** Always explicitly separate the local and convective contributions in your solution, even if the field is steady. Examiners allocate marks for "identification" and "computation" separately.

### 3.3 Acceleration in Cylindrical Coordinates (Reference Derivation)

For axisymmetric and rotating flows (turbomachinery, vortex problems), KTU students must recognize the additional **centripetal** and **Coriolis-like** terms that appear in cylindrical $(r, \theta, z)$ form:

$$a_r = \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} - \frac{v_\theta^2}{r}$$

$$a_\theta = \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$$

$$a_z = \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

The terms $-\frac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ (centripetal) and $+\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ (Coriolis-type) arise from the curvature of the coordinate system. They are the hallmark of *rotational* kinematics in polar form.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decomposition of Material Acceleration

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical architecture of the material derivative, illustrating how the Eulerian field is processed through the chain rule to produce the Lagrangian acceleration of a fluid particle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Eulerian Velocity Field Vxyzt] --> B[Identify Components u v w]
    B --> C[Partial Derivative with respect to t]
    B --> D[Spatial Gradient of Velocity Field]
    C --> E[Local Acceleration Term: dV/dt]
    D --> F[Convective Term Operator: V dot grad]
    F --> G[Apply Dot Product: u d/dx + v d/dy + w d/dz]
    E --> H[Sum Both Contributions]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Material Acceleration D V over D t]
    I --> J[Output: ax ay az Components]
    J --> K[Apply to Navier Stokes Momentum Equation]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Steady vs Unsteady — Which Acceleration Terms Survive?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Flow Regime] --> Q{Is the flow steady?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Local term = 0]
    Q -- No --> S[Local term nonzero]
    R --> T{Is velocity spatially uniform?}
    S --> U[Convective term also nonzero]
    T -- No --> V[Convective term nonzero]
    T -- Yes --> W[Convective term = 0]
    V --> X[Total acceleration from convection only]
    W --> Y[Zero acceleration uniform steady flow]
    U --> Z[Total acceleration from both terms]
    X --> AA[Example: Converging nozzle]
    Y --> AB[Example: Uniform flow at constant velocity]
    Z --> AC[Example: Pulsatile pipe flow]

    style P fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style X fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style Y fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    style Z fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Particle Path Through a Velocity Gradient

The following architecture diagram (serving as the physical-schematic substitute) traces the journey of a single fluid particle through a non-uniform velocity field, demonstrating why convective acceleration arises even in steady flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Fluid Particle at t = t0] --> Q1[Position x0, Velocity V0]
    Q1 --> R1[After dt Particle Moves to x0 plus V dt]
    R1 --> S1[At New Position Velocity is V0 plus dV dx times V dt]
    S1 --> T1[Change in Velocity: dV]
    T1 --> U1[Divide by dt: Convective Acceleration equals V dV dx]
    U1 --> V1[Local term is zero since steady]

    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style V1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Reference Map: Material Derivative → Downstream KTU Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Module 1: Material Derivative] --> B[Module 2: Continuity Equation]
    A --> C[Module 3: Euler and Bernoulli Equations]
    A --> D[Module 4: Navier Stokes Equation]
    A --> E[Module 5: Dimensional Analysis and Similitude]

    style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style C fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Material Derivative." Why is the material derivative required in fluid mechanics instead of the ordinary time derivative?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Material Derivative** $\frac{D}{Dt}$ represents the time rate of change of a fluid property (such as velocity, temperature, or pressure) *following a specific fluid particle* as it moves through the flow field. Mathematically:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

In fluid mechanics, the Eulerian description gives the velocity as a *field* $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$, defined at every point in space. The ordinary partial derivative $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ measures change only at a *fixed spatial point*. However, a fluid particle *moves* through space, so we must also account for the change in velocity *as the particle moves* from one location to another. The material derivative combines both effects using the chain rule, giving the *true* acceleration experienced by the fluid particle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between local acceleration and convective acceleration. Give one practical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Local Acceleration | Convective Acceleration |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ |
| **Represents** | Rate of change of velocity at a *fixed point* | Rate of change of velocity *as the particle moves* through the field |
| **Present when** | Flow is *unsteady* | Flow has *spatial velocity gradient* |
| **Example** | Water hammer transient in a pipe after sudden valve closure; pulsatile blood flow in the aorta | Steady flow through a *converging nozzle* — fluid speeds up as cross-section decreases, even though $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ |

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the acceleration of a fluid particle in a 3-D Cartesian coordinate system, starting from the Eulerian velocity field. Clearly identify the local and convective components.

**(b)** For the velocity field $\vec{V} = (2xy)\hat{i} + (3t^2 - x^2)\hat{j} + (4yz)\hat{k}$ m/s, determine the acceleration vector of a fluid particle located at the point $(1, 2, 1)$ at the instant $t = 2$ s.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* State the Eulerian velocity field as $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Define particle position $\vec{r}_p(t)$ and note that $u_p(t) = u(x_p(t), y_p(t), z_p(t), t)$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Apply the multivariable chain rule to obtain:
$$\frac{du_p}{dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\frac{dx_p}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\frac{dy_p}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\frac{dz_p}{dt}$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Substitute $\frac{dx_p}{dt} = u$, $\frac{dy_p}{dt} = v$, $\frac{dz_p}{dt} = w$ to get:
$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Write analogous expressions for $a_y$ and $a_z$.

*Step 6 (1 mark):* Combine into compact vector form:
$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Identify components: $u = 2xy$, $v = 3t^2 - x^2$, $w = 4yz$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Compute velocity at point $(1, 2, 1)$, $t = 2$ s:
- $u = 2(1)(2) = 4$ m/s
- $v = 3(4) - 1 = 11$ m/s
- $w = 4(2)(1) = 8$ m/s

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Compute partial derivatives:
- $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$, $\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 6t = 12$, $\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$
- $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2y = 4$, $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2x = 2$, $\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0$
- $\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -2x = -2$, $\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$, $\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0$
- $\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 0$, $\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 4z = 4$, $\frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 4y = 8$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Compute $a_x$:
$$a_x = 0 + 4(4) + 11(2) + 8(0) = 16 + 22 = 38 \text{ m/s}^2$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Compute $a_y$:
$$a_y = 12 + 4(-2) + 11(0) + 8(0) = 12 - 8 = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* Compute $a_z$:
$$a_z = 0 + 4(0) + 11(4) + 8(8) = 0 + 44 + 64 = 108 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = 38\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + 108\hat{k}$ m/s²

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid flow. Why is the Eulerian approach generally preferred in fluid mechanics?

**(b)** The velocity components in a flow field are given by $u = 2x + y$, $v = -x + 3y$, $w = 0$. 
   (i) Determine whether the flow is steady or unsteady.
   (ii) Compute the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 1)$.
   (iii) Is the flow one-, two-, or three-dimensional? Justify.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Define Lagrangian description — observer follows an individual fluid particle; independent variables are time and the initial position $(a, b, c)$ of the particle. Position vector is $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a, b, c, t)$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Define Eulerian description — observer is fixed at a point in space; independent variables are time $t$ and spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$. Field variables are $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$, $p = p(x, y, z, t)$, $\rho = \rho(x, y, z, t)$.

*Step 3 (3 marks):* Discuss preference for Eulerian approach:
- Fluid flow involves infinitely many particles — tracking each is impossible in practice.
- Most experimental instruments (Pitot tubes, hot-wire anemometers, PIV) measure Eulerian quantities.
- Conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) are naturally expressed in field form.
- Compatible with differential calculus and CFD solvers.
- The Material Derivative allows us to recover Lagrangian acceleration from Eulerian data.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Since $u, v, w$ are functions of $(x, y)$ only and not of $t$, the flow is **steady** (no explicit time dependence).

*Step 2 (1 mark):* The flow has two non-zero components $u$ and $v$ depending on two spatial coordinates — therefore it is **two-dimensional**.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Compute partial derivatives at $(1, 1)$:
- $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$, $\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$ (steady)
- $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2$, $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1$
- $\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -1$, $\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Evaluate velocity at $(1, 1)$:
- $u = 2(1) + 1 = 3$ m/s
- $v = -1 + 3(1) = 2$ m/s

*Step 5 (2 marks):* Compute acceleration components:
$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0 + 3(2) + 2(1) = 8 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 + 3(-1) + 2(3) = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = 8\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$ m/s²

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $d$ with $\partial$:** Many students write $\frac{d\vec{V}}{dt}$ instead of $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ for the material derivative. This *is* the same mathematically but loses the conceptual distinction the examiner tests. Always use **D/Dt** to indicate "following a fluid particle."
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the convective term in steady flow:** A common error is to compute $a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ only. Even if the flow is steady, the convective terms $u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ may be non-zero. The board explicitly tests this misconception.
> 
> 3. **Dropping terms in cylindrical coordinates:** When asked to write the acceleration in $(r, \theta, z)$ form, students often forget the centripetal term $-\frac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ and the Coriolis-type term $+\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$. These are mandatory.
> 
> 4. **Sign errors in numerical problems:** When substituting into the formula, students frequently lose 1–2 marks on sign mistakes in $\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 6t$ at the given time. Always substitute *values* of $t$ and $(x, y, z)$ *after* differentiating.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units in numerical answers:** The KTU answer key penalizes missing units (m/s²) on the final acceleration value.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Material Derivative** $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ is the most fundamental kinematic operator in fluid mechanics.
- ✅ It has **two parts**: **Local acceleration** $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ (unsteady) and **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ (spatial gradient).
- ✅ Local term is **zero in steady flow**; convective term is **zero in spatially uniform flow**.
- ✅ In **3-D Cartesian**: $a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ (and cyclic permutations).
- ✅ In **cylindrical coordinates**, watch for the extra **centripetal** ($-\frac{v_\theta^2}{r}$) and **Coriolis** ($+\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$) terms.
- ✅ The Material Derivative applies to **any field scalar**, not just velocity — temperature, pressure, density all follow the same rule.
- ✅ Used in the **inertial term** of the Navier–Stokes equations — foundation of all viscous flow analysis.
- ✅ The **Eulerian description** is preferred in fluid mechanics because of practicality and instrumentation; the **Lagrangian description** is conceptually simpler for tracking individual particles.
- ✅ For numerical problems, always: (1) identify $u, v, w$; (2) compute partial derivatives; (3) evaluate velocity at the given point; (4) assemble the material derivative term-by-term; (5) state the result with units.
- ✅ **Converging nozzle** = canonical KTU example of *steady flow with non-zero acceleration* (purely convective).
- ✅ **Water hammer** = canonical KTU example of *unsteady flow* (local term dominates).
- ✅ Total acceleration magnitude: $|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$ (for 3-D Cartesian).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

# 🌊 Module 1 — The Continuity Equation

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental governing equation in fluid mechanics that mathematically expresses the **principle of conservation of mass** applied to a fluid in motion. It states that the rate of change of mass within a control volume plus the net rate of mass efflux through its control surface is identically zero for any continuous flow field.

In its most general **3-D integral form** for a fixed control volume (CV):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

The **first term** represents the *unsteady accumulation* of mass inside the CV.
The **second term** represents the *net mass flux* leaving the CV through the control surface (CS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO1 / Bloom Level: Apply):**
> The continuity equation is a *kinematic* relation — it does **not** involve any force, pressure, or energy term. It is purely a statement of *mass accounting* and is one of the three foundational governing equations of fluid flow (alongside the Momentum and Energy equations).

---

### 🍎 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Picture

Imagine a **leaky water bucket** connected to an inlet pipe.

- Water flows **in** from the top at a rate of $5 \text{ L/min}$.
- Water leaks **out** from the bottom at a rate of $5 \text{ L/min}$.
- The water level inside remains **constant** — this is a *steady state*.

Now, if you **increase** the inlet rate to $8 \text{ L/min}$ while the outlet remains at $5 \text{ L/min}$, the bucket **fills up** at $3 \text{ L/min}$. This is a *transient/unsteady* case.

> The bucket is the **Control Volume (CV)**.
> The water crossing the boundary is the **Mass Flux**.

The continuity equation is simply a *mathematical bookkeeping* tool for this real-world process of mass entering, leaving, and accumulating inside any imaginary region of space.

---

### 📌 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Range in KTU Problems |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $\rho$ | Mass density of fluid | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **Water: 1000**, **Air: 1.225** |
| $V$ | Velocity of fluid | $\text{m/s}$ | $0.1 \text{ to } 50$ |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area | $\text{m}^2$ | $10^{-4} \text{ to } 10$ |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ | Computed |

> [!NOTE]
> For an **incompressible fluid** (e.g., water, oils at moderate speeds), $\rho$ = constant, and the equation simplifies dramatically — a fact that KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Breakdown — "Why" and "How"

The continuity equation is not derived from Newton's laws — it is derived from a **purely physical conservation principle**: *mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a classical (non-relativistic) flow field*.

The logic unfolds in **5 structured steps**:

1. **Identify a Control Volume (CV):** An *arbitrary, fixed-in-space* region bounded by a *Control Surface (CS)*. The CV may be a tiny infinitesimal element $dV$ or a macroscopic pipe section.
2. **Account for Mass Accumulation:** Within the CV, mass may change with time. Mathematically, this is $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$.
3. **Account for Mass Flux Across CS:** Mass crosses the CS wherever fluid velocity has a component along the outward normal $\hat{n}$. This is $\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$.
4. **Apply Conservation:** *Rate of Mass In − Rate of Mass Out + Rate of Accumulation = 0.* This yields the integral form.
5. **Reduce to Useful Forms:**
   - Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to obtain the differential form.
   - Apply the **Steady Flow Assumption** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) to obtain the stream-tube form.
   - Apply the **Incompressible Assumption** ($\rho$ = constant) to obtain the simplest 1-D form.

---

### 🧾 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Revision Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory table — Must be memorized for ESE & Series Exams.**

| # | Form of Continuity Equation | Mathematical Expression | Applicable Condition | Key Symbol Meaning |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **General 3-D Integral** | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \displaystyle\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \displaystyle\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$ | Always valid | $\hat{n}$ = outward unit normal |
| 2 | **Steady Flow Integral** | $\displaystyle\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$ | $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ at all points | Mass flux in = Mass flux out |
| 3 | **1-D Stream Tube (Compressible)** | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Steady, 1-D along a streamline | Subscripts 1, 2 refer to sections |
| 4 | **1-D Stream Tube (Incompressible)** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | Steady, $\rho$ = constant | $Q$ = volume flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) |
| 5 | **Differential Form** | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Always valid at a point | $\nabla \cdot$ = divergence operator |
| 6 | **Differential (Incompressible)** | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Steady or unsteady, $\rho$ = constant | Source/sink-free flow |
| 7 | **Cartesian Expansion (Incompressible)** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | $\rho$ = constant, 3-D Cartesian | $u, v, w$ = velocity components |
| 8 | **Cylindrical Expansion (Incompressible)** | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | $\rho$ = constant, 3-D cylindrical | $v_r, v_\theta, v_z$ = cylindrical components |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ for **gases** (e.g., air, steam). This is **only** valid for incompressible fluids. For gases, use $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$.

---

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Sizing the inlet of a jet engine using $\rho A V = \dot{m}$ at the diffuser.
- **Hydraulic Engineering:** Designing pipelines, penstocks, and Venturi meters — the *Continuity Equation* sets the basic velocity-area relationship.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling blood flow through arteries (treated as incompressible).
- **Chemical Process Industry:** Mass balance in reactors, nozzles, and orifice meters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 📐 Derivation 1 — Integral Form of the Continuity Equation

**Starting Point:** Apply conservation of mass to a fixed Control Volume (CV) in space.

**Step 1 — Mass inside the CV:**

The total mass contained within the CV at any instant is:

$$M_{CV} = \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Rate of change of mass inside the CV:**

Differentiating with respect to time (the CV is fixed, so limits are constant):

$$\frac{DM_{CV}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$$

Since the CV is non-deforming, the partial derivative is appropriate here.

**Step 3 — Net mass efflux through the Control Surface (CS):**

Across an infinitesimal area $dA$ of the CS, the mass flow rate is $\rho V_n \, dA$, where $V_n = \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}$ is the component of velocity along the outward normal.

The total net outflow is:

$$\dot{m}_{out} = \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Apply Conservation of Mass:**

By the **Law of Conservation of Mass**, the net efflux plus the rate of accumulation equals zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

This is the **General Integral Form of the Continuity Equation**. ∎

---

### 📐 Derivation 2 — Differential Form via the Divergence Theorem

**Step 1 — Apply the Divergence Theorem to the surface integral:**

The Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem) states:

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

> *Physical meaning:* A surface integral of flux equals the volume integral of divergence inside. This converts a surface quantity into a volume quantity.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the integral continuity equation:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single volume integral:**

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4 — Argument of Arbitrariness:**

The CV is **arbitrary**. The only way the integral of the bracketed expression can be zero for *every* choice of CV is for the integrand itself to be zero at every point.

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

This is the **Differential Form of the Continuity Equation**. ∎

---

### 📐 Derivation 3 — One-Dimensional Stream Tube Form (Compressible, Steady)

**Step 1 — Steady Flow Assumption:**

Under steady conditions, $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ everywhere. The continuity equation reduces to:

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Stream Tube:**

A **stream tube** is a tubular region whose boundary is everywhere parallel to the local velocity vector. No fluid crosses the lateral surface of a stream tube.

**Step 3 — Apply at Two Cross-Sections (1 and 2):**

At section 1: area $= A_1$, density $= \rho_1$, velocity $= V_1$.
At section 2: area $= A_2$, density $= \rho_2$, velocity $= V_2$.

The CS integral now has *three* parts:
- Lateral surface (no flow crossing) → contributes $0$.
- Section 1 (inlet, $\hat{n}$ opposite to $\vec{V}_1$): contributes $-\rho_1 A_1 V_1$.
- Section 2 (outlet, $\hat{n}$ along $\vec{V}_2$): contributes $+\rho_2 A_2 V_2$.

**Step 4 — Equate to Zero:**

$$- \rho_1 A_1 V_1 + \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}}$$

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q} \quad \text{[Volume flow rate]}$$

∎

---

### 📐 Derivation 4 — Incompressible 3-D Cartesian Form

**Step 1 — Start from the differential form:**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand the divergence of the product:**

Using the product rule: $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho$.

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply incompressibility:**

For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ = constant in both space and time:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla \rho = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$0 + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + 0 = 0$$

Since $\rho \neq 0$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0}$$

**Step 5 — Expand in Cartesian Coordinates:**

With $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0}$$

∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 1 — Control Volume Mass Balance (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional mass accounting architecture** for a generic control volume, showing how mass inflow, mass outflow, and mass accumulation interact via the continuity equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Inlet Stream] --> B[Inlet Control Surface CS1]
    B --> C[Control Volume CV Region]
    C --> D[Outlet Control Surface CS2]
    D --> E[Fluid Outlet Stream]
    C --> F[Mass Accumulation Term]
    F --> G{Is Flow Steady?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Accumulation equals ZERO]
    G -- No --> I[Accumulation is NON-ZERO]
    H --> J[Mass In equals Mass Out]
    I --> K[Mass In minus Mass Out equals Accumulation Rate]
    J --> L[Continuity Equation Satisfied]
    K --> L
```

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 2 — Stream Tube Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the **1-D stream tube model** used in the third derivation, showing how two cross-sections interact through mass conservation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Section 1: Area A1] -- Velocity V1, Density rho1 --> S2[Section 2: Area A2]
    S2 -- Velocity V2, Density rho2 --> S3[Outlet]
    S1 --> M1[Mass Flow Rate m_dot1]
    S2 --> M2[Mass Flow Rate m_dot2]
    M1 --> EQ[Continuity Equation: rho1 A1 V1 equals rho2 A2 V2]
    M2 --> EQ
    EQ --> RES[Result: m_dot1 equals m_dot2]
```

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 3 — Incompressible Flow Through a Variable-Area Duct (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pipe Section 1: Larger Area A1] -- Velocity V1 --> N[Narrowing of Duct]
    N --> P2[Pipe Section 2: Smaller Area A2]
    P2 -- Velocity V2 --> OUT[Outlet]
    P1 --> C1[V1 is SMALL because A1 is LARGE]
    P2 --> C2[V2 is LARGE because A2 is SMALL]
    C1 --> INV[Inverse Relationship between A and V]
    C2 --> INV
    INV --> APPL[Application: Venturi Meter, Nozzle Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above convey the **logical flow** of mass in a control volume. They serve as functional analogs to physical sketches (which cannot be directly rendered in a Mermaid block) of stream tubes, control surfaces, and variable-area ducts. For your KTU exam sheet, pair these block-level ideas with a hand-drawn stream tube sketch for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]

State the **continuity equation in its most general 3-D integral form** for a fixed control volume. Explain the physical meaning of each term in the equation.

**✅ Model Answer:**

The general 3-D integral form of the continuity equation for a fixed control volume is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**

- **First term** $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \displaystyle\int_{CV} \rho \, dV$ — Represents the **time rate of change of mass** contained within the control volume. If this term is non-zero, mass is either accumulating or depleting inside the CV (transient condition).

- **Second term** $\displaystyle\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ — Represents the **net rate of mass efflux** (outflow minus inflow) through the control surface. The dot product $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}$ extracts the velocity component normal to the surface.

- **Equation as a whole** — Expresses the **Law of Conservation of Mass**: mass cannot be created or destroyed in a classical flow field.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP Set A]

For an **incompressible, steady, 1-D flow**, the continuity equation reduces to $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. Justify this statement and state **two engineering applications** where this form is used.

**✅ Model Answer:**

**Justification:**

Starting from the general integral form:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

Three simplifications are applied:

1. **Steady flow** → $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \displaystyle\int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0$.
2. **Incompressible** → $\rho$ = constant, so it can be taken outside the integrals.
3. **1-D flow along a stream tube** → No fluid crosses the lateral surface; only the inlet (section 1) and outlet (section 2) contribute.

This yields:

$$- \rho A_1 V_1 + \rho A_2 V_2 = 0 \implies A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$$

**Two engineering applications:**

1. **Venturi meter flow measurement** — Determines flow rate through a constricted pipe section.
2. **Design of nozzles and diffusers** — Relates exit velocity to throat area in jet engines and rockets.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the **differential form of the continuity equation** in 3-D Cartesian coordinates, starting from its integral form. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows steadily through a **reducing pipe bend** as shown. At section 1 (inlet), the diameter is $D_1 = 0.3 \text{ m}$ and the velocity is $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$. At section 2 (outlet), the diameter is $D_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}$. Determine: **(i)** the velocity at section 2, **(ii)** the volume flow rate, and **(iii)** the mass flow rate. Take $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**✅ Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumption:** Fluid is a continuum; flow is classical (non-relativistic).

**Step 1:** Integral form of the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the surface integral:

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the continuity equation:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** Since the CV is **arbitrary**, the integrand must vanish at every point:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Expand the divergence using the product rule:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \vec{V} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute and rearrange:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) + \left( u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} \right) = 0$$

This is the **general 3-D Cartesian form** of the continuity equation. ∎

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho$ = constant), the density derivatives vanish, giving:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$
- $D_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Steady, incompressible flow

**(i) Velocity at section 2:**

Areas:
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

Apply $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.0707 \times 2}{0.01767} = \boxed{8.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Volume flow rate:**

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.0707 \times 2 = \boxed{0.1414 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.1414 = \boxed{141.4 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 / Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **continuity equation for 1-D steady compressible flow** through a stream tube. State clearly where the assumption of 1-D flow enters the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air ($\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flows steadily through a duct of **varying cross-section**. At section 1, $A_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ and $V_1 = 15 \text{ m/s}$. The duct passes through a heater that raises the air temperature, reducing the density to $\rho_2 = 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at section 2 where $A_2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$. Determine: **(i)** the mass flow rate, and **(ii)** the velocity at section 2. **(7 Marks)**

**✅ Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Concept of a stream tube:** A stream tube is an imaginary tubular region whose lateral boundary is everywhere tangent to the local velocity vector. Hence, **no fluid crosses the lateral surface** — this is precisely where the 1-D assumption enters.

**Sketch (described in words for answer sheet):**

Draw a tapered tube (narrowing from left to right) bounded by streamlines. Mark inlet section 1 (area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$, density $\rho_1$) and outlet section 2 (area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$, density $\rho_2$).

**Step 1:** Apply the integral continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** Under **steady flow**, the first term vanishes:

$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3:** The CS consists of three parts:
- **Lateral surface** (no flow crosses) → contributes $0$.
- **Inlet (Section 1)**: $\hat{n}$ points inward, opposite to $\vec{V}_1$ → contributes $-\rho_1 A_1 V_1$.
- **Outlet (Section 2)**: $\hat{n}$ points outward, along $\vec{V}_2$ → contributes $+\rho_2 A_2 V_2$.

**Step 4:** Sum the contributions:

$$- \rho_1 A_1 V_1 + \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}}$$

This is the **1-D steady compressible form** of the continuity equation. ∎

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\rho_1 = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $A_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$, $V_1 = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho_2 = 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $A_2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- Steady flow

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = 1.2 \times 0.05 \times 15 = \boxed{0.9 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**(ii) Velocity at section 2:**

Apply $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{\rho_1 A_1 V_1}{\rho_2 A_2} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.05 \times 15}{1.0 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.9}{0.04} = \boxed{22.5 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in the surface integral: $\hat{n}$ is the *outward* normal, so an inlet contributes a *negative* mass flux to the integral.
> 2. **Confusing integral and differential forms**: the integral form applies to a finite CV; the differential form applies at a *single point*. Examiners will deduct marks for mixing them up.
> 3. **Applying $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ to gases**: This is **only** valid for incompressible fluids. For compressible flow, always use $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers**: KTU examiners award **dedicated marks** for correct SI units.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption of "fixed CV"**: In derivations, state explicitly that the CV is *non-deforming* in space — this is required to replace the total derivative with a partial derivative in the accumulation term.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] The **continuity equation** expresses the **conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid.
- [x] **General integral form** (always valid): $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \displaystyle\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \displaystyle\oint_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$.
- [x] **Differential form** (point-wise): $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
- [x] **Steady flow** ⇒ time derivative vanishes ⇒ pure flux balance.
- [x] **Incompressible flow** ⇒ $\rho$ = constant ⇒ $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- [x] **1-D stream tube (incompressible)**: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$.
- [x] **1-D stream tube (compressible)**: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$.
- [x] **Cartesian incompressible expansion**: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$.
- [x] **Physical constants to remember**: $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{air (STP)}} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- [x] **Engineering uses**: Venturi meters, nozzles, diffusers, pipe bends, jet engine inlets, blood-flow models.
- [x] **Exam tip**: Always **state the assumptions** (steady/incompressible/1-D) *before* writing the final form of the equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Irrotational and Rotational Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Irrotational and Rotational Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fluid mechanics, the classification of flow into **Irrotational** and **Rotational** categories is based on the rotation of a fluid element about its own axis as it translates through the flow field. This rotation is independent of the bulk motion (pathline motion) of the element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> - **Irrotational Flow:** A flow is said to be irrotational if every fluid element within the flow field has **zero angular velocity** about its own center of mass. Mathematically, the **vorticity vector** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \vec{0}$ everywhere in the flow field.
> - **Rotational Flow:** A flow is rotational when fluid elements possess a **non-zero net angular velocity** about their own axes, i.e., $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} \neq \vec{0}$.

The vorticity vector is a kinematic property and tells us nothing about whether the fluid is actually moving in a curved path. A fluid particle can move in a straight line (translating) yet still rotate about its own center, and vice-versa.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **floating in a calm, lazy river** that flows in a perfectly straight channel at a constant velocity. Place a small, lightweight leaf (or a tiny pinwheel) in the water. The leaf will simply glide forward without any spinning or twirling — this is an **irrotational** flow.

Now imagine you are standing at the **center of a large whirlpool or a tornado**. Even though the fluid particle at the very center might not be moving anywhere, the particles surrounding it are spinning rapidly around the center, and each particle is also rotating about its own axis like a tiny spinning top. This is a **rotational** flow.

Another beautiful everyday example is a **merry-go-round**. A child sitting on the merry-go-round moves in a circle (curved path), but the child does not spin about their own axis — they are just translating along the circular path. However, if the child holds a spinning top in their hand, the top is rotating about its own axis while translating around the circle. The flow field around the merry-go-round can have irrotational characteristics if the angular velocity is uniform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Rotation refers to the spinning of the fluid element **about itself**, not about some external point. Even in a perfectly straight stream of viscous flow near a boundary, the fluid exhibits rotation (vorticity) due to shear.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Vorticity ($\omega$):** Has units of **$\text{s}^{-1}$** (radians per second), since it represents angular velocity.
- **Circulation ($\Gamma$):** Has units of **$\text{m}^2/\text{s}$**.
- **Velocity potential ($\phi$):** Has units of **$\text{m}^2/\text{s}$**.
- Standard acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is referenced in potential flow theory when combining with Bernoulli's equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vorticity: The Mathematical Heart of Rotation

Consider a fluid element of rectangular shape with sides $dx$, $dy$, and $dz$. The angular velocity of the element about the **z-axis** in the xy-plane is given by the average of the angular velocities of its two originally perpendicular edges:
