# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define stream function and velocity potential function. State the necessary conditions for their existence.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stream Function (ψ):** The stream function is a scalar function of position in a 2D incompressible flow such that its partial derivatives yield the velocity components. The defining relations are:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Velocity Potential Function (φ):** It is a scalar function whose spatial gradient gives the velocity vector. The defining relations are:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

**Conditions for Existence:**

- **Stream function ψ** exists in **any 2D incompressible flow** (condition: continuity equation $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$).
- **Velocity potential φ** exists **only if the flow is irrotational** (condition: $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a flow net? Show that streamlines and equipotential lines are mutually orthogonal.**

**Model Answer:**

**Flow Net:** A flow net is the orthogonal grid formed by the family of streamlines ($\psi = \text{constant}$) and equipotential lines ($\phi = \text{constant}$) in a 2D, incompressible, irrotational flow.

**Proof of Orthogonality:**

Slope of streamline:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} = \frac{v}{u}$$

Slope of equipotential line:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = -\frac{u}{v}$$

Product of slopes:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} \cdot \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = \frac{v}{u} \cdot \left(-\frac{u}{v}\right) = -1$$

Since the product of slopes is $-1$, the two families of curves are perpendicular at every intersection point. $\blacksquare$

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 2D incompressible flow is described by the stream function $\psi = 3x^2 y - y^3$. Determine:**
**(i) The velocity components $u$ and $v$.**
**(ii) The vorticity of the flow.**
**(iii) Whether a velocity potential function exists. If yes, find it.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find velocity components using stream function definitions:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2 y - y^3) = 3x^2 - 3y^2$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 y - y^3) = -6xy$$

**Step 2 — Compute the vorticity (z-component):**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(-6xy)}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial(3x^2 - 3y^2)}{\partial y}$$

$$\omega_z = -6y - 6y = -12y$$

**Step 3 — Check if a velocity potential exists:**

Since the flow is irrotational ONLY if $\omega_z = 0$ EVERYWHERE. Here $\omega_z = -12y \neq 0$ in general, so the flow is **rotational** and a velocity potential $\phi$ does **NOT** exist globally.

**Step 4 — Alternative check using Laplace's equation:**

$$\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 6y - 6y = 0$$

The stream function satisfies Laplace's equation, but this only confirms incompressibility — not irrotationality. Hence $\phi$ does not exist.

**Final Answer:** $u = 3x^2 - 3y^2$, $v = -6xy$, $\omega_z = -12y$, **No potential function exists** (flow is rotational).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: For the flow $\psi = x^2 - y^2$, find:**
**(i) The velocity components.**
**(ii) The equation of the streamline passing through (1, 2).**
**(iii) The volume flow rate per unit width between the streamlines passing through (1, 2) and (2, 3).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity components:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(x^2 - y^2)}{\partial y} = -2y$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial(x^2 - y^2)}{\partial x} = -2x$$

**Step 2 — Streamline through (1, 2):**

Streamlines are given by $\psi = \text{constant}$. At (1, 2):

$$\psi_1 = (1)^2 - (2)^2 = 1 - 4 = -3$$

So the streamline equation is:

$$x^2 - y^2 = -3 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y^2 - x^2 = 3$$

This is a rectangular hyperbola.

**Step 3 — Streamline through (2, 3):**

$$\psi_2 = (2)^2 - (3)^2 = 4 - 9 = -5$$

Equation: $x^2 - y^2 = -5$, i.e., $y^2 - x^2 = 5$.

**Step 4 — Volume flow rate per unit depth:**

$$q = \psi_2 - \psi_1 = -5 - (-3) = -2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

The negative sign indicates that the flow is in the opposite sense to the assumed positive direction (relative to the chosen curve orientation). The **magnitude** of volumetric flow rate per unit width is $\vert q \vert = 2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: For the velocity potential $\phi = 2xy + 3x^2 - y^2$:**
**(i) Verify that the flow is a possible incompressible, irrotational flow.**
**(ii) Find the stream function ψ.**
**(iii) Show that the C-R equations are satisfied.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity components from potential:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2y + 6x$$

$$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = 2x - 2y$$

**Step 2 — Check incompressibility (continuity):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 6 + (-2) = 4 \neq 0$$

**Conclusion:** The flow is **NOT incompressible** as given. To make it a valid potential flow, the velocity field must satisfy $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. Since it does not, the given $\phi$ is **not** physically realizable as an incompressible flow.

**Step 3 — Irrotationality check (for completeness):**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2 - 2 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Irrotationality holds, but incompressibility fails. Therefore, the flow is a **possible irrotational flow but not an incompressible one**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This question is testing whether the student checks BOTH conditions. Many students only verify irrotationality and lose marks.

**Step 4 — Hypothetical stream function (if incompressibility were satisfied):**

From the C-R equation $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 2y + 6x \Rightarrow \psi = y^2 + 6xy + f(x)$$

From the C-R equation $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 2x - 2y$$

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -2x + 2y$$

Differentiating $\psi = y^2 + 6xy + f(x)$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 6y + f'(x) = -2x + 2y$$

So:

$$f'(x) = -2x - 4y$$

This is impossible since $f'(x)$ cannot depend on $y$. This confirms that no stream function exists (because the flow is not incompressible).

**Conclusion:** The given $\phi$ does not represent a valid incompressible potential flow. **No stream function exists.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 2D flow has velocity components $u = 2x$ and $v = -2y$.**
**(i) Check if the flow is incompressible and irrotational.**
**(ii) If both, find the stream function and velocity potential.**
**(iii) Plot the streamlines and equipotential lines (sketch the flow net).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Incompressibility check:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2 + (-2) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is **incompressible**.

**Step 2 — Irrotationality check:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is **irrotational**. Both $\psi$ and $\phi$ exist.

**Step 3 — Find stream function ψ:**

From $u = \partial\psi/\partial y = 2x$:

$$\psi = 2xy + f(x)$$

From $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = -2y$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 2y \Rightarrow 2y + f'(x) = 2y \Rightarrow f'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow f(x) = C_1$$

So:

$$\boxed{\psi = 2xy + C_1}$$

**Step 4 — Find velocity potential φ:**

From $u = \partial\phi/\partial x = 2x$:

$$\phi = x^2 + g(y)$$

From $v = \partial\phi/\partial y = -2y$:

$$g'(y) = -2y \Rightarrow g(y) = -y^2 + C_2$$

So:

$$\boxed{\phi = x^2 - y^2 + C_2}$$

**Step 5 — Verify C-R equations:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2x = u \quad \checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -2y = v \quad \checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 2x = u \quad \checkmark$$

$$-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -2y = v \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Flow net analysis:**

- **Streamlines:** $2xy = C$ — these are **rectangular hyperbolas** in the first and third quadrants (for $C > 0$) and second and fourth quadrants (for $C < 0$).
- **Equipotential lines:** $x^2 - y^2 = C$ — these are also **rectangular hyperbolas** oriented along the axes.

**Step 7 — Orthogonality verification:**

Slope of streamline: $dy/dx = v/u = -2y / 2x = -y/x$.

Slope of equipotential: $dy/dx = -u/v = -2x / (-2y) = x/y$.

Product: $(-y/x)(x/y) = -1$ ✓ **Orthogonal confirmed.**

**Physical interpretation:** This flow represents a **stagnation-type flow** with fluid moving outward along the $x$-axis and inward along the $y$-axis (or vice-versa). The streamlines resemble an "X" pattern of hyperbolas.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the Negative Sign in $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:**
> This is the SINGLE most common error. Students write $v = \partial\psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. Always remember: the stream function has a built-in sign convention to make the volume flow rate $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ come out positive in the "standard" sense.
> 
> **2. Confusing Stream Function with Velocity Potential:**
> $\psi$ comes from **incompressibility** (continuity), while $\phi$ comes from **irrotationality**. Do NOT assume every flow has both. A rotating flow (like a forced vortex) has no $\phi$, while a sink/source has both.
> 
> **3. Skipping the Verification Step:**
> KTU examiners love asking "show that this is a valid flow." You must verify BOTH $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ AND C-R equations before claiming your solution is correct.
> 
> **4. Ignoring the Constant of Integration:**
> In the 7-mark questions, leaving out $C_1$ and $C_2$ in stream function and potential function can cost 1–2 marks. Always write the general solution with the constant.
> 
> **5. Misidentifying Streamlines:**
> The streamline equation is $\psi(x, y) = C$, not $u = C$ or $v = C$. Substituting the point coordinates into the stream function gives the constant for that particular streamline.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Potential and Stream Functions**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Stream function ψ:** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ — exists in 2D incompressible flow.
- **Velocity potential φ:** $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$, $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ — exists in irrotational flow.
- **Streamline:** $\psi = \text{constant}$.
- **Equipotential line:** $\phi = \text{constant}$.

### 🔑 Governing Equations
- **Continuity (2D):** $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$.
- **Irrotationality (2D):** $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$.
- **Cauchy–Riemann equations:** $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
- **Laplace equation:** $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ and $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$.

### 🔑 Key Results
- Both $\psi$ and $\phi$ exist **only** for 2D, incompressible, irrotational flow.
- $\psi$ and $\phi$ are **conjugate harmonic functions** — they are the imaginary and real parts of an analytic function $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$.
- Streamlines and equipotential lines are **mutually orthogonal**.
- Volume flow rate per unit depth: $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$.

### 🔑 Problem-Solving Protocol (Always Follow This Order)
1. **Identify** what is given ($\psi$, $\phi$, or $u, v$).
2. **Compute** velocity components using the correct formula.
3. **Verify** incompressibility and/or irrotationality.
4. **Find** the other function ($\psi$ or $\phi$) using C-R equations.
5. **Check** the orthogonality and Laplace conditions.
6. **Compute** flow rate if asked.
7. **State** the streamline and equipotential line equations.

### 🔑 Common 2D Potential Flows to Remember
- **Uniform flow:** $\psi = U_\infty y$, $\phi = U_\infty x$.
- **Source/Sink:** $\psi = (m/2\pi)\theta$, $\phi = (m/2\pi)\ln r$.
- **Vortex:** $\psi = -(K/2\pi)\ln r$, $\phi = (K/2\pi)\theta$.
- **Doublet:** $\psi = -(\mu \sin\theta)/2\pi r$, $\phi = (\mu \cos\theta)/2\pi r$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, examiners often give you $\psi$ and ask for $\phi$, OR give $\phi$ and ask for $\psi$. Master the integration of partial derivatives, and ALWAYS include the constant of integration. Practice at least 5 problems from each category to build exam confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler and Bernoulli's Theorems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Euler and Bernoulli's Theorems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Euler's Theorem (Equation of Motion Along a Streamline)

**Euler's equation of motion** is the fundamental differential equation governing the motion of an *ideal* (inviscid) fluid along a streamline. It is obtained by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a fluid element and balancing the pressure, gravity, and inertial forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Euler's equation of motion is the differential form of the momentum equation for an inviscid, incompressible, steady fluid flow, expressing the balance between pressure force, gravity force, and acceleration along a streamline.*

Mathematically expressed along a streamline $s$:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

where:
- $P$ = Static pressure
- $\rho$ = Mass density of fluid
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity ($\mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^2}$)
- $z$ = Elevation head above a datum
- $V$ = Velocity of fluid along streamline

---

### 1.2 Bernoulli's Theorem (Engineering Statement)

**Bernoulli's Theorem** is the *integrated energy form* of Euler's equation along a streamline. It states that for a *steady, incompressible, inviscid* flow, the **sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head** remains constant between any two points on a streamline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Statement:**
> *In a steady, ideal, incompressible flow, the total energy per unit weight of the fluid (pressure energy + kinetic energy + potential energy) is constant along a streamline.*

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — The Mountain River:**
Imagine water flowing down a mountain stream. As the stream descends (lower $z$), it speeds up (higher $V$) and the pressure between layers drops (lower $P$). The fluid is "trading" elevation energy for kinetic energy — Bernoulli's equation is simply the bookkeeping rule that says *no energy is lost in this trade* (under ideal conditions).

**Key Intuitive Idea:** Euler's equation is the *differential* balance; Bernoulli's equation is the *integrated* result that engineers use directly to size pipes, design Venturimeters, Pitot tubes, and aircraft wings.

> [!TIP]
> Think of Euler as the **micro-rule** (at an infinitesimally small point) and Bernoulli as the **macro-rule** (between any two points far apart). Bernoulli is essentially Euler's equation *integrated* along the streamline.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (SI) |
|----------|--------|--------------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^2}$ |
| Density of water (4 °C) | $\rho_w$ | $\mathbf{1000 \, kg/m^3}$ |
| Density of air (STP) | $\rho_a$ | $\mathbf{1.225 \, kg/m^3}$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | $\mathbf{101.325 \, kPa}$ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | $\mathbf{9810 \, N/m^3}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Euler–Bernoulli Theory

Both Euler's equation and Bernoulli's theorem are valid **only** under the following restrictive conditions. The KTU examiner expects students to list these explicitly:

- **Steady flow** — flow parameters do not change with time at a fixed point.
- **Incompressible flow** — $\rho = \text{constant}$ (e.g., liquids at low speeds).
- **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — fluid has zero viscosity; no shear stresses.
- **Along a streamline** — equation applies *along a single streamline*, not across streamlines (for non-rotational flow, it applies throughout the field).
- **No energy addition or extraction** — no pump, turbine, or shaft work between sections.
- **No heat transfer** — adiabatic flow.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Euler's Equation (The 'Why' and 'How')

1. **Select a fluid element** of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline.
2. **Identify forces acting on it:**
   - Pressure force on the upstream face: $P \, dA$
   - Pressure force on the downstream face: $\left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA$
   - Weight component along streamline: $- \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$
3. **Apply Newton's Second Law** ($F = m a$) along $s$:
   $$\text{Net force} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$
4. **Substitute and simplify** the net pressure force as $-dA \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds$.
5. **Divide** by $\rho \, dA \, ds$ to obtain Euler's equation.

> [!NOTE]
> The term $V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ is the **convective acceleration** — the rate of change of velocity *experienced by the fluid particle* as it moves along the streamline.

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Bernoulli's Equation

Starting from Euler's equation (dividing through by $\rho g$):

$$\frac{\partial P}{\rho g \, \partial s} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \frac{V}{g} \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

Recognising that $\frac{V}{g}\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \frac{1}{g}\frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right)$, integrate along the streamline between points 1 and 2:

$$\int_1^2 \frac{\partial P}{\rho g} + \int_1^2 \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} ds + \int_1^2 \frac{\partial (V^2/2)}{g \, \partial s} ds = 0$$

Result (the famous "three-term" Bernoulli equation):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the units:** Each term in Bernoulli's equation has units of **meters (m)** when expressed in *head form*, or **Pa (N/m²)** when expressed in *pressure form*.

| Form | Equation | Units | Use Case |
|------|----------|-------|----------|
| **Head Form (Energy per unit weight)** | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | m | Pipe flow, channel flow |
| **Pressure Form (Energy per unit volume)** | $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = C$ | Pa | Aerodynamics, Venturi analysis |
| **Energy Form (Energy per unit mass)** | $\dfrac{P}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + g z = C$ | J/kg | Compressible flow, nozzle design |
| **Euler's Differential Form** | $\dfrac{dP}{\rho} + g\, dz + V\, dV = 0$ | Pa | Derivation of Bernoulli |
| **Continuity Coupling** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s | Velocity from area ratio |
| **Discharge via Velocity** | $Q = A \cdot V$ | m³/s | Volumetric flow rate |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion between forms:** Multiply *head form* by $\rho g$ to get *pressure form*; divide *pressure form* by $\rho$ to get *energy form*. KTU examiners frequently test this conversion.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Industry | How Bernoulli is Used |
|-------------|----------|----------------------|
| Venturimeter | Process, chemical | Measures flow rate via pressure drop |
| Pitot-static tube | Aerospace, metrology | Measures aircraft airspeed |
| Orifice meter | Oil & gas | Flow measurement in pipelines |
| Carburettor | Automotive | Fuel-air mixing via pressure drop |
| Airfoil lift | Aerospace | Pressure difference across wing |
| Siphon | Civil engineering | Water transfer over obstacles |
| Rotameter calibration | Instrumentation | Flow visualisation |
| Draft tube | Hydropower | Recovers kinetic energy at turbine exit |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Equation of Motion

**Step 1 — Consider a fluid element.**
Take a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline. Its mass is $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces.**
The forces acting along the streamline direction $s$ are:

- Pressure force on the *back* (upstream) face: $F_1 = P \, dA$ (acts in $+s$ direction)
- Pressure force on the *front* (downstream) face: $F_2 = \left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA$ (acts in $-s$ direction)
- Component of weight along $s$: $F_g = - dm \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s} = - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s}$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign of $\partial z / \partial s$ depends on whether the streamline is rising or falling. The negative sign appears because gravity acts *downward* while $s$ is the local streamline tangent.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law.**
The net force equals mass × acceleration. The acceleration of the fluid particle along the streamline (convective acceleration, since the flow is steady) is $a_s = V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$.

$$F_{net} = dm \cdot a_s = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 4 — Write the force balance.**

$$P \, dA - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $P$ terms and simplify.**

$$- \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Divide both sides by $dA \, ds$:

$$- \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into standard Euler form.**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Euler's equation of motion along a streamline** in its most general differential form. Each term has units of $\text{Pa/m}$ (pressure gradient).

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

**Step 1 — Start with Euler's equation.**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Divide through by $\rho g$.**

$$\frac{1}{\rho g} \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \frac{V}{g} \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the convective term as a derivative.**
Using the chain rule: $\dfrac{V}{g} \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \dfrac{1}{g} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{\rho g} \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Integrate along the streamline from point 1 to point 2.**

$$\int_1^2 \frac{\partial P}{\rho g} + \int_1^2 \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} ds + \int_1^2 \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) ds = 0$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each integral.**

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + (z_2 - z_1) + \left(\frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\right) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange (point-1 terms on the left, point-2 terms on the right).**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 7 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant along a streamline}}$$

---

### 3.3 Energy Interpretation (Term-by-Term)

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning | Energy Type |
|------|------|------------------|-------------|
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | **Pressure head** | Height of fluid column equivalent to pressure $P$ | Pressure (flow) energy |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | **Velocity / Kinetic head** | Height fluid would rise to if kinetic energy converted to potential | Kinetic energy |
| $z$ | **Elevation / Datum head** | Height of the point above a chosen reference datum | Potential energy |

> [!TIP]
> The Bernoulli constant $C$ has units of **meters** and is called the **Total Head** ($H$). Energy per unit weight is preserved — this is essentially a fluid-mechanical statement of the **Law of Conservation of Energy**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Applying Bernoulli

| Scenario | Correct Treatment | Common Mistake |
|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Real (viscous) flow | Add head loss $h_L$: $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Forgetting $h_L$ → overestimates $P_2$ |
| With pump/turbine | Add pump head $H_p$ or subtract turbine head $H_t$ | Ignoring machine work |
| Compressible flow | Use Mach number corrections; $C_p$ formulation | Using incompressible form for $M > 0.3$ |
| Across streamlines | Valid only for irrotational flow | Applying between two different streamlines in rotational flow |
| Units mismatch | Use SI throughout: $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m/s, $z$ in m | Mixing CGS/English units in one problem |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Logical Architecture of Euler → Bernoulli Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Fluid Element along Streamline ds"] --> B["Identify Forces on Element"]
    B --> B1["Pressure Force: P dA"]
    B --> B2["Pressure Force: P + dP/ds * ds times dA"]
    B --> B3["Weight Component: rho g dz/ds dA ds"]
    B1 --> C["Apply Newton Second Law: F equals m a"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D["Acceleration equals V dV/ds along streamline"]
    D --> E["Balance Forces and Cancel dA ds"]
    E --> F["Euler Equation: dP/ds plus rho g dz/ds plus rho V dV/ds equals 0"]
    F --> G["Divide by rho g"]
    G --> H["Recognise Convective Term as Derivative of V squared over 2g"]
    H --> I["Integrate along Streamline from Point 1 to Point 2"]
    I --> J["Apply Boundary Conditions: substitute limits"]
    J --> K["Bernoulli Equation: P1 over rho g plus V1 squared over 2g plus z1 equals P2 over rho g plus V2 squared over 2g plus z2"]
    K --> L["Final Constant Form: P over rho g plus V squared over 2g plus z equals Constant H"]
    L --> M["Engineering Applications: Venturi, Pitot, Orifice, Airfoil, Siphon, Turbines"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bernoulli's Energy Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Total["Total Head H at any point along streamline"]
        direction TB
        PH["Pressure Head: P divided by rho g"]
        VH["Velocity Head: V squared divided by 2g"]
        EH["Elevation Head: z"]
    end

    subgraph Inputs["Boundary Inputs"]
        direction TB
        In1["Static Pressure P in Pa"]
        In2["Flow Velocity V in m/s"]
        In3["Datum Elevation z in m"]
    end

    subgraph Fluid["Fluid Property"]
        direction TB
        Prop["Specific Weight gamma equals rho g in N per m cubed"]
    end

    In1 --> PH
    In2 --> VH
    In3 --> EH
    Prop --> PH
    PH --> Sum["Sum equals Constant H in meters"]
    VH --> Sum
    EH --> Sum
    Sum --> Out["Apply to Venturimeter, Pitot, Orifice, Siphon, Airfoil, Turbine"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Streamtube and Forces (Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST["Streamtube: a bundle of streamlines forming a tube-like region"] --> P1["Section 1-1: Area A1, Pressure P1, Velocity V1, Elevation z1"]
    ST --> P2["Section 2-2: Area A2, Pressure P2, Velocity V2, Elevation z2"]
    P1 --> CON["Continuity: A1 V1 equals A2 V2"]
    P2 --> CON
    P1 --> BER["Bernoulli: P1/rho g plus V1 squared over 2g plus z1 equals P2/rho g plus V2 squared over 2g plus z2"]
    P2 --> BER
    CON --> SOLVE["Solve simultaneously for V and P at required section"]
    BER --> SOLVE
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are conceptual maps of the **derivation logic** and **equation structure**. They are not physical free-body sketches of the fluid element — that would require 3-D vector drawings, which Mermaid cannot render. The block diagrams above capture the equivalent logical flow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Bernoulli's theorem and list any four assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** *In a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant along a streamline.*

**Assumptions:**
1. The flow is **steady** (time-independent).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
3. The fluid is **inviscid** (zero viscosity, no friction).
4. The equation is applied **along a single streamline**.
5. There is **no energy addition/extraction** (no pump/turbine) and **no heat transfer**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects at least **four** assumptions. Memorise all six — examiners often deduct marks for incomplete lists.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Euler's equation and Bernoulli's equation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Euler's Equation | Bernoulli's Equation |
|---------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Form** | Differential (pointwise) | Integrated (between two points) |
| **Validity** | Local, along a streamline | Between two points on a streamline |
| **Derivation** | From Newton's 2nd Law applied to a fluid element | From Euler's equation after integration |
| **Terms** | Pressure gradient, gravity, inertia (per unit volume) | Pressure head + velocity head + elevation head |
| **Use** | Theoretical foundation, control volume analysis | Direct engineering calculations (flow measurement) |
| **Symbolic** | $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \rho V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive Euler's equation of motion along a streamline for an ideal fluid. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from Euler's equation, derive Bernoulli's equation. Interpret each term in its head form physically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Derivation of Euler's Equation:**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1**. Key points to include for full marks:

1. **Diagram description** (sketch fluid element with forces) — *1 mark*
2. Identification of forces: pressure on two faces + weight component — *2 marks*
3. Newton's 2nd Law statement with convective acceleration $V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ — *2 marks*
4. Final simplified equation — *1 mark*
5. Assumptions (steady, incompressible, inviscid) — *1 mark*

**Final equation to present:**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

---

**Solution (b) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation:**

Refer to **Section 3.2**. Key points to include:

1. Divide Euler's equation by $\rho g$ — *1 mark*
2. Recognise convective term as $\dfrac{1}{g}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$ — *1 mark*
3. Integrate along the streamline from 1 to 2 — *2 marks*
4. Final Bernoulli equation — *1 mark*
5. Physical interpretation of three terms — *2 marks* (½ mark each, then ½ mark for the "Constant" interpretation)

**Physical interpretation table:**

| Term | Interpretation |
|------|----------------|
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure energy per unit weight of fluid (flow energy) |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Kinetic energy per unit weight of fluid |
| $z$ | Potential energy per unit weight of fluid |
| Sum = Constant | Conservation of mechanical energy per unit weight along streamline |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of Bernoulli's equation. How is it modified for (i) real (viscous) fluids and (ii) flow with a pump/turbine? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 200 mm at section 1 narrows to 100 mm at section 2. If the pressure at section 1 is 150 kPa (gauge) and the velocity is 2 m/s, calculate (i) the velocity at section 2, and (ii) the pressure at section 2. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Limitations and Modifications:**

**Limitations of Bernoulli's equation:**
1. Valid only for **steady** flow.
2. Valid only for **incompressible** flow (low Mach number).
3. Valid only for **inviscid** (frictionless) flow.
4. Applied **along a streamline**; for irrotational flow only, valid across streamlines.
5. **No energy losses** assumed.
6. **No shaft work** (pump/turbine) included.

**Modifications:**

**(i) For real (viscous) fluids** — add **head loss** $h_L$:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**(ii) For flow with pump/turbine** — add/subtract machine head:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L \pm H_t$$

(where $H_p$ = pump head added; $H_t$ = turbine head extracted)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part A Pitfalls:**
> Students frequently lose **1–2 marks** by:
> - Writing "Bernoulli is invalid for real fluids" without showing the *modified* equation with $h_L$.
> - Forgetting that Bernoulli is a **per-unit-weight** equation (head form, units in metres), not energy.
> - Confusing $H_p$ and $H_t$ signs (pump **adds** energy, turbine **extracts**).

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $P_1 = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$ (gauge)
- $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Horizontal pipe → $z_1 = z_2$

**Step 1 — Compute areas.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.031416 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity to find $V_2$.**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.031416}{0.007854} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's equation (pressure form, since horizontal).**

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_2$.**

$$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

$$P_2 = 150 \times 10^3 + \frac{1}{2}(1000)(2^2 - 8^2)$$

$$P_2 = 150000 + 500 \cdot (4 - 64)$$

$$P_2 = 150000 + 500 \cdot (-60)$$

$$P_2 = 150000 - 30000 = 120000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Final answers.**

$$\boxed{V_2 = 8 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 120 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting unit conversion** ($200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, NOT 200) — costs 1 mark.
> - **Sign error** in $V_1^2 - V_2^2$ vs $V_2^2 - V_1^2$ — verify the rearrangement.
> - **Using head form** unnecessarily with large numbers — pressure form is cleaner here.
> - **Forgetting to state "gauge"** explicitly in the final answer.
> - **Ignoring $z$** (elevation) — since the pipe is horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$ so the term cancels; mention this in your solution to score full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **Euler's equation** is the **differential form** derived from Newton's 2nd Law for an inviscid fluid element along a streamline.
- [x] **Bernoulli's equation** is the **integrated form** of Euler's equation and represents **conservation of energy per unit weight** along a streamline.
- [x] The three terms of Bernoulli are: **Pressure head** ($P/\rho g$), **Velocity/Kinetic head** ($V^2/2g$), and **Elevation head** ($z$) — all in **metres**.
- [x] The **six key assumptions** are: steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no shaft work, no heat transfer — memorise all six.
- [x] For **real (viscous) flow**, add **head loss $h_L$** on the downstream side.
- [x] For **pump** flow, add $H_p$ on the **upstream** side; for **turbine** flow, add $H_t$ on the **downstream** side.
- [x] **Continuity equation** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ is used **along with** Bernoulli to solve problems with two unknowns.
- [x] Bernoulli applies **across streamlines** ONLY if the flow is **irrotational**; otherwise strictly streamline-wise.
- [x] **Conversions**: multiply head form by $\rho g$ to get pressure form (Pa); divide pressure form by $\rho$ to get energy form (J/kg).
- [x] **Engineering applications** to mention in theory answers: Venturimeter, Pitot tube, orifice meter, airfoil lift, siphon, draft tube, carburettor.
- [x] In **numerical problems**, always state the form of Bernoulli you are using (head / pressure / energy) and verify units.
- [x] **Streamline** = the path followed by an individual fluid particle; **Streamtube** = a bundle of streamlines forming a closed tube-like region.
- [x] For **horizontal flow**, $z_1 = z_2$ → Bernoulli simplifies to $P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$ (this is sometimes called the "static + dynamic" pressure balance).
- [x] The **"hump" or Venturi effect**: as cross-section decreases, velocity increases and pressure decreases — this is the core physical insight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the three heads:** **"P-V-E"** → **"Pressure - Velocity - Elevation"** → **"P over rho g, V squared over 2g, z"**. All summed equal a constant $H$ in metres.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse Momentum Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impulse Momentum Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMPULSE-MOMENTUM THEOREM (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Impulse-Momentum Theorem** states that the net force acting on a fluid mass (or a body interacting with a fluid) is equal to the **time rate of change of linear momentum** of the fluid stream crossing the control volume. Mathematically, for a steady control volume:
> $$\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$$
> where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate ($\dot{m} = \rho Q$), and $\vec{V}_{out}$ and $\vec{V}_{in}$ are the absolute velocity vectors leaving and entering the control volume respectively.

This principle is the **cornerstone of fluid dynamics** and is directly derived from **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a fluid control volume. KTU examiners treat this as a **high-priority, high-yield topic** because every hydraulic machine problem (jets on vanes, turbines, bends, nozzles) ultimately reduces to an impulse-momentum balance.

## 1.2 Physical Significance & Engineering Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this theorem so important in Engineering?**
> In fluid mechanics, we frequently need to find the **force exerted by a flowing fluid on a solid boundary** — e.g., a jet hitting a turbine blade, water striking a Pelton bucket, flow through a pipe bend, or a fire hose pushing back on a firefighter. These forces **cannot** be computed from pressure alone (since moving jets have nearly atmospheric pressure). Instead, we use the **change in momentum** of the fluid to compute these forces.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fire Hose Effect"

Imagine you are holding a garden hose spraying water horizontally. The water leaves the nozzle with velocity $V$ and mass flow rate $\dot{m}$. When you deflect the water backwards (by bending the hose), the water's momentum changes direction. By Newton's third law, the hose **pushes back** on your hand with a forward force. This reaction force is exactly what propels a **Pelton wheel turbine** or pushes a **rocket engine**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terminology You MUST Know (KTU Exam Vocabulary):**
> - **Jet:** A stream of fluid issuing from a nozzle into the atmosphere.
> - **Vane / Blade:** A solid curved or flat surface that intercepts the jet and changes its direction.
> - **Stationary Vane:** Vane is fixed; jet velocity relative to vane equals absolute jet velocity.
> - **Moving Vane:** Vane moves with velocity $u$ in the direction of the jet.
> - **Relative Velocity:** Velocity of jet **with respect to** the moving vane, $V_r = V - u$.
> - **Bucket:** A specially shaped double-cup vane used in Pelton wheels to reverse the jet by nearly $180°$.

## 1.4 Newton's Second Law — The Foundation

For a particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $V$:
$$\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a} = m \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt} = \frac{d(m\vec{V})}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

For a fluid flowing through a control volume of area $A$ with velocity $V$:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \rho Q$$
where $\rho$ is fluid density (water: $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$), and $Q$ is volumetric flow rate in m³/s.

## 1.5 The Two Critical Assumptions (Always State in KTU Exams)

1. **Frictionless flow** — no energy loss at the vane surface (ideal impact).
2. **Atmospheric pressure** on the free jet — pressure terms cancel out, leaving only momentum terms in the force equation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Impulse-Momentum Application

To apply the theorem, follow this **3-step KTU problem-solving protocol**:

### 🔹 Step 1 — Identify the Control Volume
Select a control surface that **encloses the vane and the jet region**. The boundaries should cut the jet streamlines **perpendicular** so that velocities are uniform and parallel.

### 🔹 Step 2 — Apply the Momentum Equation
Apply $\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$ in **both x and y directions** independently. Since pressure is atmospheric on all open jet boundaries, **pressure forces vanish** and only the vane reaction forces appear.

### 🔹 Step 3 — Compute Force and Power
- **Force:** $F = \dot{m} \Delta V$ in the direction of momentum change.
- **Work done / Power:** $P = F \times u$ (where $u$ is vane velocity).
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work done per second}}{\text{KE of jet per second}}$

## 2.2 Standard Cases of Jet Impact (KTU Syllabus Coverage)

| **Case** | **Vane Type** | **Vane Motion** | **Exit Angle** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Flat plate (perpendicular) | Stationary | $90°$ deflection |
| 2 | Flat plate (inclined at $\theta$) | Stationary | Glancing deflection |
| 3 | Flat plate (inclined) | Moving with $u$ | Glancing, reduced velocity |
| 4 | Curved vane (single) | Stationary | Angle of exit $\phi$ |
| 5 | Curved vane (single) | Moving with $u$ | Using relative velocity |
| 6 | Series of curved vanes | Moving | Continuous contact |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** $V$ = jet velocity, $a$ = jet cross-sectional area, $\rho$ = fluid density, $u$ = vane velocity, $\theta$ = jet angle to plate, $\phi$ = angle of exit of jet from vane tip, $\beta$ = vane angle, $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ = relative velocities at inlet/outlet.

### 📋 MASTER FORMULA TABLE — IMPULSE-MOMENTUM THEORY

| **#** | **Situation** | **Force Equation** | **Work Done / sec** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Stationary flat plate, jet hits perpendicular | $F_x = \rho a V^2$ | Zero |
| 2 | Stationary inclined plate (jet angle $\theta$) | $F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$, $F_y = \rho a V^2 \cos\theta$ | Zero |
| 3 | Moving inclined plate (jet angle $\theta$) | $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 \sin\theta$ | $W = \rho a (V-u)^2 \cdot u$ |
| 4 | Stationary curved vane, exit angle $\phi$ | $F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\phi)$ | Zero |
| 5 | Stationary curved vane, exit angle $\phi$ (y-dir) | $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\phi$ | Zero |
| 6 | Single moving curved vane | $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 (1-\cos\phi)$ | $W = F_x \cdot u$ |
| 7 | Series of moving curved vanes | $F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1-\cos\phi)$ | $W = \rho a V (V-u)(1-\cos\phi) \cdot u$ |
| 8 | Pelton wheel, bucket angle $\beta$ | $F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1+\cos\beta)$ | $W = F_x \cdot u$ |
| 9 | Maximum efficiency condition | $\dfrac{u}{V} = \dfrac{1+\cos\beta}{2}$ | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1+\cos\beta}{2}$ |
| 10 | For $\beta = 165°$ to $170°$ | $u \approx 0.44V$ to $0.48V$ | $\eta_{max} \approx 0.98$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Critical Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *"Perpendicular plate = $\rho a V^2$ — no sine, no cosine!"* For any curved vane, the $x$-force has the signature form $\rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\phi)$. Memorize this pattern and you can derive every formula.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Theory is Used

- **Pelton Wheel Turbines** — impulse turbines in hydroelectric power plants (high head, low flow).
- **Rocket Propulsion** — momentum of exhaust gases produces thrust.
- **Firefighting Monitors** — reaction force in jet deflection.
- **Aircraft Thrust Reversers** — deflecting exhaust forward to brake.
- **Ship Propellers** — momentum change produces thrust to overcome drag.
- **Hydraulic Ram/Punch** — using jet impact for forging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Stationary Flat Plate (Perpendicular Jet)

### Setup
A circular jet of diameter $d$, area $a$, velocity $V$, density $\rho$ strikes a **stationary flat plate held perpendicular** to the jet axis. The jet splashes radially outward along the plate.

### Control Volume Application

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of jet striking the plate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

**Step 2:** Choose x-axis along the jet direction. The jet enters with velocity $+V$ in x. After striking the plate, the fluid runs **along the plate surface** — its **x-component of velocity becomes ZERO** (radial flow has no axial component).

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's second law (rate of change of momentum) in x-direction:
$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x} - V_{in,x})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:
$$\sum F_x = \rho a V (0 - V) = -\rho a V^2$$

The negative sign indicates the force on the fluid is in the negative x-direction. By Newton's third law, the force **exerted by the jet on the plate** is:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 \;\; \text{N}}$$

**Step 5:** This is the **entire thrust force** — note that the y-component is zero because radial flow is symmetric.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force on a Stationary Inclined Flat Plate

### Setup
The same jet strikes a flat plate that is **tilted at angle $\theta$ to the jet direction**, where $\theta$ is the angle between the jet axis and the plate surface (NOT the normal). This means the normal to the plate makes angle $(90° - \theta)$ with the jet.

### Velocity Components After Impact

When the jet strikes the inclined plate, it splits into two streams along the plate surface. The **component of velocity normal to the plate is destroyed**, while the **component along the plate is conserved** (frictionless assumption).

**Component of jet velocity along the plate (tangential):**
$$V_t = V \cos\theta$$

**Component of jet velocity normal to plate (destroyed):**
$$V_n = V \sin\theta$$

### Force Calculation

**Step 1:** The mass flow rate striking the plate remains:
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

**Step 2:** In the x-direction (along jet axis):
$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x} - V_{in,x})$$

**Step 3:** Outgoing x-velocity (component of $V \cos\theta$ along x):
$$V_{out,x} = V \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta = V \cos^2\theta$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully. If the plate is tilted so its surface makes angle $\theta$ with the jet, the fluid flows along the plate with velocity $V \cos\theta$ (tangential component). The direction of this tangential flow along the plate must be projected back onto x-axis.

For a symmetric split (equal flow up and down the plate), the **average x-velocity of outgoing fluid is $V \cos^2\theta$** (one stream goes up the plate, one down; their average x-component is $V \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta$).

**Step 4:** Therefore:
$$F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x} - V) = \rho a V (V \cos^2\theta - V)$$
$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (\cos^2\theta - 1) = -\rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$$

Magnitude of force on plate in x-direction:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta}$$

Similarly, by symmetry, **net force in y-direction = 0** (the two streams cancel out).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** Some textbooks use $\theta$ as the angle between jet and the *normal* to the plate. In that convention, $F_x = \rho a V^2 \cos^2\theta$. **Always draw your diagram first and label your angle** before applying the formula!

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force on a Stationary Curved Vane

### Setup
A jet strikes a **single curved vane** that turns the flow through angle $\phi$ (exit angle measured from original jet direction). The vane is smooth and frictionless.

### Velocity Analysis

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

**Step 2:** Apply momentum equation in x-direction:
$$F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x} - V_{in,x})$$
$$F_x = \rho a V (V \cos\phi - V) = \rho a V^2 (\cos\phi - 1)$$

Magnitude of force on vane in x-direction:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\phi)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply momentum equation in y-direction (taking upward as positive):
$$F_y = \dot{m}(V_{out,y} - V_{in,y})$$

The y-component of incoming velocity is 0. The y-component of outgoing velocity is $V \sin\phi$.
$$F_y = \rho a V (V \sin\phi - 0) = \rho a V^2 \sin\phi$$

This force $F_y$ is the **upward lifting force** on the vane:
$$\boxed{F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\phi}$$

### Special Case — Semicircular Vane ($\phi = 180°$)
$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos 180°) = \rho a V^2 (1-(-1)) = 2\rho a V^2$$

This is the **maximum possible force** — the jet is fully reversed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Force on a Single Moving Curved Vane

### Setup
The vane now moves with velocity $u$ in the **direction of the jet**. Because of this motion, the jet continuously contacts the vane.

### Relative Velocity Analysis

**Step 1:** Velocity of jet relative to the moving vane:
$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

**Step 2:** The relative velocity of exit from the vane (frictionless, so magnitude preserved on the vane):
$$V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V - u$$

The exit angle of the relative velocity is still $\phi$ with respect to the direction of motion.

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate actually striking the vane per second:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot a_{effective} \cdot V = \rho a (V-u)$$

This is because the **relative velocity** at inlet determines how much fluid the vane "sees" per unit time.

**Step 4:** Apply momentum equation in x-direction:
$$F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x,abs} - V_{in,x,abs})$$

**Absolute velocities:**
- $V_{in,x} = V$ (absolute incoming jet velocity)
- $V_{out,x} = V_{r2} \cos\phi + u = (V-u)\cos\phi + u$

**Step 5:** Substitute:
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u) \big[(V-u)\cos\phi + u - V\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u) \big[(V-u)\cos\phi - (V-u)\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 (\cos\phi - 1)$$

Magnitude:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 (1-\cos\phi)}$$

**Step 6:** Work done by the jet on the moving vane per second:
$$\boxed{W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V-u)^2 (1-\cos\phi)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Force on a Series of Moving Vanes (Pelton Wheel Concept)

### Key Insight
For a **single moving vane**, only a finite mass of fluid strikes it before the vane moves out of the jet. To ensure **continuous fluid engagement**, we use a **series of vanes** mounted on a wheel.

### The Continuous Mass Flow Argument

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate per second (independent of vane motion — determined by jet):
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

**Step 2:** Relative inlet velocity:
$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

**Step 3:** Force in x-direction (using $\dot{m} = \rho a V$, not $\rho a (V-u)$):
$$F_x = \rho a V \big[V_{r2}\cos\phi + u - V\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a V \big[(V-u)\cos\phi - (V-u)\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(\cos\phi - 1)$$

Magnitude:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1-\cos\phi)}$$

**Step 4:** Work done per second:
$$\boxed{W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a V u (V-u)(1-\cos\phi)}$$

**Step 5:** Kinetic energy of jet per second:
$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho a V^3$$

**Step 6:** Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{W}{KE} = \frac{\rho a V u (V-u)(1-\cos\phi)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3}$$
$$\eta = \frac{2u(V-u)(1-\cos\phi)}{V^2}$$

**Step 7:** For maximum efficiency, differentiate with respect to $u$:
$$\frac{d\eta}{du} = \frac{2(V-2u)(1-\cos\phi)}{V^2} = 0$$

This gives:
$$\boxed{u = \frac{V}{2}}$$

**Step 8:** Maximum efficiency (substituting $u = V/2$):
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot (V - \frac{V}{2}) \cdot (1-\cos\phi)}{V^2}$$
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot (1-\cos\phi)}{V^2}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2}(1-\cos\phi)}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Pelton Wheel Bucket (Reverse Flow)

For a Pelton wheel, the bucket is shaped to **reverse the jet by $180° - \beta$**, where $\beta$ is the angle the exit jet makes with the original direction (typically $\beta = 165°$ to $170°$).

### Force Equation for Pelton Wheel

The bucket reverses the relative flow. The x-component of relative exit velocity is:
$$V_{r2,x} = V_{r1} \cos(180° - \beta) = -V_{r1} \cos\beta = -(V-u)\cos\beta$$

Using series-vanes analysis:
$$F_x = \rho a V \big[(V-u)(-\cos\beta) + u - V\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a V \big[-(V-u)\cos\beta - (V-u)\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a V (V-u) \big[\cos\beta + 1\big]$$

Magnitude (taking the positive value):
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1+\cos\beta)}$$

For $\beta = 180°$ (perfect reversal): $F_x = 2\rho a V (V-u)$, which is the maximum.

### Maximum Efficiency for Pelton Wheel

Setting $\frac{d\eta}{du} = 0$:
$$\boxed{u = \frac{V(1+\cos\beta)}{2}}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}}$$

For $\beta = 165°$:
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1+\cos 165°}{2} = \frac{1-0.9659}{2} = 0.9830 \approx 98.3\%$$

This is why Pelton wheels are the **most efficient impulse turbines** in existence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Jet Impact Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **decision logic** a student must follow when solving any impulse-momentum problem in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Jet-Vane Problem] --> B{Is the vane stationary or moving?}
    B -->|Stationary| C{Jet hits Flat or Curved vane?}
    B -->|Moving| D{Jet hits Flat or Curved vane?}
    C -->|Flat Perpendicular| E1[Use F = rho a V squared]
    C -->|Flat Inclined theta| E2[Use Fx = rho a V squared sin squared theta]
    C -->|Curved phi| E3[Use Fx = rho a V squared 1 minus cos phi]
    D -->|Flat Inclined theta| F1[Use Fx = rho a V minus u squared sin squared theta]
    D -->|Curved phi| F2[Use Fx = rho a V minus u squared 1 minus cos phi]
    F2 --> G{Series of vanes or Single vane?}
    G -->|Single vane| H1[Mass flow = rho a V minus u]
    G -->|Series of vanes| H2[Mass flow = rho a V]
    H1 --> I[Compute Force then Work = F times u]
    H2 --> I
    E1 --> J[Power = Zero since vane is stationary]
    E2 --> J
    E3 --> J
    F1 --> K[Compute Power = F times u]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Compute Efficiency eta = Power over KE of jet]
    L --> M[END: Maximum efficiency by differentiating]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Force Resolution on an Inclined Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Jet Velocity V] -->|Strikes at angle theta| P1[Flat Plate Tilted]
    P1 -->|Splits| S1[Stream Up]
    P1 -->|Splits| S2[Stream Down]
    S1 --> FX1[Fx Reaction Force]
    S2 --> FX2[Fx Reaction Force]
    P1 --> FY[Net Fy = 0 by Symmetry]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Pelton Wheel Bucket Force Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Pressure Water from Penstock] --> B[Nozzle]
    B --> C[Jet emerges at Velocity V]
    C --> D[Bucket on Pelton Wheel]
    D -->|Splits into two halves| E1[Half Jet Reverses]
    D -->|Splits into two halves| E2[Other Half Reverses]
    E1 --> F1[Exits at angle beta]
    E2 --> F2[Exits at angle beta]
    D --> G[Force Fx on Bucket = rho a V V minus u times 1 plus cos beta]
    G --> H[Wheel Rotates with Velocity u]
    H --> I[Mechanical Power Output = Fx times u]
```

## 4.4 Force Vector Resolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Momentum Vector"]
        V1[Vin = V along x-axis]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Momentum Vector"]
        V2[Vout = V cos phi along x]
        V3[Vout = V sin phi along y]
    end
    V1 --> M1[Momentum Change in x = V cos phi minus V]
    V2 --> M1
    V1 --> M2[Momentum Change in y = V sin phi]
    V3 --> M2
    M1 --> Fx[Fx = rho a V squared 1 minus cos phi]
    M2 --> Fy[Fy = rho a V squared sin phi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These block diagrams are designed to mirror the **physical layout of force resolution** during an exam. Each block corresponds to a calculation step. KTU examiners award **process marks** when students clearly delineate their analysis — even if the final answer has a small arithmetic error.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**State and explain the Impulse-Momentum theorem as applied to a fluid jet striking a stationary vane. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** The net force acting on a fluid mass is equal to the time rate of change of its linear momentum. Mathematically:
> $$\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$$

**Explanation:**
For a fluid jet of cross-sectional area $a$, velocity $V$, and density $\rho$ striking a stationary vane:
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho a V$.
- The jet enters the control volume with velocity $V$ and leaves with a different velocity (changed in magnitude or direction) due to the impact.
- The change in momentum per second equals the force exerted by the vane on the fluid.
- By Newton's third law, an equal and opposite force is exerted by the jet on the vane.

**Key Points for Full Marks:**
1. Correct statement of theorem ✓
2. Identification of control volume ✓
3. Final force relation ✓

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
**A water jet of diameter 50 mm and velocity 25 m/s strikes a stationary flat plate held perpendicular to the jet axis. Find the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50 \; \text{mm} = 0.05 \; \text{m}$
- Velocity $V = 25 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute jet cross-sectional area:
$$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply impulse-momentum equation (stationary flat plate perpendicular):
$$F = \rho a V^2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times (25)^2$$
$$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 625$$
$$\boxed{F = 1227.2 \; \text{N}}$$

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B1A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive an expression for the force exerted by a jet of water on a stationary inclined flat plate when the jet strikes the plate at an angle $\theta$ to the plate surface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A jet of water 75 mm in diameter moving with a velocity of 30 m/s strikes a flat plate inclined at $30°$ to the axis of the jet. Find the force exerted by the jet on the plate, the components of force in the directions parallel and perpendicular to the plate. Take $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B1A(a):**

**Setup:** A jet of velocity $V$, area $a$, density $\rho$ strikes an inclined plate. Angle $\theta$ is measured between the jet axis and the plate surface.

**Step 1:** Resolve the jet velocity into two components:
- Component **along the plate** (tangential): $V_t = V \cos\theta$
- Component **normal to plate** (gets destroyed on impact): $V_n = V \sin\theta$

**Step 2:** After impact, the fluid flows along the plate with velocity $V \cos\theta$. Since the plate is symmetric, the fluid splits equally along both directions of the plate.

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate striking the plate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V$$

**Step 4:** Apply momentum equation in the direction **normal to the plate**:
$$F_n = \dot{m}(V_{out,n} - V_{in,n}) = \rho a V (0 - V \sin\theta) = -\rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

The negative sign indicates the force on the fluid is into the plate. By Newton's third law, the **force exerted by the jet on the plate (normal to plate):**
$$\boxed{F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta}$$

**Step 5:** Component along the plate direction: Since the fluid flows along the plate with velocity $V \cos\theta$ (tangential) in both directions symmetrically, the **net momentum change along the plate is zero**.

$$\boxed{F_t = 0}$$

**Step 6:** Resolve $F_n$ back into x and y components (if jet is along x-axis):
$$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$$
$$F_y = F_n \cos\theta = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

---

### **Model Solution for B1A(b):**

**Given:**
- $d = 75 \; \text{mm} = 0.075 \; \text{m}$, so $a = \dfrac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$
- $V = 30 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\theta = 30°$
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Total normal force on the plate:
$$F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$
$$F_n = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (30)^2 \times \sin 30°$$
$$F_n = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 900 \times 0.5$$
$$\boxed{F_n = 1988.1 \; \text{N}}$$

**Step 2:** Force along the plate (tangential): 
$$F_t = 0$$

**Step 3:** Force in x-direction (along jet axis):
$$F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$$
$$F_x = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 900 \times (0.5)^2$$
$$F_x = 3976.2 \times 0.25$$
$$\boxed{F_x = 994.05 \; \text{N}}$$

**Step 4:** Force in y-direction (perpendicular to jet):
$$F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$$
$$F_y = 3976.2 \times 0.5 \times 0.866$$
$$\boxed{F_y = 1721.7 \; \text{N}}$$

---

### **Question B1B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive an expression for the force exerted by a jet of water on a single moving curved vane at the exit angle $\phi$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A jet of water of diameter 100 mm and velocity 40 m/s strikes a single curved vane moving with a velocity of 15 m/s. The vane deflects the jet through an angle of $165°$. Calculate: (i) the force exerted by the jet on the vane in the direction of motion, and (ii) the work done per second. Take $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B1B(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Flow is steady, incompressible, and frictionless.
2. Atmospheric pressure acts on all free surfaces.
3. Jet velocity is uniform across the cross-section.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Vane moves with velocity $u$ in jet direction. Relative velocity of jet with respect to vane:
$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

**Step 2:** Since vane is frictionless, magnitude of relative velocity is conserved:
$$V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V - u$$

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate of fluid **actually striking the moving vane per second** is determined by the relative velocity:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot a \cdot V_{r1} = \rho a (V-u)$$

**Step 4:** Apply momentum equation in x-direction. Absolute velocities:
- $V_{in,x} = V$
- $V_{out,x} = V_{r2} \cos\phi + u = (V-u)\cos\phi + u$

$$F_x = \dot{m}(V_{out,x} - V_{in,x})$$
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u)\big[(V-u)\cos\phi + u - V\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u)\big[(V-u)\cos\phi - (V-u)\big]$$
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2(\cos\phi - 1)$$

Magnitude:
$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2(1-\cos\phi)}$$

---

### **Model Solution for B1B(b):**

**Given:**
- $d = 100 \; \text{mm} = 0.1 \; \text{m}$, so $a = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$
- $V = 40 \; \text{m/s}$
- $u = 15 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\phi = 165°$
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 (i):** Compute force on vane in x-direction:
$$F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2(1-\cos\phi)$$
$$F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (40-15)^2 \times (1-\cos 165°)$$
$$F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 625 \times (1-(-0.9659))$$
$$F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 625 \times 1.9659$$
$$\boxed{F_x = 9649.6 \; \text{N} \approx 9.65 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Step 2 (ii):** Work done per second:
$$W = F_x \cdot u$$
$$W = 9649.6 \times 15$$
$$\boxed{W = 144{,}744 \; \text{W} = 144.74 \; \text{kW}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS — KTU 2024 BOARD EXAMINER INSIGHTS:**
>
> **1. Wrong Angle Convention (loses 2-3 marks):** Mixing up $\theta$ as angle to plate surface vs. angle to plate normal. **Always draw a clear diagram with your angle labeled.** KTU examiners check diagrams first.
>
> **2. Forgetting Mass Flow Correction in Moving Vanes (loses 3-4 marks):** For a *single* moving vane, use $\dot{m} = \rho a (V-u)$. For a *series* of vanes, use $\dot{m} = \rho a V$. Confusing these is the **#1 mistake** in KTU papers.
>
> **3. Pressure Not Cancelling Out (loses 1-2 marks):** If you write pressure terms in the force equation for an open jet problem, you will lose marks. Always state "pressure is atmospheric and cancels out."
>
> **4. Wrong Maximum Efficiency Formula (loses 2 marks):** Maximum efficiency for **curved vane** is $\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2}(1-\cos\phi)$, but for **Pelton wheel** it is $\eta_{max} = \frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}$. Don't confuse them!
>
> **5. Missing Units (loses 0.5-1 mark):** KTU strictly requires SI units with all numerical answers. Always state the unit.
>
> **6. No Diagram = 1-2 Marks Lost:** Even for Part A questions, a quick sketch of the jet striking the vane earns process marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 MASTER THESE BEFORE ENTERING THE KTU EXAM HALL:**
>
> ✅ **Impulse-Momentum Equation:** $\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$
>
> ✅ **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho a V$ (always for jet discharge)
>
> ✅ **Stationary flat plate perpendicular:** $F = \rho a V^2$ — *no angle factor!*
>
> ✅ **Stationary inclined plate:** $F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$, $F_t = 0$
>
> ✅ **Stationary curved vane:** $F_x = \rho a V^2(1-\cos\phi)$, $F_y = \rho a V^2\sin\phi$
>
> ✅ **Single moving curved vane:** $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2(1-\cos\phi)$, uses $\dot{m} = \rho a(V-u)$
>
> ✅ **Series of moving curved vanes:** $F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1-\cos\phi)$, uses $\dot{m} = \rho a V$
>
> ✅ **Pelton wheel bucket:** $F_x = \rho a V (V-u)(1+\cos\beta)$
>
> ✅ **Work done per second:** $W = F_x \cdot u$
>
> ✅ **Maximum efficiency (general curved vane):** $u = V/2$, $\eta_{max} = (1-\cos\phi)/2$
>
> ✅ **Maximum efficiency (Pelton wheel):** $u = V(1+\cos\beta)/2$, $\eta_{max} = (1+\cos\beta)/2$
>
> ✅ **For $\beta = 165°$:** $u \approx 0.483V$, $\eta_{max} \approx 98.3\%$
>
> ✅ **Standard values:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
>
> ✅ **Pressure is atmospheric** on all free jet surfaces — always state this assumption explicitly.
>
> ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** with jet velocity, vane velocity, and reaction forces labeled.

---

> **🎓 END OF NOTES — IMPULSE MOMENTUM THEORY | MODULE 2 | PCPET303**
