topics.title: Applications of Energy and Momentum Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Energy and Momentum Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Equation):** A statement of the conservation of mechanical energy for a steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow along a streamline, mathematically expressed as:
> 
> $$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

where:
- $p \vert \rho g$ = Pressure head (m of fluid)
- $V^{2} \vert 2g$ = Velocity head / Kinetic energy head (m of fluid)
- $z$ = Elevation head / Potential energy head (m)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of fluid (**kg/m³**), $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

The **Momentum Equation** (derived from Newton's second law) is the linear momentum conservation statement for a control volume:

$$\sum F = \rho Q (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

where $\sum F$ is the net external force acting on the control volume, and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Slide Energy Story":**
> Imagine sliding down a water slide. When you are at the top, you are high up (high **elevation head** $z$), moving slowly (low **velocity head** $V^{2}/2g$), and the water pressure is moderate. As you slide down, you lose height but gain speed. The *total* of all three (pressure + velocity + elevation) stays constant — energy is just being *converted* from one form to another, never created or destroyed. That is exactly what Bernoulli's equation says about flowing fluids.

For the **Momentum Equation**, imagine a fire hose ejecting water: the hose pushes water forward, and by Newton's third law, the water pushes the hose *backward* (recoil). The force you must hold the hose with equals the rate of change of momentum of the water. This principle is used to design pipe bends, jet turbines, and rocket nozzles.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho_{water}$ | Density of water | **1000 kg/m³** |
| $\gamma$ | Specific weight of water | **9810 N/m³** |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | dimensionless (0.6 – 0.98) |
| $\beta$ | Momentum correction factor | 1.0 (uniform) to 1.33 (turbulent) |
| $\alpha$ | Kinetic energy correction factor | 1.0 (uniform) to 2.0 (turbulent) |

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Weightage on this Topic:**
> - This topic bridges **Module 2 (Fluid Kinematics & Dynamics)** with practical applications.
> - Expected questions in ESE: **1 Part-A (3 marks)** and often a **Part-B sub-part (7 marks)**.
> - High-yield sub-topics: **Venturi meter, Pitot tube, force on flat/curved vanes, jet propulsion.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Applications

The energy and momentum equations are applied to two broad engineering categories:

**A. Flow Measurement Devices (Energy Equation):**
1. **Venturi Meter** — measures discharge in a closed pipe using a converging–diverging section.
2. **Orifice Meter** — measures discharge using a flat plate with a sharp-edged hole.
3. **Pitot Tube** — measures stagnation (total) pressure to determine velocity.
4. **Flow Nozzle** — measures discharge in high-velocity pipelines.
5. **Weirs and Notches** — measures open-channel flow over a rectangular/V-notch.

**B. Force Evaluation on Boundaries (Momentum Equation):**
1. **Force on a stationary flat plate** struck by a jet.
2. **Force on a stationary curved plate** (e.g., Pelton wheel bucket at rest).
3. **Force on a moving flat/curved vane** (moving series of vanes — turbines).
4. **Force on pipe bends / reducers** (anchoring forces in hydraulic systems).
5. **Jet propulsion / rocket thrust** (variable mass momentum).

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Bernoulli Energy Equation

The energy equation is derived by integrating Euler's equation of motion along a streamline, *assuming*:
- **Steady flow** (no time-dependence at a point)
- **Incompressible flow** (constant $\rho$)
- **Inviscid flow** (no viscous losses)
- **Along a single streamline** (or irrotational flow field for full 3D applicability)

**Operational Logic Step-by-Step:**

1. **Step 1 — Pressure Energy per unit weight:** $\frac{p}{\rho g}$. This represents the work done by pressure forces to move a unit weight of fluid through the system.

2. **Step 2 — Kinetic Energy per unit weight:** $\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$. This represents the kinetic energy possessed by a unit weight of fluid in motion.

3. **Step 3 — Potential Energy per unit weight:** $z$. This represents the elevation of the fluid particle above a chosen datum.

4. **Step 4 — Conservation:** The sum is constant at any two points on a streamline:
   $$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}$$

5. **Step 5 — Why it works:** It is a direct consequence of the *work-energy theorem* applied to a fluid particle, where only pressure, gravity, and inertia act on it.

### 2.3 Logical Breakdown of the Momentum Equation

The momentum equation is derived by applying Newton's second law to a control volume:

1. **Step 1 — Identify control volume (CV)** bounded by inlet section (1) and outlet section (2).

2. **Step 2 — Sum external forces** acting on the CV: pressure forces, body forces (gravity), and surface (anchor) forces.

3. **Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law** to the fluid in the CV:
   $$\sum F = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho V \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho V (V \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

4. **Step 4 — For steady flow**, the first term vanishes, leaving only the flux term:
   $$\sum F = \rho Q (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

5. **Step 5 — Why it works:** It is independent of internal friction; thus it gives the *net force* exerted by the fluid on its boundaries (e.g., pipe walls, vanes).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Device / Application | Governing Equation | Discharge / Force Formula |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Venturi Meter** | $p_1/\rho g + V_1^2/2g = p_2/\rho g + V_2^2/2g$ | $Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}\sqrt{2g\,h}$; $Q = C_d Q_{th}$ |
| 2 | **Orifice Meter** | Same as Venturi | $Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}\sqrt{2g\,h}$; $Q = C_d Q_{th}$, $C_d \approx 0.61$–$0.65$ |
| 3 | **Pitot Tube** | Stagnation + static pressure | $V = \sqrt{2g\,h} = \sqrt{2(p_0 - p)/\rho}$ |
| 4 | **Rectangular Weir** | Energy over crest | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ |
| 5 | **V-Notch Weir** | Energy over V-crest | $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}$ |
| 6 | **Force on Stationary Flat Plate (jet hits normally)** | $F = \rho a V^2$ | $F_x = \rho a V^2$, $F_y = 0$ |
| 7 | **Force on Stationary Inclined Plate** | Resolve velocity components | $F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$, $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$ |
| 8 | **Force on Stationary Curved Vane** | $F_x = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\beta)$ | $F_y = \rho Q V \sin\beta$ |
| 9 | **Force on Moving Flat Vane** | $u = V - V_{vane}$ | $F_x = \rho a (V - V_v)^2$ |
| 10 | **Force on Moving Curved Vane** | Relative velocity $u = V - V_v$ | $F_x = \rho a u^2 (1 - \cos\beta)$ |
| 11 | **Force on Pipe Bend** | Vector sum at inlet/outlet | $F = \rho Q (V_1 - V_2)$ in vector form |
| 12 | **Propulsive Thrust (Rocket/Jet)** | $F = \rho Q V_{exit}$ | $F = \rho a V^2$ for free jet |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notational Convention Used:**
> - $a$ = jet area (m²), $V$ = jet velocity (m/s), $Q = aV$ = discharge (m³/s)
> - $h$ = piezometric head difference (m) = $(p_1/\rho g + z_1) - (p_2/\rho g + z_2)$
> - $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (empirical, < 1.0)
> - $\theta$ = plate inclination with jet
> - $\beta$ = angle of curved vane deflection

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in industry:**
> - **Venturi/Orifice meters** are used in chemical plants, water distribution networks, and aircraft fuel systems.
> - **Pitot tubes** are fundamental to airspeed indicators in commercial aircraft.
> - **Momentum-based force calculations** are critical in the design of Pelton turbine buckets, ship propellers, hydraulic pipe-support systems, and fire-fighting monitors.
> - **Moving vane analysis** is the foundation of single-stage impulse turbine design (Pelton wheel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Discharge Through a Venturi Meter

**Problem Setup:** A horizontal venturi meter with inlet diameter $D_1$, throat diameter $D_2$, and piezometric head difference $h$ (measured by a differential manometer or pressure gauges). Find the theoretical discharge.

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
- Flow is horizontal → $z_1 = z_2$.
- Apply Bernoulli's equation between section 1 (inlet) and section 2 (throat).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Write Bernoulli's equation between section 1 and section 2:**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply horizontal condition:** Since the venturi is horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$, so the $z$ terms cancel:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the head difference:**

$$\left(\frac{p_1}{\rho g} - \frac{p_2}{\rho g}\right) = \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g}$$

**Step 4 — Define piezometric head difference:** $h = \dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} - \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g}$. Thus:

$$h = \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g}$$

**Step 5 — Apply continuity equation (incompressible flow):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, so $V_1 = (A_2 / A_1) V_2$. Substituting:

$$h = \frac{V_2^{2} - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} V_2^{2}}{2g} = \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for throat velocity $V_2$:**

$$V_2^{2} = \frac{2g\,h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2g\,h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Theoretical discharge:** $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2g\,h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$$

**Step 8 — Apply empirical coefficient of discharge $C_d$** (typically 0.95–0.98 for venturi, 0.61–0.65 for orifice):

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2g\,h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity From a Pitot Tube

**Problem Setup:** A Pitot tube measures the difference between stagnation pressure $p_0$ and static pressure $p$ at a point in a flow of velocity $V$.

**Step 1 — Write Bernoulli's equation** between a point in the flow (static) and the stagnation point (where the fluid is brought to rest):

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \frac{p_0}{\rho g} + \frac{0^{2}}{2g} + z$$

**Step 2 — Cancel elevation** (since both points are at the same height):

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_0}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $V$:**

$$\frac{V^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_0 - p}{\rho g}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $2g$:**

$$V^{2} = \frac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{2g\,h}}$$

where $h = (p_0 - p) \vert \rho g$ is the stagnation-head rise shown by the Pitot-static tube's manometer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Force on a Stationary Flat Plate Struck by a Jet Normally

**Problem Setup:** A jet of cross-sectional area $a$ and velocity $V$ impinges normally on a stationary flat plate. Find the force exerted by the jet on the plate.

**Step 1 — Identify the control volume (CV):** a small envelope around the impact region. Inlet = the jet (section 1); Outlet = the spreading water film on the plate (section 2). The film velocity in the direction of the jet equals $V$ (since plate is stationary, no friction loss assumed; in practice slightly less, hence $C_v$).

**Step 2 — Initial momentum flux (along jet axis, $x$-direction):**

$$\dot{M}_{1,x} = \rho Q V = \rho (a V) V = \rho a V^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Final momentum flux:** After hitting the plate, water flows along the plate at $90°$ to the jet. The $x$-component of outlet velocity is **zero**:

$$\dot{M}_{2,x} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-flow momentum equation** in the $x$-direction:

$$F_x = \dot{M}_{1,x} - \dot{M}_{2,x} = \rho a V^{2} - 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the force on the fluid (and hence on the plate by Newton's third law):**

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^{2} \quad \text{(newtons)}}$$

**Step 6 — $y$-direction:** The flow is symmetric, so $F_y = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The jet's *full* forward momentum is destroyed in one instant, producing a high stagnation pressure — the same principle that allows a fire hose to knock a person down.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Force on a Stationary Inclined Flat Plate

**Problem Setup:** A jet of area $a$ and velocity $V$ strikes a flat plate inclined at angle $\theta$ to the jet. Find $F_x$ and $F_y$.

**Step 1 — Decompose jet velocity** into components along and perpendicular to the plate:
- Along-plate component: $V \cos\theta$
- Normal-to-plate component: $V \sin\theta$ (this component is destroyed upon impact)

**Step 2 — The mass flow rate** is $\dot{m} = \rho a V$.

**Step 3 — Force normal to the plate (caused by destruction of normal momentum):**

$$F_n = \dot{m} \cdot V \sin\theta = \rho a V^{2} \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Cartesian components** by multiplying with $\sin\theta$ (for $F_x$) and $\cos\theta$ (for $F_y$):

$$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = \rho a V^{2} \sin^{2}\theta$$

$$F_y = F_n \cos\theta = \rho a V^{2} \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^{2} \sin^{2}\theta, \quad F_y = \rho a V^{2} \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}\rho a V^{2} \sin 2\theta}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Force on a Stationary Curved Vane (Pelton Bucket at Rest)

**Problem Setup:** A jet of area $a$ and velocity $V$ enters a curved vane that turns the flow through an angle $\beta$. Find the forces $F_x$ and $F_y$.

**Step 1 — Define inlet and outlet velocities:**
- Inlet: $V_1 = V$ (along $+x$ axis)
- Outlet: $V_2 = V$ (in magnitude, assuming no friction; direction turned by $\beta$)
- Outlet components: $V_{2x} = V \cos\beta$, $V_{2y} = V \sin\beta$

**Step 2 — Apply momentum equation in $x$:**

$$F_x = \rho Q (V_{1x} - V_{2x}) = \rho a V (V - V\cos\beta) = \rho a V^{2}(1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 3 — Apply momentum equation in $y$** (assuming $V_{1y} = 0$):

$$F_y = \rho Q (V_{1y} - V_{2y}) = \rho a V (0 - V\sin\beta) = -\rho a V^{2} \sin\beta$$

**Step 4 — The negative sign** indicates that $F_y$ acts in the $-y$ direction (downward). Magnitudes:

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^{2}(1 - \cos\beta), \quad F_y = \rho a V^{2} \sin\beta}$$

**Step 5 — Work done by jet on the vane** (in case of moving vane, see next):

$$W = F_x \cdot (\text{displacement of vane in } x \text{-direction per second})$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Force on a Single Moving Flat Vane

**Problem Setup:** A jet of velocity $V$ and area $a$ impinges on a vane moving with velocity $u$ in the same direction as the jet.

**Step 1 — Relative velocity of jet with respect to vane:**

$$V_r = V - u$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate striking the vane (relative):**

$$\dot{m}_r = \rho \cdot a_r \cdot V_r$$

where $a_r = a$ (the jet is uniform, vane intercepts the same cross-section).

**Step 3 — Apply the momentum equation** in the frame of the vane (Galilean transform, valid for $u \ll c$):

$$F_x = \dot{m}_r \cdot V_r = \rho a (V - u)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Work done per second on the vane:**

$$W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency (ratio of work to kinetic energy supplied by jet per second):**

$$\eta = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}V^{2}} = \frac{\rho a u (V - u)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho a V \cdot V^{2}} = \frac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Maximize efficiency** by setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{du} = 0$. Result: $u = V \vert 3$ gives maximum efficiency of:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot (V/3) \cdot (2V/3)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \frac{2 \cdot V/3 \cdot 4V^{2}/9}{V^{3}} = \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.593$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)^{2}, \quad W = \rho a u(V - u)^{2}, \quad \eta_{max} = \frac{8}{27}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 8/27 matters:** The result $u = V/3$ and $\eta = 8/27$ is the **single-jet maximum efficiency** for a flat moving vane. This is a recurring favorite in KTU Part-B problems.

---

### 3.7 Derivation 7: Force on a Moving Curved Vane (General Case)

**Step 1 — Relative velocity at entry:** $V_{r1} = V - u$.

**Step 2 — Relative velocity at exit:** $V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V - u$ (assuming frictionless, same magnitude; turned by angle $\beta$).

**Step 3 — Force on vane in $x$-direction (using relative velocity analysis):**

$$F_x = \rho a (V - u)^{2} (1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 4 — Work done per second:**

$$W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)^{2} (1 - \cos\beta)$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)^{2}(1 - \cos\beta)}$$

**Step 5 — Optimum vane speed for maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{2u(V-u)^{2}(1-\cos\beta)}{V^{3}}$$

Setting $d\eta/du = 0$ gives $u = V/3$, independent of $\beta$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{8}{27}(1 - \cos\beta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pelton Wheel Insight:** For a $180°$ curved vane, $\cos\beta = \cos 180° = -1$, so $1 - \cos\beta = 2$, giving $\eta_{max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$. A Pelton wheel with a single jet can never exceed this efficiency.

---

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference Summary of All Derivations

| Application | Key Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|------------|-------------|---------------|
| Venturi meter | $Q = C_d \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $h$ = piezometric difference |
| Pitot tube | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ | $h$ = stagnation head |
| Stationary flat plate | $F = \rho a V^2$ | — |
| Inclined flat plate | $F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$ | $\theta$ = inclination |
| Curved vane (stationary) | $F_x = \rho a V^2(1-\cos\beta)$ | $\beta$ = turning angle |
| Moving flat vane | $F = \rho a (V-u)^2$ | $u$ = vane speed |
| Moving curved vane | $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 (1-\cos\beta)$ | — |
| Max efficiency (single jet) | $\eta_{max} = \frac{8}{27}$ | at $u = V/3$ |
| Force on pipe bend | $F = \rho Q (V_1 - V_2)$ | vector form |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, 3 marks]**
*"State Bernoulli's theorem and write its equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. List any three assumptions."*

**Model Answer:**

Bernoulli's theorem states that in a steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow, the total mechanical energy per unit weight of fluid remains constant along a streamline.

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$)
3. Inviscid (no viscous losses)
4. Flow along a streamline
5. No energy addition or extraction between the two points

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 marks]**
*"Define coefficient of discharge $C_d$ for a venturi meter. What is its typical range?"*

**Model Answer:**

The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

**Typical values:**
- Venturi meter: $C_d = 0.95$ to $0.98$
- Orifice meter: $C_d = 0.61$ to $0.65$
- Borda mouthpiece: $C_d = 0.5$

The value is less than 1.0 because of viscous losses and vena-contracta effects.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-2 Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions on this topic typically test **derivation + numerical application**. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. KTU allows the student to answer EITHER Set-A OR Set-B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a venturi meter fitted in a horizontal pipe. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A venturi meter has a main diameter of 200 mm and a throat diameter of 100 mm. Water flows through it and the differential manometer shows a head difference of 0.5 m of water. Take $C_d = 0.98$. Calculate: (i) the velocity at the throat, and (ii) the discharge through the meter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Derivation 1 in SECTION 3** (Section 3.1) for the full step-by-step derivation. Key result:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2g\,h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$$

**Assumptions** (must list in answer):
1. Steady, incompressible flow
2. No friction loss
3. Horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$)
4. No vena-contracta effect (unlike orifice)
5. Velocity distribution is uniform at each section

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $D_1 = 200$ mm = 0.2 m, $D_2 = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $h = 0.5$ m, $C_d = 0.98$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Calculate areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^{2} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^{2} = 0.00785 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Area ratio:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.1}{0.2}\right)^{2} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Throat velocity (theoretical):**

$$V_{2,th} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.5}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.25^{2}}} = \frac{\sqrt{9.81}}{\sqrt{0.9375}} = \frac{3.132}{0.9683} = 3.234 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Actual throat velocity** (using $C_v$, but here $C_d = 0.98 \approx C_v$ for venturi):

$$V_2 \approx 3.17 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Actual discharge:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot V_{2,th} = 0.98 \times 0.00785 \times 3.234$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.02488 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 24.88 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** A jet of water with diameter 50 mm and velocity 20 m/s strikes a stationary flat plate normally. Calculate the force exerted by the jet on the plate and the work done per second. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a single jet striking a series of moving flat vanes. Show that the maximum efficiency is 8/27. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $D = 0.05$ m, $V = 20$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Jet area:**

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^{2} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Force on plate** (using $F = \rho a V^{2}$):

$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^{2}$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 400$$

$$\boxed{F = 785.4 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3 — Work done per second:**

Since the plate is stationary, the work done by the jet on the plate is **zero** (no displacement of the plate). The kinetic energy of the jet is dissipated as heat in the spreading water film.

$$\boxed{W = 0 \text{ W (for stationary plate)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Reinforcement:** This illustrates why turbines use *moving* vanes — a stationary plate dissipates all kinetic energy; a moving vane converts it to useful mechanical work.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Refer to **Derivation 6 in SECTION 3** (Section 3.6) for the full step-by-step derivation.

**Summary of key results:**

$$F = \rho a (V - u)^{2}$$

$$W = F \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)^{2}$$

$$\eta = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}V^{2}} = \frac{2u(V - u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$$

**Setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{du} = 0$:**

$$\frac{d\eta}{du} = \frac{2}{V^{3}}\left[(V-u)^{2} - 2u(V-u)\right] = 0$$

$$(V - u)^{2} - 2u(V - u) = 0$$

$$(V - u)(V - u - 2u) = 0$$

$$(V - u)(V - 3u) = 0$$

Since $u \neq V$ (otherwise no relative motion), we get $V - 3u = 0$, i.e., $u = V/3$.

**Substituting back into $\eta$:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot (V/3) \cdot (V - V/3)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \frac{2V/3 \cdot (2V/3)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \frac{2V/3 \cdot 4V^{2}/9}{V^{3}}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{8V^{3}/27}{V^{3}} = \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.593 = 59.3\%$$

$$\boxed{u_{opt} = \frac{V}{3}, \quad \eta_{max} = \frac{8}{27} \approx 59.3\%}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mistakes students make in this topic (verified from KTU answer scripts):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the area ratio term** $\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$ in the venturi/orifice formula. A common incorrect version $Q = A_2\sqrt{2gh}$ is **wrong** unless $A_2 \ll A_1$. Always include the correction term.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $C_d$, $C_c$, and $C_v$**: $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ where $C_c$ = coefficient of contraction, $C_v$ = coefficient of velocity. For venturi, $C_c = 1$. For orifice, $C_c \approx 0.62$–$0.65$.
> 
> 3. **Using absolute velocity $V$ in moving-vane problems** instead of relative velocity $V - u$. This is the #1 error. *Always* subtract the vane speed.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting that work done on a stationary plate is zero.** The force is non-zero, but with no displacement, mechanical work is zero.
> 
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: Mixing mm with m, or using $g = 9.8$ vs $g = 9.81$ inconsistently. Stick to SI throughout and convert diameters to meters first.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Applications of Energy & Momentum Equations"**

**1. Energy Equation Mastery:**
- Bernoulli's equation is for *steady, incompressible, inviscid* flow along a streamline.
- The total head $p/\rho g + V^{2}/2g + z$ is constant if there are no losses.
- Practical devices (venturi, orifice, pitot, weir) all rely on the conversion between pressure head and velocity head.

**2. Discharge Measurement Devices:**
- **Venturi:** $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.98$; minimum head loss.
- **Orifice:** $C_d \approx 0.61$–$0.65$; high loss but cheap.
- **Pitot tube:** measures $V = \sqrt{2gh}$, NOT $Q$ directly.
- **Rectangular weir:** $Q = (2/3)\,C_d\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$.
- **V-notch weir:** $Q = (8/15)\,C_d\,\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$.

**3. Momentum Equation Mastery:**
- The momentum equation is a *vector* equation; always resolve into $x$ and $y$ components.
- The result is the **net force** on the boundary (or anchor) — it does NOT require knowing pressure or friction inside the fluid.

**4. Force Formulas — must memorize:**

| Situation | $F_x$ Formula |
|-----------|---------------|
| Stationary flat plate | $\rho a V^2$ |
| Inclined plate | $\rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$ |
| Curved vane (stationary) | $\rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\beta)$ |
| Moving flat vane | $\rho a (V - u)^2$ |
| Moving curved vane | $\rho a (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\beta)$ |

**5. The Magic Number 8/27:**
- For a single jet impinging on moving vanes, the **maximum possible efficiency is 8/27 ≈ 59.3%**, occurring at vane speed $u = V/3$.
- For a curved vane that turns flow by angle $\beta$, the maximum efficiency is $\eta_{max} = (8/27)(1 - \cos\beta)$.
- For a Pelton wheel with $180°$ cup: $\eta_{max} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$.

**6. Pitot vs Venturi — Don't Confuse:**
- **Venturi/Orifice** → measures **discharge $Q$** (uses two-section analysis).
- **Pitot tube** → measures **velocity $V$** (uses stagnation vs static pressure at one point).

**7. KTU Exam-Ready Mnemonic — "PAVZ":**
- **P** = Pressure head
- **A** = (kinetic) Acceleration / velocity head
- **V** = Velocity
- **Z** = Zenith / elevation head
- Total **PAVZ** is constant along a streamline (Bernoulli).

**8. Remember the Assumption Hierarchy:**
- Bernoulli → ideal flow (no losses).
- Real systems → add head loss $h_L$: $p_1/\rho g + V_1^2/2g + z_1 = p_2/\rho g + V_2^2/2g + z_2 + h_L$
- Use this modified form for venturi/orifice when asked about losses.

**9. Key Constants to Remember:**
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use 9.81, not 9.8, in KTU answers)
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225$ kg/m³ (only if asked)

**10. The Most Likely KTU Question Pattern:**
- "Derive the discharge formula for a venturi meter and apply to given data" → 14 marks.
- "A jet strikes a moving vane. Find force, work, and efficiency" → 14 marks.
- "Explain Pitot tube with derivation" → 3 marks (Part A) or 7 marks (Part B sub-part).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start every derivation with a clearly labeled diagram (CV with inlet/outlet sections), then list the *assumptions* explicitly — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks just for this discipline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipe Flow Systems: Darcy-Weisbach Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pipe Flow Systems: Darcy-Weisbach Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** is the universal empirical-analytical equation used in fluid mechanics to calculate the **head loss (or pressure drop)** caused by friction along a given length of pipe conveying a fluid under steady, incompressible flow conditions. Formulated independently by Henry Darcy (1857) and Julius Weisbach (1845), it is considered the most accurate and universally accepted model for calculating frictional resistance in closed conduits, spanning both **laminar** and **turbulent** flow regimes.

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = frictional head loss (in meters)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
- $L$ = length of the pipe (in meters)
- $D$ = internal diameter of the pipe (in meters)
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (in m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Darcy-Weisbach equation is universally applicable — it works for **laminar, transitional, and turbulent flows**, and for **all pipe geometries (circular and non-circular)** when the hydraulic diameter is substituted. This universality makes it the gold standard in KTU board examinations over empirical formulas like Hazen-Williams (water-only) or Manning's (open channel).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a long, narrow straw.

- If you use a **wider straw**, the milkshake flows easily — *low friction, low head loss*.
- If you **double the length** of the straw, you have to suck harder because friction acts over a longer distance — *head loss is directly proportional to L*.
- If the milkshake is **thicker** (more viscous), the resistance increases — *head loss is proportional to viscosity*.
- If you **suck harder** (higher velocity), the turbulent churning inside the straw intensifies dramatically — *head loss grows with V²*.
- If the inside of the straw is **rough** (like an old rusty pipe), friction multiplies — *the friction factor f increases with roughness*.

The Darcy-Weisbach equation mathematically captures **all** of these intuitions into a single elegant formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why V²/2g?** The term $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ is the **velocity head** — the kinetic energy per unit weight of the flowing fluid. Friction head loss is essentially a fraction of this velocity head, scaled by the length-to-diameter ratio $(L/D)$ and the friction factor $(f)$. This is why friction loss grows with the *square* of velocity — a critical insight in pump selection.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance of the Friction Factor $f$

The **Darcy friction factor $f$** is the dimensionless coefficient that encapsulates:
1. The **flow regime** (laminar vs. turbulent)
2. The **pipe roughness characteristics** (relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$)
3. The **Reynolds number** ($Re$)

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number ($Re$) | Friction Factor Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | $f = 64/Re$ (theoretical, independent of roughness) |
| **Transitional** | $2000 < Re < 4000$ | Unstable — empirical interpolation required |
| **Turbulent (smooth)** | $Re > 4000$, smooth pipe | Blasius, Prandtl-von Karman equations |
| **Turbulent (rough)** | $Re > 4000$, rough pipe | Colebrook-White, Swamee-Jain, Haaland equations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test whether students know that in **fully rough turbulent flow**, the friction factor becomes **independent of Reynolds number** and depends *only* on relative roughness. This is called the **fully turbulent regime** on the Moody chart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Pipe Friction

When a real (viscous) fluid flows through a pipe, a **no-slip condition** at the pipe wall creates a velocity gradient from zero (at the wall) to maximum (at the centerline). This gradient produces:
- **Viscous shear stress** $\tau_w$ at the wall
- A **pressure drop** $\Delta P$ along the pipe length to overcome this shear

By applying a **force balance** on a cylindrical control volume of fluid in the pipe:

$$\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Solving for wall shear stress:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$$

The **Darcy-Weisbach equation** is derived by recognizing that the head loss $h_f = \Delta P / (\rho g)$, and introducing a **friction factor** $f = 4 \tau_w / \left(\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2\right)$ (i.e., a wall-shear-to-dynamic-pressure ratio scaled by 4):

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} \quad \text{(Darcy-Weisbach form)}$$

---

### 2.2 The Friction Factor — Why It Is the Heart of the Equation

The friction factor $f$ is **not constant** — it is a function of two dimensionless parameters:

$$f = \phi(Re, \varepsilon/D)$$

Where:
- $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ — Reynolds number (inertia vs. viscous forces)
- $\varepsilon/D$ — **relative roughness** (absolute roughness $\varepsilon$ ÷ pipe diameter $D$)

#### 2.2.1 The Moody Chart — The Engineer's Bible

The **Moody Chart** (Lewis F. Moody, 1944) is a graphical plot of $f$ vs. $Re$ with $\varepsilon/D$ as the parametric family of curves. It visually unifies:
- Laminar ($f = 64/Re$)
- Transitional (interpolated band)
- Turbulent (blended with Colebrook equation)

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Regime | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | General frictional head loss | Darcy-Weisbach (head form) |
| 2 | $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop (Pa) | Multiply (1) by $\rho g$ |
| 3 | $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{4 f L \rho Q^2}{2 \pi^2 D^5}$ | Pressure drop in terms of $Q$ | Substitute $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ |
| 4 | $f_{lam} = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Laminar flow only | Exact, theoretical (Hagen-Poiseuille) |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | All flows | Critical: $Re < 2000$ laminar, $> 4000$ turbulent |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Implicit, all turbulent flow | Colebrook-White equation (1939) |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$ | Explicit approximation | Swamee-Jain (1976) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{Re}\right]$ | Explicit approximation | Haaland (1983) |
| 9 | $f = 0.316 Re^{-0.25}$ | Turbulent, smooth, $Re < 10^5$ | Blasius equation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log_{10}(\varepsilon/D) + 1.14$ | Fully rough turbulent | Nikuradse (limit of Colebrook) |
| 11 | $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Continuity | Mean velocity from discharge |
| 12 | $h_L = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minor losses | Entrance, exit, fittings, valves |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Darcy-Weisbach equation is the **workhorse of pipeline engineering**:
- **Water supply networks**: Sizing pipes for municipal distribution.
- **Oil & gas pipelines**: Computing pumping power for crude oil transport over hundreds of kilometers.
- **HVAC systems**: Duct and chilled water pipe sizing.
- **Power plant condensers**: Cooling water circuits.
- **Fire protection systems**: Sprinkler hydraulic calculations.

The friction factor $f$ ultimately determines **pump power consumption**:
$$P_{pump} = \rho g Q h_f \quad \text{[Watts]}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define a cylindrical control volume**

Consider a horizontal pipe of constant diameter $D$ with steady, fully-developed flow. Take a fluid cylinder of length $L$ as the control volume.

Forces acting on this control volume along the flow direction:
- Pressure force at inlet: $P_1 \cdot A = P_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (acts forward, +)
- Pressure force at outlet: $P_2 \cdot A = P_2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (acts backward, −)
- Wall shear force: $\tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$ (acts backward, opposes motion, −)

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-flow momentum equation (no acceleration)**

For fully-developed flow, there is **no net momentum change**:

$$\sum F_x = 0$$

$$P_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - P_2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for wall shear stress**

$$(P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Dividing both sides by $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

Defining the **pressure drop** as $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Darcy friction factor**

The **Darcy friction factor** $f$ is a dimensionless number defined as four times the ratio of wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$f = \frac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V^2}$$

This definition (note the factor of 8 vs. Fanning's factor of 2) is the **convention used universally in mechanical and civil engineering** and is the version tested in KTU examinations.

Solving for wall shear stress:

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \rho V^2}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\tau_w$ into the pressure drop equation**

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

This is the **Darcy-Weisbach equation in pressure form**.

**Step 6 — Convert to head loss form**

The head loss is defined as:

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$$

Substituting:

$$h_f = \frac{f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}}{\rho g}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

This is the celebrated **Darcy-Weisbach equation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Laminar Friction Factor ($f = 64/Re$)

For **laminar flow** in a circular pipe, the velocity profile is exactly parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille flow), derivable from the Navier-Stokes equations.

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate from the parabolic profile**

For fully-developed laminar flow, the velocity distribution is:

$$u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right] = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) R^2}{4 \mu L}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

The mean velocity is half the maximum velocity:

$$V = \frac{u_{max}}{2} = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) R^2}{8 \mu L} = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

**Step 2 — Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation**

Solving for pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate to Darcy-Weisbach form**

Setting the Hagen-Poiseuille result equal to the Darcy-Weisbach pressure form:

$$f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f$**

Cancel $L$, $V$, and one $D$:

$$\frac{f \rho V}{2 D} = \frac{32 \mu}{D^2}$$

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

$$\boxed{f_{laminar} = \frac{64}{Re}}$$

This is an **exact, theoretical** result — no empirical constants, no assumptions about roughness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Discharge Form $\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L \rho Q^2}{2 \pi^2 D^5}$

Starting from the Darcy-Weisbach pressure form:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

From continuity for circular pipe:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi D^2}{4}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

So:

$$V^2 = \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$

Substituting back:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho}{2} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$

$$\Delta P = f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot Q^2 \cdot \frac{16}{2 \pi^2 D^5}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Some textbooks write $\frac{4 f L \rho Q^2}{2 \pi^2 D^5}$ because they retain the factor 4 inside and use $V = Q/A$ differently. Both forms are equivalent; verify by substitution. The canonical form is $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$.

---

### 3.4 The Colebrook-White Equation — Implicit Turbulent Friction Factor

For turbulent flow, no closed-form exact solution exists for $f$. The **Colebrook-White equation (1939)** is the accepted semi-empirical implicit formula:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

**Rationale:**
- The $\varepsilon/D$ term captures the **wall roughness contribution** (rough-wall asymptote).
- The $2.51/(Re\sqrt{f})$ term captures the **viscous sublayer contribution** (smooth-wall asymptote).
- The "+" inside the log reflects the **additive resistance** of viscous and roughness effects — physically a **parallel-resistance model**.

**Fully smooth limit** ($\varepsilon \to 0$):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

**Fully rough limit** ($Re \to \infty$):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right) \implies f \text{ independent of } Re$$

---

### 3.5 Explicit Approximation — Swamee-Jain Equation

Because Colebrook-White is implicit in $f$, KTU numerical problems often use the explicit **Swamee-Jain (1976)** approximation:

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

**Validity range:** $10^{-6} < \varepsilon/D < 10^{-2}$, $5000 < Re < 10^8$.

**Error:** Within **±2%** of Colebrook-White — acceptable for all engineering work.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Water at **20 °C** flows through a **commercial steel pipe** of diameter $D = 50$ mm and length $L = 100$ m at a mean velocity of $V = 3$ m/s. Compute the head loss using the Darcy-Weisbach equation.

**Given:**
- $\nu_{water @ 20°C} = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (kinematic viscosity)
- $\varepsilon_{commercial\ steel} = 0.045$ mm
- $D = 0.050$ m
- $L = 100$ m
- $V = 3$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.050}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.15}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.494 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 2 — Compute Relative Roughness**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction factor using Swamee-Jain**

Compute the inner term:

$$\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 2.432 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{(1.494 \times 10^5)^{0.9}}$$

Computing $(1.494 \times 10^5)^{0.9}$:

$$\log_{10}(1.494 \times 10^5) = 5.174$$

$$0.9 \times 5.174 = 4.657 \implies (1.494 \times 10^5)^{0.9} \approx 10^{4.657} \approx 4.537 \times 10^4$$

$$\frac{5.74}{4.537 \times 10^4} = 1.265 \times 10^{-4}$$

Sum inside log:

$$2.432 \times 10^{-4} + 1.265 \times 10^{-4} = 3.697 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\log_{10}(3.697 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.432$$

$$[\log_{10}(\cdot)]^2 = 11.78$$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{11.78} = 0.0212$$

**Step 4 — Compute head loss**

$$h_f = 0.0212 \times \frac{100}{0.050} \times \frac{(3)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0212 \times 2000 \times \frac{9}{19.62}$$

$$h_f = 0.0212 \times 2000 \times 0.4587$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 19.45 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute pressure drop**

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 998 \times 9.81 \times 19.45 \approx 1.904 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.90 \text{ bar}$$

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State the Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe. Explain the physical significance of the friction factor $f$ and how it varies in laminar and turbulent flow regimes.

**Model Answer:**

The Darcy-Weisbach equation for frictional head loss is:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Physical significance of $f$:** The Darcy friction factor is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure of the flow (scaled by 8). It quantifies the resistance offered by the pipe wall to the flowing fluid.

**Variation with flow regime:**
- **Laminar flow** ($Re < 2000$): $f = 64/Re$. Friction factor depends **only on Reynolds number** and is independent of pipe roughness.
- **Turbulent flow** ($Re > 4000$): $f$ depends on **both** $Re$ and the relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$. It is determined using the Colebrook-White equation or its explicit approximations (Swamee-Jain, Haaland).
- **Fully rough turbulent flow**: At very high $Re$, $f$ becomes **independent of $Re$** and depends solely on $\varepsilon/D$ (horizontal asymptote on Moody chart).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 2 marks for explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Differentiate between the Darcy friction factor and the Fanning friction factor. State the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Darcy Friction Factor ($f_D$) | Fanning Friction Factor ($f_F$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $f_D = 8 \tau_w / (\rho V^2)$ | $f_F = 2 \tau_w / (\rho V^2)$ |
| Pipe head loss formula | $h_f = f_D \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $h_f = 4 f_F \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ |
| Used in | Civil, Mechanical, Chemical engineering | Chemical engineering (older texts) |
| Laminar value | $f_D = 64/Re$ | $f_F = 16/Re$ |

**Mathematical relationship:**

$$f_D = 4 \cdot f_F$$

That is, the Darcy friction factor is always **four times** the Fanning friction factor.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for differentiation table, 1 mark for relationship)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation from first principles, starting from a force balance on a cylindrical control volume of fluid in a pipe. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter $D = 100$ mm and length $L = 200$ m at a flow rate of $Q = 0.02$ m³/s. Using the Darcy-Weisbach equation, compute:
  (i) The mean flow velocity
  (ii) The Reynolds number and identify the flow regime
  (iii) The Darcy friction factor using the Swamee-Jain equation
  (iv) The head loss and pumping power required

Take $\varepsilon$ for commercial steel = **0.045 mm**.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Uniform cross-section (constant $D$).
3. Fully-developed flow (velocity profile unchanging along length).
4. Horizontal pipe (or constant elevation).
5. Newtonian fluid.

**Step 1:** Consider a cylindrical control volume of fluid of length $L$ inside the pipe.

**Step 2:** Identify forces in the flow direction:
- Inlet pressure force: $P_1 A = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Outlet pressure force: $P_2 A = P_2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Wall shear force: $\tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$

**Step 3:** Apply steady-flow momentum equation (no acceleration):

$$P_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - P_2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L = 0$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 5:** Define Darcy friction factor $f = \dfrac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V^2}$, so $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$.

**Step 6:** Substitute:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to head loss $h_f = \Delta P / (\rho g)$:

$$\boxed{h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

*(Full marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 for definition of f, 1 for final form)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**(i) Mean velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.02}{\frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4}} = \frac{0.02}{0.007854} = 2.546 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.546 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**(iii) Friction factor using Swamee-Jain:**

Relative roughness:

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Compute inner terms:

$$\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 1.216 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$Re^{0.9} = (2.546 \times 10^5)^{0.9}$$
$$\log_{10}(2.546 \times 10^5) = 5.406$$
$$0.9 \times 5.406 = 4.865$$
$$Re^{0.9} = 10^{4.865} = 7.328 \times 10^4$$

$$\frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{7.328 \times 10^4} = 7.832 \times 10^{-5}$$

Sum:

$$1.216 \times 10^{-4} + 7.832 \times 10^{-5} = 1.999 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.999 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.699$$

$$[\ ]^2 = 13.68$$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{13.68} = 0.01828$$

**(iv) Head loss and pumping power:**

$$h_f = 0.01828 \times \frac{200}{0.1} \times \frac{(2.546)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.01828 \times 2000 \times \frac{6.482}{19.62}$$

$$h_f = 0.01828 \times 2000 \times 0.3304 = 12.08 \text{ m}$$

Pumping power (assuming 100% pump efficiency):

$$P = \rho g Q h_f = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 \times 12.08 = 2370 \text{ W} \approx 2.37 \text{ kW}$$

*(Full marks: 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 distributed appropriately)*

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the Moody chart in detail. Discuss the variation of the Darcy friction factor $f$ with Reynolds number and relative roughness. How is the friction factor determined in:
  (i) Laminar flow
  (ii) Turbulent flow using the Colebrook-White equation
  (iii) Fully rough turbulent regime **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Crude oil ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s) is pumped through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 150$ mm and length $L = 500$ m at a mean velocity of $V = 1.5$ m/s. Determine:
  (i) The Reynolds number
  (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Blasius equation (assume smooth pipe)
  (iii) The head loss and pressure drop
  (iv) The pumping power required **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Moody Chart Explanation (7 marks)**

The **Moody chart** is a log-log plot of the Darcy friction factor $f$ (y-axis) against the Reynolds number $Re$ (x-axis), with the relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ as a parametric family of curves. It was introduced by **Lewis F. Moody in 1944** and remains the standard engineering tool for friction factor determination.

**Variation of $f$ with $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$:**

- **Laminar zone** ($Re < 2000$): A single straight line $f = 64/Re$ with slope $-1$ on log-log plot. **Independent of $\varepsilon/D$** because viscous forces dominate and roughness is submerged in the laminar sublayer.
- **Transitional zone** ($2000 < Re < 4000$): Flow is unstable; values are interpolated or empirical.
- **Turbulent zone** ($Re > 4000$): A family of curves, each corresponding to a specific $\varepsilon/D$.
  - For **smooth pipes** ($\varepsilon/D \to 0$), the curves decrease monotonically.
  - For **rougher pipes**, the curves flatten and approach a horizontal asymptote.
  - As $Re \to \infty$, every curve becomes horizontal — the **fully rough regime** where $f$ depends only on $\varepsilon/D$.

**Friction factor determination:**

**(i) Laminar flow:** $f = 64/Re$ (exact, Hagen-Poiseuille).

**(ii) Turbulent flow (Colebrook-White):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

This is **implicit** in $f$ and requires iterative solution.

**(iii) Fully rough turbulent regime:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right) = 2.0 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3.7}{\varepsilon/D}\right)$$

Or equivalently: $f = \left[\dfrac{1}{2.0 \log_{10}(3.7D/\varepsilon)}\right]^2$ — independent of $Re$.

*(Full marks: 2 for chart description, 3 for variation, 2 for f determination in each regime)*

---

**(b) Crude Oil Pipeline Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.15}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.225}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.25 \times 10^4$$

Since $Re > 4000$, **turbulent flow**.

**(ii) Friction factor using Blasius (smooth pipe, $Re < 10^5$):**

$$f = 0.316 \cdot Re^{-0.25} = 0.316 \times (2.25 \times 10^4)^{-0.25}$$

Compute $Re^{0.25}$:

$$\log_{10}(2.25 \times 10^4) = 4.352$$
$$0.25 \times 4.352 = 1.088$$
$$Re^{0.25} = 10^{1.088} = 12.25$$

$$f = \frac{0.316}{12.25} = 0.0258$$

**(iii) Head loss:**

$$h_f = 0.0258 \times \frac{500}{0.15} \times \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0258 \times 3333.33 \times \frac{2.25}{19.62}$$

$$h_f = 0.0258 \times 3333.33 \times 0.1147 = 9.86 \text{ m}$$

Pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 850 \times 9.81 \times 9.86 = 8.22 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.822 \text{ bar}$$

**(iv) Pumping power:**

Discharge $Q = V A = 1.5 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 1.5 \times 0.01767 = 0.0265$ m³/s

$$P = \rho g Q h_f = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.0265 \times 9.86 = 2185 \text{ W} \approx 2.19 \text{ kW}$$

*(Full marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 distributed across sub-parts)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU board exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Darcy vs. Fanning friction factor.** KTU uses the **Darcy** convention. Using $f = 16/Re$ (Fanning) instead of $f = 64/Re$ (Darcy) leads to **head loss errors of 4×** — instant full-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** Diameter must be in **meters** (not mm) for SI consistency. Velocity in m/s. Length in m. A common error: using $D = 150$ instead of $0.15$ m, causing $Re$ off by 100×.
>
> 3. **Wrong viscosity used.** For water at 20 °C, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. **Don't confuse dynamic viscosity $\mu$ with kinematic viscosity $\nu$**. If $Re$ formula uses $\nu$, then $\rho$ is **not** in the numerator.
>
> 4. **Using Blasius outside its range.** The Blasius equation $f = 0.316 Re^{-0.25}$ is valid only for **smooth pipes** with $Re < 10^5$. Outside this range, use Colebrook-White or Swamee-Jain.
>
> 5. **Missing the + in Colebrook-White.** Writing $\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} - \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}$ instead of **adding** them gives physically wrong results (and possibly negative log arguments).
>
> 6. **Unit confusion in pumping power.** Power formula is $P = \rho g Q h_f$ in **Watts** when $\rho$ is in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $Q$ in m³/s, $h_f$ in m. Don't divide by 1000 unnecessarily.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach Equation:** $h_f = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (V^2/2g)$ — universally valid for all flow regimes in pipes.
- ✅ **Pressure form:** $\Delta P = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (\rho V^2/2)$ — multiply head form by $\rho g$.
- ✅ **Discharge form:** $\Delta P = (8 f L \rho Q^2)/(\pi^2 D^5)$ — substitute $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$.
- ✅ **Laminar friction factor:** $f = 64/Re$ — exact, derived from Hagen-Poiseuille.
- ✅ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ — critical $Re$ is 2000 (laminar) and 4000 (turbulent).
- ✅ **Colebrook-White (implicit, turbulent):** $1/\sqrt{f} = -2.0 \log_{10}[\varepsilon/(3.7D) + 2.51/(Re\sqrt{f})]$.
- ✅ **Swamee-Jain (explicit, turbulent):** $f = 0.25/\{\log_{10}[\varepsilon/(3.7D) + 5.74/Re^{0.9}]\}^2$ — error ≤ 2%.
- ✅ **Blasius (smooth, $Re < 10^5$):** $f = 0.316 Re^{-0.25}$.
- ✅ **Fully rough regime:** $f$ depends only on $\varepsilon/D$, independent of $Re$.
- ✅ **Darcy = 4 × Fanning** — never mix conventions.
- ✅ **Mean velocity:** $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ for circular pipes.
- ✅ **Pumping power:** $P_{pump} = \rho g Q h_f$ (assuming 100% efficiency).
- ✅ **Moody chart** graphically unifies all friction factor regimes using $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$.
- ✅ **Relative roughness** $\varepsilon/D$: typical values — commercial steel ~0.045 mm, drawn tubing ~0.0015 mm, galvanized iron ~0.15 mm, concrete ~0.3–3 mm.
- ✅ **KTU favorite questions:** Derivation, friction factor calculation (Swamee-Jain), Moody chart interpretation, minor losses via $K$-factor, energy equation across pipe systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Losses in Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Losses in Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In real fluid flow through pipelines, the mechanical energy of the fluid continuously decreases along the direction of flow due to **viscous effects** and **local flow disturbances**. This irreversible degradation of mechanical energy is termed **Energy Loss** or **Head Loss**, and it is a direct consequence of the Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy generation due to friction).

Energy losses in pipelines are classified into two broad categories as per KTU Module 2:

1. **Major Energy Losses (h_f)** — Continuous frictional losses occurring uniformly along the straight length of the pipe due to the shearing action between adjacent fluid layers and the pipe wall.
2. **Minor Energy Losses (h_m)** — Localized (or form) losses caused by pipe fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions, sudden contractions, and entrance/exit effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Although termed "minor," these losses can constitute **50% or more** of the total head loss in short pipelines with many fittings — a frequent KTU numerical question setup.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine water flowing through a long, horizontal garden hose. If you measure the water pressure at the tap (inlet) and at the nozzle (outlet), you will notice a pressure drop even though the hose is horizontal (no elevation change) and the flow rate is constant. **Where did this energy go?**

- **Major Loss Analogy:** Picture dragging a heavy suitcase across a long, rough carpet — the friction acts continuously over the entire distance. Similarly, fluid molecules continuously "rub" against the pipe wall and against each other.
- **Minor Loss Analogy:** Now imagine the suitcase suddenly hitting a curb (an obstacle). A sudden, localized dissipation of energy occurs. A pipe elbow, valve, or sudden change in diameter creates an analogous **localized disturbance** that dissipates kinetic energy into heat via turbulent eddies.

> [!NOTE]
> The total head loss in a pipeline system is the **algebraic sum** of all major and minor losses:
> $$h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$$

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Head loss | $h_L$ | m (metres of fluid) |
| Pipe length | L | m |
| Pipe diameter | D | m |
| Mean velocity | V | m/s |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Fluid density | ρ | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity | μ | Pa·s or N·s/m² |
| Kinematic viscosity | ν = μ/ρ | m²/s |
| Friction factor (Darcy) | f | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reynolds Number — The Gatekeeper of Flow Regimes

Before any friction calculation, the flow regime must be identified. The dimensionless **Reynolds Number (Re)** quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Flow Regime Classification:**
- **Laminar Flow:** Re < 2000 — smooth, layered, predictable streamlines.
- **Transitional Flow:** 2000 < Re < 4000 — unstable, intermittent.
- **Turbulent Flow:** Re > 4000 — chaotic, eddies, mixing-dominated.

## 2.2 Major Energy Losses — Darcy-Weisbach Equation

The cornerstone equation for major (frictional) head loss, valid for **both laminar and turbulent** flow in circular pipes:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

where **f** is the **Darcy friction factor** (sometimes called the Fanning friction factor $f'$ where $f = 4f'$; KTU uses Darcy convention).

**Pressure Form of Darcy-Weisbach:**

$$\Delta p_f = \rho g h_f = \frac{f L \rho V^2}{2 D}$$

### Friction Factor Determination

**(a) Laminar Flow (Re < 2000):** Derived analytically from Hagen-Poiseuille flow:

$$f = \frac{64}{Re}$$

**(b) Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000):** No closed-form analytical solution exists. Use the **implicit Colebrook-White equation**:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

where **ε** = absolute roughness of pipe material, **ε/D** = relative roughness.

**Explicit Approximations (KTU-favoured for numerical speed):**

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2} \quad \text{(Swamee-Jain)}$$

## 2.3 Minor Energy Losses — Form Losses

Localized losses are expressed using a **loss coefficient K** (dimensionless):

$$h_m = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Common K values (K refers to velocity in the smaller/different section as specified):

| Fitting | Typical K value |
|---|---|
| Sudden enlargement | $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ |
| Sudden contraction | $K = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$ |
| 90° standard elbow | 0.9 |
| 45° standard elbow | 0.4 |
| Gate valve (fully open) | 0.2 |
| Globe valve (fully open) | 10.0 |
| Entrance (sharp) | 0.5 |
| Exit (to reservoir) | 1.0 |

## 2.4 Equivalent Length Method

Each fitting is replaced by an equivalent straight length $L_e$ that produces the same head loss:

$$h_m = f \frac{L_e}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$L_e = \frac{K D}{f}$$

This is particularly useful when friction factor f is already known/iterated.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = VD/\nu$ | Flow regime | All flows |
| 2 | $h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)$ | Major loss (Darcy) | All regimes |
| 3 | $f = 64/Re$ | Darcy friction factor | Laminar only |
| 4 | Colebrook equation | Darcy friction factor | Turbulent |
| 5 | Swamee-Jain (explicit) | Quick friction factor | Turbulent |
| 6 | $h_m = KV^2/(2g)$ | Minor loss | Localized |
| 7 | $L_e = KD/f$ | Equivalent length | All fittings |
| 8 | $\Delta p = \rho g h_L$ | Pressure drop | All flows |
| 9 | Hagen-Poiseuille: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p / (8\mu L)$ | Laminar flow rate | Re < 2000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Energy loss calculations are the foundation of pipeline design in **water supply networks, oil & gas transmission, HVAC systems, chemical process plants, and fire-fighting systems**. An error of even 5% in friction factor estimation can lead to undersized pumps (causing cavitation) or oversized ones (wasting energy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation (from Shear Stress)

**Setup:** Consider a horizontal pipe of length L and diameter D carrying steady, incompressible flow. Apply the linear momentum equation to a cylindrical control volume of fluid.

**Step 1 — Pressure Force Balance:**
The net pressure force pushing the fluid forward equals the resisting shear force at the wall.

$$(p_1 - p_2) \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Wall Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{(p_1 - p_2) D}{4 L}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Head Form:**
Divide both sides by $\rho g$ to convert to head:

$$\frac{\tau_0}{\rho g} = \frac{(p_1 - p_2)}{\rho g} \cdot \frac{D}{4L} = h_f \cdot \frac{D}{4L}$$

Therefore:

$$h_f = \frac{4 L \tau_0}{\rho g D}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Friction Factor:**
By definition, the Darcy friction factor relates wall shear stress to dynamic pressure:

$$\tau_0 = f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Final Substitution:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8}$$

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Friction Factor for Laminar Flow (f = 64/Re)

**Step 1 — Velocity Profile in Laminar Pipe Flow:**
For steady, fully-developed laminar flow (Hagen-Poiseuille), the velocity distribution is parabolic:

$$u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\left(\frac{dp}{dx}\right)(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 2 — Mean Velocity:**
Integrate across the cross-section and divide by area:

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$V = -\frac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(\frac{dp}{dx}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Wall Shear Stress from Velocity Profile:**
Differentiate u(r) and evaluate at r = R:

$$\tau_0 = -\mu \left.\frac{du}{dr}\right|_{r=R} = -\frac{R}{2}\left(\frac{dp}{dx}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Using Mean Velocity:**
From Step 2: $\dfrac{dp}{dx} = -\dfrac{8\mu V}{R^2}$

$$\tau_0 = -\frac{R}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{8\mu V}{R^2}\right) = \frac{4\mu V}{R} = \frac{8\mu V}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Equate with Friction Factor Definition:**

$$\tau_0 = f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{8} = \frac{8\mu V}{D}$$

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Loss Coefficient for Sudden Enlargement (Borda-Carnot)

**Setup:** Flow expands abruptly from area A₁ (small) to area A₂ (large).

**Step 1 — Apply Momentum Equation:**
Between sections 1 and 2 (separated by the expansion zone):

$$(p_1 - p_2) A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) = \rho A_2 V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Difference:**

$$p_1 - p_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 3 — Energy Equation (Bernoulli with loss):**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

**Step 4 — Combine and Simplify:**
Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$h_L = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + \frac{V_2(V_2 - V_1)}{g} = \frac{V_2^2 - 2V_1 V_2 + V_1^2}{2g} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of V₁:**

$$h_L = \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Borda-Carnot result is independent of fluid viscosity — it is a **purely inertial, inviscid** loss caused by flow separation and turbulent mixing at the expansion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pipeline Energy Loss Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Energy Loss hL] --> B[Major Loss hf]
    A --> C[Minor Loss hm]
    B --> D[Darcy-Weisbach Equation]
    C --> E[Fittings Valves Bends]
    C --> F[Sudden Expansion Contraction]
    C --> G[Entrance Exit Effects]
    D --> H{Laminar or Turbulent}
    H -->|Re less than 2000| I[f equals 64 over Re]
    H -->|Re greater than 4000| J[Colebrook Equation]
    J --> K[Implicit Solve or Swamee-Jain]
    E --> L[Loss Coefficient K]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    L --> M[hm equals K V squared over 2g]
    I --> N[Compute hf and hm]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Total hL equals hf plus sum hm]
```

## 4.2 Moody Diagram Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs Re and relative roughness] --> B{Check Re}
    B -->|less than 2000| C[Laminar Zone f equals 64 over Re]
    B -->|2000 to 4000| D[Transitional Zone Interpolation]
    B -->|greater than 4000| E[Turbulent Zone]
    E --> F[Hydraulically Smooth Line]
    E --> G[Fully Rough Zone]
    E --> H[Transition Zone Colebrook]
    C --> I[Read f from Chart]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Darcy-Weisbach]
```

## 4.3 Pipeline System Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Reservoir 1] -->|Entrance Loss 0.5 V2 over 2g| A
    A[Pipe Section 1 Length L1 Diameter D1] -->|hf1| B
    B{Pipe Joints} -->|Gate Valve K equals 0.2| C
    C[Pipe Section 2 Length L2 Diameter D2] -->|hf2| D
    D{Elbow 90 deg K equals 0.9} --> E
    E[Pipe Section 3 Length L3 Diameter D3] -->|hf3| F
    F[Exit to Reservoir 2 K equals 1.0] --> P2[Reservoir 2]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between major and minor energy losses in pipe flow. Under what circumstances can minor losses become significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Major Loss ($h_f$) | Minor Loss ($h_m$) |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Viscous friction along pipe wall | Local flow disturbances |
| Location | Distributed over entire length | Localized at fittings |
| Governing Equation | Darcy-Weisbach: $fLV^2/(2gD)$ | $K V^2/(2g)$ |
| Dependency | Function of L, D, V, f, ε | Function of fitting geometry |
| Magnitude | Dominant in long pipelines | Dominant in short pipelines with many fittings |

**Conclusion:** Minor losses become significant in **short pipelines (L/D < 1000)**, systems with numerous fittings, and low-velocity systems where frictional drag is small.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the Reynolds number in pipeline flow? State the equations for Darcy friction factor in laminar and turbulent regimes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number $Re = VD/\nu$ is the **dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces** and serves three key purposes:
1. Classifies flow as laminar (Re < 2000), transitional (2000 < Re < 4000), or turbulent (Re > 4000).
2. Determines the velocity profile shape (parabolic vs flat).
3. Governs the choice of friction factor correlation.

**Friction Factor Equations:**

- **Laminar:** $f = 64/Re$ (analytical, from Hagen-Poiseuille)
- **Turbulent:** Colebrook-White implicit equation:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

Or Swamee-Jain explicit approximation:
$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + 5.74/Re^{0.9}\right)\right]^2}$$

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A horizontal cast-iron pipe ($\varepsilon = 0.26$ mm) of diameter 200 mm carries water at a mean velocity of 3 m/s over a length of 500 m. The system includes **one fully open gate valve, two 90° standard elbows, and a sudden contraction from 200 mm to 100 mm**. Take kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

**(a)** Determine the major head loss in the pipe. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the total minor head losses and the overall head loss. (7 marks)

#### **Solution (a): Major Head Loss**

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{VD}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.2}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 6 \times 10^5$$

Since Re > 4000, flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 2 — Relative Roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.26 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2} = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Friction Factor via Swamee-Jain:**

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{1.3 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{(6 \times 10^5)^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

Computing each term:
- $\dfrac{1.3 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} = 3.514 \times 10^{-4}$
- $(6 \times 10^5)^{0.9} = 6^{0.9} \times 10^{4.5} = 5.292 \times 10^{4.5} = 1.671 \times 10^5$
- $\dfrac{5.74}{1.671 \times 10^5} = 3.435 \times 10^{-5}$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}(3.514 \times 10^{-4} + 3.435 \times 10^{-5})\right]^2} = \frac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(3.858 \times 10^{-4})]^2}$$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{[-3.4135]^2} = \frac{0.25}{11.65} = 0.02146$$

**Step 4 — Major Head Loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.02146 \times 500 \times 3^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2} = \frac{96.57}{3.924} = 24.61 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 24.61 \text{ m}}$$

#### **Solution (b): Minor Losses**

**K-values:**
- Gate valve (fully open): K = 0.2
- 90° elbow (×2): K = 2 × 0.9 = 1.8
- Sudden contraction (A₂/A₁ = (100/200)² = 0.25): $K = 0.5(1 - 0.25) = 0.375$

**Total K = 0.2 + 1.8 + 0.375 = 2.375**

$$h_m = \sum K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 2.375 \times \frac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 2.375 \times 0.4587 = 1.089 \text{ m}$$

**Total Head Loss:**

$$h_L = h_f + h_m = 24.61 + 1.09 = 25.70 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_L \approx 25.70 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** (1) Many students compute K for sudden contraction using the larger area ratio — recall the formula uses $A_2/A_1$ (small/large). (2) Students often forget to multiply elbow K by 2 for two elbows. (3) Swamee-Jain should be evaluated to **at least 4 significant figures** in the logarithm — rounding errors propagate to f.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

Crude oil ($\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s) flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe ($\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm) of diameter 150 mm at a rate of 0.025 m³/s.

**(a)** Determine the Darcy friction factor and verify the flow regime. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the pipe length is 2 km and the system includes a sudden enlargement to 300 mm diameter followed by a globe valve, compute the total head loss. (7 marks)

#### **Solution (a): Friction Factor and Flow Regime**

**Step 1 — Mean Velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.025}{\pi (0.15)^2/4} = \frac{0.025}{0.01767} = 1.415 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.025}{880} = 2.841 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{VD}{\nu} = \frac{1.415 \times 0.15}{2.841 \times 10^{-5}} = 7471$$

Since Re > 4000, flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 4 — Relative Roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Friction Factor (Swamee-Jain):**

- $\dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 8.108 \times 10^{-5}$
- $\dfrac{5.74}{(7471)^{0.9}} = \dfrac{5.74}{3551} = 1.616 \times 10^{-3}$
- Sum = $8.108 \times 10^{-5} + 1.616 \times 10^{-3} = 1.697 \times 10^{-3}$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(1.697 \times 10^{-3})]^2} = \frac{0.25}{[-2.7702]^2} = \frac{0.25}{7.674} = 0.0326$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0326, \text{ Flow is Turbulent}}$$

#### **Solution (b): Total Head Loss**

**Major Loss (L = 2000 m):**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.0326 \times 2000 \times (1.415)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15} = \frac{130.55}{2.943} = 44.36 \text{ m}$$

**Minor Loss — Sudden Enlargement (150 mm → 300 mm):**

Velocity in 300 mm pipe: $V_2 = V_1 (A_1/A_2) = 1.415 \times (0.15/0.30)^2 = 1.415 \times 0.25 = 0.354$ m/s

$$K_{exp} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$h_{m1} = 0.5625 \times \frac{(1.415)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.5625 \times 0.1020 = 0.0574 \text{ m}$$

**Minor Loss — Globe Valve (fully open, K = 10.0):** Velocity reference: larger section V₂

$$h_{m2} = 10.0 \times \frac{(0.354)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 10.0 \times 0.00639 = 0.0639 \text{ m}$$

**Total Head Loss:**

$$h_L = 44.36 + 0.0574 + 0.0639 = 44.48 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_L \approx 44.48 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** (1) For sudden enlargement, the velocity in the loss equation must be the **upstream velocity (V₁)**, but for the globe valve **after** enlargement, use the **new V₂**. (2) When Re is in the transitional zone (2000–4000), KTU expects a clear statement that the **Colebrook equation is not strictly valid** — use Moody chart interpolation. (3) Always state the flow regime **before** selecting the friction factor correlation — losing 1 mark here is common.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reynolds Number Threshold:** Laminar (Re < 2000), Transitional (2000 < Re < 4000), Turbulent (Re > 4000). Always state the regime first.
- **Darcy-Weisbach Equation:** $h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)$ — universal formula; only the friction factor f changes with flow regime.
- **Laminar Friction Factor:** $f = 64/Re$ — exact analytical result, no need for Moody chart.
- **Turbulent Friction Factor:** Use **Colebrook-White** (implicit) or **Swamee-Jain** (explicit) for KTU numericals; depends on both Re and ε/D.
- **Borda-Carnot Loss:** $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ for sudden enlargement — **viscosity-independent**.
- **Sudden Contraction:** $K = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$ — small area over large area.
- **Equivalent Length:** $L_e = KD/f$ — useful when f is already determined iteratively.
- **Total Head Loss = Major + Sum of All Minors** — never forget a fitting in series-parallel problems.
- **Velocity Reference for K:** Always use the velocity **in the section where the fitting is located** (not the upstream/downstream indiscriminately).
- **Common KTU Mistake:** Using Fanning friction factor ($f' = f/4$) — KTU 2024 scheme uses **Darcy convention** exclusively. Verify with formula sheet.
- **Units Consistency:** V in m/s, D in m, L in m, g = 9.81 m/s² → h in metres; or convert to pressure form via $\Delta p = \rho g h_L$.
- **Real Engineering Insight:** In long pipelines, minor losses are < 5% of total; in short pipe systems (HVAC, process piping), minor losses can exceed 50%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minor Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minor Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In pipe flow analysis, the total head loss between two sections of a pipeline carrying a real (viscous) fluid is classified into two distinct categories:

**Major Losses (h_f):** Caused by frictional resistance along the straight length of the pipe. These are computed using the Darcy-Weisbach equation or the Hagen-Poiseuille equation.

**Minor Losses (h_m):** Localized head losses caused by flow disturbances introduced by pipe fittings, valves, bends, sudden area changes, and entry/exit conditions. Although each individual fitting may cause a small loss, the cumulative effect in a system with many fittings can be significant or even dominate the major friction losses.

The **Minor Loss Coefficient (K)** is a dimensionless parameter that characterizes the energy dissipated by a particular fitting when the fluid undergoes a change in velocity or direction.

The generic form of the minor loss equation (KTU 2024 syllabus standard form) is:

$$h_L = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Minor losses are the head losses due to disturbances in the flow caused by fittings, valves, bends, expansions, contractions, and other accessories, expressed as a fraction of the velocity head using a dimensionless coefficient K."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are walking down a long, smooth, straight corridor at a constant pace. The friction between your shoes and the polished floor represents the **major loss (friction loss)** in a pipe.

Now consider the same corridor but with several obstacles along the way:
- A sudden **narrow doorway** (sudden contraction)
