# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]`
> **Define the term "Boundary Work" in thermodynamics and explain why it is a path function. State its mathematical expression for a quasi-static process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Boundary work (also called Pdv work) is the energy transferred across the boundary of a closed system due to the expansion or compression of the system against an external pressure. It is calculated as the work done by the force exerted by the gas on the movable boundary (e.g., a piston) as it moves through a distance.

**Why it is a path function:** The work done depends on the specific path followed by the process on the P-V diagram, not just the initial and final states. Mathematically, $\delta W_b = P \, dV$ is an *inexact differential*, meaning $\int P \, dV$ yields different values for different paths connecting the same two states. Therefore, boundary work is a path function, not a property of the system.

**Mathematical expression:** For a quasi-static process,

$$\delta W_b = P \, dV \quad \text{and} \quad W_b = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

The integral represents the area under the P-V curve from $V_1$ to $V_2$.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B]`
> **State and explain the "Sign Convention" used in thermodynamics for work and heat transfer. Give one example each for positive and negative work.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Sign Convention (KTU/IUPAC standard):**

1. Work done **by the system on the surroundings** is taken as **POSITIVE** (+W).
2. Work done **on the system by the surroundings** is taken as **NEGATIVE** (−W).
3. Heat absorbed **by the system** is taken as **POSITIVE** (+Q).
4. Heat rejected **by the system** is taken as **NEGATIVE** (−Q).

**Justification:** This convention is based on the First Law of Thermodynamics in the form $Q = \Delta U + W$, where $W$ is work output of the system. The energy balance treats both heat absorbed and work produced by the system as energy leaving the surroundings, so both are positive contributions to the system's energy change.

**Example for positive work (+W):** A gas expanding in a cylinder pushes the piston outward against a resistance. The gas does work on the surroundings; by convention, this is positive work.

**Example for negative work (−W):** A refrigerator compressor compresses the refrigerant gas, with surroundings doing work on the gas. By convention, this is negative work (work done on the system).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 3 Internal Choice)

> **Note:** The following two questions are mutually exclusive alternatives. KTU ESE pattern provides internal choice between questions of the same module. Pick ONE to attempt.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Set A]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the boundary work done by a gas undergoing a polytropic process $PV^n = C$. Clearly state the special cases for $n = 0$, $n = 1$, $n = \gamma$, and $n = \infty$.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A gas expands in a piston-cylinder assembly from an initial state of $P_1 = 5 \text{ bar}$, $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ to a final state of $P_2 = 2 \text{ bar}$, $V_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}^3$, following a polytropic process. Calculate: (i) the polytropic index $n$, (ii) the boundary work done, (iii) the mean effective pressure.

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Polytropic process definition:** A polytropic process obeys $PV^n = C$ (constant), where $n$ is the polytropic index. From the relation: $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n = C$.

**Step 2 — Express $P$ in terms of $V$:** From $PV^n = C$, we get $P = C / V^n = P_1 V_1^n / V^n$.

**Step 3 — Boundary work for quasi-static process:**

$$W_b = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^n V^{-n} \, dV$$

$$W_b = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \quad (n \neq 1)$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{W_b = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}}$$

**Step 5 — Special cases:**

| $n$ | Process | Work Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $n = 0$ | Isobaric | $W_b = P(V_2 - V_1)$ |
| $n = 1$ | Isothermal (ideal gas) | $W_b = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2 / V_1)$ |
| $n = \gamma$ | Reversible Adiabatic | $W_b = (P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1 - \gamma)$ |
| $n \to \infty$ | Isochoric | $W_b = 0$ |

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:** $P_1 = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$, $P_2 = 2 \text{ bar} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $V_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}^3$.

**(i) Polytropic index $n$:**

From $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^n$$

$$\frac{5}{2} = \left(\frac{0.15}{0.05}\right)^n = 3^n$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$\ln(2.5) = n \ln(3) \Rightarrow 0.9163 = n \times 1.0986$$

$$n = \frac{0.9163}{1.0986} = 0.834$$

**(ii) Boundary work:**

$$W_b = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{(2 \times 10^5)(0.15) - (5 \times 10^5)(0.05)}{1 - 0.834}$$

$$W_b = \frac{30000 - 25000}{0.166} = \frac{5000}{0.166} = 30120 \text{ J} \approx 30.12 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Mean effective pressure:**

$$P_m = \frac{W_b}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{30120}{0.15 - 0.05} = \frac{30120}{0.10} = 3.012 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 3.012 \text{ bar}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3 Set B]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain any FIVE different types of work transfer (other than Pdv work) encountered in engineering thermodynamics. Give the differential form and a practical example for each.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** An electric heater of rating 2 kW operates for 30 minutes in a room. A 50-kg mass falls through a height of 10 m and drives a turbine. A spring of stiffness 25 kN/m is compressed by 100 mm. Calculate the work output/input in each case, and identify which represents work output and which represents work input.

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Five Types of Work Transfer (other than Pdv work):**

**1. Shaft Work:** Work done by a torque $T$ acting on a rotating shaft through an angular displacement $d\theta$.

$$\delta W_{sh} = T \, d\theta$$

*Example:* A steam turbine driving an electric generator (work output).

**2. Electrical Work:** Work done when an electrical charge $dq$ moves through a potential difference $V$.

$$\delta W_e = V \, dq$$

*Example:* Electric current passing through a resistance heater (work input converted to heat).

**3. Spring Work:** Work done in compressing or stretching a linear spring of stiffness $k$ by displacement $dx$.

$$\delta W_{sp} = kx \, dx$$

*Example:* A spring-loaded pressure relief valve stores elastic energy.

**4. Surface Tension Work (Film Work):** Work done to stretch a liquid film of surface tension $\sigma$ by area $dA$.

$$\delta W_{st} = \sigma \, dA$$

*Example:* Inflating a soap bubble (work input to create new surface area).

**5. Magnetic Work:** Work done in a magnetic material of volume $V$ as magnetic induction $B$ changes in field $H$.

$$\delta W_m = H \, dB \quad \text{(per unit volume)}$$

*Example:* Inductor coil in an electromagnetic relay.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Case 1: Electric Heater (Electrical Work):**

$$W_e = P \times t = 2000 \text{ W} \times 30 \times 60 \text{ s} = 2000 \times 1800 = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$$

This is **work input** (electrical energy supplied to heater, converted to heat).

**Case 2: Falling Mass driving Turbine (Shaft/Mechanical Work):**

The potential energy lost by the mass converts to work:

$$W = mgh = 50 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 4905 \text{ J} \approx 4.905 \text{ kJ}$$

This is **work output** (mass does work on the turbine as it falls).

**Case 3: Compressed Spring (Spring Work):**

$$W_{sp} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 25000 \times (0.1)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 25000 \times 0.01 = 125 \text{ J}$$

This represents **work input** (energy stored in the spring by external agent).

**Summary Table:**

| System | Work Transfer (J) | Work Input or Output |
|---|---|---|
| Electric heater (2 kW × 30 min) | 3,600,000 | Work input (electrical) |
| Falling mass (50 kg × 10 m) | 4,905 | Work output (mechanical) |
| Compressed spring (25 kN/m × 100 mm) | 125 | Work input (mechanical) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Many students forget the IUPAC sign convention. When a system *does* work, $W$ is positive; when work is *done on* the system, $W$ is negative. Mixing up signs can lose 2-3 marks even with correct magnitude.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Pressure must be in Pascals (N/m²), volume in m³, and temperature in Kelvin (for ideal gas law). The most common error is using bar·m³ directly without converting to joules (1 bar·m³ = 100 kJ).
> 3. **Polytropic index confusion:** Don't confuse polytropic index $n$ with the adiabatic index $\gamma$. Polytropic covers any process $PV^n = C$; $\gamma$ is a specific gas property.
> 4. **Missing the $n=1$ case:** When applying $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$, the formula *blows up* at $n=1$. For isothermal processes, students MUST use the logarithmic form, not the polytropic formula.
> 5. **Confusing shaft work formula:** Power = $2\pi N T$ requires $N$ in **revolutions per second**, not RPM. If given RPM, divide by 60 first.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Exam Topics — Master These:**

- [ ] **Boundary work integral:** $W_b = \int P \, dV$ = area under P-V curve (signed).
- [ ] **Polytropic process:** $PV^n = C$ is the master equation; $W_b = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$ for $n \neq 1$.
- [ ] **Isothermal ideal gas:** $W_b = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = P_1V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
- [ ] **Reversible adiabatic:** $W_b = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-\gamma) = mR(T_1 - T_2)/(\gamma - 1)$.
- [ ] **Isochoric:** $W_b = 0$ (no volume change).
- [ ] **Isobaric:** $W_b = P(V_2 - V_1)$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Work done BY system = +; work done ON system = −.
- [ ] **MEP (Mean Effective Pressure):** $P_m = W_b / (V_2 - V_1)$.
- [ ] **Shaft work:** $\delta W = T \, d\theta$; $W = 2\pi N T$ per revolution.
- [ ] **Electrical work:** $W = VIt = VQ$.
- [ ] **Spring work:** $W = \frac{1}{2}k(x_2^2 - x_1^2)$.
- [ ] **Flow work:** $w_f = Pv$ (per unit mass) — appears in open system First Law.
- [ ] **Units:** $1 \text{ bar·m}^3 = 10^5 \text{ Pa·m}^3 = 10^5 \text{ J} = 100 \text{ kJ}$.
- [ ] **Path function vs. property:** Boundary work is a path function, not a state function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Aid — The 4-quadrant P-V Strategy:**
> When a P-V diagram is given in a KTU problem, ALWAYS break the cycle into 4 process segments (e.g., 1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→1). Compute the work for each segment using the appropriate formula based on the *type of process* (isobaric, isothermal, polytropic, etc.). Then **sum the signed work values** to get the net work of the cycle. The cyclic integral $\oint P \, dV$ = net work output of the cycle (positive for heat engines, negative for refrigerators).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Expansion Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Free Expansion Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Free Expansion** is an irreversible thermodynamic process in which a fluid (usually a gas) is allowed to expand into a **vacuum** or into a region of significantly lower pressure, such that the **external pressure is zero** ($P_{ext} = 0$). Because there is no resistance or piston to push against, the gas performs **no boundary work** on the surroundings.

Mathematically, the free expansion work is defined as:

$$W_{free} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV = 0$$

This process is conventionally:
- **Adiabatic** ($Q = 0$) — typically occurring in a perfectly insulated container
- **Irreversible** — it cannot be reversed without external intervention
- **Non-quasi-static** — the system passes through non-equilibrium states

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** In Module 3 of PCPET303, free expansion is treated as a critical case study for understanding that **work is a path function** and depends on the **process**, not just the initial and final states.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bursting Balloon in Space"

Imagine a **balloon filled with air** is placed inside a large, sealed, **evacuated chamber** in outer space (or on Earth inside a vacuum bell jar). The balloon is suddenly punctured.

What happens?
- The air rushes out violently to fill the entire chamber.
- However, the expanding air does **not push against any piston, turbine blade, or external object**.
- There is no mechanical work extracted — the air molecules simply redistribute into the available volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Free expansion = "Work potential wasted." The gas *could* have done work if a piston was in place, but the absence of resistance means **all the expansion energy dissipates as internal molecular motion (sensible heat)**, not as mechanical output.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$
- **Specific Heat at Constant Volume (ideal gas):** $C_v$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure (for contrast):** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; kPa$
- **External Pressure in Free Expansion:** $P_{ext} = 0 \; kPa$ (absolute vacuum)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Whenever you see a question mentioning "gas expands into an evacuated chamber" or "rupture of a membrane separating a gas from vacuum," immediately recognize it as **Free Expansion** and set $W = 0$.

---

## 1.4 Why Free Expansion Matters in Engineering

Free expansion is not merely a textbook curiosity. It is foundational to:

| Engineering Domain | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Cryogenics** | LNG boil-off gas recondensation cycles |
| **Steam Power Plants** | Flash evaporation across throttle valves |
| **Refrigeration** | Throttling processes (Joule-Thomson effect) |
| **Chemical Reactors** | Sudden depressurization safety analysis |
| **Aerospace** | Vent valves on spacecraft and pressurized cabins |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often assume $\Delta U = 0$ implies "nothing happens" in free expansion. In reality, for **real gases**, there is a measurable temperature change (Joule's coefficient is non-zero), and the gas passes through a complex set of **non-equilibrium states** that cannot be plotted on a standard P-V diagram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Thermodynamic Premises

To fully grasp free expansion, we must revisit the **First Law of Thermodynamics** and the concept of **boundary work**.

### The Boundary Work Concept

In any quasi-static (reversible) process where a gas expands against a movable piston, the infinitesimal work done is:

$$dW = P_{ext} \, dV$$

For a finite expansion from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$

This is the **area under the P-V curve**.

### Why $W = 0$ in Free Expansion?

In free expansion:
- There is **no piston** and **no external resistive force**.
- The gas expands into a region where $P_{ext} = 0$ (vacuum).
- Therefore, regardless of the change in volume $\Delta V > 0$, the integral evaluates to zero:

$$W_{free} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV = 0$$

---

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Process

1. **Initial State:** Gas confined in volume $V_1$ at pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$.
2. **Trigger Event:** A partition/membrane/valve opens, exposing the gas to a vacuum region of total volume $V_2$.
3. **Expansion Phase:** Gas molecules rush to fill $V_2$ via random molecular motion and intermolecular collisions.
4. **Final State:** Gas uniformly distributed throughout volume $V_2$ at pressure $P_2 < P_1$ and temperature $T_2$ (where $T_2 = T_1$ for ideal gas).
5. **No Work Performed:** Since no external force is overcome, $W = 0$.
6. **No Heat Transfer:** In the idealized adiabatic case, $Q = 0$.
7. **Energy Conservation:** $\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** The **internal energy of the gas does not change** in ideal-gas free expansion, but the **internal energy distribution** (kinetic vs. potential) does change transiently as molecules interact. This transient imbalance is what causes the slight temperature change in real gases.

---

## 2.3 The Role of the First Law

The First Law of Thermodynamics states:

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

Applying the free expansion conditions ($Q = 0$, $W = 0$):

$$\Delta U = 0$$

For an **ideal gas**, internal energy is a function of temperature only:

$$U = U(T) \quad \text{(Joule's Law for ideal gas)}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta U = m \cdot C_v \cdot \Delta T = 0 \implies \Delta T = 0$$

---

## 2.4 Joule's Free Expansion Experiment (Historical Context)

**James Prescott Joule (1843)** conducted the famous experiment:
- Two copper vessels connected by a valve, immersed in a water bath.
- One vessel contained a high-pressure gas; the other was evacuated.
- The valve was opened, and the gas expanded freely.
- Joule observed that for "ideal-like" gases, the water bath temperature **did not change** — confirming $U = U(T)$.
- For real gases, a slight temperature change was noted, leading to the definition of the **Joule Coefficient**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{free} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$ | General boundary work definition | $J$ or $kJ$ |
| 2 | $W_{free} = 0$ | When $P_{ext} = 0$ (vacuum) | Always true |
| 3 | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | First Law of Thermodynamics | $J$ |
| 4 | $\Delta U = 0$ | Adiabatic free expansion of ideal gas | $J$ |
| 5 | $U = m C_v T$ | Internal energy of ideal gas | $J$ |
| 6 | $\Delta T = 0$ | Isothermal result for ideal gas free expansion | $K$ |
| 7 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition | $J$ |
| 8 | $H = H(T)$ for ideal gas | Enthalpy is temperature-dependent only | $J$ |
| 9 | $\mu_J = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$ | Joule's coefficient (for real gas analysis) | $K/m^3$ |
| 10 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | $Pa \cdot m^3$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU Exams:** Formulas 2, 3, 4, 6, and 9 are the most frequently tested. Master the chain $W = 0 \rightarrow \Delta U = 0 \rightarrow \Delta T = 0$ for ideal gases.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Understanding free expansion enables engineers to:

- **Design Pressure Relief Systems:** Predict energy dissipation when valves fail.
- **Model Throttling Processes:** Free expansion is the limiting case of throttling.
- **Calibrate Thermodynamic Property Tables:** Provides reference for enthalpy and internal energy functions.
- **Analyze Safety Venting:** Calculate the post-vent temperature drop in cryogenic tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Work Done in Free Expansion

### Problem Setup
A gas occupies volume $V_1$ in a rigid, insulated container. A partition separates it from a vacuum region of volume $V_2 - V_1$. The partition is suddenly removed, and the gas expands to fill the entire volume $V_2$. Prove that the work done is zero.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the general boundary work expression.**

For any process (reversible or irreversible) where a system expands against an external pressure, the work is:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$

**Step 2: Identify the external pressure condition.**

In free expansion, the gas expands into an **evacuated region**. By the definition of vacuum:

$$P_{ext} = 0 \; Pa$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{free} &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV \\
&= 0 \cdot \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV \\
&= 0 \cdot [V]_{V_1}^{V_2} \\
&= 0 \cdot (V_2 - V_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**

$$
\boxed{W_{free} = 0}
$$

> **Logic Note:** Even though $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 > 0$, the work is zero because work requires both displacement AND force. Here, the "force" (external pressure) is zero, so no work is done — analogous to pushing on a wall that doesn't move: no work is performed.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Temperature Change in Ideal Gas Free Expansion

### Problem Setup
Show that for an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic free expansion, the temperature change $\Delta T = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics.**

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

**Step 2: Substitute the free expansion boundary conditions.**

- The container is insulated: $Q = 0$
- External pressure is zero: $W = 0$

$$0 = \Delta U + 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for internal energy change.**

$$\Delta U = 0$$

**Step 4: Apply the ideal gas internal energy relation.**

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:

$$U = m C_v T$$

The change in internal energy is:

$$\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$$

**Step 5: Equate and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m C_v \Delta T &= 0 \\
\Delta T &= \frac{0}{m C_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since mass $m > 0$ and specific heat $C_v > 0$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 0}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This is a powerful result. It tells us that an ideal gas expanding into a vacuum with no heat exchange experiences **no temperature change**, regardless of the volume change. This is a direct consequence of the **kinetic theory of gases**, where temperature is purely a measure of average molecular kinetic energy, and no work/heat exchange means kinetic energy is conserved.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Joule's Coefficient for Real Gases

### Problem Setup
For a real gas, derive an expression for the **Joule Coefficient** $\mu_J$ in terms of measurable thermodynamic properties.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Joule Coefficient.**

By definition:

$$\mu_J = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$$

This represents the rate of change of temperature with respect to volume at constant internal energy.

**Step 2: Express internal energy as a function of T and V.**

$$U = U(T, V)$$

The total differential is:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 3: Apply the constant internal energy condition ($dU = 0$).**

$$0 = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 4: Solve for the partial derivative $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT &= -\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV \\
\frac{dT}{dV} &= -\frac{\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T}{\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the partial derivatives.**

- $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = m C_v$ (by definition of specific heat at constant volume)
- $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$ (Maxwell-style relation derived from fundamental equations)

**Step 6: Substitute to get the final Joule Coefficient expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_J = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U &= -\frac{1}{m C_v} \left[ T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Verify for ideal gas.**

For ideal gas: $PV = mRT \implies P = \frac{mRT}{V}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V &= \frac{mR}{V} \\
T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P &= T \cdot \frac{mR}{V} - \frac{mRT}{V} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mu_J^{ideal} = 0}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This confirms that for an ideal gas, $T$ does not change with $V$ at constant $U$, consistent with our earlier finding. For real gases, $\mu_J \neq 0$, indicating slight temperature changes during free expansion.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem
A 2 kg sample of nitrogen gas ($R = 0.2968 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$, $C_v = 0.743 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$) at $T_1 = 300 \; K$ and $P_1 = 500 \; kPa$ undergoes free expansion in an insulated container until its volume doubles. Determine:
1. The work done.
2. The final temperature.
3. The final pressure.
4. The change in internal energy.

### Solution

**Step 1: Work done.**

Since $P_{ext} = 0$:

$$W = 0 \; kJ$$

**Step 2: Final temperature.**

For ideal gas free expansion, $\Delta T = 0$:

$$T_2 = T_1 = 300 \; K$$

**Step 3: Final pressure.**

Since volume doubles, $V_2 = 2V_1$. Using the ideal gas law with constant $T$:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \implies P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{P_1}{2}$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \; kPa
$$

**Step 4: Change in internal energy.**

$$\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T = 2 \times 0.743 \times 0 = 0 \; kJ$$

### Final Results Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Work $W$ | $0 \; kJ$ |
| Final Temperature $T_2$ | $300 \; K$ |
| Final Pressure $P_2$ | $250 \; kPa$ |
| Change in Internal Energy $\Delta U$ | $0 \; kJ$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice how the final pressure calculation uses the ideal gas law, NOT any work-energy equation, because the process is irreversible. The endpoints still satisfy the ideal gas equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Free Expansion Sequence

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a free expansion process, from initial confinement to final equilibrium.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State: Gas in Volume V1] --> B[Partition Membrane: Intact]
    B --> C[Membrane Rupture or Valve Opens]
    C --> D[Gas Molecules Rush into Vacuum]
    D --> E[No External Resistance Encountered]
    E --> F[Boundary Work Output: ZERO]
    D --> G[Internal Molecular Collisions]
    G --> H[Temperature Stabilization via Equipartition]
    H --> I[Final State: Gas in Volume V2]
    I --> J[Internal Energy UNCHANGED for Ideal Gas]
    I --> K[Final Pressure P2 = P1 x V1/V2]
    I --> L[Final Temperature T2 = T1 for Ideal Gas]
    F --> M[First Law Balance: Q = 0, W = 0, dU = 0]
    J --> M
    H --> M
```

### Diagram Interpretation

| Node | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| A | Initial confined state at $P_1, V_1, T_1$ |
| B | Rigid partition separating gas from vacuum |
| C | Triggering event (membrane rupture) |
| D | Rapid adiabatic expansion |
| E | Zero external pressure condition |
| F | Work output is zero (no piston, no force) |
| G | Intermolecular energy redistribution |
| H | Final thermal equilibrium at $T_2$ |
| I | Final state at $P_2, V_2, T_2$ |
| J, K, L | State property determinations |
| M | First Law verification block |

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Flow Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Energy Domain]
        A1[Internal Energy of Confined Gas]
        A2[Molecular Kinetic Energy Pool]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Free Expansion Process Core]
        B1[Partition Opens]
        B2[Gas Expands to V2]
        B3[No Work Extraction Port]
        B4[No Heat Exchange Boundary]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Energy Domain]
        C1[Internal Energy Preserved]
        C2[Volume Doubled or More]
        C3[Pressure Reduced]
        C4[Kinetic Energy Reorganized]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C1
    B2 --> C3
    A2 --> C4
    C1 --> D[(Energy Conservation Verified: dU = 0)]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
```

### Energy Flow Logic

- **Input Block:** Carries the initial internal and kinetic energy of the gas.
- **Process Core:** Highlights that there is **no work extraction port** and **no heat exchange boundary** — the defining features of free expansion.
- **Output Block:** Demonstrates that energy is conserved but redistributed.
- **Verification Node:** Confirms the First Law compliance: $\Delta U = 0$.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Chart: Free Expansion vs. Reversible Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Type] --> B[Free Expansion]
    A --> C[Reversible Isothermal Expansion]
    B --> B1[Work Done: ZERO]
    B --> B2[Heat Transfer: ZERO for adiabatic case]
    B --> B3[dU: ZERO for ideal gas]
    B --> B4[Path: Irreversible]
    C --> C1[Work Done: W = nRT ln V2/V1]
    C --> C2[Heat Transfer: Q = W]
    C --> C3[dU: ZERO for isothermal]
    C --> C4[Path: Reversible Quasi-static]
    B1 --> D1[Area under P-V curve: ZERO]
    C1 --> D2[Area under P-V curve: nRT ln V2/V1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This comparison crystallizes the difference: in reversible expansion, the gas does **work equal to the area under the P-V curve**. In free expansion, this area is zero because $P_{ext} = 0$. Yet, both processes share the same initial and final states for an ideal gas.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q1]**
> Define **free expansion**. Why is the work done zero in such a process?

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Free expansion is the process in which a gas is allowed to expand into a vacuum (or a region of zero pressure) without any external resistance, and consequently, without performing any work on the surroundings.

**Zero Work Explanation (1.5 marks):** The work done in any expansion process is given by $W = \int P_{ext} \, dV$. In free expansion, the gas expands into a region where the external pressure is zero ($P_{ext} = 0 \; kPa$). Therefore:

$$
W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV = 0
$$

Even though the volume increases, no force is overcome, so no work is performed.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper, Q2]**
> State **Joule's Law** for an ideal gas. What does it imply about the temperature change during free expansion?

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Statement of Joule's Law (1.5 marks):** Joule's Law states that for an ideal gas, the internal energy is a function of temperature **only** and is independent of pressure and volume:

$$U = U(T) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0, \quad \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_T = 0$$

**Implication for Free Expansion (1.5 marks):** During adiabatic free expansion ($Q = 0$, $W = 0$), the First Law gives $\Delta U = 0$. Since $U$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas, this means $\Delta T = 0$. Hence, the temperature of an ideal gas remains **unchanged** during free expansion.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The two alternatives below represent a true internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted, Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**A container of volume $0.5 \; m^3$ is divided into two equal halves by a thin membrane. One side contains $1.5 \; kg$ of air at $350 \; K$, and the other side is completely evacuated. The membrane is suddenly ruptured, and the air is allowed to expand freely into the evacuated half. The container is perfectly insulated. Given: $R = 0.287 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$ and $C_v = 0.718 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$ for air. Determine:**

**(a)** The work done during the process and the change in internal energy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The final temperature and final pressure of the air. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to Part (a): Work Done and Change in Internal Energy (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the process type.**

The gas expands into a vacuum without external resistance, in an insulated container. This is **adiabatic free expansion**.

**Step 2: Calculate the work done.**

Since $P_{ext} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV \\
&= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV \\
&= 0 \; kJ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{W = 0 \; kJ}$ — **(2 marks awarded)**

**Step 3: Apply the First Law.**

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

Given adiabatic conditions: $Q = 0$

**Step 4: Solve for $\Delta U$.**

$$0 = \Delta U + 0 \implies \Delta U = 0 \; kJ$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\Delta U = 0 \; kJ}$ — **(2 marks awarded)**

**Step 5: Verification using $C_v$ formula.**

For ideal gas: $\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T = 1.5 \times 0.718 \times 0 = 0 \; kJ$ ✓ **(1 mark for verification)**

**Step 6: Additional conceptual explanation (2 marks).**

- Free expansion is **irreversible**; the system passes through non-equilibrium states.
- The work done is zero because there is no external resistive force — no piston, no atmospheric pressure, no turbine.

---

**Solution to Part (b): Final Temperature and Final Pressure (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Determine final temperature.**

For ideal gas, $U = U(T)$. Since $\Delta U = 0$:

$$\Delta T = 0 \implies T_2 = T_1 = 350 \; K$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T_2 = 350 \; K}$ — **(2 marks awarded)**

**Step 2: Identify initial and final volumes.**

- Initial volume: $V_1 = 0.5/2 = 0.25 \; m^3$
- Final volume: $V_2 = 0.5 \; m^3$ (entire container)

**Step 3: Calculate initial pressure using ideal gas law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 V_1 &= m R T_1 \\
P_1 &= \frac{m R T_1}{V_1} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.287 \times 350}{0.25} \\
&= \frac{150.675}{0.25} \\
&= 602.7 \; kPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $P_1 = 602.7 \; kPa$ — **(2 marks awarded)**

**Step 4: Calculate final pressure using ideal gas law with $T_2 = T_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} &= \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} \\
P_2 &= P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_2 = T_1$:

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} = 602.7 \times \frac{0.25}{0.5} = 301.35 \; kPa
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_2 = 301.35 \; kPa}$ — **(2 marks awarded)**

**Step 5: Summary statement (1 mark).**

The free expansion of an ideal gas in an insulated container is an **isothermal process** ($T$ constant), even though no heat is exchanged, because the absence of work means no internal energy change.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted, Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Joule Coefficient** $\mu_J$ for a real gas and show that it equals zero for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas undergoes free expansion from $V_1 = 0.1 \; m^3$ to $V_2 = 0.3 \; m^3$. The initial pressure is $P_1 = 800 \; kPa$ and the gas behaves ideally. Determine the work done, heat transferred, and change in internal energy. Comment on the irreversibility of the process. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to Part (a): Joule Coefficient Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark).**

$$\mu_J = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$$

**Step 2: Express $U$ as a function of $T$ and $V$ (1 mark).**

$$U = U(T, V) \implies dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 3: Apply constant internal energy condition $dU = 0$ (1 mark).**

$$0 = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$ (2 marks).**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U = -\frac{\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T}{\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the partial derivatives (1 mark).**

- $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = m C_v$
- $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$

**Step 6: Final expression and ideal gas verification (1 mark).**

$$
\mu_J = -\frac{1}{m C_v}\left[T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P\right]
$$

For ideal gas: $P = mRT/V$, so $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = mR/V$, and the bracket term becomes zero:

$$\mu_J^{ideal} = 0$$

---

**Solution to Part (b): Numerical Free Expansion Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the process — free expansion (1 mark).**

**Step 2: Work done (2 marks).**

$$
W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV = 0 \; kJ
$$

**Step 3: Heat transferred (1 mark).**

Container is adiabatic:

$$Q = 0 \; kJ$$

**Step 4: Change in internal energy (2 marks).**

By First Law:

$$\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0 \; kJ$$

**Step 5: Irreversibility comment (1 mark).**

Free expansion is **irreversible** because:
- It occurs spontaneously without external intervention.
- The system passes through non-equilibrium states.
- It cannot be reversed without work input from surroundings.
- No well-defined P-V path exists on a thermodynamic diagram.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**

1. **Forgetting to set $P_{ext} = 0$:** Students often attempt to calculate work using $P_1$ or $P_2$, leading to grossly incorrect answers. **Always identify free expansion first and set $W = 0$.**

2. **Confusing $\Delta U = 0$ with "no change in state":** The gas *does* change state (P, V change), but internal energy is path-independent for ideal gas, and the path (free expansion) happens to leave $U$ invariant.

3. **Mixing up free expansion with throttling:** Throttling (Joule-Thomson) is a **steady-flow** process across a porous plug where $H$ is constant, NOT $U$. Free expansion is a **closed system** process where $U$ is constant for ideal gas.

4. **Using reversible work formulas:** Do NOT use $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ for free expansion. That formula applies only to **reversible isothermal expansion**.

5. **Plotting P-V curve:** Students sometimes draw a P-V curve for free expansion. This is technically incorrect because the process is irreversible and does not pass through equilibrium states. The curve should be replaced by dashed lines or labeled as "non-quasi-static."

6. **Assuming temperature change for all gases:** Remember, $\Delta T = 0$ holds **strictly for ideal gases only**. For real gases, use the Joule coefficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Free Expansion Work**

### 🔑 Core Principles
- ✅ Free expansion = expansion into vacuum with $P_{ext} = 0$
- ✅ Work done: $W = 0$ (always, regardless of $\Delta V$)
- ✅ Adiabatic case: $Q = 0$ (typical assumption)
- ✅ First Law: $\Delta U = Q - W = 0$
- ✅ Joule's Law: $U = U(T)$ for ideal gas
- ✅ Result for ideal gas: $\Delta T = 0$, $\Delta U = 0$, $W = 0$, $Q = 0$

### 📐 Key Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $W = \int P_{ext} \, dV$
- ✅ $Q = \Delta U + W$ (First Law)
- ✅ $\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$
- ✅ $\mu_J = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$
- ✅ $PV = mRT$ (ideal gas law)
- ✅ $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ when $T$ is constant

### ⚠️ Common Traps
- ❌ Don't use $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ — that's only for constant-pressure processes
- ❌ Don't confuse with throttling (Joule-Thomson, $H$ constant)
- ❌ Don't apply $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ — that's for reversible isothermal
- ❌ Don't draw a standard P-V curve — the process is irreversible

### 🎯 Engineering Relevance
- 🔧 Pressure relief valve design
- 🔧 Cryogenic venting
- 🔧 Throttling process limiting case
- 🔧 Safety analysis in chemical reactors

### 📊 Process Identification Checklist
- ✅ Look for keywords: "evacuated chamber," "vacuum," "rupture," "burst," "into empty space"
- ✅ If seen, immediately invoke: $W = 0$, $\Delta U = 0$, $\Delta T = 0$ (for ideal gas)
- ✅ Use ideal gas law to find $P_2$ given $\Delta V$ and constant $T$

### 🏆 KTU Exam Confidence Boosters
- 🎓 This topic is **high-yield** — appears in 8 out of 10 KTU papers
- 🎓 Direct 3-mark definition question is a **frequent repeat**
- 🎓 14-mark numerical problems typically involve **1-2 ideal gas law applications**
- 🎓 Always state assumptions clearly: **ideal gas, adiabatic, rigid insulated container**

> **Final Note:** Free expansion is conceptually simple but conceptually profound. It teaches students that **work is a path function** — the same initial and final states can yield vastly different work outputs depending on the process chosen. Master this distinction, and you master the heart of thermodynamics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat and Heat Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat and Heat Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Heat ($Q$)** is defined as the form of energy that is transferred across the boundary of a thermodynamic system as a result of a temperature difference existing between the system and its surroundings. Heat is a **path function** (an inexact differential, $đQ$), meaning its magnitude depends on the process path taken between the initial and final states, not solely on the state endpoints.

**Heat Capacity ($C$)** is defined as the quantity of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a body (or system) by **one degree** of temperature (1 K or 1 °C). It is an extensive property — its value depends on the amount (mass) of the substance present.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Distinguish Carefully**
> - **Heat ($Q$)**: Energy *in transit* across a system boundary.
> - **Heat Capacity ($C$)**: A *property* of the system describing its ability to store thermal energy.
> - **Internal Energy ($U$)**: The total microscopic kinetic and potential energy *contained within* the system at a given state.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Kettle**
> Imagine you are boiling water in an electric kettle. The kettle is the *system*, the heating coil delivers **heat** ($Q$) into the water. The water's **heat capacity** is essentially the kettle's "thermal appetite" — how much heat energy it must absorb before its temperature rises by 1 °C.
> - A small cup of water (low mass $\rightarrow$ low heat capacity) heats up quickly.
> - A large bucket of water (high mass $\rightarrow$ high heat capacity) takes far longer, even with the same heater.
> - During phase change (boiling at 100 °C), the water absorbs **latent heat** but its temperature does *not* rise — this is the "hidden" component of heat capacity.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Specific heat of liquid water:** $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard temperature reference:** $T_0 = 273.15 \text{ K}$ (0 °C)
- **SI unit of Heat:** Joule (**J**) or kilojoule (**kJ**)
- **SI unit of Heat Capacity:** J/K (or kJ/K)
- **SI unit of Specific Heat:** J/(kg·K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note (PCPET303, Module 3):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to (a) distinguish between *sensible* and *latent* heat, (b) apply the first law to non-flow processes involving $Q$, and (c) compute heat capacity for solids, liquids, and ideal gases (constant $C_p$, $C_v$, and the ratio $\gamma$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The Taxonomy of Heat

Heat entering or leaving a system can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**: by *temperature effect* and by *mechanism of absorption*.

#### A. By Temperature Effect
1. **Sensible Heat ($Q_s$):** Heat transfer that causes a *measurable change in temperature* of the substance, without a phase change.
   - Governed by: $Q_s = m \, c \, \Delta T$
2. **Latent Heat ($Q_L$):** Heat transfer that causes a *phase change* (solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid $\leftrightarrow$ gas) at constant temperature.
   - Governed by: $Q_L = m \, L$ (where $L$ is the latent heat — fusion $L_f$ or vaporisation $L_v$).

#### B. By Mechanism (for ideal gases)
1. **Heat at Constant Volume ($Q_v$):** All heat supplied goes into raising internal energy (no boundary work since $dV = 0$).
2. **Heat at Constant Pressure ($Q_p$):** Heat supplied raises internal energy *and* performs boundary work $W = P\,\Delta V$.

### 2.2 Why and How — Physical Reasoning

- **Why $Q$ is a path function:** Because heat is energy in transit, the *route* (constant $P$ vs. constant $V$ vs. polytropic) determines how much is transferred for a given $\Delta T$. The same state change from $T_1$ to $T_2$ can require different amounts of heat.
- **Why $C_p > C_v$ (always, for ideal gases):** At constant pressure, part of the supplied heat is "spent" doing expansion work against the surroundings. Thus, more heat must be supplied to achieve the same $\Delta T$ compared to a constant-volume process.
- **Why solids have low $C_p$:** In solids, atoms vibrate about fixed lattice positions; there are fewer degrees of freedom to excite (Dulong-Petit law: $C_v \approx 3R$ per mole for solids at high $T$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** for Module 3 numerical problems. All symbols use SI units. Use `\vert` for absolute value to keep the table syntax intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible Heat | $Q = m\,c\,\Delta T$ | J | $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ |
| 2 | Latent Heat | $Q = m\,L$ | J | $L = L_f$ (fusion) or $L_v$ (vaporisation) |
| 3 | Heat Capacity (extensive) | $C = Q / \Delta T$ | J/K | Property of the *whole body* |
| 4 | Specific Heat (intensive) | $c = Q / (m \cdot \Delta T)$ | J/(kg·K) | Independent of mass |
| 5 | Molar Specific Heat | $c_m = Q / (n \cdot \Delta T)$ | J/(mol·K) | Used in gas thermodynamics |
| 6 | Constant-$V$ Molar Heat | $C_v$ | J/(mol·K) | For ideal gas: $C_v = f R / 2$ |
| 7 | Constant-$P$ Molar Heat | $C_p$ | J/(mol·K) | For ideal gas: $C_p = (f+2) R / 2$ |
| 8 | Mayer's Relation | $C_p - C_v = R$ | J/(mol·K) | **Valid only for ideal gases** |
| 9 | Specific Heat Ratio | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | dimensionless | Monatomic: 1.667; Diatomic: 1.4 |
| 10 | Dulong-Petit Limit (solids) | $C_v \approx 3R$ | J/(mol·K) | Holds at high $T$ for many solids |
| 11 | Heat in Constant-$V$ Process | $Q_v = n C_v \Delta T$ | J | All $\rightarrow$ internal energy |
| 12 | Heat in Constant-$P$ Process | $Q_p = n C_p \Delta T$ | J | Equals $\Delta H$ (enthalpy change) |
| 13 | Enthalpy Definition | $H = U + PV$ | J | $dH = đQ_p$ at constant $P$ |
| 14 | Total Heat (sensible + latent) | $Q = m c \Delta T + m L$ | J | Used during phase transition |

> where $f$ = degrees of freedom (3 translational for monatomic, 5 for diatomic at moderate $T$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plant design:** Boiler heat duty = $m c_p \Delta T + m L_v$ (sensible heating of feedwater + vaporisation).
- **HVAC systems:** Cooling load calculations use $Q = m c \Delta T$ for air-handling units.
- **IC engines:** Otto and Diesel cycle analysis depends critically on $\gamma = C_p / C_v$.
- **Cryogenics:** Storage and transport of LNG/LH₂ require accurate latent heat and specific heat data.
- **Refrigeration:** Latent heat of vaporisation of refrigerants (R-134a, R-410A) determines cycle performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sensible Heat Equation $Q = m c \Delta T$ from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system in differential form is:

$$đQ = dU + đW$$

For a simple compressible substance undergoing a quasi-static process:

$$đW = P \, dV$$

$$dU = m \, c_v \, dT$$

Substituting these into the First Law:

$$đQ = m \, c_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

Now invoke the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**: $PV = mRT$ per unit mass, so $P \, dV + V \, dP = mR \, dT$.

For a **constant-pressure** process, $dP = 0$, hence $P \, dV = mR \, dT$.

Substituting this into the First Law expression:

$$đQ = m \, c_v \, dT + mR \, dT$$

$$đQ = m (c_v + R) \, dT$$

By **Mayer's relation** (proved below), $c_p = c_v + R$ for an ideal gas. Therefore:

$$đQ = m \, c_p \, dT$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\int_{Q_1}^{Q_2} đQ = m \, c_p \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$\boxed{Q = m \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1) = m \, c_p \, \Delta T}$$

> This is the **general sensible heat equation**. For liquids and solids, $c_p \approx c_v \approx c$ (since volume change is negligible), so $Q = m c \Delta T$ applies universally.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mayer's Relation: $C_p - C_v = R$ for an Ideal Gas

**Starting Premise:** Consider one mole of an ideal gas. The First Law gives:

$$đQ = dU + P \, dV$$

**Step 1 — Constant Volume Process** ($dV = 0$):
All supplied heat goes into raising internal energy:

$$(đQ)_V = dU = C_v \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Constant Pressure Process** ($dP = 0$):
Heat supplied at constant pressure is by definition the change in **enthalpy** $H = U + PV$:

$$(đQ)_P = dH = d(U + PV) = dU + P \, dV + V \, dP$$

Since $dP = 0$:

$$(đQ)_P = dU + P \, dV = C_p \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Subtract and use the Ideal Gas Law:**
From the two expressions above:

$$C_p \, dT - C_v \, dT = (dU + P \, dV) - dU = P \, dV$$

$$(C_p - C_v) \, dT = P \, dV$$

For one mole of an ideal gas at constant pressure, $PV = RT$. Differentiating with $dP = 0$:

$$P \, dV = R \, dT$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$(C_p - C_v) \, dT = R \, dT$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(per mole of ideal gas)}}$$

> This relation is **exact** for ideal gases and an excellent approximation for real gases at low pressures and moderate temperatures.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Heat Ratio $\gamma$ in Terms of Degrees of Freedom $f$

**Equipartition Theorem:** Each degree of freedom contributes $\frac{1}{2}R$ per mole to internal energy at thermal equilibrium.

For an ideal gas with $f$ active degrees of freedom:

$$U = \frac{f}{2} n R T \quad \text{(for $n$ moles)}$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $V$:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{f}{2} R$$

Applying Mayer's relation:

$$C_p = C_v + R = \frac{f}{2} R + R = \frac{f+2}{2} R$$

Therefore the specific heat ratio is:

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v} = \frac{(f+2)/2}{f/2} = \frac{f+2}{f}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 1 + \frac{2}{f}}$$

**Numerical verification:**

| Gas Type | $f$ | $C_v$ | $C_p$ | $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monatomic (He, Ar) | 3 | $\frac{3}{2}R$ | $\frac{5}{2}R$ | 1.667 |
| Diatomic (N₂, O₂) | 5 | $\frac{5}{2}R$ | $\frac{7}{2}R$ | 1.400 |
| Polyatomic (CO₂, CH₄) | 6 | $3R$ | $4R$ | 1.333 |

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Required for a Process with Phase Change (Analytical Matrix)

When a substance undergoes a temperature change *and* a phase change, the total heat is the **sum of three sequential contributions**:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= m \, c_1 \, (T_{\text{phase}} - T_1) &&\text{(sensible heat in initial phase)} \\
Q_2 &= m \, L &&\text{(latent heat during phase change at } T_{\text{phase}}) \\
Q_3 &= m \, c_2 \, (T_2 - T_{\text{phase}}) &&\text{(sensible heat in final phase)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> For example, to convert 1 kg of ice at $-10$ °C to steam at 110 °C, one must compute all three terms using $c_{\text{ice}}$, $L_f$, $c_{\text{water}}$, $L_v$, and $c_{\text{steam}}$ in sequence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Energy Flow through a Thermodynamic System

The following block diagram illustrates how heat input partitions into internal energy change and boundary work, and how the classification of heat (sensible vs. latent) branches based on the system state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Energy Q Supplied to System]:::inputNode
    B{System State at Supply Moment}:::decision
    C[Pure Temperature Change]:::path
    D[Phase Transition Occurring]:::path
    E[Sensible Heat Qs = m c dT]:::output
    F[Latent Heat QL = m L]:::output
    G[Internal Energy dU]:::output
    H[Boundary Work W = P dV]:::output

    A --> B
    B -->|No Phase Change| C
    B -->|Phase Change at Constant T| D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> G

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Mayer's Relation Logical Derivation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Start: 1 mole of ideal gas]:::base
    stepB[Constant V: dQv = dU = Cv dT]:::proc
    stepC[Constant P: dQp = dH = Cp dT]:::proc
    stepD[Subtract: Cp dT minus Cv dT = P dV]:::proc
    stepE[Apply PV = RT with dP = 0]:::proc
    stepF[Result: P dV = R dT]:::proc
    stepG[Final Identity: Cp minus Cv = R]:::final

    stepA --> stepB
    stepA --> stepC
    stepB --> stepD
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG

    classDef base fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000

```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Total Heat with Phase Change

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    state1[Initial State T1 Phase Solid]:::node
    stage1[Stage 1: Heat to Melting Point Tfus using c_solid]:::stage
    state2[Phase Change at Tfus Solid to Liquid]:::node
    stage2[Stage 2: Latent Heat of Fusion Lf]:::stage
    state3[Liquid Phase Heating Tfus to Tboil using c_liquid]:::stage
    state4[Phase Change at Tboil Liquid to Vapour]:::node

    state5[Final State T2 Phase Vapour]:::node

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish clearly between sensible heat and latent heat. Give one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat transfer that produces a **measurable temperature change** in a substance | Heat transfer that produces a **phase change** at constant temperature |
| Formula | $Q_s = m \, c \, \Delta T$ | $Q_L = m \, L$ |
| Temperature | Changes during transfer | Remains constant during transfer |
| Example | Heating water from 20 °C to 80 °C in a geyser | Melting ice at 0 °C to water at 0 °C |

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the distinguishing feature, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

**Q.A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Mayer's relation. Why is $C_p$ always greater than $C_v$ for an ideal gas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mayer's relation states that for **one mole of an ideal gas**, the difference between the molar specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume equals the universal gas constant:

$$C_p - C_v = R$$

**Why $C_p > C_v$ (2 marks):** In a constant-pressure process, part of the heat supplied is utilised in performing **boundary work** ($W = P \, \Delta V$) to expand the gas against the surroundings, in addition to raising its internal energy. In a constant-volume process, no such work is done, and *all* the supplied heat contributes to raising internal energy. Hence, for the same temperature rise, more heat must be supplied at constant pressure than at constant volume, making $C_p > C_v$ by an amount exactly equal to $R$ (the work done per mole per kelvin).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive **Mayer's relation** $C_p - C_v = R$ for one mole of an ideal gas, starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air ($C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K) is heated from 300 K to 600 K in a closed, rigid vessel. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   1. The mass-specific heat supplied.
   2. The change in internal energy per kg of air.
   3. The boundary work done.
   4. The heat rejected if the same amount of air is now cooled back to 300 K at constant pressure.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Mayer's Relation (7 marks):**

Consider **one mole** of an ideal gas. The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system is:

$$đQ = dU + P \, dV$$

**Case 1: Constant Volume** ($dV = 0$)

The boundary work term vanishes, and all heat raises the internal energy:

$$(đQ)_V = dU = C_v \, dT \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Case 2: Constant Pressure** ($dP = 0$)

By definition, the heat supplied at constant pressure equals the change in **enthalpy** $H = U + PV$:

$$(đQ)_P = dH = dU + P \, dV + V \, dP$$

Since $dP = 0$:

$$(đQ)_P = dU + P \, dV = C_p \, dT \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract equation (1) from (2):**

$$C_p \, dT - C_v \, dT = P \, dV$$

$$(C_p - C_v) \, dT = P \, dV \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Use the ideal gas law** for one mole: $PV = RT$. Differentiating at constant $P$:

$$P \, dV = R \, dT \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (4) into (3):**

$$(C_p - C_v) \, dT = R \, dT$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

**Assumptions used (mention explicitly for full marks):**
1. The gas behaves ideally ($PV = nRT$ holds exactly).
2. The process is quasi-static (system is in internal equilibrium at every step).
3. One mole of gas is considered (relation is molar).
4. $C_p$ and $C_v$ are constant over the temperature range.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 600$ K, closed rigid vessel.

**1) Heat supplied (constant volume process since rigid vessel, $dV = 0$):**

$$Q = m \, C_v \, \Delta T$$

Per kg basis:

$$q = C_v \, (T_2 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (600 - 300)$$

$$q = 0.718 \times 300 = 215.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**2) Change in internal energy per kg:**

For a closed rigid system, $đQ = dU$ (no work), so:

$$\Delta u = q = 215.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**3) Boundary work done:**

Rigid vessel $\Rightarrow dV = 0$:

$$W = \int P \, dV = 0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**4) Heat rejected on cooling at constant pressure from 600 K back to 300 K:**

$$q_{\text{reject}} = m \, C_p \, (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (600 - 300)$$

$$q_{\text{reject}} = 1.005 \times 300 = 301.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

(The negative sign indicates heat *out* of the system.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss in Part (b):**
> 1. **Misreading "rigid vessel" as constant pressure** — A rigid vessel implies **constant volume**, so use $C_v$, not $C_p$, for the heating step. Many students incorrectly use $C_p$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention for heat rejected** — KTU expects the magnitude; explicitly state "heat *out* of the system" or attach a negative sign.
> 3. **Forgetting the "per kg" basis** — Write the answer with explicit units (kJ/kg) to avoid losing the unit mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **specific heat capacity** and **molar specific heat capacity**. With the help of the **equipartition theorem**, derive expressions for $C_v$, $C_p$, and $\gamma$ in terms of the degrees of freedom $f$ for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper block of mass 5 kg is initially at 20 °C. It is heated until it reaches 80 °C. Given that the specific heat capacity of copper is 0.386 kJ/(kg·K), determine:
   1. The heat supplied.
   2. If the same amount of heat is supplied to 5 kg of water (initially at 20 °C, $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K), what final temperature is reached?
   3. Comment on the engineering significance of the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Specific heat capacity ($c$):** The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a **unit mass** of a substance by one kelvin. SI unit: J/(kg·K). *(1 mark)*

**Molar specific heat capacity ($C_m$):** The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **one mole** of a substance by one kelvin. SI unit: J/(mol·K). *(1 mark)*

**Equipartition Theorem statement:** In thermal equilibrium, the total energy of a system is equally distributed among all its accessible degrees of freedom, with each degree contributing $\frac{1}{2} k_B T$ per molecule, or equivalently $\frac{1}{2} R T$ per mole. *(1 mark)*

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a monatomic ideal gas with $f = 3$ translational degrees of freedom, the molar internal energy is:

$$U = \frac{3}{2} R T$$

Generalising to $f$ active degrees of freedom:

$$U = \frac{f}{2} R T \quad \text{(per mole)}$$

Differentiating at constant volume:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{f}{2} R$$

By Mayer's relation:

$$C_p = C_v + R = \frac{f}{2} R + R = \frac{f+2}{2} R$$

The specific heat ratio:

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v} = \frac{(f+2)/2}{f/2} = \frac{f+2}{f} = 1 + \frac{2}{f}$$

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{f}{2} R, \quad C_p = \frac{f+2}{2} R, \quad \gamma = 1 + \frac{2}{f}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $m = 5$ kg, $T_1 = 20$ °C, $T_2 = 80$ °C, $c_{\text{Cu}} = 0.386$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K).

**1) Heat supplied to copper block:**

$$Q = m \, c_{\text{Cu}} \, \Delta T = 5 \times 0.386 \times (80 - 20)$$

$$Q = 5 \times 0.386 \times 60 = 115.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**2) Final temperature of water if same heat is supplied:**

$$Q = m \, c_w \, (T_f - T_i)$$

$$115.8 = 5 \times 4.186 \times (T_f - 20)$$

$$115.8 = 20.93 \, (T_f - 20)$$

$$T_f - 20 = \frac{115.8}{20.93} = 5.53$$

$$T_f = 25.53 \text{ °C}$$

**3) Engineering significance:**

- Copper requires **far less heat** to achieve the same temperature rise as water (since $c_{\text{Cu}} \ll c_w$).
- This is why copper is used as a **heat exchanger material** and in **cooking utensil bases** — it responds quickly to heat input.
- Conversely, water's high specific heat makes it an excellent **coolant** (in car radiators, industrial cooling towers) and **thermal storage medium** (in solar water heaters).
- The ratio $c_w / c_{\text{Cu}} \approx 4.186 / 0.386 \approx 10.85$ — water stores about **11 times more energy per kg per kelvin** than copper.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss in Question B:**
> 1. **Mixing up $c$ and $C$** — $c$ is per kg (specific), $C$ is per mole (molar). The problem uses mass, so use $c_w$, not $C_w$.
> 2. **Unit conversion** — If $c$ is given in kJ/(kg·K) and $m$ in kg, $\Delta T$ in K, then $Q$ is in kJ. Mixing J and kJ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the "comment" sub-part** — KTU awards 2 marks for the engineering interpretation. Just stating numbers without physical insight loses those marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 (Heat and Heat Capacity)**

- **Heat ($Q$)** is energy in *transit*, not stored. It is a **path function** ($đQ$, inexact differential).
- **Heat Capacity ($C$)** is a state property — it is the *capacity* of a system to store thermal energy.
- **Sensible heat** $\rightarrow$ temperature change only: $Q = m c \Delta T$.
- **Latent heat** $\rightarrow$ phase change at constant $T$: $Q = m L$.
- **Total heat with phase change** = $m c_1 \Delta T_1 + m L + m c_2 \Delta T_2$ (apply sequentially).
- **Mayer's Relation** $C_p - C_v = R$ is valid **only for ideal gases** and is a *molar* relation.
- For solids and liquids, $C_p \approx C_v \approx c$ (incompressibility makes the difference negligible).
- **Specific heat ratio** $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1 + 2/f$.
- Degrees of freedom: Monatomic $f = 3$, Diatomic $f = 5$, Polyatomic $f = 6$ (at moderate $T$).
- **Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$, and at constant pressure, $đQ_p = dH$.
- A **rigid vessel** $\Rightarrow dV = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ use $C_v$, not $C_p$.
- A **closed piston-cylinder** at constant external pressure $\Rightarrow$ use $C_p$.
- The value $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the **per-mole** constant; the per-kg form is $R/M$ where $M$ is molar mass.
- **Dulong-Petit Law:** $C_v \approx 3R$ per mole for many solid elements at high $T$ (~25 J/mol·K).
- Watch for **sign conventions**: heat *in* is positive, heat *out* is negative (KTU thermodynamic sign convention).
- **Common exam trap:** If the problem says "at constant pressure," $Q = n C_p \Delta T$, **not** $n C_v \Delta T$.

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Heat and Heat Capacity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The First Law of Thermodynamics (FLOT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> The First Law of Thermodynamics is the principle of **Conservation of Energy** applied to thermodynamic systems. It states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another or transferred between a system and its surroundings.

Mathematically, for a **closed system** undergoing a cycle, the net cyclic integral of heat equals the net cyclic integral of work:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

For a **change of state** (process) of a closed system:

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

Where:
- **Q** = Heat transferred **to** the system (**kJ**)
- **W** = Work done **by** the system (**kJ**)
- **ΔU** = Change in internal energy (**kJ**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Think of your bank account. Money deposited (heat added, Q) increases your balance. Money withdrawn (work done, W) decreases it. The net change in your balance (ΔU) is always exactly equal to deposits minus withdrawals. The First Law is the "energy accounting equation" of the universe — no energy is ever lost, only converted or relocated.

---

## 1.2 Enthalpy (H)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Enthalpy** is a thermodynamic property defined as the sum of the internal energy of a system and the product of its pressure and volume. It represents the total heat content of a system at constant pressure.
>
> $$H = U + PV$$

Where:
- **H** = Enthalpy (**kJ**)
- **U** = Internal energy (**kJ**)
- **P** = Absolute pressure (**kPa**)
- **V** = Volume (**m³**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine a balloon full of hot gas. The gas has internal energy (the jiggle of its molecules — **U**). But it also has to "push the atmosphere out of the way" to exist at pressure P inside volume V. That pushing work (**PV**) is also energy the system possesses. Enthalpy = the *total* thermal energy content of a substance flowing through pipes, boilers, and turbines. This is why enthalpy is the preferred property in **open systems** like turbines, compressors, and nozzles — there, the work to push fluid in/out (flow work) is naturally bundled in.

---

## 1.3 Specific Heats

### 1.3.1 Specific Heat at Constant Volume (Cv)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Specific heat at constant volume (Cv)** is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **unit mass** of a substance by **one degree** (K or °C) while the **volume is held constant**.

$$C_v = \left( \frac{\partial Q}{\partial T} \right)_V = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial T} \right)_V \quad [\text{kJ/kg·K}]$$

### 1.3.2 Specific Heat at Constant Pressure (Cp)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Specific heat at constant pressure (Cp)** is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **unit mass** of a substance by **one degree** while the **pressure is held constant**.

$$C_p = \left( \frac{\partial Q}{\partial T} \right)_P = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P \quad [\text{kJ/kg·K}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Heating water in a **rigid steel container** (constant volume) is like **Cv** — all the heat goes into raising temperature only.
>
> Heating water in an **open pot on a stove** (constant pressure = atmospheric) is like **Cp** — the heat not only raises temperature but also does work to push back the atmosphere as water expands slightly. That's why **Cp > Cv always**.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Property | Real-World Use |
|----------|----------------|
| First Law | Energy audit of power plants, IC engines, refrigerators |
| Enthalpy | Boiler design, turbine work, throttle processes |
| Cv | Analysis of rigid containers, bomb calorimeters |
| Cp | Open-flow processes, heat exchangers, HVAC systems |

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the First Law

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. **Energy is Conserved**: Total energy of an **isolated system** is constant.
2. **System-Surroundings Interaction**: Energy crosses the system boundary in only **two forms**: Heat (Q) and Work (W).
3. **Sign Convention** (KTU standard):
   - Heat **added to** system → **Q is positive (+Q)**
   - Work **done by** system → **W is positive (+W)**
4. **Energy Balance Equation**:
   $$\text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} = \text{Change in Stored Energy}$$
   $$Q_{in} - W_{out} = \Delta U_{system}$$
5. **For a cyclic process**: ΔU = 0 (since U is a property, it returns to original value)
   $$\therefore Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

### Why Enthalpy Emerges Naturally:
In **open systems** (control volumes), fluid enters and exits carrying **flow work (PV)** in addition to internal energy. To avoid repeatedly calculating this flow work, engineers define the composite property:

$$H \equiv U + PV$$

This makes energy equations for turbines, compressors, and nozzles extremely compact.

---

## 2.2 Mayer's Relation — The Cp – Cv Connection

### The "Why":
For **one mole** of an **ideal gas**, consider the two paths from state 1 to state 2 (where temperature changes by dT):

- **Path A (Constant V):** All heat → internal energy change: $\delta Q_V = nC_v\,dT = dU$
- **Path B (Constant P):** All heat → enthalpy change: $\delta Q_P = nC_p\,dT = dH$

Since $H = U + PV$ and for ideal gas $PV = nRT$:

$$dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + nR\,dT$$

Substituting:

$$nC_p\,dT = nC_v\,dT + nR\,dT$$

Dividing throughout by $n\,dT$:

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mayer's Relation (per mole basis):**
> $$C_p - C_v = R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$$

### Specific Heat Ratio (γ):

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$$

**Common values for ideal gases:**
- **Monatomic** (Ar, He): $\gamma = 1.667$
- **Diatomic** (N₂, O₂, Air): $\gamma = 1.4$
- **Triatomic** (CO₂): $\gamma \approx 1.28$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Master These for KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q - W = \Delta U$ | First Law (Closed System, Sign Convention: Q in, W out) | kJ |
| 2 | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | First Law (Cyclic Process) | kJ |
| 3 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of Enthalpy | kJ |
| 4 | $dH = dU + d(PV)$ | Differential form of Enthalpy | kJ |
| 5 | $dU = m\,C_v\,dT$ | Internal energy change (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 6 | $dH = m\,C_p\,dT$ | Enthalpy change (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 7 | $C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Definition of Cv | kJ/kg·K |
| 8 | $C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Definition of Cp | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | **Mayer's Relation** (per unit mass: $c_p - c_v = R/m$) | kJ/kg·K |
| 10 | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ | Specific heat ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ | Cp in terms of R and γ | kJ/kg·K |
| 12 | $C_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$ | Cv in terms of R and γ | kJ/kg·K |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

| Engineering Field | Application |
|-------------------|-------------|
| **Steam Power Plants** | Rankine cycle uses enthalpy of steam at boiler inlet/turbine exit |
| **Gas Turbines** | Brayton cycle uses Cp for compressor/turbine work calculations |
| **IC Engines** | Otto/Diesel cycles rely on Cv and Cp for thermal efficiency |
| **Refrigeration** | Vapour compression cycle uses enthalpy at evaporator/condenser |
| **Rocket Propulsion** | Throat and exit conditions in nozzles use enthalpy conservation |
| **Chemical Process** | Reactor design uses Cp for sensible heat, ΔH for reaction heat |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First Law for a Closed System from Fundamentals

### Statement:
When a closed system undergoes a process, the net heat transfer equals the net work transfer plus the change in internal energy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider a system with internal energy **U₁** in state 1. Heat **Q** is supplied to it and it does work **W** on the surroundings, reaching state 2 with internal energy **U₂**.

$$U_1 + Q = U_2 + W$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging for energy balance:

$$Q - W = U_2 - U_1$$

**Step 3:** Defining $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1$:

$$\boxed{Q - W = \Delta U}$$

This is the **First Law equation for a closed system**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of Enthalpy (H = U + PV)

### Concept:
Enthalpy is derived from the need to express the **total energy carried by a fluid element** entering or leaving a control volume, including the work required to push that fluid into the system against the prevailing pressure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider a mass **m** entering a control volume at pressure **P** and volume **V**. To push this mass in, the surroundings must do work equal to **P·V** (flow work).

$$\text{Flow Work} = P \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** The total energy entering with the fluid is the sum of:
- Internal Energy (U)
- Flow Work (PV)
- Kinetic Energy (KE)
- Potential Energy (PE)

**Step 3:** For a stationary control volume (neglecting KE and PE), the relevant "heat-like" energy content is:

$$\text{Total Energy Content} = U + PV$$

**Step 4:** This composite property is defined as **Enthalpy**:

$$\boxed{H = U + PV}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiating:

$$dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$) — Exhaustive

### Step 1: Define Cp and Cv from First Principles
For an ideal gas at constant volume:
$$dU = n C_v \, dT$$

For an ideal gas at constant pressure:
$$dH = n C_p \, dT$$

### Step 2: Express Enthalpy in Terms of Internal Energy
By definition:
$$H = U + PV$$

For an ideal gas, from the equation of state $PV = nRT$:
$$H = U + nRT$$

### Step 3: Differentiate Both Sides
$$dH = dU + nR \, dT$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Expressions for dH and dU
$$n C_p \, dT = n C_v \, dT + n R \, dT$$

### Step 5: Divide Through by n dT
$$C_p = C_v + R$$

### Step 6: Final Result

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

Where **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** is the universal gas constant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cp and Cv in Terms of γ and R

Given:
$$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{and} \quad \gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$$

### Step 1: Express Cp as γ·Cv
$$C_p = \gamma C_v$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Mayer's Relation
$$\gamma C_v - C_v = R$$

$$C_v (\gamma - 1) = R$$

$$C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1}$$

### Step 3: Multiply Cv by γ to get Cp
$$C_p = \gamma \cdot \frac{R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$$

### Step 4: Final Boxed Results

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{C_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: First Law Applied to Constant Pressure Process — Why H appears

For a **constant pressure process** ($P = $ const, so $dP = 0$):

**Step 1:** From the First Law:
$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W = dU + P\,dV$$

**Step 2:** Since $P$ is constant:
$$\delta Q = dU + d(PV) = d(U + PV)$$

**Step 3:** Recognizing $H = U + PV$:
$$\delta Q_P = dH$$

**Step 4:** Integrating:
$$\boxed{Q_P = \Delta H = m C_p \Delta T}$$

This is **why enthalpy is THE property for constant-pressure processes** — heat added at constant pressure equals enthalpy change directly.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: First Law Applied to Constant Volume Process

For a **constant volume process** ($V = $ const, so $dV = 0$):

**Step 1:** Work done is zero since $\delta W = P\,dV = 0$

**Step 2:** First Law becomes:
$$\delta Q_V = dU$$

**Step 3:** Integrating:
$$\boxed{Q_V = \Delta U = m C_v \Delta T}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Process Type | Work Done | Heat Transfer | Energy Property Used |
|--------------|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Constant Volume | W = 0 | Q = ΔU = mCvΔT | Internal Energy (U) |
| Constant Pressure | W = PΔV | Q = ΔH = mCpΔT | Enthalpy (H) |
| Isothermal (Ideal Gas) | W = Q | ΔU = 0, ΔH = 0 | None change |
| Adiabatic | Q = 0 | W = –ΔU | Internal Energy (U) |
| Cyclic | W_net ≠ 0 | Q_net = W_net | ΔU = 0 |
