# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system and explain the sign convention used.**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system states that:

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

Where **Q** is the heat transferred **to** the system and **W** is the work done **by** the system.

**Sign Convention (KTU standard):**
- Heat **supplied to** the system → **Q is Positive (+Q)**
- Heat **rejected by** the system → **Q is Negative (−Q)**
- Work **done by** the system (expansion) → **W is Positive (+W)**
- Work **done on** the system (compression) → **W is Negative (−W)**

This convention ensures that energy added to a system increases its internal energy, consistent with conservation of energy.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define enthalpy. Why is it preferred over internal energy for analyzing open systems?**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Definition:**
Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property defined as the sum of internal energy (U) and the product of pressure and volume (PV):

$$H = U + PV$$

**Why preferred for open systems:**
In open systems (control volumes), fluid continuously enters and exits. To push a unit mass into the control volume against the system pressure, the surroundings must perform **flow work (PV)** in addition to carrying internal energy. Bundling U and PV into a single property **H = U + PV** simplifies energy equations by automatically accounting for flow work. Hence enthalpy is the natural property for boilers, turbines, compressors, and nozzles.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B – Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive **Mayer's relation** $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas, starting from the definitions of enthalpy and the ideal gas equation.

**(b)** A gas of mass **2 kg** is heated at constant pressure from **300 K to 500 K**. If $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, find:
  (i) Heat supplied
  (ii) Work done
  (iii) Change in internal energy
  (iv) Change in enthalpy

#### ✅ Solution:

### **Part (a) — Derivation of Mayer's Relation**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of enthalpy:
$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ideal gas equation: $PV = mRT$ (for mass m, specific gas constant R)
$$H = U + mRT$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature:
$$dH = dU + mR\,dT$$

**Step 4:** Use the specific heat definitions:
$$dH = m C_p \, dT \quad \text{and} \quad dU = m C_v \, dT$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:
$$m C_p \, dT = m C_v \, dT + m R \, dT$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $m\,dT$:
$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $m = 2$ kg
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, $\Delta T = 200$ K
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K
- Process: **Constant Pressure**

#### (i) Heat Supplied (Q)
For constant pressure: $Q = m C_p \Delta T$

$$Q = 2 \times 1.005 \times 200 = \boxed{402 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (ii) Work Done (W)
For constant pressure: $W = P\Delta V = mR\Delta T = m(C_p - C_v)\Delta T$

$$W = 2 \times (1.005 - 0.718) \times 200$$

$$W = 2 \times 0.287 \times 200 = \boxed{114.8 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (iii) Change in Internal Energy (ΔU)

$$\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T = 2 \times 0.718 \times 200 = \boxed{287.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (iv) Change in Enthalpy (ΔH)

$$\Delta H = m C_p \Delta T = 2 \times 1.005 \times 200 = \boxed{402 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (First Law):** $Q - W = 402 - 114.8 = 287.2$ kJ $= \Delta U$ ✓

---

### **Question B – Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Define specific heats at constant volume and constant pressure. Show that for an ideal gas $C_p > C_v$.

**(b)** A rigid container of volume **0.5 m³** contains air at **200 kPa and 27°C**. Heat is supplied until the temperature rises to **127°C**. Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas with $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, calculate:
  (i) Mass of air
  (ii) Heat supplied
  (iii) Work done
  (iv) Change in enthalpy

#### ✅ Solution:

### **Part (a) — Definitions and Proof that Cp > Cv**

**Specific heat at constant volume (Cv):**
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of a substance by one degree while the volume is kept constant.

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Specific heat at constant pressure (Cp):**
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of a substance by one degree while the pressure is kept constant.

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Proof that Cp > Cv:**
At constant pressure, the supplied heat not only raises the temperature (and hence internal energy) but also performs work to expand the gas against the surroundings:

$$Q_P = \Delta U + W_{\text{expansion}}$$

$$m C_p \Delta T = m C_v \Delta T + P\Delta V$$

Since $P\Delta V > 0$ for an expanding gas during heating:

$$m C_p \Delta T > m C_v \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{C_p > C_v}$$

Equivalently, by Mayer's relation: $C_p - C_v = R > 0$ for any real gas.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $V = 0.5$ m³ (constant — rigid container)
- $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K
- $T_2 = 127 + 273 = 400$ K
- $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
- $C_p = C_v + R = 0.718 + 0.287 = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K

#### (i) Mass of Air
Using ideal gas equation: $PV = mRT$

$$m = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{100}{86.1}$$

$$\boxed{m = 1.1614 \text{ kg}}$$

#### (ii) Heat Supplied
Since the container is **rigid** (constant volume), heat supplied changes internal energy:

$$Q = \Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$$

$$Q = 1.1614 \times 0.718 \times (400 - 300)$$

$$Q = 1.1614 \times 0.718 \times 100 = \boxed{83.39 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (iii) Work Done
Since the volume is **constant**, $\Delta V = 0$:

$$\boxed{W = 0 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### (iv) Change in Enthalpy
Enthalpy change depends on temperature change only (for ideal gas):

$$\Delta H = m C_p \Delta T = 1.1614 \times 1.005 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = 116.72 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (First Law):** $Q - W = 83.39 - 0 = 83.39$ kJ $= \Delta U$ ✓

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder**: Students often write $W - Q = \Delta U$ instead of $Q - W = \Delta U$. This single sign error cascades through the entire problem — **always use the KTU convention: Q in, W out.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Cv and Cp**: In **constant volume** problems, use **Cv**. In **constant pressure** problems, use **Cp**. Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose 7 marks per sub-question.
>
> 3. **Wrong Unit for R**: Use $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) only with **universal** R and **molar** quantities. Use $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) for air with **mass-based** calculations.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Temperature in Kelvin**: Always convert °C to K before substituting into gas equations: $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Verify with First Law**: Always cross-check by computing $Q - W$ and confirming it equals $\Delta U$. This catches arithmetic errors and earns "method marks" even if the final answer has a slip.
>
> 6. **Enthalpy in Closed System**: Students sometimes think enthalpy is only for open systems. Wrong — enthalpy is **defined** for any system; it's just that it equals heat at constant pressure, making it convenient for open systems.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ⭐ **First Law (Closed System):** $Q - W = \Delta U$ (Sign: Q in, W out)
- ⭐ **First Law (Cyclic):** $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$, and $\Delta U = 0$
- ⭐ **First Law (Open System / Steady Flow):** $h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$
- ⭐ **Enthalpy Definition:** $H = U + PV$ → Differential: $dH = dU + d(PV)$
- ⭐ **Cv Definition:** $C_v = (\partial U / \partial T)_V$ → Used for constant volume processes
- ⭐ **Cp Definition:** $C_p = (\partial H / \partial T)_P$ → Used for constant pressure processes
- ⭐ **Mayer's Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ (always, for ideal gases)
- ⭐ **Specific Heat Ratio:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v$
- ⭐ **Air Specific Heats (memorize):** $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$
- ⭐ **Constant Volume:** $Q = \Delta U = mC_v\Delta T$, $W = 0$
- ⭐ **Constant Pressure:** $Q = \Delta H = mC_p\Delta T$, $W = P\Delta V = mR\Delta T$
- ⭐ **Adiabatic:** $Q = 0$, so $W = -\Delta U$
- ⭐ **Cp > Cv Always:** Because part of heat at constant pressure goes into expansion work
- ⭐ **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K
- ⭐ **Temperature MUST be in Kelvin** for all ideal gas calculations
- ⭐ **Always verify** numerical answers using $Q - W = \Delta U$

topics.title: Second Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Second Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** is a fundamental principle that governs the **direction of natural processes** and the **quality of energy transformations**. Unlike the First Law, which quantifies the conservation of energy, the Second Law establishes that not all energy conversions are equally feasible — it introduces the concept of **energy quality**, **irreversibility**, and **entropy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Second Law of Thermodynamics is the principle that establishes the **direction of energy transfer** and asserts that processes occur in a certain direction, not in the reverse direction spontaneously. It is commonly stated through the **Kelvin–Planck Statement** and the **Clausius Statement**.

### 1.1.1 Two Classical Statements

**1. Kelvin–Planck Statement:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single body (reservoir) and the production of an equivalent amount of work."*

**2. Clausius Statement:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without any external work input."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy (The Spilling Glass):**
> Imagine a glass of water spilling onto a table. The water naturally flows *downward* and spreads out — this is the **forward direction** of nature. We never see the water spontaneously gather itself up and jump back into the glass, even though both states conserve mass and energy (First Law is satisfied!). 
>
> This is the essence of the Second Law: **not everything that conserves energy is allowed by nature**. Nature imposes a one-way "arrow" on processes. The Second Law tells us *which way* the arrow points — and how much "useful work" we can extract as we go.

**Real-World Engineering Example:** A car engine burns fuel to produce work, but the exhaust gases are hot and cannot be fully converted back to fuel + work. The Second Law quantifies this **loss of useful energy**.

## 1.3 Why Is the Second Law Necessary?

The First Law alone cannot tell us:

| Scenario | First Law | Reality (Second Law) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Heat flowing from cold to hot | Allowed (conserves energy) | **Forbidden** (Clausius) |
| Heat engine with 100% efficiency | Allowed (energy in = work out) | **Impossible** (Kelvin–Planck) |
| Perpetual motion machine | Allowed by energy balance | **Impossible** (violates 2nd Law) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant (highlighted):**
> - The **universal gas constant** $R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
> - **Absolute zero**: $0 \, \text{K} = -273.15 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure**: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
> - The **Carnot (maximum) efficiency** depends *only* on the absolute temperatures of the reservoirs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Engine and Refrigerator — The Core Devices

### 2.1.1 Heat Engine
A **heat engine** operates in a cycle, extracting heat $Q_H$ from a **high-temperature reservoir** (at $T_H$), producing net work $W_{net}$, and rejecting heat $Q_L$ to a **low-temperature reservoir** (at $T_L$).

### 2.1.2 Refrigerator / Heat Pump
A **refrigerator** (or heat pump) operates in a cycle, extracting heat $Q_L$ from a **low-temperature reservoir**, with work input $W_{net}$, and rejecting heat $Q_H$ to a **high-temperature reservoir**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Second Law

- **Step 1:** The First Law (energy conservation) is satisfied in *all* possible processes.
- **Step 2:** However, not all such processes actually occur in nature.
- **Step 3:** The Second Law introduces **directionality** — the "arrow of time" for thermal processes.
- **Step 4:** This directionality is mathematically captured by the **entropy function** $S$.
- **Step 5:** For an **isolated system**, entropy can only *increase* or remain *constant* — never *decrease*.
- **Step 6:** For a **reversible process** in an isolated system, $\Delta S_{iso} = 0$. For an **irreversible process**, $\Delta S_{iso} > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Second Law so important for engineers?**
> It sets the **theoretical upper limit** on the efficiency of every heat engine, refrigerator, and power plant. Without it, we would perpetually chase 100% efficiency, which is **physically impossible**.

## 2.3 Equivalence of the Two Statements

The **Kelvin–Planck** and **Clausius** statements are **logically equivalent**. A violation of one implies a violation of the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof Sketch (often asked in KTU exams):**
> Assume a Clausius-violating refrigerator exists. Couple it with a regular heat engine. The combined system would violate Kelvin–Planck. Therefore, the two statements are equivalent.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency of Heat Engine | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 2 | Coefficient of Performance — Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{Q_H/Q_L - 1}$ | Dimensionless, $COP_R > 1$ typically |
| 3 | Coefficient of Performance — Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - Q_L/Q_H}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Carnot (Maximum) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | $T$ must be in **Kelvin** |
| 5 | Carnot COP — Refrigerator | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum possible COP |
| 6 | Carnot COP — Heat Pump | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum possible COP |
| 7 | Entropy Definition (Thermodynamic) | $dS = \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$ | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 8 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ | Equality for reversible cycle |
| 9 | Entropy Change — Ideal Gas | $\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ |
| 10 | Entropy Generation (Isolated System) | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{iso} \ge 0$ | $\ge 0$ always |
| 11 | Availability / Exergy | $\phi = (u - u_0) + P_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| 12 | Reversible Work | $W_{rev} = -\Delta \phi$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| 13 | Irreversibility | $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = T_0 \, S_{gen} \ge 0$ | $\text{kJ}$ |
| 14 | Entropy of a Reservoir at $T$ | $\Delta S = \dfrac{Q}{T}$ | Constant $T$ reservoir |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert Celsius to **Kelvin** when applying Carnot efficiency. The temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$ MUST be in Kelvin. Using Celsius will give a wrong (and physically meaningless) answer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Determines maximum efficiency of steam, gas, and nuclear power plants.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Establishes the minimum work required to maintain cold storage.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Quantifies why real engines always fall short of ideal performance.
- **Cryogenics:** Guides design of systems approaching absolute zero.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Governs exergy analysis and energy optimization.
- **Renewable Energy:** Sets fundamental limits on solar thermal, geothermal, and ocean thermal systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carnot Efficiency from First and Second Laws

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a **Carnot cycle** consisting of two **isothermal** processes (at $T_H$ and $T_L$) and two **adiabatic** (isentropic) processes. Let the working fluid be an ideal gas.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the **First Law** to the complete cycle. Since the system returns to its initial state, the change in internal energy over a cycle is zero:

$$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

By the First Law for a closed cycle: $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.

**Step 2:** Write the net heat transfer. Heat is absorbed only during the high-temperature isotherm ($Q_H$) and rejected during the low-temperature isotherm ($Q_L$). The adiabatic processes exchange no heat.

$$Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the First Law expression for the cycle:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 4:** Apply the definition of thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the expression:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 6:** Now apply the **Second Law (Clausius equality)** to the reversible Carnot cycle:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$

Breaking this into the two isotherms (the adiabatics contribute zero):

$$\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the heat ratio:

$$\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute this result into the efficiency expression from Step 5:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 9:** Therefore, the **Carnot efficiency** — the maximum possible efficiency for any heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs — is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The Carnot efficiency depends **only** on the absolute temperatures of the two reservoirs. It is **independent of the working fluid**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clausius Inequality from the Second Law

### 3.2.1 Setup
Consider an **irreversible cycle** that exchanges heat $\delta Q$ with a reservoir at temperature $T_R$. We wish to show that:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_R} \le 0$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Introduce a **reversible heat engine** that operates between the reservoir at $T_R$ and a reference reservoir at $T_0$.

**Step 2:** Combine the irreversible cycle and the reversible heat engine so that the irreversible cycle rejects heat $\delta Q$ to the reservoir at $T_R$, and the reversible engine absorbs the same heat.

**Step 3:** The combined system exchanges heat only with the single reservoir at $T_0$.

**Step 4:** By the **Kelvin–Planck statement**, this combined device cannot produce net work:

$$W_{combined} \le 0$$

**Step 5:** For the irreversible cycle alone, the net work is $W_{irr}$. For the reversible engine, $W_{rev} = \eta_{Carnot} \, Q_0$.

**Step 6:** Combining and simplifying using the Carnot relation:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_R} \le 0$$

**Step 7:** Equivalently, for the working-fluid temperature $T$:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

**Step 8:** Equality holds for **reversible** cycles; strict inequality holds for **irreversible** cycles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas

### 3.3.1 Setup
For a closed system undergoing a reversible process:

$$dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

For an ideal gas, the First Law gives:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + \delta W_{rev} = m c_v \, dT + P \, dV$$

**Step 1:** From the ideal gas law: $PV = mRT$, so $P = \dfrac{mRT}{V}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$dS = \dfrac{m c_v \, dT + \dfrac{mRT}{V} \, dV}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Separate into two integrals:

$$dS = m c_v \, \dfrac{dT}{T} + mR \, \dfrac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from state 1 to state 2:

$$\Delta S = m c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + mR \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$$

**Step 5:** Convert volumes to pressures using $V = mRT/P$:

$$\ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$$

**Step 6:** Use $c_p = c_v + R$ to obtain the alternative form:

$$\boxed{\Delta S = m \left[ c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right]}$$

**Step 7:** Using specific quantities (per unit mass):

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reversible vs. Irreversible Processes

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Direction | Both forward and reverse possible | Only one direction (spontaneous) |
| $\Delta S$ (isolated system) | $\Delta S_{iso} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{iso} > 0$ |
| Work output | Maximum | Less than maximum |
| Equilibrium state | System always in equilibrium | System passes through non-equilibrium states |
| Friction / Unrestrained expansion | Absent | Present |
| Heat transfer with finite $\Delta T$ | Quasi-static (infinitesimal $\Delta T$) | Finite temperature difference |
| Mixing of different substances | No spontaneous mixing | Spontaneous mixing |
| Real-world example | **Frictionless, quasi-static** idealization | All real processes |
| Clausius inequality | Equality ($\oint \delta Q / T = 0$) | Strict inequality ($\oint \delta Q / T < 0$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** No real process is fully reversible. Engineers design systems (e.g., **Carnot cycle, Brayton cycle with regeneration**) to *approach* reversibility and thus minimize entropy generation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. **(1 Mark)**

**Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. **(1 Mark)**

**Relationship:** The two statements are **logically equivalent**. A violation of one implies a violation of the other. They are two different ways of expressing the same physical principle. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Clausius inequality and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** The Clausius inequality states that for any cycle (reversible or irreversible),

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

where $T$ is the **absolute temperature** at the boundary where $\delta Q$ is transferred. **(1 Mark)**

**Equality case:** The equality holds for a **reversible cycle**, and the strict inequality holds for an **irreversible cycle**. **(1 Mark)**

**Significance:** The Clausius inequality forms the mathematical basis for the definition of **entropy**. It establishes entropy as a **property of state** (path-independent) and is used to determine whether a process is reversible, irreversible, or impossible. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

A Carnot heat engine operates between a high-temperature reservoir at **$T_H = 800 \text{ K}$** and a low-temperature reservoir at **$T_L = 300 \text{ K}$**. The engine receives **$Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$** of heat per cycle from the high-temperature reservoir.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the thermal efficiency, the network output per cycle, and the heat rejected to the low-temperature reservoir per cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same engine is now used as a **refrigerator** (i.e., reversed cycle) operating between the same two reservoirs, calculate its **COP** and the heat extracted from the cold reservoir when the same work input is supplied.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Heat Engine Mode [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$, $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$, $Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$

**Step 1:** Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

**Step 2:** Thermal efficiency in %: $\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 3:** Network output per cycle:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** Heat rejected to low-temperature reservoir using the First Law:

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Verification using the temperature ratio:

$$\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} \Rightarrow Q_L = 1000 \times \dfrac{300}{800} = 375 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Final Answers (Part a):** $\eta = 62.5\%$, $W_{net} = 625 \text{ kJ}$, $Q_L = 375 \text{ kJ}$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Refrigerator Mode [7 Marks]**

**Given:** Same reservoirs, $W_{net} = 625 \text{ kJ}$ (reversed direction).

**Step 1:** Carnot COP for refrigerator:

$$COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{300}{800 - 300} = \dfrac{300}{500} = 0.6$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 2:** Heat extracted from the cold reservoir:

$$Q_L = COP_R \cdot W_{net} = 0.6 \times 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 3:** Heat rejected to the hot reservoir:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 375 + 625 = 1000 \text{ kJ}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** Physical interpretation: Notice that reversing the cycle exchanges the magnitudes of $Q_H$ and $Q_L$ exactly — confirming the reversibility of the Carnot cycle. **(1 Mark)**

**Final Answers (Part b):** $COP_R = 0.6$, $Q_L = 375 \text{ kJ}$ (extracted from cold), $Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$ (rejected to hot).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` *(Alternative Choice)*

A rigid tank contains **$m = 2 \text{ kg}$** of air initially at **$T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$** and **$P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$**. The air is heated at constant volume until the temperature becomes **$T_2 = 600 \text{ K}$**. Assume air is an ideal gas with **$c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$**, **$c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$**, and **$R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the entropy change of the air, the entropy change of the surroundings (assume the tank is in a reservoir at $T_2 = 600 \text{ K}$), and verify the Second Law.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same process is carried out **reversibly** between the same end states, calculate the entropy change of the system, entropy change of the surroundings, and entropy generation.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Irreversible Heating [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $m = 2 \text{ kg}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 600 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $c_v = 0.718$, $c_p = 1.005$, $R = 0.287$ (all in kJ/kg·K or kJ/kg·K).

**Step 1:** Final pressure from ideal gas law at constant volume:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2} \Rightarrow P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 200 \times \dfrac{600}{300} = 400 \text{ kPa}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Entropy change of air (ideal gas, closed system, property change):

$$\Delta S_{air} = m \left[ c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + R \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right]$$

Since $V_2 = V_1$ (rigid tank):

$$\Delta S_{air} = m \, c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 2 \times 0.718 \times \ln \dfrac{600}{300}$$

$$\Delta S_{air} = 2 \times 0.718 \times \ln 2 = 2 \times 0.718 \times 0.6931 = 0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 3:** Heat transferred to the air (constant volume):

$$Q = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300) = 430.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 4:** Entropy change of surroundings (surroundings are at $T_{surr} = T_2 = 600 \text{ K}$, and they *lose* this heat):

$$\Delta S_{surr} = -\dfrac{Q}{T_{surr}} = -\dfrac{430.8}{600} = -0.718 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Total entropy change (isolated system = air + surroundings):

$$\Delta S_{iso} = \Delta S_{air} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.9952 - 0.718 = +0.2772 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 6:** Verification of Second Law: $\Delta S_{iso} = +0.2772 > 0$ $\checkmark$ — process is **irreversible and feasible**. **(1 Mark)**

**Final Answers (Part a):** $\Delta S_{air} = 0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$, $\Delta S_{surr} = -0.718 \text{ kJ/K}$, $\Delta S_{iso} = +0.2772 \text{ kJ/K}$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Reversible Heating [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** The system (air) entropy change is a **property** — it depends only on end states, not on path:

$$\Delta S_{air,rev} = \Delta S_{air} = 0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** For a reversible process, the heat must be transferred through an **infinite series of reservoirs** with temperatures ranging from $T_1$ to $T_2$, or equivalently, the air is in contact with a reservoir at the air's instantaneous temperature.

**Step 3:** Entropy change of the reversible heat source (which is the air receiving heat quasi-statically):

$$\Delta S_{source} = -Q \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{1}{T} dT \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{unit}}$$

Alternatively, using the standard relation:

$$\Delta S_{surr,rev} = -m \, c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = -0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** Total entropy change (isolated system):

$$\Delta S_{iso,rev} = \Delta S_{air} + \Delta S_{surr,rev} = 0.9952 - 0.9952 = 0 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Verification of Second Law: $\Delta S_{iso} = 0$ $\checkmark$ — the process is **reversible** (boundary case). **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6:** Entropy generation: $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{iso} = 0$ for reversible; $S_{gen} = 0.2772 \text{ kJ/K}$ for irreversible. **(1 Mark)**

**Final Answers (Part b):** $\Delta S_{air} = 0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$, $\Delta S_{surr} = -0.9952 \text{ kJ/K}$, $S_{gen} = 0$ (reversible case).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to Kelvin** when applying $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ — the most common mistake, **deduct 2 marks** if committed.
> 2. **Confusing the sign of entropy change** of surroundings — surroundings LOSE heat, so $\Delta S_{surr} = -Q/T_{surr}$ is **negative**.
> 3. **Mixing up heat engine vs. refrigerator COP formulas** — for a refrigerator, $COP_R = Q_L / W$; for a heat pump, $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W$. These are **different** and frequently confused.
> 4. **Forgetting that entropy is a property** — even if a process is irreversible, the entropy change of the system is calculated using a **reversible path** between the same end states.
> 5. **Using the wrong sign for the Clausius inequality** in derivations — $\oint \delta Q / T \le 0$ (NOT $\ge 0$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- The Second Law is a **directional** law, not a conservation law.
- Two equivalent statements: **Kelvin–Planck** (no 100% efficient heat engine) and **Clausius** (no spontaneous cold-to-hot heat transfer).
- **Carnot cycle** gives the **maximum possible efficiency** for any heat engine between two temperatures.

### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas
- $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**).
- $COP_{R,Carnot} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.
- $COP_{HP,Carnot} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$.
- $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$ (ideal gas).
- $\oint \delta Q / T \le 0$ (Clausius inequality).
- $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{iso} \ge 0$.

### ⚠️ Critical Conversion Rules
- **Celsius to Kelvin:** $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
- **Heat engine:** $W = Q_H - Q_L$; **Refrigerator:** $Q_H = Q_L + W$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = W/Q_H \le 1$ — always less than unity for real engines.

### 🎯 KTU-Favored Question Types
1. State the two statements of the Second Law and prove their equivalence. **(7 Marks)**
2. A Carnot engine/refrigerator numerical with two reservoirs. **(7+7 Marks)**
3. Entropy change calculation for ideal gas in a closed system. **(7 Marks)**
4. Verification of the Second Law using $\Delta S_{iso} \ge 0$. **(7 Marks)**
5. Compare reversible and irreversible processes; derive Clausius inequality. **(7 Marks)**

### 🚫 Frequently Lost Marks
- Unit errors (Celsius vs. Kelvin).
- Sign errors in entropy of surroundings.
- Confusing heat engine efficiency with refrigerator COP.
- Skipping the "verification step" of the Second Law in numerical problems.
- Writing the Clausius inequality with the wrong inequality sign.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Reservoir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Reservoir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermal Reservoir** (also called a **Heat Reservoir** or **Thermal Bath**) is defined as a hypothetical body of sufficiently large thermal capacity (heat capacity) that can absorb or reject an unlimited quantity of heat without undergoing any appreciable change in its thermodynamic state — specifically, without any measurable change in its **temperature**.

Mathematically, a thermal reservoir is characterized by the limiting condition:

$$\lim_{C_{th} \to \infty} \Delta T = 0$$

where $C_{th}$ is the thermal heat capacity of the body and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A thermal reservoir is a body to or from which heat can be transferred indefinitely (isothermally) without any change in its own temperature. It is an idealized concept that models large bodies of water (oceans, rivers, lakes), the atmosphere, and large industrial furnaces or condensers.

## 1.2 Classification of Thermal Reservoirs

Thermal reservoirs are broadly classified into **two** fundamental categories based on the direction of heat flow:

### (a) Thermal Source (Hot Reservoir / Heat Source)
A thermal reservoir that **supplies** heat to a system is called a **Thermal Source**. It is maintained at a **higher temperature** $T_H$ relative to the system.

- **Temperature:** $T_H$ = constant (high)
- **Heat Interaction:** $Q_H$ flows **out** of reservoir **into** the system
- **Sign Convention:** $Q_H > 0$ (heat leaving source)

### (b) Thermal Sink (Cold Reservoir / Heat Sink)
A thermal reservoir that **absorbs** heat rejected by a system is called a **Thermal Sink**. It is maintained at a **lower temperature** $T_L$ relative to the system.

- **Temperature:** $T_L$ = constant (low)
- **Heat Interaction:** $Q_L$ flows **from** the system **into** the sink
- **Sign Convention:** $Q_L > 0$ (heat entering sink)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Ocean as a Massive Thermal Bath**
>
> Imagine trying to boil a small cup of water (250 mL) by pouring it into the Arabian Sea. Even though heat is technically transferred, the **temperature of the ocean remains absolutely unchanged** because the ocean's mass and heat capacity are astronomically larger than that of the cup. The ocean thus behaves exactly like a **thermal reservoir** — it absorbs or supplies heat without showing any noticeable temperature variation.
>
> Similarly, the Earth's atmosphere acts as a thermal reservoir for surface heat exchange processes. In engineering systems, **boilers** behave as thermal sources (furnace gases at constant high temperature) and **condensers** behave as thermal sinks (cooling water returning at nearly the same temperature as inlet).

## 1.4 Key Physical Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Properties of an Ideal Thermal Reservoir:**
> 1. **Infinite Heat Capacity:** It can absorb or supply finite amounts of heat with negligible temperature change.
> 2. **Constant Temperature Operation:** The temperature remains spatially uniform and time-invariant during heat transfer.
> 3. **Quasi-Equilibrium Behavior:** The reservoir itself is always in internal thermodynamic equilibrium regardless of heat exchange.
> 4. **No Internal Irreversibilities:** Since temperature is uniform, no finite temperature gradient exists within the reservoir, hence no entropy generation inside it.
> 5. **Externally Controlled Temperature:** The temperature is fixed by the reservoir's surroundings (e.g., ambient atmosphere at ~25°C, geothermal source at constant deep-earth temperature).

---

## 1.5 Common Engineering Examples

| Engineering Application | Type of Reservoir | Typical Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace/Combustion chamber in a power plant | **Source** ($T_H$) | 1500 K – 2500 K |
| Boiler flue gas reservoir | **Source** ($T_H$) | 800 K – 1200 K |
| Cooling tower / river water | **Sink** ($T_L$) | 290 K – 305 K |
| Atmospheric air (ambient) | **Sink** ($T_L$) | 298 K (≈ 25°C) |
| Ocean / deep sea water | **Sink** ($T_L$) | 275 K – 285 K |
| Geothermal hot spring | **Source** ($T_H$) | 350 K – 500 K |
| Condenser in Rankine cycle | **Sink** ($T_L$) | 300 K – 320 K |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation

The concept of a thermal reservoir arises directly from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the limits of finite heat transfer. In any **real** heat transfer process between two finite bodies, both temperatures change continuously until equilibrium is reached. However, when one of the bodies has heat capacity orders of magnitude larger than the other, its temperature change becomes **negligible**, allowing us to idealize it as a thermal reservoir.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Condition for a Reservoir

For a body receiving heat $\delta Q$:

$$\delta Q = m \cdot c_v \cdot dT$$

For a reservoir: as $m \cdot c_v \to \infty$, we must have $dT \to 0$ for any finite $\delta Q$.

$$\boxed{m \cdot c_v \cdot dT = \delta Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lim_{m c_v \to \infty} dT = 0}$$

## 2.2 Heat Interaction with a System

When a system at temperature $T_{sys}$ interacts thermally with a reservoir at $T_{res}$:

### Case 1: System is **Hotter** than Reservoir ($T_{sys} > T_{res}$)
- Heat flows **from** system **to** reservoir.
- Reservoir acts as a **SINK**.

### Case 2: System is **Colder** than Reservoir ($T_{sys} < T_{res}$)
- Heat flows **from** reservoir **to** system.
- Reservoir acts as a **SOURCE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Concept:** The classification of a body as a Source or Sink is **relative** to the interacting system — the same body (e.g., atmospheric air at 25°C) can be a **sink** for a hot engine cylinder at 800°C and a **source** for cold refrigerant at -10°C.

## 2.3 Entropy Change of a Thermal Reservoir

The thermal reservoir plays a central role in the entropy analysis of heat engines and refrigerators. Since a reservoir's temperature remains constant, its entropy change is given by the elegant relation:

$$\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

where:
- $Q_{res}$ = heat transferred **to** the reservoir (positive when absorbed, negative when released)
- $T_{res}$ = absolute temperature of the reservoir (in **Kelvin**, never °C)

### Sign Convention for Entropy:
- Heat absorbed by reservoir: $Q_{res} > 0 \Rightarrow \Delta S_{res} > 0$
- Heat rejected by reservoir: $Q_{res} < 0 \Rightarrow \Delta S_{res} < 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because a reservoir's temperature does not change, its internal irreversibility is **zero**. Hence, reservoirs do **not** themselves generate entropy — they merely **exchange** entropy with systems. The Clausius inequality for a reservoir simplifies to equality: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_{res}} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **all high-yield formulas** for the Thermal Reservoir topic, organized for last-minute KTU exam revision.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m \cdot c_v \cdot dT = \delta Q$ | Heat absorbed by a finite body | J |
| 2 | $\lim_{m c_v \to \infty} dT = 0$ | Defining condition of a reservoir | — |
| 3 | $\Delta S_{res} = \dfrac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$ | Entropy change of thermal reservoir | J/K |
| 4 | $Q_{res} = T_{res} \cdot \Delta S_{res}$ | Heat exchanged with reservoir | J |
| 5 | $T_H = \text{const}$, $T_L = \text{const}$ | Constant temperature reservoir condition | K |
| 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency using reservoir temps | dimensionless |
| 7 | $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Refrigerator COP using reservoir temps | dimensionless |
| 8 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Heat Pump COP using reservoir temps | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$ | Second Law (reservoir + system) | J/K |
| 10 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \dfrac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$ | Total entropy change of universe | J/K |

**Note on notation:** In the table, the symbol `\vert` (or `\mid`) is used for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — never use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal reservoirs are foundational to **all** cyclic heat devices:

1. **Power Generation Cycles (Carnot, Rankine, Brayton):**
   The high-temperature reservoir $T_H$ supplies heat $Q_H$ to the working fluid during the heat-addition process, while the low-temperature reservoir $T_L$ absorbs waste heat $Q_L$ during heat rejection.

2. **Refrigeration and Heat Pump Cycles:**
   The reservoir at $T_L$ acts as the space to be cooled (cold reservoir supplies heat to refrigerant), while $T_H$ is the ambient sink where heat is dumped.

3. **HVAC Systems:**
   Chilled water coils (cold reservoir) absorb heat from building air; cooling towers (warm reservoir) reject condenser heat to atmosphere.

4. **Geothermal and Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC):**
   Deep ocean water (cold reservoir at ~4°C) and warm surface water (hot reservoir at ~25°C) drive a thermodynamic cycle.

5. **Cryogenics and Liquefaction:**
   Liquid nitrogen baths at 77 K serve as thermal reservoirs to cool materials for superconductivity experiments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Entropy of a Thermal Reservoir

### Starting Point — Fundamental Heat-Transfer Relation
For any **reversible** heat transfer process to a body of constant temperature $T$:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = T \cdot dS$$

### Step 1: Apply the Reservoir Condition
Since the reservoir temperature $T_{res}$ does **not** change during the heat exchange:

$$T_{res} = \text{constant}$$

### Step 2: Integrate the Differential Form
For a finite heat transfer $Q_{res}$ from initial state to final state:

$$\int_1^2 \delta Q_{rev} = \int_1^2 T_{res} \cdot dS$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral
Because $T_{res}$ is constant, it can be taken outside the integral:

$$Q_{res} = T_{res} \int_1^2 dS$$

### Step 4: Apply Integration Limits

$$Q_{res} = T_{res} \cdot (S_2 - S_1)$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}}$$

This is the **defining entropy relation** for a thermal reservoir.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carnot Efficiency Using Two Reservoirs

A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$. We derive its efficiency from first principles.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Engine
Applying the First Law to the cyclic engine (no change in internal energy over a cycle):

$$Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$$

Rearranging:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

### Step 2: Entropy Balance on the Engine
For a **reversible** cycle, the net entropy change is zero:

$$\Delta S_{cycle} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

> **Logic Note:** The hot reservoir **loses** heat $-Q_H$, contributing $-Q_H/T_H$. The cold reservoir **gains** heat $+Q_L$, contributing $+Q_L/T_L$. Sum = 0 for a reversible cycle.

### Step 3: Express $Q_L$ in Terms of $Q_H$
Solving the entropy equation for $Q_L$:

$$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

$$Q_L = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Energy Balance

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_H \left(1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}\right)$$

### Step 5: Define Thermal Efficiency
Thermal efficiency is defined as $\eta = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Refrigerator COP Between Two Reservoirs

A refrigerator extracts heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir (at $T_L$) and rejects $Q_H$ to a hot reservoir (at $T_H$), with work input $W$.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Refrigerator
First Law applied to the cyclic refrigerator:

$$W_{in} + Q_L = Q_H$$

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$$

### Step 2: Coefficient of Performance Definition
For a refrigerator, COP is defined as:

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$$

### Step 3: Entropy Balance (Reversible Cycle)
For maximum (Carnot) performance:

$$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

$$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Energy Balance

$$W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)$$

### Step 5: Form the COP

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Source vs. Sink Determination

The following decision matrix helps KTU students correctly classify a thermal body for any given scenario during problem-solving:

| Given Condition | Heat Flow Direction | Reservoir Role | Entropy Sign |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{body} > T_{sys}$ | Body $\to$ System | **Source** ($T_H$) | $\Delta S_{res} < 0$ |
| $T_{body} < T_{sys}$ | System $\to$ Body | **Sink** ($T_L$) | $\Delta S_{res} > 0$ |
| $T_{body} = T_{sys}$ | No flow (equilibrium) | Not a reservoir | $\Delta S_{res} = 0$ |
| Body releases finite $Q$ but $T$ unchanged | Body $\to$ System | **Source** | $\Delta S_{res} < 0$ |
| Body absorbs finite $Q$ but $T$ unchanged | System $\to$ Body | **Sink** | $\Delta S_{res} > 0$ |

## 3.5 Sample Worked Numerical (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800$ K and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300$ K. The engine draws $Q_H = 1000$ kJ from the hot reservoir per cycle. Assuming **reversible** (Carnot) operation, calculate:
> (a) The thermal efficiency.
> (b) The net work output per cycle.
> (c) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir.
> (d) The total entropy change of the universe per cycle.

### Solution:

**(a) Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%}$$

**(b) Net Work Output:**

$$W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 625 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(c) Heat Rejected to Cold Reservoir:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625$$

$$\boxed{Q_L = 375 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Verification using entropy:**
$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{1000}{800} = 1.25 \text{ kJ/K}, \quad \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{375}{300} = 1.25 \text{ kJ/K} \quad \checkmark$$

**(d) Total Entropy Change of Universe:**

For a reversible cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state every cycle, so $\Delta S_{sys} = 0$. The two reservoirs exchange entropy:

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{1000}{800} = -1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

$$\Delta S_L = +\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = +\frac{375}{300} = +1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_L = -1.25 + 1.25$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{univ} = 0 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

This confirms the **Second Law equality** for a reversible (Carnot) cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat Engine Interacting with Two Thermal Reservoirs

The following diagram illustrates the canonical interaction between a cyclic heat engine, a hot reservoir (thermal source), and a cold reservoir (thermal sink). Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`) and labels contain only raw uppercase text — no markdown formatting inside the quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeB -->|"WNET = 625 kJ"| nodeC["WORK OUTPUT SINK"]
    nodeB -->|"QL = 375 kJ"| nodeD["COLD RESERVOIR TL = 300 K"]
    nodeE["SECOND LAW CHECK"] -.->|"dSUNIV = 0 reversible"| nodeB
    nodeA -.->|"dSH = -1.25 kJ/K"| nodeE
    nodeD -.->|"dSL = +1.25 kJ/K"| nodeE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Refrigerator Between Two Reservoirs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["COLD RESERVOIR TL = 263 K"] -->|"QL = 500 kJ"| node2["REFRIGERATOR CYCLE"]
    node3["ELECTRICAL WORK WIN = 100 kJ"] -->|"drives compressor"| node2
    node2 -->|"QH = 600 kJ"| node4["HOT RESERVOIR TH = 315 K"]
    node2 -.->|"COP = QL/WIN = 5.0"| node5["PERFORMANCE METRIC"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: Role of Reservoirs in a Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantBoundary["THERMAL POWER PLANT SYSTEM"]
        nodeF["FURNACE / COMBUSTOR - THERMAL SOURCE - FUEL BURNING AT 1500 K"]
        nodeG["BOILER - HEAT TRANSFER SECTION - WATER TO STEAM"]
        nodeH["TURBINE - WORK EXTRACTION - SHAFT OUTPUT"]
        nodeI["CONDENSER - HEAT REJECTION SECTION"]
        nodeJ["PUMP - WORK INPUT TO FLUID"]
    end
    nodeExt1["ATMOSPHERE TH SINK 300 K"]
    nodeExt2["COOLING TOWER OR RIVER TL SINK 290 K"]
    nodeF -->|"QH supply"| nodeG
    nodeG -->|"steam"| nodeH
    nodeH -->|"WNET shaft"| nodeExtPower["ELECTRICAL GENERATOR"]
    nodeH -->|"exhaust steam"| nodeI
    nodeI -->|"QL rejected"| nodeExt2
    nodeJ -->|"pump work"| nodeG
    nodeExt1 -.->|"ambient reference"| nodeF
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Energy-Flow Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent actual energy transfers (heat $Q$ or work $W$).
> - **Dashed arrows** represent information flow (entropy values, monitoring parameters).
> - **Rectangular blocks** with rounded corners denote thermal reservoirs (sources/sinks).
> - **Central block** denotes the active thermodynamic system or cycle.
> - All reservoir temperatures must be in **Kelvin (K)** for entropy calculations; conversions from Celsius must be: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

## 4.5 Alternative Block Architecture: Single Reservoir — System Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["THERMAL RESERVOIR T = CONSTANT"] -->|"finite dQ reversible"| nodeS["SYSTEM UNDER STUDY"]
    nodeS -->|"dS SYS"| nodeR
    nodeR -->|"dS RES = dQ/T"| nodeCalc["ENTROPY ACCOUNTANT"]
    nodeS -->|"dS SYS"| nodeCalc
    nodeCalc -->|"dSUNIV greater than 0 IRREVERSIBLE"| nodeResult["SECOND LAW VERDICT"]
    nodeCalc -->|"dSUNIV equals 0 REVERSIBLE LIMIT"| nodeResult
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define a thermal reservoir. Differentiate between a thermal source and a thermal sink with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A thermal reservoir is a body of very large heat capacity that can absorb or supply a finite amount of heat without undergoing any appreciable change in its own temperature.

- **Thermal Source:** A reservoir at higher temperature that *supplies* heat to a system. *Example:* Furnace gases in a boiler.
- **Thermal Sink:** A reservoir at lower temperature that *absorbs* heat rejected by a system. *Example:* Cooling water in a condenser.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Derive the expression for entropy change of a thermal reservoir.**

**Model Answer:**

For a reversible heat transfer $\delta Q_{rev}$ to a body at constant temperature $T$:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = T \cdot dS$$

Since $T = T_{res} = \text{constant}$ for a reservoir, integrating:

$$\int_1^2 \delta Q_{rev} = T_{res} \int_1^2 dS$$

$$Q_{res} = T_{res} \cdot (S_2 - S_1)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module-level internal choice provides two alternatives; the student answers **one**. Each choice contains two sub-parts of equal marks (typically 7 + 7).

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**A. (a)** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 900$ K and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300$ K. The engine draws $Q_H = 1500$ kJ of heat from the hot reservoir per cycle.
   **(i)** Determine the thermal efficiency of the engine.
   **(ii)** Calculate the net work output per cycle.
   **(iii)** Calculate the heat rejected to the cold reservoir per cycle.
   **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 66.67\%}$$

**(ii) Net Work Output:**

$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \cdot Q_H = 0.6667 \times 1500$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 1000 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Cold Reservoir:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1500 - 1000$$

$$\boxed{Q_L = 500 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**A. (b)** A reversible refrigerator maintains a cold space at $T_L = -10°C$. It rejects heat to the surroundings at $T_H = 30°C$. If 2000 kJ of heat is extracted from the cold space per hour, determine:
   **(i)** The coefficient of performance (COP) of the refrigerator.
   **(ii)** The power input required to drive the refrigerator (in kW).
   **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Temperature Conversion (mandatory for KTU):**

$$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}, \quad T_H = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

**(i) COP of the Refrigerator:**

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40}$$

$$\boxed{COP_R = 6.575}$$

**(ii) Power Input:**

Using $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$:

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{2000 \text{ kJ/hr}}{6.575}$$

$$W_{in} = 304.18 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Converting to kW (1 kW = 1 kJ/s, so divide by 3600):

$$P_{in} = \frac{304.18}{3600}$$

$$\boxed{P_{in} = 0.0845 \text{ kW} \approx 84.5 \text{ W}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative to Question A

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of a thermal reservoir with a neat sketch. State **four** essential properties of an ideal thermal reservoir.
**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for B(a):

**Definition:**
A thermal reservoir is a hypothetical body with effectively infinite heat capacity that can supply or absorb any amount of heat without undergoing a change in temperature.

**Neat Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the canonical heat-engine-plus-two-reservoirs schematic.)

**Four Essential Properties:**
1. **Infinite Heat Capacity:** Its temperature remains constant for any finite heat exchange — i.e., $C_{th} \to \infty$.
2. **Constant Temperature Operation:** Spatially uniform and time-invariant temperature $T_{res}$.
3. **Always in Internal Equilibrium:** No internal gradients; hence no internal irreversibilities.
4. **No Entropy Generation Inside the Reservoir:** The reservoir merely exchanges entropy with systems, never generates it internally.

Additionally:
5. **Acts as Source or Sink depending on Relative Temperature** with respect to the system.

---

**B. (b)** A thermal power plant rejects $Q_L = 500$ MJ of waste heat every hour to a river acting as a cold reservoir at $T_L = 290$ K. The plant's hot reservoir operates at $T_H = 800$ K.
   **(i)** Calculate the entropy change of the river (cold reservoir) per hour.
   **(ii)** If the actual cycle efficiency is 30%, find the heat input $Q_H$ required from the hot reservoir.
   **(iii)** Calculate the entropy change of the hot reservoir and the **total entropy generation** in the universe.
   **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**(i) Entropy Change of River (Cold Reservoir):**

$$\Delta S_L = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{500 \times 10^6 \text{ J}}{290 \text{ K}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_L = 1.724 \times 10^6 \text{ J/K} = 1724.14 \text{ kJ/K per hour}}$$

**(ii) Heat Input from Hot Reservoir:**

Using $\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ and $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$0.30 = \frac{Q_H - 500}{Q_H}$$

$$0.30 \cdot Q_H = Q_H - 500$$

$$500 = Q_H (1 - 0.30) = 0.70 \cdot Q_H$$

$$\boxed{Q_H = 714.29 \text{ MJ per hour}}$$

**(iii) Entropy Change of Hot Reservoir and Total Entropy Generation:**

Entropy change of hot reservoir (it loses heat, so sign is negative):

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{714.29 \times 10^6}{800}$$

$$\Delta S_H = -892.86 \text{ kJ/K per hour}$$

**Total Entropy Generation in the Universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_L = -892.86 + 1724.14$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{univ} = +831.28 \text{ kJ/K per hour} > 0}$$

> **Interpretation:** $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ confirms that the cycle is **irreversible** (as expected for an actual plant with 30% efficiency, which is less than the Carnot limit of 63.75%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Thermal Reservoir Problems:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** The most common deduction (-2 to -3 marks) is using **Celsius** instead of **Kelvin** in entropy, COP, or efficiency formulas. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. Watch for negative Celsius values like $-10°C = 263 K$.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Mix-up:** Students frequently use $Q_L$ as heat lost by the system but plug it as positive into $\Delta S_{univ}$. Remember: $\Delta S_{res} = Q_{res}/T_{res}$ where $Q_{res}$ is the heat **entering** the reservoir.
>
> 3. **Confusing Source vs. Sink:** A reservoir is a source if it is **hotter than the system**, not merely "hot." The same atmospheric air can be source or sink depending on context.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Unit Consistency in COP:** $COP$ is dimensionless — do not write units. However, the heat values plugged in must be in consistent energy units (kJ with kJ, J with J).
>
> 5. **Not Stating Reservoir Assumption Explicitly:** Always write "Assuming the river/ocean acts as a thermal reservoir, its temperature remains constant at $T_L$" in the solution.
>
> 6. **Mark-Loss in Sketches:** In Part B questions worth 7 marks, a clearly labeled diagram with temperatures marked carries at least **1 mark** in KTU valuation. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Thermal Reservoir (Module 3):**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** A body of infinite heat capacity that absorbs/supplies heat isothermally — $dT \approx 0$.
>
> ☐ **Two Types:** **Source** (hot, supplies $Q_H$) and **Sink** (cold, absorbs $Q_L$); classification is *relative to system*.
>
> ☐ **Engineering Examples:** Furnace, boiler, condenser, cooling tower, atmosphere, ocean, river.
>
> ☐ **Key Formula:** $\Delta S_{res} = Q_{res}/T_{res}$ — applies because $T_{res}$ is constant.
>
> ☐ **No Internal Irreversibility:** Reservoir does not generate entropy internally; only exchanges.
>
> ☐ **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ — maximum possible efficiency between two reservoirs.
>
> ☐ **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_R = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ — *increases* as $T_H - T_L$ decreases.
>
> ☐ **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = T_H/(T_H - T_L) = COP_R + 1$ — always greater than 1.
>
> ☐ **Second Law Check:** $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_H + \Delta S_L \geq 0$; equality holds for reversible Carnot cycle.
>
> ☐ **Always Use Kelvin:** Conversion $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ is mandatory for all reservoir calculations.
>
> ☐ **Sketch in Part B:** Always draw the system-reservoir block diagram with arrows for $Q_H$, $Q_L$, $W_{net}$ clearly labeled — earns easy valuation marks.
>
> ☐ **Reservoir Properties to State:** (1) Infinite $C_{th}$, (2) Constant $T$, (3) Internally reversible, (4) No entropy generation internally, (5) Acts as source or sink depending on relative temperature.
>
> ☐ **Common Pitfall:** Don't confuse "hot reservoir" with "source" — the source must be **hotter than the system**.
>
> ☐ **KTU Favorite Questions:** Carnot engine between two reservoirs, COP calculations, total entropy change of universe with irreversible cycles, identification of source/sink from temperature data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Heat Engine: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heat Engine** is a thermodynamic device or system that converts thermal energy (heat) into mechanical work. It operates by transferring energy from a high-temperature reservoir (source) to a low-temperature reservoir (sink), extracting a portion of this energy as useful work in the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> A heat engine operates on a thermodynamic cycle and exchanges heat with at least two thermal reservoirs. It receives heat $Q_H$ from the high-temperature source, rejects heat $Q_L$ to the low-temperature sink, and delivers net work $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ per cycle.

### Key Terminology
- **Working Substance:** The fluid (gas/vapor) that undergoes cyclic processes to facilitate energy conversion.
- **Source (High-Temperature Reservoir):** Supplies heat $Q_H$ to the engine at temperature $T_H$.
- **Sink (Low-Temperature Reservoir):** Absorbs rejected heat $Q_L$ at temperature $T_L$.
- **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$):** The ratio of net work output to heat input, dimensionless.
- **Power Cycle:** A cycle in which the system delivers net positive work to the surroundings (heat engine).
- **Refrigeration/Heat Pump Cycle:** A cycle in which work is consumed to transfer heat from cold to hot reservoir.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel of Heat

Imagine a **water wheel** in a river. Water flows from a high elevation (source) to a low elevation (sink), and the wheel captures part of this energy difference to grind grain (work output). The rest of the water flows past without being captured (rejected heat).

A heat engine works identically:
- **Heat from hot source** is the "high water level."
- **Work output** is the "grain being ground."
- **Waste heat to sink** is the "water that flows past unused."

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reality:** The energy difference between the hot and cold reservoirs is what drives the engine. Without a temperature difference ($T_H > T_L$), NO heat engine can operate — this is a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius Statement).**

## 1.3 Classification of Heat Engines

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **External Combustion Engine (ECE)** | Combustion occurs outside the working fluid | Steam engine, Stirling engine |
| **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** | Combustion occurs inside the working fluid | Otto (petrol), Diesel, Gas turbine |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** Students often confuse the direction of heat flow. Always remember: **Heat flows spontaneously from HOT to COLD** — the engine does NOT push heat from cold to hot. That would be a refrigerator/heat pump.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Module 3 — Heat Engine: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Three-Stage Energy Flow

A heat engine executes a **closed thermodynamic cycle** returning the working fluid to its initial state. The fundamental energy balance per cycle is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

This breaks down into:

1. **Heat Intake ($Q_H$):** The working fluid absorbs thermal energy from the high-temperature source during the heat addition process.
2. **Work Output ($W_{net}$):** The fluid expands against moving boundaries (e.g., piston), performing useful mechanical work.
3. **Heat Rejection ($Q_L$):** The fluid contracts and releases the remaining thermal energy to the low-temperature sink.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Heat Addition:** The working fluid is brought into thermal contact with the source. Heat $Q_H$ enters the system at temperature $T_H$.
- **Step 2 — Expansion:** The heated gas expands, doing work $W_{out}$ on the surroundings (piston rotates crankshaft, turbine spins).
- **Step 3 — Heat Rejection:** The expanded, low-pressure gas contacts the sink, releasing heat $Q_L$ at temperature $T_L$.
- **Step 4 — Compression:** The gas is compressed back to its original state, requiring work $W_{in}$ to be done ON the system.
- **Step 5 — Net Work:** $W_{net} = W_{out} - W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L$ (closed cycle, $\Delta U = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Must It Be a Cycle?** For continuous operation, the working fluid must return to its initial state so that the process can be repeated. The internal energy change over a complete cycle is zero: $\oint dU = 0$.

## 2.2 Thermal Efficiency — The Performance Metric

The **thermal efficiency** quantifies how effectively a heat engine converts supplied heat into useful work:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

- $W_{net}$ = Net work output per cycle (J)
- $Q_H$ = Heat supplied from the source per cycle (J)
- $Q_L$ = Heat rejected to the sink per cycle (J)

Since $Q_L > 0$ always (Second Law), it follows that **$\eta_{th} < 1$ (or 100%)** for any real heat engine.

## 2.3 The Carnot Heat Engine — The Theoretical Benchmark

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot's Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** No heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs. Furthermore, all reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the SAME efficiency.

### Carnot Cycle — Four Reversible Processes
1. **Isothermal Expansion at $T_H$** (heat $Q_H$ absorbed)
2. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion** (temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$)
3. **Isothermal Compression at $T_L$** (heat $Q_L$ rejected)
4. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression** (temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$)

For a Carnot engine operating between two reservoirs:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

where $T_H$ and $T_L$ are **absolute temperatures (Kelvin)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Temperatures in the Carnot efficiency equation MUST be in **Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius (°C) or Fahrenheit (°F). A common student error is to substitute 300°C and 30°C directly, giving a wrong answer. Convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | First Law energy balance for a cycle | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency (general) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency (KELVIN only) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{Carnot} - 1}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \eta_{Carnot}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Heat Pump) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H$ | Work output from efficiency definition | J |
| 7 | $\dot{W}_{net} = \eta_{th} \times \dot{Q}_H$ | Power output using rate quantities | W (Watts) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Carnot efficiency serves as the **upper bound** benchmark. Real engine efficiencies are always lower. Modern combined-cycle power plants achieve ~62%, while Otto and Diesel engines reach 25–40%. The formula guides design choices on operating temperatures — to increase $\eta_{th}$, one must raise $T_H$ or lower $T_L$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Module 3 — Heat Engine: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Thermal Efficiency from the First Law

We begin with the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system executing a cycle:**

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

Since the system returns to its initial state, the change in internal energy over a complete cycle is zero ($dU = 0$). The heat transferred per cycle and the work done per cycle are therefore equal in magnitude.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the two heat transfer terms.**
Per cycle, the working fluid experiences:
- Heat absorbed from the source: $Q_H$ (positive, in)
- Heat rejected to the sink: $Q_L$ (positive in magnitude, out)

**Step 2 — Write the net heat transfer.**
The net heat input to the system is the difference between heat absorbed and heat rejected:

$$Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3 — Equate net heat to net work (First Law for cycle).**
For a cyclic process, $\Delta U = 0$, so:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 4 — Define thermal efficiency as work-to-heat ratio.**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by dividing both terms by $Q_H$:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

> **Interpretation:** The efficiency is **1 (100%)** only if $Q_L = 0$ (no heat rejected). However, the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius statement) prohibits a cyclic device from producing net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir. Therefore, $Q_L > 0$ always, and $\eta_{th} < 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carnot Efficiency (Reversible Engine)

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the heat transfers during the two isothermal processes are:

$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{(during isothermal expansion)}$$

$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{(during isothermal compression)}$$

> **Why is this so?** For a reversible isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS$. Integrating at constant temperature gives $Q = T \cdot \Delta S$. The entropy change magnitude $\vert \Delta S \vert$ is the same for both isotherms (entropy is a state function and the two adiabatic steps have zero entropy change).

**Step 1 — Apply the general efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the reversible heat expressions:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{T_H \cdot \Delta S}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\Delta S$ (non-zero for a real cycle):**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of limits:**
- If $T_L = 0\,K$ (unattainable per Third Law): $\eta_{Carnot} = 1$ (100%).
- If $T_L = T_H$ (no temperature difference): $\eta_{Carnot} = 0$ (engine cannot run).
- If $T_H = 500\,K$ and $T_L = 300\,K$: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 300/500 = 0.40 = 40\%$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A heat engine operates between a source at $T_H = 800\,K$ and a sink at $T_L = 300\,K$. It receives $Q_H = 1000\,kJ$ of heat per cycle. Assuming the engine operates on the **Carnot cycle**:
- (a) Determine the thermal efficiency.
- (b) Calculate the net work output per cycle.
- (c) Calculate the heat rejected to the sink per cycle.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{300\,K}{800\,K}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$$

**Part (b) — Net Work Output:**

$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H$$

$$W_{net} = 0.625 \times 1000\,kJ$$

$$W_{net} = 625\,kJ \text{ per cycle}$$

**Part (c) — Heat Rejected to Sink:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000\,kJ - 625\,kJ = 375\,kJ$$

**Verification using the ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{800} = 0.375 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L = 0.375 \times 1000 = 375\,kJ \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cycle Comparisons (KTU High-Yield Snapshot)

| Cycle | Heat Addition | Heat Rejection | Typical $\eta_{th}$ |
|-------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Carnot** | Isothermal at $T_H$ | Isothermal at $T_L$ | $1 - T_L/T_H$ (max) |
| **Otto** | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | $1 - 1/r^{k-1}$ |
| **Diesel** | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{k-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^k - 1}{k(r_c - 1)}$ |
| **Brayton** | Constant Pressure | Constant Pressure | $1 - 1/r_p^{(k-1)/k}$ |

> **Key variables:** $r$ = compression ratio, $r_c$ = cut-off ratio, $r_p$ = pressure ratio, $k = c_p/c_v$ (specific heat ratio ≈ 1.4 for air).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Module 3 — Heat Engine: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Heat Engine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: High-Temperature Reservoir at T_H] -->|Heat Q_H| B[Heat Engine: Working Substance]
    B -->|Net Work W_net| C[Surroundings: Mechanical Output]
    B -->|Waste Heat Q_L| D[Sink: Low-Temperature Reservoir at T_L]
    C -->|e.g. Crankshaft Turbine Piston| E[Load: Vehicle Generator Pump]
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#A88,color:#000000
    style C fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#363,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#136,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#C780FA,stroke:#519,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Block Legend:**
- **Source (Red):** Supplies thermal energy — e.g., combustion chamber, nuclear reactor, solar concentrator.
- **Heat Engine (Yellow):** Working substance performs thermodynamic cycle.
- **Work Output (Green):** Net mechanical work delivered per cycle.
- **Sink (Blue):** Ambient environment — atmosphere, river, cooling tower.
- **Load (Purple):** End-use application of mechanical work.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Carnot Cycle on P-V Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Carnot_Cycle_PV_Diagram["P-V Diagram Representation of the Carnot Cycle"]
        direction TB
        P1["Process 1-2: Isothermal Expansion at T_H, Heat Q_H IN"] --> P2
        P2["Process 2-3: Adiabatic Reversible Expansion, T drops T_H to T_L"] --> P3
        P3["Process 3-4: Isothermal Compression at T_L, Heat Q_L OUT"] --> P4
        P4["Process 4-1: Adiabatic Reversible Compression, T rises T_L to T_H"] --> P1
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#A88,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#363,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#136,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#000
```

**Process Explanation:**
- **1 → 2 (Isothermal Expansion):** Volume increases at constant $T_H$; gas absorbs heat $Q_H$ from source.
- **2 → 3 (Adiabatic Expansion):** Volume increases, no heat transfer; temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **3 → 4 (Isothermal Compression):** Volume decreases at constant $T_L$; gas rejects heat $Q_L$ to sink.
- **4 → 1 (Adiabatic Compression):** Volume decreases, no heat transfer; temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$, closing the cycle.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Energy Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Q_H : Heat Input from Source"]:::energy
    Q1 --> Engine["Heat Engine Boundary"]:::engine
    Engine -->|"W_net = Q_H - Q_L"| W1["Useful Work Output"]:::work
    Engine -->|"Q_L : Waste Heat"| Q2["Heat Rejected to Sink"]:::sink
    Engine -->|"Internal Energy Cycle ΔU = 0"| Cycle["Cyclical Process Returns to Initial State"]:::cycle
    Cycle --> Engine
    classDef energy fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#FFF
    classDef work fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#363,color:#FFF
    classDef sink fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#136,color:#FFF
    classDef engine fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#A88,color:#000
    classDef cycle fill:#C780FA,stroke:#519,color:#FFF
```

> **Interpretation:** The internal energy of the working fluid over a complete cycle is zero (state function). Hence, by the First Law, all the net heat input must emerge as net work output plus waste heat. This is the foundational energy balance of every heat engine.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Module 3 — Heat Engine: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define a heat engine and state the Second Law limitation on its thermal efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

A heat engine is a cyclic device that converts heat energy into mechanical work by operating between a high-temperature source and a low-temperature sink. Per cycle, it absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the source at $T_H$, rejects heat $Q_L$ to the sink at $T_L$, and produces net work $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$.

The thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Second Law Limitation (Kelvin-Planck Statement):** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work. Hence, $Q_L > 0$ always, and therefore $\eta_{th} < 1$ (100%). The maximum possible efficiency between two reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$ is the Carnot efficiency: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Explain why temperatures in the Carnot efficiency formula must be in Kelvin.**

**Model Answer:**

The Carnot efficiency is derived from the entropy change of a reversible isothermal process. The relationship $Q = T \cdot \Delta S$ requires $T$ to be an **absolute temperature** because entropy is defined using absolute temperature in the relation $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$.

Only on the absolute (Kelvin) scale does the Second Law take its simplest mathematical form — the Clausius inequality $\oint \delta Q/T \leq 0$ — and only at $T = 0\,K$ does the Carnot efficiency become 100% (the unattainable limit). On the Celsius scale, the zero point is arbitrary (e.g., ice point at 0°C = 273.15 K), so substituting Celsius values would yield a physically meaningless result. Therefore, **always convert to Kelvin** using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ before applying $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Carnot heat engine receives $500\,kJ$ of heat per cycle from a source at $900\,K$ and rejects heat to a sink at $300\,K$. Determine: (i) thermal efficiency, (ii) net work output per cycle, (iii) heat rejected per cycle. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal and two adiabatic reversible processes.**

**Step 2 — During isothermal expansion at $T_H$, the heat absorbed is:**

$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$$
