Consider an arbitrary closed cycle 1-2-1 exchanging differential heat $\delta Q$ with surroundings at temperature $T$ at each point. Let $\delta Q$ be **positive when entering** the system.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Auxiliary Engine Network:**
Imagine a set of reversible Carnot engines, each operating between the surroundings at $T$ and a single common reservoir at $T_0$. Each engine absorbs $\delta Q$ from the surroundings at $T$ and rejects $\delta Q_0$ to the reservoir at $T_0$. By Carnot's reversibility:
$$\frac{\delta Q_0}{T_0} = \frac{\delta Q}{T} \implies \delta Q_0 = \delta Q \cdot \frac{T_0}{T}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Composite System:**
Combine the original system with all auxiliary engines. Over one cycle: original system $\to$ $\Delta U = 0$; each auxiliary engine $\to$ closed cycle, $\Delta U = 0$. So composite $\Delta U = 0$.

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Second Law Application:**
By the First Law for composite: $W_{comp} = Q_{comp} = \sum \delta Q - \sum \delta Q_0$. The composite system exchanges heat **only** with the reservoir at $T_0$. By the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law, $W_{comp} \leq 0$:
$$\sum \delta Q - \sum \delta Q_0 \leq 0 \implies \sum \delta Q \leq \sum \delta Q_0$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Final Inequality:**
Substituting $\delta Q_0$ and converting sum to integral:
$$\oint \delta Q \leq \oint \delta Q \cdot \frac{T_0}{T}$$
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

Equality holds only if all processes are reversible (i.e., no net work required by auxiliary engines).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Identify data:**
- $Q_H = +5000$ kJ at $T_H = 800$ K
- $Q_{loss} = -800$ kJ at $T_{loss} = 350$ K
- $Q_L = -3000$ kJ at $T_L = 300$ K

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — First Law Check:**
$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_{loss} - Q_L = 5000 - 800 - 3000 = 1200 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Compute Clausius Integral:**
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{5000}{800} + \frac{-800}{350} + \frac{-3000}{300}$$
$$= 6.250 - 2.286 - 10.000 = -6.036 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Verify Inequality:**
Since $-6.036 < 0$, the Clausius Inequality is **satisfied**. The cycle is **irreversible**.

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Entropy Generation:**
For a closed cycle, $\Delta S_{sys} = 0$. The entropy generation equals the negative of the Clausius integral:
$$S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -(-6.036) = +6.036 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $S_{gen} > 0$, the cycle is **physically realizable** and **irreversible** (consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 SET B]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Starting from the Clausius Inequality, prove that entropy is a thermodynamic property (state function) of a system. Define entropy generation and discuss its significance.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A refrigeration cycle extracts 1500 kJ of heat from a cold space at 250 K and rejects it to the surroundings at 320 K. The compressor consumes 800 kJ of work. Additionally, 100 kJ of heat leaks into the cold space from the surroundings at 320 K. Compute the Clausius Integral and state whether the cycle obeys the Second Law. Find the entropy generation of the universe per cycle.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Entropy as State Function (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Equality Condition:**
For a reversible cycle 1-A-2-B-1, the Clausius Inequality becomes equality:
$$\oint_{rev} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \int_{1A2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} + \int_{2B1} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Path Independence:**
Rearranging:
$$\int_{1A2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = -\int_{2B1} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \int_{1B2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

The integral between states 1 and 2 is **the same regardless of path** (A or B). Therefore, it depends only on the end states — defining a **state function**.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Definition and Significance:**
We define entropy $S$ such that:
$$S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} \quad \text{or} \quad dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$

**Entropy generation** $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{total} - \int \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \geq 0$. Its significance:
- Quantifies the **degree of irreversibility** in a process.
- Used in **exergy analysis** to compute lost work: $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$.
- Forms the basis for the **Gouy-Stodola theorem** in power plant design.
- **Always non-negative** by the Second Law; zero only for reversible processes.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Identify data:**
- Heat extracted from cold space: $Q_{cold} = +1500$ kJ at $T_{cold} = 250$ K (heat entering the system)
- Heat rejected to surroundings: $Q_{hot} = -(1500 + 800) = -2300$ kJ at $T_{hot} = 320$ K
- Heat leakage: $Q_{leak} = -100$ kJ at $T_{leak} = 320$ K (heat entering the cold space, but exiting the refrigeration system boundary back to surroundings)

Wait — re-interpreting: The 100 kJ leaks from surroundings at 320 K **into the cold space**. From the perspective of the refrigeration system cycle, this is a heat input at 320 K:
- $Q_{in,leak} = +100$ kJ at $T = 320$ K (entering the system boundary somewhere)

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Net heat rejected:**
$$Q_{reject,net} = -(1500 + 800 - 100) = -2200 \text{ kJ at } 320 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Compute Clausius Integral:**
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{+1500}{250} + \frac{+100}{320} + \frac{-2200}{320}$$
$$= 6.000 + 0.3125 - 6.875 = -0.5625 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Verify Second Law:**
Since $-0.5625 \leq 0$, the Clausius Inequality is **satisfied**. The cycle obeys the Second Law.

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Entropy Generation:**
$$S_{gen,universe} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -(-0.5625) = +0.5625 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $S_{gen} > 0$, the cycle is **physically realizable** but **irreversible** (the heat leak is the main source of irreversibility).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (1–2 marks lost):** The KTU 2024 scheme requires consistent sign convention. Mark heat *entering* the system as **positive** and heat *leaving* as **negative**. Mixing signs is the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical problems.
> 2. **Missing "Boundary Temperature" Phrase (1 mark lost):** The denominator in the Clausius Integral is $T_{boundary}$, not $T_{system}$. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting to Compute Entropy Generation (1 mark lost):** Many students stop after the inequality sign and never compute $S_{gen}$. KTU expects both verification *and* quantification.
> 4. **Reversible vs. Irreversible Confusion (1 mark lost):** Equality sign $\neq$ "zero work" — it means the cycle is entirely reversible. Be precise.
> 5. **Units Missing (0.5 mark lost per occurrence):** Always write J/K or kJ/K with the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Clausius Inequality**

### Core Concept
- **Statement:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \leq 0$ for any closed cycle.
- **Equality** holds for **completely reversible** cycles; **strict inequality** for cycles with **any irreversibility**.
- The quantity $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}$ is the **Clausius Integral** (or **Clausius Summation**).

### Key Sign Conventions
- Heat **entering** the system $\to$ **positive** $Q$.
- Heat **leaving** the system $\to$ **negative** $Q$.
- $T$ in the denominator is **always the boundary temperature** (in Kelvin).

### Connections to Other Concepts
- **Reversible equality** $\implies$ defines entropy: $dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$.
- **Closed cycle** $\implies$ $\oint dS = 0 \implies$ entropy is a state function.
- **Entropy generation**: $S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \geq 0$.
- **Isolated systems**: $\Delta S \geq 0$ (the **entropy principle**).
- **Combined 1st + 2nd Law**: $T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$ (reversible form).

### Numerical Problem Checklist
1. ✅ List all heat transfers with their **reservoir (boundary) temperatures**.
2. ✅ Apply **First Law** to check energy balance: $\sum Q = W_{net}$.
3. ✅ Compute the **Clausius Integral** by summing $\frac{Q_i}{T_i}$ over all reservoirs.
4. ✅ Check the **sign**: zero (reversible), negative (irreversible and feasible), positive (impossible — violates Second Law).
5. ✅ Compute **entropy generation**: $S_{gen} = -(\text{Clausius Integral}) \geq 0$.

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Writing $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ for irreversible cycles.
- ❌ Using system temperature instead of boundary temperature.
- ❌ Confusing $\delta Q$ (inexact) with $dQ$ (exact).
- ❌ Forgetting units (J/K or kJ/K).
- ❌ Mixing up heat rejection sign in refrigeration problems.

### KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Aspects
- 🔥 **Derivation** of the inequality using the auxiliary reversible engine network.
- 🔥 **Two-reservoir** form: $\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \leq 0$.
- 🔥 **Numerical verification** with 2 or 3 thermal reservoirs.
- 🔥 **Entropy generation** computation.
- 🔥 **Physical significance** of equality vs. inequality.

### Quick Reference Numbers to Memorize
- **0°C** = **273.15 K**
- **Standard temperature** for exergy analysis: **T₀ = 298.15 K** (25°C)
- **Universal gas constant**: R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy - Entropy Changes in Various Thermodynamic Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Entropy - Entropy Changes in Various Thermodynamic Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Entropy: Entropy Changes in Various Thermodynamic Processes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Entropy

In classical thermodynamics, **entropy (S)** is a state function (or point function) that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder or randomness of a thermodynamic system. It is defined through the **Clausius Theorem** for a quasi-static (reversible) process as:

$$dS = \left( \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \right)_{system}$$

For any real (irreversible) process, the second law imposes the **Clausius Inequality**:

$$dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

where the equality holds only for reversible (internally and externally balanced) processes, and the strict inequality holds for irreversible (spontaneous, finite-gradient) processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Entropy is an *extensive* property (units: **J/K** or **kJ/K**). Specific entropy is *intensive* (units: **J/(kg·K)** or **kJ/(kg·K)**). The standard symbol is **S** (total) and **s** (specific).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Messy Room" Intuition

Imagine your hostel room. In the morning, your room is **neat and organized** (low entropy). By evening, after studying, eating, and living in it, clothes, books, and snacks are scattered everywhere (high entropy). 

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy Decoded:** 
> - A neat room corresponds to a system with **ordered molecular arrangement** (crystalline solid, compressed gas) → **low entropy**.
> - A messy room corresponds to a system with **disordered molecular arrangement** (hot gas, mixture) → **high entropy**.
> - Spontaneous natural processes always proceed from order to disorder (low to high entropy). To restore order, you must **invest energy** (work input). Similarly, to decrease entropy, work must be done on the system.

### 1.3 The Physical Meaning of Entropy

From a microscopic (statistical thermodynamics) viewpoint, entropy is a measure of the number of specific microscopic arrangements (microstates) that are consistent with the macroscopic state of the system:

$$S = k_B \ln \Omega$$

where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant = 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** and $\Omega$ is the number of accessible microstates. This is Boltzmann's entropy equation, etched on his tombstone in Vienna.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Entropy:** 
> 1. Determines the **direction** of a thermodynamic process (Second Law).
> 2. Calculates the **isentropic efficiency** of turbines, compressors, and nozzles.
> 3. Quantifies **irreversibility** and **lost work (exergy destruction)**.
> 4. Governs the performance of **refrigeration cycles, Rankine cycles, and Brayton cycles**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Statements of the Second Law (Entropy Form)

1. **Clausius Statement:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body (entropy of the universe increases for such a process).
2. **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a reservoir and converting it entirely to work.

Both statements are mathematically captured by:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 \quad \text{(for any cyclic process)}$$

### 2.2 Tds Equations (The Thermodynamic Master Relations)

Starting from the First Law for a closed, simple compressible system:

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W_{rev} = dU + P\,dV$$

Substituting $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$:

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV \quad \text{(1st Tds relation)}$$

Since $H = U + PV$, we get $dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$. Substituting in (1):

$$T\,dS = dH - V\,dP \quad \text{(2nd Tds relation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Tds relations matter:** They are the **bridge** between entropy, internal energy, enthalpy, and the equation of state. Every entropy-change formula in the cheat sheet below is derived from these two master equations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Process / Quantity | Entropy Change Formula (Specific) | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reversible heat transfer | $ds = \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}$ | Any reversible process |
| 2 | Isothermal (Ideal Gas) | $\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = -R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | T = const, ideal gas |
| 3 | Constant Volume (Isochoric) | $\Delta s = C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | v = const |
| 4 | Constant Pressure (Isobaric) | $\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | P = const |
| 5 | Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) | $\Delta s = 0$ | $\delta q = 0$, reversible |
| 6 | Polytropic ($Pv^n =$ const) | $\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | General ideal gas |
| 7 | Free Expansion (Joule) | $\Delta s = mR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$ | Adiabatic, w = 0, ideal gas |
| 8 | Adiabatic Irreversible | $\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) > 0$ | $\delta q = 0$, irreversible |
| 9 | Incompressible Substance | $\Delta s = C \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | C = C_p ≈ C_v |
| 10 | Clausius Inequality | $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ | All real processes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** For mass-specific entropy, use **kJ/(kg·K)** and multiply by mass *m* (kg) to get total entropy *S* in **kJ/K**.

### 2.4 The Principle of Entropy Increase

For an **isolated system** ($\delta Q = 0$, no mass transfer):

$$dS_{isolated} \geq 0$$

This is the **Principle of Entropy Increase** — the mathematical statement that an isolated system always evolves toward a state of maximum entropy (thermodynamic equilibrium).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Concept:** "Entropy of the universe tends to a maximum." This is the basis for identifying the **direction** of any spontaneous process and is heavily tested in the descriptive portions of the university exam.

### 2.5 Isentropic Process — The Ideal Benchmark

An **isentropic process** is both adiabatic ($\delta Q = 0$) AND reversible. For an ideal gas undergoing an isentropic process:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{v_1}{v_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the **specific heat ratio**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Entropy Change for an Ideal Gas (General Expression)

We start from the **first Tds relation**:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:

$$du = C_v\,dT$$

The ideal gas equation of state gives:

$$P\,dv = R\,dT - v\,dP \quad \text{(differential form of } Pv = RT\text{)}$$

Substituting both into the Tds relation:

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + (R\,dT - v\,dP)$$

$$T\,ds = (C_v + R)\,dT - v\,dP$$

Using **Mayer's relation**: $C_p = C_v + R$, we get:

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - v\,dP$$

This is the **second Tds relation** for an ideal gas, written per unit mass.

#### Integrating for specific entropy change:

$$ds = C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P}$$

$$s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation A is the most important formula for KTU exams.** Any ideal-gas entropy change can be computed from it. Note the units of $C_p$ and $R$ must match (e.g., both in kJ/kg·K).

#### Equivalent forms using the equation of state:

From $Pv = RT$, we can derive the companion expression:

$$s_2 - s_1 = C_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

This is obtained by substituting the first Tds relation $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + P\,dv = C_v\,dT + RT\,\frac{dv}{v}$.

#### Cross-verification via the isentropic case:

For an isentropic process ($\Delta s = 0$), Equation A yields:

$$0 = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{R}{C_p} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

Exponentiating and using $\gamma = C_p / C_v$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{R/C_p} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

This is the well-known isentropic relation — confirming our derivation is consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Change in Constant-Volume Process (Isochoric)

Starting from the first Tds relation:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$

At constant volume, $dv = 0$, so:

$$T\,ds = du = C_v\,dT$$

Therefore:

$$ds = C_v \frac{dT}{T}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\boxed{\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = C_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \quad \text{[kJ/kg·K]}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Heat added at constant volume increases internal energy and temperature, which in turn increases molecular agitation and hence entropy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Entropy Change in Constant-Pressure Process (Isobaric)

Starting from the second Tds relation:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

At constant pressure, $dP = 0$, so:

$$T\,ds = dh = C_p\,dT$$

Therefore:

$$ds = C_p \frac{dT}{T}$$

Integrating:

$$\boxed{\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \quad \text{[kJ/kg·K]}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At constant pressure, heat input increases enthalpy (sensible heating). Since $C_p > C_v$ (Mayer's relation), for the same temperature change, the entropy rise at constant pressure is **greater** than at constant volume.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Entropy Change in Isothermal Process (Ideal Gas)

For an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process, $T_2 = T_1$, hence from Equation B:

$$\Delta s = C_v \ln(1) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = -R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{[kJ/kg·K]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Note that $R = C_p - C_v$. So for an isothermal process with an ideal gas, the entropy change is **non-zero** even though $\Delta T = 0$. This is because the *spatial* distribution of molecules changes (volume changes), increasing the number of accessible microstates.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Entropy Change in an Adiabatic Irreversible Process

Consider a gas in a rigid, insulated container that is divided by a partition. One side has gas at high pressure $P_1$; the other side is vacuum. When the partition is broken, the gas undergoes **free expansion** to fill the entire volume.

**Step 1 — Apply First Law to the system:**

The container is insulated, so $\delta Q = 0$. The gas expands into a vacuum, so it does no boundary work ($W = 0$). By the first law:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W = 0 - 0 = 0$$

For an ideal gas, $dU = C_v\,dT$, so:

$$dT = 0 \implies T_2 = T_1$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the entropy change:**

Since entropy is a *state function*, the path is irrelevant. We can imagine a fictitious **reversible isothermal** path between the same end states:

$$\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) > 0 \quad \text{(since } v_2 > v_1\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Entropy of the universe:**

$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} = mR \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) - 0 > 0$$

This **positive entropy generation** confirms the irreversibility of free expansion, in agreement with the Second Law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Even though $\delta Q = 0$ along the actual (irreversible) path, the entropy of the gas *increases*! This is the central paradox that distinguishes entropy from heat. Entropy can increase without heat transfer — it is a measure of internal disorder, not energy content.

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Entropy Generation in Heat Transfer Across a Finite Temperature Difference

Consider a hot reservoir at $T_H$ transferring an amount of heat $Q$ to a cold reservoir at $T_C$ ($T_H > T_C$) through a finite-conductance wall.

**Entropy change of hot reservoir:**

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q}{T_H}$$

**Entropy change of cold reservoir:**

$$\Delta S_C = +\frac{Q}{T_C}$$

**Total entropy change (universe):**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = -\frac{Q}{T_H} + \frac{Q}{T_C} = Q\left(\frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_H}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{univ} = \frac{Q\,(T_H - T_C)}{T_H T_C} > 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This is the fundamental source of **lost work** in heat engines. The greater the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$, the greater the irreversibility, and the lower the thermal efficiency. This is why **regenerators** and **recuperators** are used in gas turbines and cryogenic systems.

---

### 3.7 Derivation: Isentropic Efficiency (Turbine Example)

The **isentropic efficiency** of a turbine is defined as the ratio of the actual work output to the work output if the process were reversible (isentropic), for the same inlet conditions and same outlet pressure:

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{isentropic}} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2,actual}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

For an ideal gas with constant $C_p$:

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_1 - T_{2,actual}}{T_1 - T_{2s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hotspot:** The "s" subscript denotes the **isentropic exit state**, which lies on a vertical line in the T-s diagram (constant entropy). Always locate the isentropic point first, then compare to the actual point, which lies to the right (higher entropy) for an irreversible adiabatic process.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Tree for Selecting the Correct Entropy Formula

The following block-level functional architecture diagram maps the logical procedure a KTU student should follow to compute $\Delta S$ for a closed system of an ideal gas:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Initial State 1 and Final State 2] --> B{Is the system an ideal gas?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Tabulated Property Tables or ds = C dT/T for incompressible substances]
    B -- Yes --> D{Which property is constant along the process?}
    D -- Temperature T --> E[Use isothermal formula: ds = R ln v2/v1]
    D -- Pressure P --> F[Use isobaric formula: ds = Cp ln T2/T1]
    D -- Volume v --> G[Use isochoric formula: ds = Cv ln T2/T1]
    D -- None constant --> H{Is the process reversible and adiabatic?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Isentropic Process: ds = 0]
    H -- No --> J[Use the general formula: ds = Cp dT/T minus R dP/P]
    E --> K[Compute numerical answer in kJ per kg K]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    C --> K
    K --> L[Check sign: positive if heating or expansion; negative if cooling or compression]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Entropy Generation in an Open System

This functional architecture diagram illustrates the entropy balance for a control volume, a frequently tested KTU concept:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Inlet Stream 1 with entropy m1 s1] --> CV[(Control Volume)]
    IN2[Inlet Stream 2 with entropy m2 s2] --> CV
    Q[Heat Transfer Q across boundary] --> CV
    CV --> OUT1[Outlet Stream 1 with entropy m3 s3]
    CV --> OUT2[Outlet Stream 2 with entropy m4 s4]
    CV --> GEN[Entropy Generation Sgen greater than or equal 0]
    CV --> S2[Net Entropy Change of Universe]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a **steady-flow** control volume, the entropy balance is:
> $$\dot{S}_{gen} = \sum \dot{m}_e s_e - \sum \dot{m}_i s_i - \sum \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k}$$
> For an **adiabatic, reversible** steady flow (e.g., ideal nozzle, ideal turbine), $\dot{S}_{gen} = 0$ and the process is **isentropic**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: T-s Diagram Regions and Their Physical Meaning

The temperature-entropy (T-s) diagram is the *most important* diagram for entropy analysis in KTU exams. Below is a conceptual map of its key features:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TS[T-s Diagram for an Ideal Gas] --> R1[Reversible Adiabatic = Vertical Line: ds equals 0]
    TS --> R2[Reversible Isothermal = Horizontal Line: dT equals 0]
    TS --> R3[Reversible Isobaric = Exponential Curve sloping upward]
    TS --> R4[Reversible Isochoric = Steeper Exponential Curve]
    TS --> R5[Irreversible Adiabatic = Curve displaced to the right of vertical, ds greater than 0]
    TS --> R6[Area under curve equals Heat Transfer Q]
    TS --> R7[Closed loop area equals Net Heat Transfer in Cycle]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Tip:** In the T-s diagram, the area under a reversible process curve is the **heat transfer**:
> $$Q_{rev} = \int_1^2 T\,ds$$
> This is why the T-s diagram is the thermodynamicist's "gold mine" for cycle analysis (Rankine, Brayton, Carnot, Refrigeration).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define entropy. State the Clausius inequality and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

Entropy is a thermodynamic state function defined for a reversible process as:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)$$

The **Clausius inequality** is:

$$dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

The equality holds for reversible processes and the strict inequality holds for irreversible processes. Its physical significance is that it provides a **criterion for the direction** of spontaneous processes — entropy of an isolated system can never decrease. It is a quantitative statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Show that for an isentropic process, the change in entropy is zero. Justify why an isentropic process must be both adiabatic and reversible.

**Model Answer:**

An **isentropic process** is defined as a process with constant entropy, so $\Delta s = 0$. For a reversible process:

$$ds = \frac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}$$

If the process is also adiabatic, $\delta q_{rev} = 0$, hence $ds = 0$. Conversely, if $ds = 0$ along a reversible path, then $\delta q_{rev} = T\,ds = 0$, meaning no heat is transferred. Thus, the process must be both reversible and adiabatic to be isentropic. If it were adiabatic but irreversible (e.g., free expansion), entropy would *increase* despite $\delta q = 0$.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, usually carrying **7 marks each**. Students must answer **only one** of the two alternatives (internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A. (a)** [7 Marks] Air (ideal gas, $C_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $R$ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K) expands in a piston-cylinder device from 2 bar, 300 K to 0.5 bar, 400 K. Calculate the entropy change of the air. State whether the process is reversible, irreversible, or impossible.

**A. (b)** [7 Marks] 0.5 kg of an ideal gas with $C_v$ = 0.718 kJ/kg·K undergoes an isothermal expansion from 1 m³ to 3 m³ at 400 K. Determine (i) the entropy change of the gas, (ii) the entropy change of the surroundings if the gas receives 500 kJ of heat, and (iii) the total entropy change of the universe. Is the process possible?

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Solution to A(a):

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 2$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 0.5$ bar, $T_2 = 400$ K
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**Formula (per unit mass):**

$$\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Substitution:**

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{400}{300}\right) - 0.287 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{2}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \times \ln(1.3333) - 0.287 \times \ln(0.25)$$

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \times 0.28768 - 0.287 \times (-1.3863)$$

$$\Delta s = 0.2891 + 0.3979$$

$$\Delta s = 0.6870 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\Delta s > 0$ and the gas has expanded, the process is **irreversible** (and certainly possible, consistent with the Second Law).

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- $m = 0.5$ kg, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $T = 400$ K
- $v_1 = 1$ m³, $v_2 = 3$ m³
- $Q = 500$ kJ

**Compute $R$ using Mayer's relation** (assume $C_p = C_v + R$ is not given; we use ideal gas table or assume the gas is air-like):

For a typical ideal gas (diatomic): $C_p = 0.718 + 0.287 = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. So $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step (i) — Entropy change of the gas (isothermal):**

$$\Delta S_{gas} = mR \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = 0.5 \times 0.287 \times \ln(3)$$

$$\Delta S_{gas} = 0.1435 \times 1.0986 = 0.1576 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step (ii) — Entropy change of the surroundings:**

The surroundings lose heat $Q = 500$ kJ at a temperature (assumed) $T_{surr} = 400$ K (since the gas is in contact with a reservoir at the same temperature for isothermal condition):

$$\Delta S_{surr} = -\frac{Q}{T_{surr}} = -\frac{500}{400} = -1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step (iii) — Total entropy change of the universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{gas} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.1576 - 1.25 = -1.0924 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Conclusion:** $\Delta S_{univ} < 0$, which **violates** the Second Law. Therefore, the process is **impossible** as described. The heat transfer of 500 kJ is inconsistent with the isothermal expansion at 400 K from 1 m³ to 3 m³.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B. (a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Tds relations for a closed, simple compressible system. Using them, show that the entropy change of an ideal gas between two states is given by:

$$\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**B. (b)** [7 Marks] A reversible heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at 800 K and a cold reservoir at 300 K, receiving 1000 kJ of heat. Determine the entropy change of each reservoir, the total entropy change of the universe, and the maximum work output.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Solution to B(a):

**Derivation of Tds relations:**

The first law for a closed system undergoing a reversible process:

$$\delta q_{rev} = du + \delta w_{rev} = du + P\,dv$$

For a reversible process, $\delta q_{rev} = T\,ds$. Substituting:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{...(1)}$$

This is the **first Tds relation**.

Since enthalpy $h = u + Pv$, differentiating: $dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dP$. Substituting $du + P\,dv = dh - v\,dP$ into equation (1):

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \quad \text{...(2)}$$

This is the **second Tds relation**.

**Derivation of entropy change for ideal gas:**

For an ideal gas: $du = C_v\,dT$ and $dh = C_p\,dT$. The equation of state $Pv = RT$ in differential form gives:

$$P\,dv + v\,dP = R\,dT$$

From equation (1):

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + P\,dv$$

Substitute $P\,dv = R\,dT - v\,dP$:

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + R\,dT - v\,dP = (C_v + R)\,dT - v\,dP = C_p\,dT - v\,dP$$

Dividing by $T$ and using $v/T = R/P$:

$$ds = C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\boxed{\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

This is the required result.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $T_H = 800$ K, $T_C = 300$ K, $Q_H = 1000$ kJ

**Step 1 — Carnot efficiency (maximum):**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 0.625$$

**Step 2 — Maximum work output:**

$$W_{max} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected to cold reservoir:**

$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{max} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of hot reservoir:**

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{1000}{800} = -1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 5 — Entropy change of cold reservoir:**

$$\Delta S_C = +\frac{Q_C}{T_C} = +\frac{375}{300} = +1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 6 — Total entropy change of the universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_C = -1.25 + 1.25 = 0 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Conclusion:** For a **reversible** (Carnot) heat engine, $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$, confirming maximum work output of 625 kJ.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperatures to Kelvin** in logarithmic terms: $\ln(T_2/T_1)$ is dimensionless, but the *ratio* is dimensionless only if both are in the *same unit* (K or °C). Always use Kelvin.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_v$**: Isochoric uses $C_v$, isobaric uses $C_p$. Using the wrong one is a **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Ignoring the sign of $\Delta S_{univ}$**: A negative $\Delta S_{univ}$ indicates an impossible process. The Second Law says $\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$.
> 4. **Conflating isentropic with adiabatic**: Isentropic = adiabatic + reversible. Adiabatic alone is not necessarily isentropic.
> 5. **Using $|x|$** in tables: In KTU's answer sheet, write absolute values using $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ — never use single pipes in markdown-rendered table cells.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Entropy (S)** is an **extensive** state function. Specific entropy *s* has units of **kJ/(kg·K)**.
- ☐ **Definition:** $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$. For any real process: $dS \geq \delta Q/T$.
- ☐ **Isentropic process** = reversible + adiabatic. $\Delta s = 0$. On a T-s diagram, it is a **vertical line**.
- ☐ **General ideal-gas formula:** $\Delta s = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1) = C_v \ln(T_2/T_1) + R \ln(v_2/v_1)$.
- ☐ **Isothermal** (ideal gas): $\Delta s = R \ln(v_2/v_1) = -R \ln(P_2/P_1)$. Note: $\Delta T = 0$ but $\Delta s \neq 0$.
- ☐ **Constant volume**: $\Delta s = C_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$.
- ☐ **Constant pressure**: $\Delta s = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$.
- ☐ **Free expansion** is irreversible and adiabatic, but $\Delta s > 0$ because entropy is a state function — we calculate it via a fictitious reversible isothermal path.
- ☐ **Second Law:** $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ for all real processes; equality for reversible, strict inequality for irreversible.
- ☐ **Isentropic efficiency** (turbine): $\eta = (h_1 - h_2)/(h_1 - h_{2s})$; (compressor): $\eta = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_2 - h_1)$.
- ☐ **Entropy of universe = entropy of system + entropy of surroundings**. Always check both for compliance with the Second Law.
- ☐ **Mayer's Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ (for ideal gases).
- ☐ **Tds Master Relations:** $Tds = du + Pdv$ and $Tds = dh - vdP$. These are the *foundation* of all entropy-change derivations.
- ☐ **Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$** generates entropy: $\Delta S_{gen} = Q(T_H - T_C)/(T_H T_C) > 0$.
- ☐ **Remember:** Entropy *generation* $S_{gen} \geq 0$ always. Entropy *transfer* can be positive, negative, or zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Increase of Entropy and Its Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Increase of Entropy and Its Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Principle of Increase of Entropy** is the second-law corollary that governs the direction of natural processes. It is a direct consequence of the **Clausius Inequality** and may be formally stated as:

> **"The entropy of an isolated system during any process (reversible or irreversible) always increases or remains constant; it can never decrease."**

Mathematically, for any process undergone by a system and its surroundings considered together as an **isolated (universe) system**:

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} \;=\; \Delta S_{\text{system}} \;+\; \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} \;\;\geq\;\; 0$$

The equality holds for a **reversible (ideal)** process, while the strict inequality holds for a **spontaneous (irreversible/real)** process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Note:** Examiners expect the precise distinction between *system entropy change* (a property, exact differential) and *entropy transfer with heat* ($dS = \delta Q/T$ only for reversible heat transfer). Conflating these is a common mark-loss area.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a freshly brewed cup of hot coffee (say 90 °C) placed in a quiet, insulated room (25 °C). Energy slowly leaks out as heat from the coffee to the room air.

- The coffee cools down (its molecular disorder, i.e. **entropy**, decreases).
- The room air warms up by an imperceptibly small amount, but the **entropy gained by the room is far greater** than the entropy lost by the coffee.
- **Net effect:** the total (universe) entropy **increases** — coffee molecules stop jiggling so wildly, but the colossal number of air molecules pick up tiny amounts of extra jiggling, and the sum total of microscopic disorder is up.

You will *never* see the reverse happen spontaneously — the coffee getting hotter while the room gets colder. That is the **principle of increase of entropy** in everyday life: the universe has a one-way arrow of "spreading out" of energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants / standards used in this module**
> - Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$
> - Standard reference (dead-state) temperature for availability: $T_0 = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (KTU convention: 300 K for problems)
> - Standard ambient pressure: $p_0 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$

### 1.3 Auxiliary Definitions Required for This Topic

- **Isolated System:** A system across whose boundary neither mass nor energy (heat or work) crosses.
- **Clausius Inequality:** The foundational inequality $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ from which the principle is derived.
- **Entropy Generation (S_gen):** The internal production of entropy due to irreversibilities; always $S_{\text{gen}} \geq 0$.
- **Irreversibility (I):** The loss of work potential due to real (non-ideal) processes; equal to $T_0 \cdot S_{\text{gen}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Foundation: From Clausius to Increase of Entropy

The derivation chain is the heart of this module. The progression is:

1. **Kelvin–Planck & Clausius Statements** of the Second Law.
2. **Carnot's Theorem** — no engine can exceed the reversible (Carnot) efficiency.
3. **Clausius Inequality** — extending Carnot's theorem to any arbitrary cycle.
4. **Principle of Increase of Entropy** — direct corollary for any isolated system.
5. **Applications** — calculating $S_{\text{gen}}$, irreversibility, and maximum useful work.

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

- **Step 1 — Entropy is a property.** For any process, $dS = \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{\text{rev}}$ — *only* along a reversible path. This is the *defining* equation of entropy.
- **Step 2 — Real heat transfer is across finite $\Delta T$.** For a reversible process, the system and surroundings are in *thermal equilibrium*; heat crosses at the *system's own* temperature. For an *irreversible* process, heat crosses across a finite temperature difference.
- **Step 3 — Clausius sum over a cycle.** Over any cyclic process, $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$, with equality iff reversible.
- **Step 4 — Apply to a two-state process within a universe.** Considering initial state $i$ and final state $f$ of an isolated composite, the integral splits into a forward ($i \rightarrow f$) and a reversible-return ($f \rightarrow i$) segment. Algebra yields $S_f - S_i \geq 0$.
- **Step 5 — Entropy balance.** The *entropy change of a system* equals *entropy transferred in with heat/mass* plus *entropy generated within*:
$$dS_{\text{sys}} = \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} + dS_{\text{gen}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta S_{\text{sys}} = \int \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} + S_{\text{gen}}$$
- **Step 6 — Combine system + surroundings = isolated universe.** $S_{\text{gen,univ}} = \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + \Delta S_{\text{surr}} \geq 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:** The principle is the *only* physical law that gives *time a direction*. Newton's laws, Maxwell's equations, and quantum mechanics are all time-symmetric — they permit the movie to run forwards or backwards. Only the entropy principle forbids the reverse of real processes, hence its deep connection with *availability*, *exergy*, and *work-potential destruction*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ | Clausius inequality | Any cycle, reversible or irreversible |
| 2 | $\Delta S_{\text{univ}} = \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + \Delta S_{\text{surr}} \geq 0$ | Principle of increase | Isolated system check for feasibility |
| 3 | $\Delta S_{\text{sys}} = \int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q_{\text{rev}}}{T}$ | Property change | Computing entropy *change* of a substance |
| 4 | $\Delta S_{\text{surr}} = -\dfrac{Q_{\text{sys}}}{T_{\text{surr}}}$ | Entropy change of reservoir | Surroundings acts as a thermal reservoir |
| 5 | $S_{\text{gen}} = \Delta S_{\text{univ}} = \Delta S_{\text{sys}} - \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k}$ | Entropy generation | When system interacts with multiple reservoirs |
| 6 | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{\text{gen}}$ | Irreversibility (= lost work) | Availability / second-law efficiency problems |
| 7 | $W_{\max,\text{closed}} = (U_2 - U_1) - T_0(S_2 - S_1) + p_0(V_2 - V_1)$ | Max. work, closed system | Constrained to $p_0, T_0$ |
| 8 | $w_{\max,\text{open}} = (h_2 - h_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$ | Max. work, steady flow | Per-unit-mass steady-flow availability |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\text{Exergy recovered}}{\text{Exergy supplied}} = 1 - \dfrac{I}{\text{Exergy supplied}}$ | Second-law efficiency | Comparative performance of devices |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta S_{\text{cold,actual}}}{\Delta S_{\text{cold,ideal}}}$ | Heat-exchanger effectiveness (entropy) | Counter-flow heat exchanger analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation trap:** $T$ in the Clausius integral is the **boundary temperature** $T_b$ (at which heat crosses), **not** the system's internal temperature $T_{\text{sys}}$ for an irreversible process. Mixing them up is the most common 3-mark deduction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-plant design:** Quantifies how much *potential work* is destroyed by condenser temperature lift, turbine blade friction, and pressure-drop throttling.
- **Refrigeration and HVAC:** Sets the theoretical minimum compressor work; any real refrigerator exceeds this by an amount equal to $T_0 \cdot S_{\text{gen}}$.
- **Chemical-process industry:** Determines whether a proposed reaction sequence is even *thermodynamically feasible* under given $T_0, p_0$.
- **Nozzle / diffuser / turbine:** Establishes the *isentropic* design ideal and the *second-law efficiency* of real machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Clausius Inequality

**Statement:** For any thermodynamic cycle (reversible or irreversible),

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$$

**Proof sketch (the exact KTU 2024 expected derivation):**

Consider a system undergoing an arbitrary cycle. Imagine a *reversible* auxiliary engine $R$ that exchanges heat with the system at the system's boundary temperature $T_b$ in such a way that together they form a composite system.

Let the system absorb heat $\delta Q$ at boundary temperature $T_b$, and let the auxiliary engine exchange the *same* $\delta Q$ reversibly with a single reservoir at $T_0$ (by the equality of Clausius for a reversible engine, $Q_0 / T_0 = Q / T$).

Combining the system and the auxiliary engine into one composite, the Clausius sum is:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;=\; \oint \dfrac{\delta Q_0}{T_0}$$

If the composite violates the Kelvin–Planck statement (i.e. produces net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir), the second law is broken. Hence:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q_0}{T_0} \leq 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0\,}$$

Equality holds for a completely reversible cycle.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Principle of Increase of Entropy

Take any process taking a system from state $1$ to state $2$. Complete the cycle by a reversible process from $2$ back to $1$. Applying Clausius:

$$\int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;+\; \int_2^1 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{\text{rev}} \;\leq\; 0$$

Recognize the reversible-path integral as the *entropy change*:

$$\int_2^1 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{\text{rev}} \;=\; S_1 - S_2$$

Substitute and rearrange:

$$\int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;\leq\; S_2 - S_1$$

Now apply the entropy balance over the *same* process (entropy in via heat + entropy generated = entropy change):

$$S_2 - S_1 \;=\; \int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;+\; S_{\text{gen}}$$

Subtracting the previous inequality term-by-term:

$$S_{\text{gen}} \;\geq\; 0 \quad\text{and}\quad \boxed{\,\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + \Delta S_{\text{surr}} \geq 0\,}$$

Equality indicates a reversible process; strict inequality indicates an irreversible one. This is the **Principle of Increase of Entropy**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Irreversibility (Lost Work) = $T_0 \cdot S_{\text{gen}}$

Consider a closed system that exchanges heat $Q$ with a single large reservoir at $T_0$ while doing work $W_{\text{actual}}$. The first law gives:

$$W_{\text{actual}} = Q - \Delta U$$

The *maximum* possible work (reversible) would use the same energy balance but with reversible heat transfer at $T_0$:

$$W_{\max} = Q_{\text{rev}} - \Delta U \quad\text{with}\quad \Delta S_{\text{sys}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{rev}}}{T_0}$$

Hence:

$$W_{\max} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{sys}} - \Delta U$$

The lost work (irreversibility) is:

$$I = W_{\max} - W_{\text{actual}} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{sys}} - \Delta U - (Q - \Delta U)$$

$$I = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{sys}} - Q$$

But $Q$ crosses the system boundary at $T_0$ (reservoir), so $-\dfrac{Q}{T_0} = \Delta S_{\text{surr}}$. Therefore:

$$I = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{surr}} = T_0 \left( \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + \Delta S_{\text{surr}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\,I = T_0 \cdot S_{\text{gen,universe}} \geq 0\,}$$

This single result is the cornerstone of all **second-law (availability) applications**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Useful Work for a Closed System (Availability)

Combining the first and second laws for a closed system at ambient $T_0, p_0$:

$$W_{\max} = (U_2 - U_1) - T_0(S_2 - S_1) + p_0(V_2 - V_1)$$

For a **steady-flow open system** (per unit mass), the same analysis yields:

$$w_{\max} = (h_2 - h_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked for "maximum useful work that *can be obtained*", the formula above gives it directly. When asked for "lost work" or "irreversibility", compute the difference between $W_{\max}$ and $W_{\text{actual}}$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — A Typical KTU 3-Mark Style

> A 2 kg block of copper at 500 K is dropped into a large lake at 290 K. Take $c_p = 0.386 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Compute $\Delta S_{\text{universe}}$.

**Solution (complete, KTU-board style):**

**Step 1** — Entropy change of the block (constant $c_p$ assumption):

$$\Delta S_{\text{block}} = m \, c_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_f}{T_i}\right) = 2 \times 0.386 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{290}{500}\right)$$

$$\Delta S_{\text{block}} = 0.772 \times (-0.5453) = -0.4210 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 2** — Heat rejected by the block (energy balance, lake is huge so $T_{\text{lake}}$ unchanged):

$$Q = m \, c_p (T_f - T_i) = 2 \times 0.386 \times (290 - 500) = -162.12 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3** — Entropy change of the lake (a reservoir at 290 K):

$$\Delta S_{\text{lake}} = \dfrac{-Q}{T_{\text{lake}}} = \dfrac{162.12}{290} = +0.5590 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 4** — Total (universe) entropy change:

$$\Delta S_{\text{univ}} = -0.4210 + 0.5590 = +0.1380 \text{ kJ/K} > 0$$

**Step 5** — Interpretation: $\Delta S_{\text{univ}} > 0$ confirms the process is **feasible and irreversible**, in perfect agreement with the **Principle of Increase of Entropy**.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — A Typical KTU 14-Mark Application (see Part B for full solution)

> Air at 1 bar, 300 K enters a compressor at 5 m/s and leaves at 3 bar, 450 K and 10 m/s. Heat loss = 50 kJ/kg to surroundings at 295 K. Find (a) actual work input, (b) minimum (reversible) work input, (c) irreversibility, (d) second-law efficiency. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

This will be solved in full inside Section 5 (Question Bank).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: System + Surroundings as an Isolated Universe

The principle of increase of entropy is best visualized by treating the *system of interest* and its *surroundings* as one combined **isolated universe**. The total entropy change of this combined entity is the metric of feasibility.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef universeBox fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sysBox fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef surrBox fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outBox fill:#000000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#00ff00

    Universe["ISOLATED UNIVERSE — Total entropy never decreases"]:::universeBox
    Sys["SYSTEM UNDER STUDY — State changes from 1 to 2"]:::sysBox
    Surr["SURROUNDINGS — Reservoir at T0 and p0"]:::surrBox
    Qheat["Heat Q crosses boundary at T_b"]:::outBox
    Wwork["Work W done / received"]:::outBox
    Sgen["S_gen greater than or equal 0 — internal entropy production"]:::outBox
    Result["Result: Delta S_univ greater than or equal 0"]:::outBox

    Universe --- Sys
    Universe --- Surr
    Sys ---|"Q, W"| Surr
    Sys -.->|"irreversibility arises here"| Sgen
    Surr -.->|"entropy gained equals minus Q divided by T0"| Sgen
    Sgen --> Result

    style Universe rx:12,ry:12
    style Sys rx:8,ry:8
    style Surr rx:8,ry:8
```

### 4.2 T–s Diagram: Carnot Cycle with the Clausius Sum Visualized

A reversible cycle traces its path such that the **net area under the curve on a T–s plot** equals the **net heat transfer**. For an irreversible cycle, the enclosed area is *less than* the equivalent Carnot area — directly demonstrating the Clausius inequality.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pointNode fill:#222831,stroke:#eeeeee,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffd369
    classDef arrowNode fill:#393e46,stroke:#32e0c4,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff
    classDef labelNode fill:#000000,stroke:#ff5722,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    A1["State 1: Saturated liquid — T_high entry"]:::pointNode
    A2["State 2: Saturated vapor — isothermal heat addition Q_in at T_high"]:::pointNode
    A3["State 3: After isentropic expansion — T_low entry"]:::pointNode
    A4["State 4: After isothermal heat rejection Q_out at T_low"]:::pointNode

    A1 -->|"Process 1 to 2: Isothermal heat addition Q_in = T_high times Delta S"| A2
    A2 -->|"Process 2 to 3: Isentropic expansion s constant"| A3
    A3 -->|"Process 3 to 4: Isothermal heat rejection Q_out = T_low times Delta S"| A4
    A4 -->|"Process 4 to 1: Isentropic compression s constant — cycle closes"| A1

    Note1["Enclosed area on T-s plane equals net work W_net = Q_in minus Q_out"]:::labelNode
    Note2["Clausius sum: Q_in divided by T_high plus Q_out divided by T_low equals 0 (reversible)"]:::labelNode

    Note1 ~~~ A2
    Note2 ~~~ A4

    style A1 rx:6,ry:6
    style A2 rx:6,ry:6
    style A3 rx:6,ry:6
    style A4 rx:6,ry:6
    style Note1 rx:4,ry:4
    style Note2 rx:4,ry:4
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — How to Apply the Principle in a KTU Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#264653,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#f4a261,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branchNode fill:#e76f51,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    classDef outNode fill:#000000,stroke:#00ff00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff

    Start(["KTU Problem: System and surroundings given"]):::startNode
    Step1["Step 1: Identify the system boundary and isolate it together with surroundings"]:::procNode
    Step2["Step 2: Compute Delta S_system using property tables or c_p ln T2/T1 minus R ln p2/p1"]:::procNode
    Step3["Step 3: Compute Delta S_surroundings equals minus Q divided by T_surroundings"]:::procNode
    Step4["Step 4: Add to get Delta S_universe"]:::procNode
    Check{"Delta S_universe greater than or equal 0 ?"}:::branchNode
    Feas["Process is feasible — agree with Second Law"]:::outNode
    NotFeas["Process is impossible — violates Second Law"]:::outNode
    Irrev["Strict inequality: irreversible — compute I equals T0 times S_gen"]:::outNode
    Rev["Equality: reversible — ideal benchmark"]:::outNode

    Start --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Check
    Check -->|"Yes, strict greater than"| Feas
    Check -->|"No, less than 0"| NotFeas
    Check -->|"Yes, strict greater than"| Irrev
    Check -->|"Yes, equal 0"| Rev

    style Start rx:10,ry:10
```
