Concave Down"] --> B3["T_max = 272 C at center"] --> B4["Symmetric Drop"] --> B5["T2 = 200 C"] end classDef caseStyle fill:#fff9db,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px class CASE1,CASE2 caseStyle ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Takeaway:** > - **No heat generation** → straight-line (linear) temperature profile. > - **Uniform heat generation** → parabolic profile with maximum at the centerline (if symmetric boundary conditions). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent) --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (3 Marks) **State Fourier's Law of heat conduction. Explain the significance of the negative sign.** **Model Answer:** Fourier's Law states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient in the direction of flow: $$Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$ **Significance of Negative Sign:** - The negative sign ensures that **Q is positive** when heat flows from a higher temperature to a lower temperature (i.e., dT/dx is negative, so −dT/dx is positive). - It indicates that heat always flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature**, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. - Without the negative sign, the equation would predict heat flowing from cold to hot, which is physically impossible. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (3 Marks) **Define thermal resistance. Write the expression for thermal resistance of a plane wall.** **Model Answer:** **Thermal resistance (R_th)** is the opposition offered by a medium to the flow of heat, defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across the medium to the steady-state heat transfer rate: $$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} \quad [\text{K/W}]$$ **For a plane wall:** $$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA}$$ **Significance:** It allows the use of **electrical network analogy** (Ohm's law) for solving complex multi-layer heat transfer problems, where resistances in series add up just like electrical resistors. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (ESE Pattern — 14 Marks) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** A composite furnace wall is made of three layers in series: | Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) | *k* [W/(m·K)] | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Firebrick | 100 | 1.5 | | 2 | Insulating brick | 80 | 0.30 | | 3 | Red brick | 150 | 0.90 | The inner surface temperature is 900 K and the outer surface is at 330 K. The wall area is 5 m². Calculate: - (i) The steady-state heat loss through the wall. **[4 Marks]** - (ii) The temperature at each interface. **[3 Marks]** **(b)** A plane wall 0.2 m thick has a thermal conductivity that varies linearly with temperature as *k* = k₀(1 + βT), where k₀ = 0.85 W/(m·K) and β = 0.0005 K⁻¹. The two faces are at T₁ = 400 K and T₂ = 300 K. Derive the temperature distribution and find the heat flux. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Solution **Step 1:** Compute individual thermal resistances. For A = 5 m²: $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.100}{1.5 \times 5} = 0.01333 \text{ K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.080}{0.30 \times 5} = 0.05333 \text{ K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.150}{0.90 \times 5} = 0.03333 \text{ K/W}$$ **Step 2:** Total resistance: $$R_{total} = 0.01333 + 0.05333 + 0.03333 = 0.1000 \text{ K/W}$$ **Step 3:** Heat loss: $$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{900 - 330}{0.1000} = 5700 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4:** Interface temperatures: $$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_1 = 900 - 5700 \times 0.01333 = 900 - 76 = 824 \text{ K}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - Q \cdot R_2 = 824 - 5700 \times 0.05333 = 824 - 304 = 520 \text{ K}$$ **Verification:** T₄ = T₃ − Q·R₃ = 520 − 5700 × 0.03333 = 520 − 190 = 330 K ✓ #### Part (b) — Solution **Step 1:** Fourier's Law with variable *k*: $$Q = -k(T) A \frac{dT}{dx} = -k_0(1 + \beta T) A \frac{dT}{dx}$$ **Step 2:** Separate variables (steady state → Q = const): $$Q \int_0^L dx = -k_0 A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1 + \beta T) dT$$ **Step 3:** Integrate: $$Q \cdot L = -k_0 A \left[ T + \frac{\beta T^2}{2} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$ $$Q \cdot L = k_0 A \left[ (T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2) \right]$$ **Step 4:** Plug in values: T₁ = 400 K, T₂ = 300 K, β = 0.0005 K⁻¹ $$T_1 - T_2 = 100 \text{ K}$$ $$T_1^2 - T_2^2 = 160000 - 90000 = 70000 \text{ K}^2$$ $$\frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2) = \frac{0.0005}{2} \times 70000 = 17.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5:** Heat flux (q" = Q/A): $$q'' = \frac{k_0 \left[ (T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2) \right]}{L} = \frac{0.85 \times (100 + 17.5)}{0.2} = \frac{0.85 \times 117.5}{0.2} = 499.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 6:** Temperature distribution (re-derive by integration with variable limit): The general relation is: $$Q \cdot x = k_0 A \left[ (T_1 - T) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T^2) \right]$$ Substituting Q/A = 499.4 W/m² and solving for T(x) iteratively, or using the linearized *average* conductivity *k_avg* = k₀[1 + β(T₁+T₂)/2] = 0.85[1 + 0.0005 × 350] = 0.85 × 1.175 = 0.999 W/(m·K), the approximate temperature distribution is: $$T(x) \approx T_1 - \frac{q'' x}{k_{avg}} = 400 - 500x \quad [\text{K}]$$ --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** Derive an expression for the steady-state temperature distribution in a plane wall with **uniform internal heat generation** and both surfaces maintained at the same temperature T_s. Sketch the temperature profile. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A wall of thickness 0.3 m, thermal conductivity 1.2 W/(m·K), and area 10 m² has its surfaces at 600 K and 400 K. If the wall has a contact resistance of 0.0005 K·m²/W at the interface, find the heat transfer rate per unit area. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Solution The governing equation for 1-D steady conduction with generation is: $$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = 0$$ **Integration 1:** $$\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} x + C_1$$ **Integration 2:** $$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k} x^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$ **Boundary Conditions** (symmetric wall, surfaces at x = ±L): - BC1: T(+L) = T_s → −(q̇_gen/2k)L² + C₁L + C₂ = T_s - BC2: dT/dx|_{x=0} = 0 (by symmetry, center is hottest) → C₁ = 0 **Final Solution:** $$\boxed{T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k}(L^2 - x^2)}$$ **Maximum temperature at x = 0:** $$T_{max} = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}$$ **Sketch:** Parabolic curve, opening downward, peak at center (x = 0), dropping to T_s at both x = ±L. #### Part (b) — Solution **Given:** L = 0.3 m, k = 1.2 W/(m·K), A = 10 m², T₁ = 600 K, T₂ = 400 K, R"_c = 0.0005 K·m²/W **Step 1:** Conduction resistance of the wall: $$R''_{cond} = \frac{L}{k} = \frac{0.3}{1.2} = 0.25 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W}$$ **Step 2:** Total resistance per unit area (conduction + contact): $$R''_{total} = R''_{cond} + R''_c = 0.25 + 0.0005 = 0.2505 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W}$$ **Step 3:** Heat flux: $$q'' = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R''_{total}} = \frac{600 - 400}{0.2505} = \frac{200}{0.2505} = 798.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 4:** Heat transfer rate: $$Q = q'' \times A = 798.4 \times 10 = 7984 \text{ W} \approx 7.98 \text{ kW}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting units** — Always state units in every numerical answer (W, K/W, m²). Marks are deducted for unitless final answers. > 2. **Mixing up L and k** — A higher *L* means **more** resistance, but higher *k* means **less** resistance. Many students invert these. > 3. **Not stating assumptions** — A KTU 14-mark answer without an "Assumptions" section typically loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Wrong sign in Fourier's Law** — The negative sign is **mandatory**; examiners specifically check for it. > 5. **Interface temperature order** — T₂ comes after Layer 1, T₃ after Layer 2, etc. Get the order wrong and lose all 3 marks in part (a). > 6. **Ignoring contact resistance** — If the problem says "with contact resistance," you MUST add R"_c to the denominator. Many students skip it. > 7. **Inconsistent units** — If thickness is given in mm, convert to m FIRST. Mixing mm and m is a fatal error. > 8. **Forgetting area** — When using $R = L/(kA)$, always multiply *A*. The flux form $q" = k\Delta T/L$ doesn't need *A* (don't accidentally include it). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Steady-State Plane Walls** - [x] **Fourier's Law:** $Q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ — the master equation of conduction. - [x] **Steady State** → ∂T/∂τ = 0 → reduces PDE to ODE → linear T-profile. - [x] **Single Plane Wall:** $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ — most basic result. - [x] **Composite Wall (Series):** Resistances **add**: $R_{tot} = \sum L_i/(k_i A_i)$. - [x] **Electrical Analogy:** ΔT ↔ Voltage, Q ↔ Current, R_th ↔ Resistance. - [x] **Heat Flux:** $q'' = Q/A = k\Delta T/L$ — useful when area is unknown. - [x] **Interface Temperature:** Drop T from the hot side step-by-step: $T_j = T_1 - Q \cdot \sum R_i$. - [x] **Wall with Generation:** Parabolic T-profile, $T_{max} = T_s + \dfrac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}$. - [x] **Contact Resistance:** Adds to total R — never ignore if mentioned. - [x] **Assumptions to state:** 1-D, steady, constant k, no generation (unless stated), isothermal surfaces. - [x] **Units to memorize:** k [W/(m·K)], Q [W], q" [W/m²], R_th [K/W], α [m²/s]. - [x] **Common KTU trick question:** What happens if *k* varies with T? Use the **mean conductivity** approximation $k_m = k_0[1 + \beta(T_1+T_2)/2]$ for linear variation. --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Steady-State Heat Conduction in Plane Walls** *Prepared strictly per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020 Aligned) Syllabus — PCPET303*

topics.title: Multi-Layer Walls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multi-Layer Walls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Layer (Composite) Wall:** A structural element composed of two or more homogeneous material layers bonded in **series** (in the direction of heat flow) or **parallel**, used to impede heat transfer between two regions maintained at different temperatures. In steady-state, one-dimensional conduction, the heat flux through each layer is identical, while the temperature drop across each layer is governed by its individual thermal resistance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, multi-layer walls form the foundation of **composite heat conduction**, the physics behind building insulation, refractory furnace linings, boiler tubes, steam pipelines, and cryogenic storage vessels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tollbooth Highway"

Imagine a highway where vehicles (heat) must pass through a series of tollbooths (layers) before reaching their destination.

- Each tollbooth slows the traffic by a fixed amount.
- A **booth with poor service** (low thermal conductivity) creates a **long queue** (high thermal resistance).
- The **total delay** experienced by every vehicle equals the **sum of delays at each booth**.
- After exiting the last booth, the vehicles (heat) are no longer slowed, and the **flow rate is the same through every booth** (steady-state, equal Q through each layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as the total toll delay is the sum of individual delays, the **total thermal resistance of a composite wall is the arithmetic sum of the individual layer resistances** when heat flows perpendicular to the layers (series configuration).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Practical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | $\text{W/(m·K)}$ | $0.02$ (insulation) – $400$ (copper) |
| Wall thickness | $L$ | $\text{m}$ | $0.001$ – $0.5$ |
| Heat transfer rate | $Q$ | $\text{W}$ | $1$ – $10^{6}$ |
| Thermal resistance | $R$ | $\text{K/W}$ | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{2}$ |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient | $U$ | $\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ | $0.1$ – $1000$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Single-Layer Plane Wall (Foundation Recap)

For a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$, surface area $A$, with surface temperatures $T_1$ (hot side) and $T_2$ (cold side), Fourier's law of conduction gives:

$$Q = \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Rewritten in terms of thermal resistance:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R}, \quad \text{where} \quad R = \frac{L}{kA}$$

### 2.1.2 Multi-Layer (Composite) Plane Wall — Series Configuration

Consider an $n$-layer plane wall with perfect thermal contact at interfaces:

- **Layer 1**: thickness $L_1$, conductivity $k_1$, inner temp $T_1$
- **Layer 2**: thickness $L_2$, conductivity $k_2$, interface temp $T_2$
- **Layer $i$**: thickness $L_i$, conductivity $k_i$, interface temp $T_i$
- **Layer $n$**: thickness $L_n$, conductivity $k_n$, outer temp $T_{n+1}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-State Boundary Conditions:**
> 1. Heat flux through every layer is identical: $Q_1 = Q_2 = \ldots = Q_n = Q$
> 2. The temperature profile is **piecewise linear** (drops more steeply across low-$k$ layers).
> 3. Continuity of temperature at interfaces (assuming perfect contact).

### 2.1.3 Derivation Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law to each layer in steady state.
- **Step 2:** Sum the individual temperature drops (since $\Delta T$ is an extensive variable for series paths).
- **Step 3:** Recognize that the resulting expression is of the form $Q = \Delta T_{\text{total}} / R_{\text{total}}$.
- **Step 4:** Identify $R_{\text{total}}$ as the sum of individual layer resistances — a direct analogue of **Ohm's law** in electrical circuits.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the complete cheat sheet for Multi-Layer Wall problems in KTU ESE. **Memorize these forms — they constitute 90% of numerical answers.**

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Thermal Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single plane wall | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1-T_2)}{L}$ | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ |
| 2 | $n$-layer plane wall | $Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i/(k_i A)}$ | $R_{\text{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ |
| 3 | Single cylindrical shell | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ |
| 4 | $n$-layer cylindrical shell | $Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}}$ | $R_{\text{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$ |
| 5 | Overall U (plane) | $U = \dfrac{1}{L_{\text{tot}}/k_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum L_i/k_i}$ | $R_{\text{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{UA}$ |
| 6 | Interface temperature | $T_i = T_1 - Q \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \dfrac{L_j}{k_j A}$ | Cumulative drop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safety:** In the table above, all absolute-value bars and "given-by" phrases have been deliberately reworded to avoid the `\` character that breaks markdown table rendering. The logarithmic forms are mathematically identical to the standard textbook expressions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Energy Codes:** Determining $R$-values of insulated walls, roofs, and double-glazed windows.
- **Furnace & Boiler Design:** Refractory brick + steel shell composites.
- **Cryogenics:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) for LNG and superconducting magnets.
- **Electronics Cooling:** Heat sink base plates with TIM (thermal interface material).
- **Pipeline Insulation:** Underground steam lines with multiple concentric cylindrical layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Three-Layer Plane Wall

Consider three layers in series, with inner surface temperature $T_1$ and outer surface temperature $T_4$:

### Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law to Layer 1

$$Q_1 = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}$$

Solving for the temperature drop across Layer 1:

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q_1 L_1}{k_1 A}$$

### Step 2: Apply Fourier's Law to Layer 2

$$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q_2 L_2}{k_2 A}$$

### Step 3: Apply Fourier's Law to Layer 3

$$T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q_3 L_3}{k_3 A}$$

### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Condition

Under steady-state, by conservation of energy, the heat rate through each layer is identical:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = Q$$

### Step 5: Sum the Temperature Drops

$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) = T_1 - T_4$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1–3:

$$\frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A} = T_1 - T_4$$

### Step 6: Factor Out Q

$$Q \left[ \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} \right] = T_1 - T_4$$

### Step 7: Solve for Q

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A}}$$

### Step 8: Identify Total Thermal Resistance

Defining $R_i = L_i / (k_i A)$:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$$

The compact final form:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Interface Temperature

To find $T_2$ (the temperature at the interface between Layer 1 and Layer 2), we use the known $Q$:

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}$$

Therefore:

$$T_2 = T_1 - \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}$$

Generalized for the $i$-th interface:

$$T_i = T_1 - Q \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \frac{L_j}{k_j A}$$

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Multi-Layer Cylindrical Wall

For a composite pipe with $n$ concentric layers (e.g., inner pipe, insulation, cladding), Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates yields:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k_i L (T_i - T_{i+1})}{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}$$

Solving for the temperature drop:

$$T_i - T_{i+1} = \frac{Q \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$$

Summing across all $n$ layers:

$$T_1 - T_{n+1} = \frac{Q}{2\pi L} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}$$

Final compact form:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 pattern]**
> A furnace wall is composed of three layers: (i) a $0.10\,\text{m}$ thick firebrick ($k = 1.4\,\text{W/m·K}$), (ii) a $0.08\,\text{m}$ thick insulating brick ($k = 0.35\,\text{W/m·K}$), and (iii) a $0.025\,\text{m}$ thick steel plate ($k = 45\,\text{W/m·K}$). The wall area is $A = 2.5\,\text{m}^2$. The inner surface is at $T_1 = 1100\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer surface is at $T_4 = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$. Find: (a) the heat loss per hour, and (b) the temperature at the interface between the firebrick and the insulating brick.

### Solution

**Step (a): Heat Loss**

Compute each layer's thermal resistance:

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{1.4 \times 2.5} = 0.02857\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.08}{0.35 \times 2.5} = 0.09143\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.025}{45 \times 2.5} = 0.000222\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.02857 + 0.09143 + 0.000222 = 0.12022\,\text{K/W}$$

Heat loss:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1100 - 60}{0.12022} = 8647.5\,\text{W}$$

Heat loss per hour:

$$Q_{\text{hourly}} = 8647.5 \times 3600 = 31{,}131{,}000\,\text{J} = 31.13\,\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step (b): Interface Temperature $T_2$**

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q R_1 = 1100 - (8647.5)(0.02857) = 1100 - 247.1 = 852.9\,^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The insulating brick ($k = 0.35$) — although thinner than the firebrick — accounts for $\approx 76\%$ of the total thermal resistance. This is why a thin insulation layer dramatically reduces heat loss, a principle exploited in furnace design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Layer Plane Wall & Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HOT FLUID SIDE T1"] --> B["LAYER 1 k1 L1"]
    B --> C["INTERFACE T2"]
    C --> D["LAYER 2 k2 L2"]
    D --> E["INTERFACE T3"]
    E --> F["LAYER 3 k3 L3"]
    F --> G["INTERFACE T4"]
    G --> H["LAYER 4 k4 L4"]
    H --> I["COLD FLUID SIDE T5"]

    J["THERMAL RESISTANCE NETWORK"] --> J1["R1 = L1 k1 A"]
    J1 --> J2["R2 = L2 k2 A"]
    J2 --> J3["R3 = L3 k3 A"]
    J3 --> J4["R4 = L4 k4 A"]
    J4 --> K["Q = DELTA T TOTAL divided by R TOTAL"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Composite Cylindrical Wall

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INNER FLUID at T1 radius r1"] --> B["INNER PIPE WALL k1 from r1 to r2"]
    B --> C["INSULATION LAYER k2 from r2 to r3"]
    C --> D["OUTER CLADDING k3 from r3 to r4"]
    D --> E["AMBIENT FLUID at T4 radius r4"]

    F["RADIAL HEAT FLOW Q"] --> G["Q equals 2 pi L delta T divided by sum of ln r out divided by r in over k"]
    G --> H["CRITICAL RADIUS r crit equals k insulation divided by h outside"]
    H --> I["INSULATION EFFECTIVE ONLY if r3 greater than r crit"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Layer Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START Identify Wall Type"] --> S1{"Plane or Cylindrical?"}
    S1 -- PLANE --> S2["Use Q = delta T divided by sum of L over k A"]
    S1 -- CYLINDRICAL --> S3["Use Q = 2 pi L delta T divided by sum of ln r out over r in divided by k"]
    S2 --> S4["Check Contact Resistance at each interface"]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5["Compute Interface Temperatures using cumulative drop"]
    S5 --> S6["Validate: temperature profile must be monotonically decreasing"]
    S6 --> S7["Report Q and T interface values"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts emphasize the **architectural decision path** a KTU student must take when encountering a composite wall problem — first identify the geometry (plane vs cylindrical), then apply the correct resistance formula, then validate the result by checking the monotonic decrease of the temperature profile.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Define **thermal resistance** for a plane wall and state the analogous electrical quantity it represents.

**Model Answer:**
Thermal resistance $R$ of a plane wall is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across the wall to the steady-state heat transfer rate through it. Mathematically:

$$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{L}{kA}$$

It is the direct thermal analogue of **electrical resistance** in Ohm's law ($V = IR$). The **driving potential** is $\Delta T$ (analogous to voltage), the **flow** is $Q$ (analogous to current), and $R$ is the material's opposition to heat flow (analogous to electrical resistance).

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> For a three-layer composite wall, write the expression for the total thermal resistance in the direction perpendicular to heat flow.

**Model Answer:**
For three layers in series, the total thermal resistance is the arithmetic sum of the individual layer resistances:

$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A}$$

The heat transfer rate is then:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]**
> A composite wall consists of three layers in series. Layer 1 is a $100\,\text{mm}$ thick layer of fireclay brick ($k_1 = 1.10\,\text{W/m·K}$), Layer 2 is a $75\,\text{mm}$ thick layer of insulating brick ($k_2 = 0.25\,\text{W/m·K}$), and Layer 3 is a $25\,\text{mm}$ thick layer of building brick ($k_3 = 0.70\,\text{W/m·K}$). The surface area of the wall is $A = 1.5\,\text{m}^2$. The inner surface temperature is $T_1 = 800\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer surface temperature is $T_4 = 50\,^\circ\text{C}$.

#### Part (a) — Calculate the steady-state heat transfer rate through the wall. (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the individual layer resistances.

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.100}{1.10 \times 1.5} = 0.06061\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.075}{0.25 \times 1.5} = 0.20000\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.025}{0.70 \times 1.5} = 0.02381\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total resistance.

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.06061 + 0.20000 + 0.02381 = 0.28442\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the series-resistance heat conduction equation.

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{800 - 50}{0.28442} = 2635.6\,\text{W}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{Q \approx 2635.6\,\text{W} \approx 2.64\,\text{kW}}$

#### Part (b) — Determine the interface temperatures $T_2$ and $T_3$. (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $T_2$ using the drop across Layer 1.

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q R_1 = 800 - (2635.6)(0.06061) = 800 - 159.77 = 640.23\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $T_3$ using the additional drop across Layer 2.

$$T_3 = T_2 - Q R_2 = 640.23 - (2635.6)(0.20000) = 640.23 - 527.12 = 113.11\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3:** Validation.

The remaining drop must equal $Q R_3$:

$$T_3 - T_4 = 113.11 - 50 = 63.11\,^\circ\text{C}$$
$$Q R_3 = (2635.6)(0.02381) = 62.75\,^\circ\text{C}$$

The small discrepancy is due to rounding; using the exact values yields perfect closure. ✓

**Answer:** $\boxed{T_2 \approx 640.2\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_3 \approx 113.1\,^\circ\text{C}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]**
> A steam pipe of outer radius $r_1 = 50\,\text{mm}$ is lagged (insulated) with a $40\,\text{mm}$ thick layer of insulation having $k_2 = 0.05\,\text{W/m·K}$. The bare pipe surface temperature is $T_1 = 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer surface of the insulation is at $T_2 = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$. The pipe wall is steel with $k_{\text{steel}} = 45\,\text{W/m·K}$ and thickness $5\,\text{mm}$. The length of pipe considered is $L = 10\,\text{m}$.

#### Part (a) — Calculate the steady-state heat loss per unit length of the pipe. (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the geometry. This is a **two-layer cylindrical wall** — inner steel pipe + outer insulation. The heat flows radially outward.

**Step 2:** Compute the thermal resistance of the steel pipe.

Inner radius: $r_{\text{inner}} = 50 - 5 = 45\,\text{mm} = 0.045\,\text{m}$
Outer radius: $r_1 = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.050\,\text{m}$

$$R_{\text{steel}} = \frac{\ln(r_1 / r_{\text{inner}})}{2\pi k_{\text{steel}} L} = \frac{\ln(0.050 / 0.045)}{2\pi \times 45 \times 10}$$

$$R_{\text{steel}} = \frac{\ln(1.1111)}{2827.43} = \frac{0.10536}{2827.43} = 3.726 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the thermal resistance of the insulation.

Outer radius of insulation: $r_2 = 50 + 40 = 90\,\text{mm} = 0.090\,\text{m}$

$$R_{\text{ins}} = \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2\pi k_2 L} = \frac{\ln(0.090 / 0.050)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 10}$$

$$R_{\text{ins}} = \frac{\ln(1.8)}{3.1416} = \frac{0.5878}{3.1416} = 0.1871\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4:** Total resistance.

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{steel}} + R_{\text{ins}} = 0.0000373 + 0.1871 = 0.18714\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 5:** Heat loss for the 10 m pipe.

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{250 - 60}{0.18714} = 1015.3\,\text{W}$$

**Step 6:** Heat loss per unit length.

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{1015.3}{10} = 101.5\,\text{W/m}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{Q/L \approx 101.5\,\text{W/m}}$

#### Part (b) — Determine the temperature at the interface between the steel pipe and the insulation. (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** The interface temperature $T_{\text{int}}$ is on the outer surface of the steel pipe (which equals the inner surface of the insulation).

**Step 2:** Use the temperature drop across the steel pipe.

$$T_1 - T_{\text{int}} = Q R_{\text{steel}}$$

$$T_{\text{int}} = T_1 - Q R_{\text{steel}} = 250 - (1015.3)(3.726 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$T_{\text{int}} = 250 - 0.0378 = 249.96\,^\circ\text{C} \approx 250\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3:** Validation by computing drop across insulation.

$$T_{\text{int}} - T_2 = Q R_{\text{ins}} = (1015.3)(0.1871) = 189.96\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_2 = 249.96 - 189.96 = 60.0\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{T_{\text{int}} \approx 250\,^\circ\text{C}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The interface temperature is essentially equal to the inner pipe temperature. This is because the steel layer has **negligible thermal resistance** compared to the insulation (over 5000 times smaller). In practical insulation problems, the bare-pipe surface temperature can be used as the "hot side" boundary condition without significant error.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Multi-Layer Wall Problems**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks typical):** Students often mix mm with m in thickness. Always convert all dimensions to **metres** before computing resistance.
> 
> 2. **Cylindrical vs. Plane Confusion (–3 to –5 marks):** For pipes, **never** use $L/kA$ — you must use $\ln(r_o/r_i) / (2\pi k L)$. Check the problem statement for keywords like "pipe," "radius," or "diameter."
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Perfect Contact Assumption (–1 mark):** If the problem does not specify interfacial resistance, assume continuity of temperature at the interface.
> 
> 4. **Summing Resistances in Parallel by Mistake (–4 marks):** Multi-layer walls in the direction **perpendicular** to heat flow are **series** — add the resistances. Parallel configurations occur only when heat flows **through** alternate paths side-by-side, not through stacked layers.
> 
> 5. **Arithmetic Slip in $R_{\text{total}}$ (–2 marks):** Re-compute the sum and verify that it is dimensionally a K/W value.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the Final Validation (–1 mark):** Always verify the final interface temperature by computing the drop from the other side. Examiners reward explicit checks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Multi-Layer Walls**
> 
> - **Core equation (plane):** $Q = \Delta T_{\text{total}} / \sum (L_i / k_i A)$
> - **Core equation (cylinder):** $Q = 2\pi L \Delta T_{\text{total}} / \sum [\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i) / k_i]$
> - **Series rule:** $R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_2 + \ldots + R_n$ (heat flow perpendicular to layers).
> - **Steady-state condition:** Heat rate is **identical** through every layer.
> - **Interface temperature:** $T_i = T_1 - Q \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} R_j$.
> - **Overall U-value:** $U = 1 / \sum (L_i / k_i)$ for plane walls; $U = 1 / \{R_{\text{tot}} \cdot A\}$.
> - **Lowest $k$ dominates:** A thin layer of insulation can provide more resistance than thick layers of high-$k$ material.
> - **Critical radius (cylindrical):** $r_{\text{cr}} = k_{\text{ins}} / h_{\text{outside}}$ — insulation is **counterproductive** below this radius.
> - **Check monotonicity:** Interface temperatures must decrease (or stay equal) from hot to cold side.
> - **Default assumption:** Perfect thermal contact at all interfaces unless otherwise stated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** When you see a multi-layer problem, **draw the wall sketch first** labeling all temperatures, thicknesses, conductivities, and the direction of heat flow. Then compute $R_i$ for each layer one by one, summing into $R_{\text{total}}$, and finally apply $Q = \Delta T / R_{\text{total}}$. This systematic approach guarantees full marks on KTU ESE numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Conduction in Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat Conduction in Cylinders # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State, One-Dimensional Heat Conduction in a Cylindrical (Radial) Coordinate System** is the mode of heat transfer in which thermal energy flows exclusively in the radial direction ($r$-direction) through the wall of a hollow cylinder, with the inner and outer surface temperatures remaining constant with respect to time. The isothermal surfaces are concentric cylindrical surfaces, and the heat flow lines are purely radial. In **KTU 2024 Scheme** parlance, this topic is often phrased as: > *“Derive the expression for steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction through a hollow cylinder and hence obtain the thermal resistance and the concept of critical radius of insulation.”* The governing scalar field is the **radial temperature distribution** $T(r)$, and the goal is to find: 1. The **temperature profile** $T(r)$ inside the cylinder wall. 2. The **heat transfer rate** $\dot{Q}$ (or simply $Q$) through the cylinder. 3. The **thermal resistance** $R_{cyl}$ of the cylindrical wall. 4. The **critical radius of insulation** $r_{cr}$ — a counterintuitive result in heat transfer. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hot Tea Pipe" Imagine a long **steam pipe** carrying superheated steam at $200\,^\circ\text{C}$ in a factory. The pipe is metallic and hot, but the outside is at room temperature ($30\,^\circ\text{C}$). To save energy, an engineer wraps the pipe with a layer of **insulation** (say, glass wool or rock wool). - The **steam** is the heat source inside the pipe. - Heat must travel **radially outward** through the pipe wall, then through the insulation, and finally dissipate to the surrounding air by **convection**. - If the insulation is very thin, will adding *more* insulation always reduce heat loss? **Surprisingly — NO!** If the insulation's outer radius falls below a certain *critical* value, adding insulation actually **increases** the heat loss. This is the famous "critical radius of insulation" phenomenon, first popularised by engineers designing electrical cables and steam pipelines. > [!NOTE] > **Why cylindrical?** Many engineering components are cylindrical: pipes, boiler tubes, nuclear fuel rods, overhead electrical conductors, pressure vessels, and condenser tubes. Heat loss from these geometries must be analysed in **cylindrical coordinates** — *not* cartesian — because the area through which heat flows ($A = 2\pi r L$) is **not constant**, but increases with $r$. --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Geometric Parameters | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | $k$ | Thermal conductivity of cylinder material | $\text{W/m·K}$ | | $r_i$ | Inner radius of cylinder | $\text{m}$ | | $r_o$ | Outer radius of cylinder | $\text{m}$ | | $L$ | Length of cylinder | $\text{m}$ | | $T_i$ | Inner surface temperature | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ | | $T_o$ | Outer surface temperature | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ | | $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient on outside | $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ | | $T_\infty$ | Ambient (free-stream) temperature | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ | | $\dot{Q}$ | Steady-state heat transfer rate (rate of heat flow) | $\text{W}$ (Joule/s) | | $R_{th}$ | Thermal resistance | $\text{K/W}$ | > [!TIP] > **Memorise the units!** In KTU numerical problems, you will lose marks if you mix up $\text{W/m·K}$ (conduction) and $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ (convection). A classic board-exam trap. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Assumptions for the Standard 1-D Radial Conduction Model For the derivations that follow, KTU standardly assumes: 1. **Steady-state**: $\partial T / \partial t = 0$. The temperature at any point does not change with time. 2. **One-dimensional**: Heat flows only in the radial direction. $\partial T / \partial \theta = 0$ and $\partial T / \partial z = 0$. 3. **Constant thermal conductivity** $k$: independent of temperature. 4. **No internal heat generation** within the cylinder wall ($q''' = 0$). 5. **Constant surface temperatures** $T_i$ and $T_o$ (Dirichlet boundary conditions). 6. **Isotropic and homogeneous** material. --- ## 2.2 The Logic of Heat Flow — Why the Area Matters In **plane wall** conduction (cartesian), the area $A$ is constant. So the temperature profile is **linear**. But in a cylinder, the cross-sectional area through which heat flows at radius $r$ is: $$A(r) = 2\pi r L$$ Because $A$ grows linearly with $r$, the heat flux $q''(r) = \dot{Q}/A(r)$ **decreases** as $r$ increases. For $\dot{Q}$ to remain constant (steady-state, energy conservation), the temperature gradient $dT/dr$ must become **less steep** as $r$ grows. Therefore, the temperature profile $T(r)$ in a hollow cylinder is **logarithmic**, not linear. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains the **complete, examination-ready formula set** for Heat Conduction in Cylinders. Every line here is a potential KTU board question. | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $A(r) = 2\pi r L$ | Lateral surface area at radius $r$ | Always — fundamental | | 2 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{2\pi k L \,(T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$ | Heat transfer rate through a hollow cylinder | Single-material cylinder, given surface temps | | 3 | $T(r) = T_i - (T_i - T_o)\,\dfrac{\ln(r / r_i)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$ | Temperature distribution within the wall | Finding $T$ at any radius | | 4 | $T(r) = T_o + (T_i - T_o)\,\dfrac{\ln(r_o / r)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$ | Alternate form of temperature distribution | Sometimes more convenient at $r$ near $r_o$ | | 5 | $R_{cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ | Thermal resistance of cylindrical wall | Resistance-network problems | | 6 | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_o L}$ | External convective resistance | When fluid surrounds the cylinder | | 7 | $R_{tot} = \dfrac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_o L}$ | Total resistance (conduction + convection) | Insulated pipe losing heat to ambient | | 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{tot}}$ | Ohm's-law analogy for heat transfer | Always — use with $R_{tot}$ | | 9 | $R_{tot,comp} = \sum \dfrac{\ln(r_{n+1}/r_n)}{2\pi k_n L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_{outer} L}$ | Composite cylindrical resistance (multi-layer) | Pipe + insulation + lagging, etc. | | 10 | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k}{h}$ | **Critical radius of insulation** | Deciding insulation thickness | | 11 | $r_{cr}^* = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$ | Critical radius expressed in terms of insulation $k$ | Practical use | | 12 | $r_o^{opt} = r_{cr}$ | Optimum outer radius for maximum heat dissipation | Heat fin / wire design | --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Formulas Save Real Energy > [!NOTE] > **Real-world applications of cylindrical conduction analysis:** > - **Steam and process pipelines** in refineries and power plants — sizing insulation thickness for energy conservation. > - **Nuclear fuel rods** — cladding (Zircaloy) acts as a cylindrical thermal barrier between $\text{UO}_2$ pellet and coolant. > - **Underground power cables** — XLPE insulation + sheath; critical radius decides whether heat dissipation is helped or harmed by the insulation. > - **Heat exchanger tubes** — shell-and-tube exchangers often have multi-layer cylindrical walls. > - **Boiler tubes, superheater tubes** — high-pressure, high-temperature service. > - **Cryogenic storage tanks** — vacuum-insulated double-walled cylinders; conduction is minimised. > - **Electrical resistance wires / heating elements** — where $r_{cr}$ decides maximum heat transfer to the surroundings. --- ## 2.5 Composite (Multi-Layer) Cylinders — The Generalisation In practice, a "cylinder" often has **multiple concentric layers**: a metal pipe, then insulation, then a protective jacket. Each layer has its own $k$ and geometry. The thermal resistances are simply **added in series** (just like resistors in an electrical circuit): $$R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L} + \frac{\ln(r_4/r_3)}{2\pi k_3 L} + \cdots + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_{outer} L}$$ This is the **electrical–thermal analogy**: $\dot{Q} \leftrightarrow I$, $\Delta T \leftrightarrow \Delta V$, $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R_{elec}$. KTU examiners **love** this analogy; it appears in nearly every past paper. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Temperature Distribution & Heat Transfer Rate in a Hollow Cylinder > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important derivation** in the entire module. It has appeared in KTU exams in at least 4 of the last 6 semesters. Memorise the logic, not just the formula. ### Step 1 — Set up the physical model Consider a long hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$. The inner surface is held at temperature $T_i$ and the outer surface at $T_o$, with $T_i > T_o$. Heat flows radially outward. ### Step 2 — Apply Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction in the radial direction Fourier's law in vector form is $\vec{q}'' = -k\,\nabla T$. For 1-D radial flow in a cylinder, the **heat transfer rate** through a cylindrical surface at radius $r$ is: $$\dot{Q}_r = -k \cdot A(r) \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$ where the lateral area is $A(r) = 2\pi r L$. Substituting: $$\dot{Q}_r = -k\,(2\pi r L)\,\frac{dT}{dr}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the steady-state energy conservation At steady state, the heat rate must be the **same** through every cylindrical surface at any $r$ (otherwise energy would accumulate). Therefore: $$\dot{Q}_r = \dot{Q} = \text{constant (independent of } r\text{)}$$ ### Step 4 — Separate the variables Rearranging the Fourier equation to isolate $dT$ and $dr$: $$\dot{Q} = -2\pi k L \cdot r \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$ $$dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$ ### Step 5 — Integrate between the boundary conditions Integrate $T$ from $T_i$ to $T_o$ as $r$ goes from $r_i$ to $r_o$: $$\int_{T_i}^{T_o} dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r}$$ $$T_o - T_i = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\,\bigl[\ln r\bigr]_{r_i}^{r_o}$$ $$T_o - T_i = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $\dot{Q}$ Multiply both sides by $-1$: $$T_i - T_o = \frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\;\dot{Q} = \frac{2\pi k L\,(T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **principal result** for steady-state, 1-D radial conduction in a hollow cylinder. Notice the appearance of $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ — this is the **cylindrical analogue** of the cartesian $(L/kA)$ in the plane-wall formula. The cross-section grows with $r$, so the $\log$ function naturally appears. ### Step 7 — Obtain the temperature distribution $T(r)$ To find the temperature at any arbitrary radius $r$ (where $r_i \le r \le r_o$), integrate from $r_i$ to $r$ instead of to $r_o$: $$\int_{T_i}^{T(r)} dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\int_{r_i}^{r} \frac{dr}{r}$$ $$T(r) - T_i = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_i}\right)$$ Substitute the value of $\dot{Q}$ from Step 6: $$T(r) - T_i = -\frac{(T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}\cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_i}\right)$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\;T(r) = T_i - (T_i - T_o)\,\frac{\ln(r / r_i)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}\;}$$ ### Step 8 — Sanity checks (always do this in the exam!) - At $r = r_i$: $T(r_i) = T_i - 0 = T_i$ ✓ - At $r = r_o$: $T(r_o) = T_i - (T_i - T_o)\cdot 1 = T_o$ ✓ - The profile is **logarithmic**, not linear, as predicted by theory. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Thermal Resistance of a Hollow Cylinder Comparing the result of Step 6 with the Ohm's-law analogy $\dot{Q} = \Delta T / R_{th}$: $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_o}{R_{cyl}} = \frac{2\pi k L\,(T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$$ Hence, by inspection: $$\boxed{\;R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **electrical–thermal analogy** is a critical KTU exam hook. Map the variables: $\dot{Q} \leftrightarrow I$ (current), $\Delta T \leftrightarrow \Delta V$ (voltage), $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R$ (resistance). All series/parallel resistance rules from electrical circuits apply verbatim to thermal networks. --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation > [!WARNING] > This is a **tricky derivation** and a guaranteed KTU favourite. Many students confuse "increasing insulation" with "decreasing heat loss" — this derivation shows that is **not always true**. ### Step 1 — Set up the problem An insulated cylindrical pipe has: - Pipe outer radius (fixed): $r_i$ - Insulation outer radius (variable): $r$ - Insulation thermal conductivity: $k_{ins}$ - Outer convection coefficient (constant): $h_o$ - Pipe inner surface temperature (fixed): $T_i$ - Ambient temperature: $T_\infty$ The total thermal resistance from pipe surface to ambient is **conduction through insulation + convection outside**: $$R_{tot}(r) = \underbrace{\frac{\ln(r / r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins} L}}_{\text{insulation conduction}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r L}}_{\text{outer convection}}$$ The corresponding heat loss is: $$\dot{Q}(r) = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{tot}(r)} = \frac{2\pi L\,(T_i - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r/r_i)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r}}$$ ### Step 2 — To find the radius that gives maximum heat transfer, minimise $R_{tot}(r)$ Because $\dot{Q} \propto 1/R_{tot}$, **maximising $\dot{Q}$** is equivalent to **minimising $R_{tot}$**. Take the derivative of $R_{tot}$ with respect to $r$ and set it to zero: $$\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{k_{ins}\,r} - \frac{1}{h_o\,r^2}\right] = 0$$ (Note: derivative of $\ln(r/r_i)$ with respect to $r$ is $1/r$, and derivative of $1/r$ is $-1/r^2$.) ### Step 3 — Solve for $r$ $$\frac{1}{k_{ins}\,r} = \frac{1}{h_o\,r^2}$$ Multiply both sides by $k_{ins} h_o r^2$: $$h_o\,r = k_{ins}$$ $$r = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$ $$\boxed{\;r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}\;}$$ ### Step 4 — Confirm it is a minimum (second derivative test) $$\frac{d^2 R_{tot}}{dr^2} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[-\frac{1}{k_{ins}\,r^2} + \frac{2}{h_o\,r^3}\right]$$ At $r = k_{ins}/h_o$: $$\frac{d^2 R_{tot}}{dr^2}\bigg|_{r=r_{cr}} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[-\frac{h_o^2}{k_{ins}^3} + \frac{2 h_o^3}{h_o\,k_{ins}^3}\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\cdot\frac{h_o^2}{k_{ins}^3} > 0$$ > ✓ The second derivative is **positive**, confirming that $R_{tot}$ is **minimised** at $r = r_{cr}$, and therefore $\dot{Q}$ is **maximised** at $r = r_{cr}$. ### Step 5 — Physical interpretation - If the **insulation outer radius $r < r_{cr}$**: adding more insulation **increases** the heat loss. (The added surface area dominates the added conduction resistance.) - If $r > r_{cr}$: adding more insulation **decreases** the heat loss, as expected. - If the **bare pipe radius $r_i$ is already greater than $r_{cr}$**: any insulation will reduce heat loss from the start. - If $r_i < r_{cr}$: there is an "unfavourable" range where adding insulation initially *hurts* before *helting*. > [!TIP] > **Practical rule for the board exam:** Always check whether $r_i \ge r_{cr}$. If yes → insulation always helps. If $r_i < r_{cr}$ → the engineer should first add insulation up to $r_{cr}$ *knowing* that heat loss peaks there, then add more beyond $r_{cr}$ to actually reduce it. --- ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Composite (Multi-Layer) Cylinders For a pipe with two concentric layers (e.g., metal pipe + insulation), let the inner radius be $r_1$, interface radius $r_2$, outer radius $r_3$, conductivities $k_1$ and $k_2$. At steady state, the same $\dot{Q}$ passes through each layer. Adding resistances in series: $$R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_3 L}$$ Then: $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{tot}}$$ And the interface temperature $T_2$ is found from: $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_2}{R_{pipe}} = \frac{2\pi k_1 L\,(T_i - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ Solving for $T_2$: $$T_2 = T_i - \frac{\dot{Q}\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L}$$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Heat Flow Path in an Insulated Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HEAT SOURCE
Inner Fluid at T_i"] B["INNER PIPE WALL
Radius r_i, Conductivity k_pipe"] C["INSULATION LAYER
Outer Radius r_o, Conductivity k_ins"] D["OUTER SURFACE
Temperature T_s"] E["AMBIENT FLUID
Temperature T_inf, Coeff h_o"] F["R_cyl = ln(r_o/r_i) / 2 pi k_ins L"] G["R_conv = 1 / h_o 2 pi r_o L"] H["Total Resistance R_tot = R_cyl + R_conv"] I["Heat Transfer Rate Q = T_i - T_inf / R_tot"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E B -.->|"Conduction"| F D -.->|"Convection"| G F --> H G --> H H --> I style A fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 style B fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style C fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style D fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style E fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style I fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Heat (yellow arrow path) flows from the inner fluid, through the pipe wall, through the insulation, into the ambient. The thermal resistance network is shown as the **dashed lines** linking each physical region to its resistance formula, and these all combine to give the final heat transfer rate $Q$. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for "Should I Add Insulation?" ```mermaid flowchart TD J["Start: Bare pipe radius r_i known
Insulation k_ins known, h_o known"] K["Compute r_cr = k_ins / h_o"] L{"Compare r_i with r_cr"} M["r_i greater than r_cr
Add insulation: heat loss decreases monotonically"] N["r_i less than r_cr
Unfavourable zone: initial insulation INCREASES heat loss"] O["Step 1: Add insulation up to r = r_cr
Heat loss peaks here"] P["Step 2: Add further insulation r greater than r_cr
Heat loss decreases beyond peak"] Q["Final decision: Insulate to desired r greater than r_cr"] J --> K --> L L -->|"Yes"| M L -->|"No"| N --> O --> P --> Q style J fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style K fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style L fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style M fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style N fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style O fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style P fill:#80cbc4,stroke:#004d40,color:#000 style Q fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam answer shortcut:** "If the bare pipe radius $r_i$ is greater than the critical radius $r_{cr} = k/h$, insulation always helps. If $r_i < r_{cr}$, there exists an initial range where insulation increases heat loss, peaking at $r = r_{cr}$, after which heat loss decreases." This three-sentence answer alone fetches 3–4 marks in KTU 14-mark questions. --- ## 4.3 Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical–Thermal Analogy) ``` T_i ----[ R_pipe ]----[ R_ins ]----[ R_conv ]---- T_∞ | | +------------------- Q (constant) -------------+ R_pipe = ln(r_2/r_1) / (2π k_pipe L) R_ins = ln(r_3/r_2) / (2π k_ins L) R_conv = 1 / (h_o · 2π r_3 L) Q = (T_i − T_∞) / (R_pipe + R_ins + R_conv) ``` > [!NOTE] > The electrical–thermal analogy is a **direct mapping**: > - Voltage $\Delta V$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Temperature difference $\Delta T$ > - Current $I$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Heat rate $\dot{Q}$ > - Resistance $R$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ > - Capacitance $C$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Thermal capacitance $C_{th} = mc_p$ > > Series resistances add; parallel resistances combine by the reciprocal rule — **exactly as in an electrical circuit**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]** **State the expression for thermal resistance of a hollow cylinder for one-dimensional steady-state heat conduction. Mention its units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The thermal resistance of a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$ for 1-D radial heat conduction is given by: > > $$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$ > > **SI unit:** $\text{K/W}$ (Kelvin per Watt). **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** - Correct formula: 2 marks - Correct SI unit: 1 mark --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Define critical radius of insulation. Why is it important in the design of insulated pipes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **critical radius of insulation** $r_{cr}$ is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from an insulated cylinder is **maximum** (or equivalently, the total thermal resistance is minimum). Mathematically: > > $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$ > > **Significance:** > - If the bare pipe radius $r_i > r_{cr}$, any additional insulation reduces heat loss. > - If $r_i < r_{cr}$, adding insulation up to $r_{cr}$ first **increases** the heat loss; only beyond $r_{cr}$ does further insulation reduce the heat loss. > - This is critical in the design of **electrical cables, steam pipes, and heat exchanger tubes** to avoid a counterintuitive increase in heat dissipation. **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** - Definition with formula: 1.5 marks - Significance with practical implication: 1.5 marks --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ## ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4 Model QP]** **(a)** Derive the expression for steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction through a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$, with inner and outer surface temperatures $T_i$ and $T_o$ respectively. Hence obtain the expression for thermal resistance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steam pipe of outer diameter $10\,\text{cm}$ is covered with a $5\,\text{cm}$ thick insulation having $k = 0.20\,\text{W/m·K}$. The outer surface of the insulation is exposed to ambient air at $30\,^\circ\text{C}$ with a convection coefficient of $15\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The temperature at the outer surface of the bare pipe is $180\,^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate: (i) the heat loss per metre length of the insulated pipe, and (ii) the critical radius of insulation. Comment on whether the insulation is effective. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks] Refer to **DERIVATION 1** and **DERIVATION 2** in SECTION 3. The model answer is reproduced here in exam-ready form. **Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law in the radial direction: $$\dot{Q} = -k\,(2\pi r L)\,\frac{dT}{dr}$$ **Step 2:** Under steady state, $\dot{Q}$ is constant. Separate variables and integrate from $r_i$ to $r_o$: $$\int_{T_i}^{T_o} dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r}$$ **Step 3:** Perform the integration: $$T_o - T_i = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to get the heat transfer rate: $$\dot{Q} = \frac{2\pi k L\,(T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$$ **Step 5:** By Ohm's-law analogy $\dot{Q} = \Delta T / R_{th}$, identify the thermal resistance: $$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$ > **Mark split:** Steps 1–2: 2 marks; Step 3 (integration): 1.5 marks; Step 4 (final formula): 1.5 marks; Step 5 (resistance identification): 2 marks. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks] **Given data:** - Pipe outer diameter $= 10\,\text{cm} \Rightarrow r_i = 0.05\,\text{m}$ - Insulation thickness $= 5\,\text{cm} \Rightarrow r_o = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10\,\text{m}$ - $k_{ins} = 0.20\,\text{W/m·K}$ - $T_i = 180\,^\circ\text{C}$ (pipe outer surface = inner of insulation) - $T_\infty = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ - $h_o = 15\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ - $L = 1\,\text{m}$ (per metre length) **Step 1 — Compute conduction resistance of insulation:** $$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.10 / 0.05)}{2\pi \times 0.20 \times 1}$$ $$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln 2}{1.2566} = \frac{0.6931}{1.2566} = 0.5516\,\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Compute convective resistance at outer surface:** $$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_o L} = \frac{1}{15 \times 2\pi \times 0.10 \times 1}$$ $$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{9.4248} = 0.1061\,\text{K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Total resistance and heat loss:** $$R_{tot} = R_{ins} + R_{conv} = 0.5516 + 0.1061 = 0.6577\,\text{K/W}$$ $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{tot}} = \frac{180 - 30}{0.6577} = \frac{150}{0.6577}$$ $$\boxed{\;\dot{Q} \approx 228.07\,\text{W per metre length}\;}$$ **Step 4 — Critical radius of insulation:** $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \frac{0.20}{15} = 0.01333\,\text{m} = 1.333\,\text{cm}$$ **Step 5 — Comment on effectiveness:** - The bare pipe outer radius is $r_i = 5\,\text{cm}$, which is **much larger** than $r_{cr} = 1.33\,\text{cm}$. - Since $r_i > r_{cr}$, the insulation is in the "favourable" region — adding insulation will **monotonically decrease** heat loss. - Therefore, the given $5\,\text{cm}$ insulation thickness is **effective** in reducing heat loss from the steam pipe. > **Mark split (Part b):** Step 1: 1.5 marks; Step 2: 1.5 marks; Step 3: 1.5 marks; Step 4: 1 mark; Step 5: 1.5 marks. --- ## ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Model QP]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the critical radius of insulation for a cylindrical pipe. Explain with the help of a graph how heat transfer rate varies with insulation outer radius. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pipe of inner diameter $8\,\text{cm}$ and outer diameter $10\,\text{cm}$ carries a hot fluid. The pipe is made of a material with $k = 50\,\text{W/m·K}$. The pipe is lagged with $4\,\text{cm}$ thick insulation having $k = 0.10\,\text{W/m·K}$. The inner wall of the pipe is at $T_i = 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the ambient temperature is $T_\infty = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $h_o = 8\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Calculate: (i) the heat loss per metre length of the pipe, and (ii) the temperature at the interface between the pipe and insulation. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Critical Radius Derivation [7 marks] **Step 1 — Set up the resistance expression:** For an insulated cylinder with bare radius $r_i$, insulation outer radius $r$, conductivity $k_{ins}$, and outer convection coefficient $h_o$: $$R_{tot}(r) = \frac{\ln(r / r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r L}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate $R_{tot}$ with respect to $r$ and equate to zero:** $$\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{k_{ins}\,r} - \frac{1}{h_o\,r^2}\right] = 0$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $r$:** $$h_o\,r = k_{ins} \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Second derivative test** confirms a minimum in $R_{tot}$, hence a maximum in $\dot{Q}$. **Step 5 — Graphical description:** > [!IMPORTANT] > The heat transfer rate $\dot{Q}$ as a function of insulation outer radius $r$ shows a **maximum at $r = r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$**: > - For $r < r_{cr}$: $\dot{Q}$ **increases** with $r$ (parabolic rise). > - At $r = r_{cr}$: $\dot{Q}$ is **maximum**. > - For $r > r_{cr}$: $\dot{Q}$ **decreases** with $r$ (asymptotic decay toward the un-insulated-bare value). > - As $r \to 0$: $\dot{Q} \to 0$ (no surface, no convection). > - As $r \to \infty$: $\dot{Q} \to 0$ (infinite conduction resistance). > **Mark split (Part a):** Resistance expression: 1.5 marks; Differentiation: 2 marks; Solving for $r_{cr}$: 1 mark; Graph description / physical explanation: 2.5 marks. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks] **Given data:** - Pipe inner radius $r_1 = 4\,\text{cm} = 0.04\,\text{m}$ - Pipe outer radius $r_2 = 5\,\text{cm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$ - $k_{pipe} = 50\,\text{W/m·K}$ - Insulation thickness $= 4\,\text{cm} \Rightarrow r_3 = 0.05 + 0.04 = 0.09\,\text{m}$ - $k_{ins} = 0.10\,\text{W/m·K}$ - $T_i = 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ (inner wall of pipe) - $T_\infty = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ - $h_o = 8\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ - $L = 1\,\text{m}$ **Step 1 — Pipe conduction resistance:** $$R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2\pi k_{pipe} L} = \frac{\ln(0.05/0.04)}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1}$$ $$R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(1.25)}{314.159} = \frac{0.2231}{314.159} = 7.103 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Insulation conduction resistance:** $$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_3 / r_2)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.09/0.05)}{2\pi \times 0.10 \times 1}$$ $$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(1.8)}{0.6283} = \frac{0.5878}{0.6283} = 0.9355\,\text{K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Outer convective resistance:** $$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_3 L} = \frac{1}{8 \times 2\pi \times 0.09 \times 1} = \frac{1}{4.5239} = 0.2210\,\text{K/W}$$ **Step 4 — Total resistance and heat loss:** $$R_{tot} = 7.103 \times 10^{-4} + 0.9355 + 0.2210 = 1.1572\,\text{K/W}$$ $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{tot}} = \frac{250 - 30}{1.1572} = \frac{220}{1.1572}$$ $$\boxed{\;\dot{Q} \approx 190.11\,\text{W per metre length}\;}$$ **Step 5 — Temperature at pipe–insulation interface ($T_2$):** The heat flow from inner pipe wall (at $T_i = 250\,^\circ\text{C}$) to the interface at $T_2$ passes only through the pipe wall resistance: $$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_i - T_2}{R_{pipe}} \;\Rightarrow\; T_i - T_2 = \dot{Q} \times R_{pipe}$$ $$T_i - T_2 = 190.11 \times 7.103 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1350\,^\circ\text{C}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_2 \approx 250 - 0.135 \approx 249.87\,^\circ\text{C}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Almost the entire temperature drop occurs across the insulation layer, not the metallic pipe. This is because $k_{pipe} \gg k_{ins}$, so $R_{pipe} \ll R_{ins}$. The metal is an excellent thermal conductor; the insulation is the bottleneck. > **Mark split (Part b):** Step 1: 1 mark; Step 2: 1.5 marks; Step 3: 1 mark; Step 4: 1.5 marks; Step 5 (interface temperature): 2 marks. --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > **Pitfall 1 — Mixing up $r_i$ and $r_o$:** Many students confuse the **inner radius of the cylinder** with the **inner radius of the insulation layer**. In an insulated pipe problem, the bare pipe outer radius is the **starting radius for the insulation** — that is, the inner radius of the insulation. The outer radius of the insulation is the **outer radius for the convective resistance calculation**. > > **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the $\ln$ function:** Students occasionally write $T(r) = T_i - (T_i - T_o)\,(r-r_i)/(r_o - r_i)$ (a linear profile). This is the **plane-wall** formula and is **wrong** for cylinders. The correct cylindrical profile is **logarithmic**. > > **Pitfall 3 — Unit mismatch:** $k$ is in $\text{W/m·K}$ and $h$ is in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Mixing them is an instant 1-mark deduction. Also, ensure radius and length are converted from cm to **metres** before substituting. > > **Pitfall 4 — Wrong formula for $r_{cr}$:** Some students write $r_{cr} = h/k$ (inverted). The correct expression is $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$. > > **Pitfall 5 — Ignoring the outer convection resistance:** A common error is to compute heat loss using **only the conduction resistance** of the insulation, omitting the $R_{conv} = 1/(h_o \cdot 2\pi r_o L)$ term. This gives a **higher** (incorrect) heat loss value. The KTU marking scheme deducts 2 marks for this omission. > > **Pitfall 6 — Sign convention in derivation:** $T_i > T_o$ for outward heat flow. If the student writes $T_o - T_i$ in the numerator, the **negative sign** in Fourier's law compensates, but the final $\dot{Q}$ value should be **positive**. Always state the assumed direction explicitly. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute KTU exam revision — the 12 most critical points on Heat Conduction in Cylinders:** > > 1. **Governing equation:** $\dot{Q} = 2\pi k L\,(T_i - T_o) / \ln(r_o / r_i)$ — derived from Fourier's law + steady-state energy balance. > 2. **Temperature profile is logarithmic**, not linear: $T(r) = T_i - (T_i - T_o)\,\ln(r/r_i) / \ln(r_o/r_i)$. > 3. **Thermal resistance** of a hollow cylinder: $R_{cyl} = \ln(r_o/r_i) / (2\pi k L)$ — SI unit: $\text{K/W}$. > 4. **Electrical–thermal analogy:** $\dot{Q} \leftrightarrow I$, $\Delta T \leftrightarrow \Delta V$, $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R$. Series resistances add. > 5. **Composite cylinders:** Sum the $\ln(r_{n+1}/r_n)/(2\pi k_n L)$ terms for each layer, plus $1/(h_o \cdot 2\pi r_{outer} L)$ for outer convection. > 6. **Critical radius of insulation:** $r_{cr} = k_{ins} / h_o$ — the radius at which heat loss is maximum. > 7. **Effectiveness rule:** If bare radius $r_i > r_{cr}$, insulation always reduces heat loss. If $r_i < r_{cr}$, there is a transient "unfavourable" range before insulation starts helping. > 8. **Asymptotic behaviour:** $\dot{Q} \to 0$ as $r \to 0$ (no surface) and as $r \to \infty$ (infinite conduction resistance). > 9. **Units discipline:** Convert all lengths to **metres** before substitution. $k$ in $\text{W/m·K}$, $h$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, $L$ in $\text{m}$. > 10. **Assumptions checklist:** Steady-state, 1-D radial, constant $k$, no heat generation, isotropic material. State these at the start of every derivation. > 11. **Practical applications:** Steam pipes, electrical cables, nuclear fuel cladding, heat exchanger tubes, cryogenic tanks. > 12. **Most-tested topics in KTU:** (i) Derivation of $\dot{Q}$ for hollow cylinder, (ii) Numerical on composite cylinder, (iii) Critical radius derivation + numerical, (iv) Interface temperature calculation.

topics.title: Thermal Resistance Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Resistance Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Resistance (R_th)** is the characteristic of a material or a heat transfer path that impedes the flow of thermal energy under a steady temperature gradient. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across the medium to the steady-state heat transfer rate through it.

$$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} \quad \left[\frac{K}{W} \text{ or } \frac{°C}{W}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Standard Board Terminology):**
> *"Thermal resistance is the opposition offered by a medium to the flow of heat. It is analogous to electrical resistance in Ohm's Law, where temperature difference acts as the driving potential and heat flow rate acts as the current."*

### 1.2 The Electrical–Thermal Analogy (Intuitive View)

Imagine an **electrical circuit** with a battery (voltage source) driving current through a resistor. The thermal resistance concept works in exactly the same way, but instead of electrons flowing, we have **thermal energy (heat)** flowing.

| Electrical Domain | Thermal Domain |
|-------------------|----------------|
| Voltage, $V$ [V] | Temperature Difference, $\Delta T$ [K] |
| Current, $I$ [A] | Heat Flow Rate, $Q$ [W] |
| Resistance, $R$ [$\Omega$] | Thermal Resistance, $R_{th}$ [K/W] |
| Ohm's Law: $V = IR$ | Fourier/Newton Law: $\Delta T = Q \cdot R_{th}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of heat flowing through a wall on a hot summer day. The hotter side "pushes" heat through the wall toward the cooler side. Just like a narrow pipe restricts water flow more than a wide pipe, a thick, low-conductivity wall "resists" heat flow more than a thin, high-conductivity wall. This resistance is quantified as the **Thermal Resistance**.

### 1.3 Classification of Thermal Resistances

Based on the mode of heat transfer, thermal resistances are classified into three fundamental categories:

1. **Conduction Thermal Resistance** ($R_{cond}$): Resistance offered by solid media to heat conduction.
2. **Convection Thermal Resistance** ($R_{conv}$): Resistance offered by the fluid boundary layer during convective heat transfer.
3. **Radiation Thermal Resistance** ($R_{rad}$): Resistance encountered during radiative heat exchange between surfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** Whenever a heat transfer problem has **multiple modes** (e.g., convection + conduction + convection), the thermal resistance network approach simplifies the problem enormously — exactly like solving Kirchhoff's laws for an electrical circuit!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind the Formulas

The thermal resistance concept emerges directly from rearranging the governing equations of each heat transfer mode:

- **Fourier's Law** (Conduction): $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ → rearranged to $\Delta T = Q \cdot \dfrac{L}{kA}$
- **Newton's Law of Cooling** (Convection): $Q = hA \Delta T$ → rearranged to $\Delta T = Q \cdot \dfrac{1}{hA}$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** (Radiation, linearized): $Q = h_r A \Delta T$ → rearranged to $\Delta T = Q \cdot \dfrac{1}{h_r A}$

In **every case**, the structure is identical: $\Delta T = Q \cdot R_{th}$. This is why the electrical analogy is so powerful and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any thermal resistance problem in KTU exams. Memorize the geometry-specific conduction resistances — they appear in nearly every Module 4 question paper.

| # | Heat Transfer Mode / Geometry | Governing Equation | Thermal Resistance, $R_{th}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction – Plane Wall | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1-T_2)}{L}$ | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ |
| 2 | Conduction – Cylindrical Wall | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1-T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ |
| 3 | Conduction – Spherical Shell | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1-T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ |
| 4 | Convection (any geometry) | $Q = hA(T_s-T_\infty)$ | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ |
| 5 | Radiation (between surfaces) | $Q = h_r A \Delta T$ | $R_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{h_r A}$ |
| 6 | Composite Plane Wall (series) | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\sum R_i}$ | $R_{tot} = \dfrac{1}{h_1 A}+\sum_{i}\dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}+\dfrac{1}{h_2 A}$ |
| 7 | Overall Heat Transfer Coeff. | $Q = UA \Delta T$ | $R_{tot} = \dfrac{1}{UA}$ |
| 8 | Critical Radius of Insulation | $r_c = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$ | Applies to cylindrical pipes only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $h_r$** in row 5 is the **linearized radiation heat transfer coefficient**:
> $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$

### 2.3 Series, Parallel & Combined Thermal Resistance Networks

#### (a) Series Combination (most common in KTU exams)
When heat flows through multiple layers in sequence (e.g., fluid film → wall → insulation → fluid film):

$$R_{tot} = R_{conv,1} + R_{cond,wall} + R_{cond,ins} + R_{conv,2}$$

$$\frac{1}{UA} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$

#### (b) Parallel Combination
When heat flows **simultaneously** through two or more paths (e.g., convection **and** radiation from the same surface):

$$\frac{1}{R_{tot}} = \frac{1}{R_{conv}} + \frac{1}{R_{rad}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{tot} = \frac{1}{(h + h_r)A}$$

#### (c) Combined Mode (Wall with Convection–Conduction–Convection)

$$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{\dfrac{1}{h_1 A} + \dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 A}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This resistance-network approach is the foundation of designing **building walls, furnace linings, heat exchanger walls, cryogenic insulation, and spacecraft thermal protection systems (TPS)**. Every modern energy-efficient building is analyzed using this method.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Resistance of a Plane Wall (Series Conduction–Convection System)

**Problem Setup:** Steady, one-dimensional heat flow from a hot fluid ($T_{\infty,1}$, $h_1$) through a plane wall ($k$, $L$) to a cold fluid ($T_{\infty,2}$, $h_2$).

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state conditions (temperatures constant with time).
2. One-dimensional heat flow (no edge losses).
3. Constant thermal conductivity ($k$).
4. Perfect thermal contact between wall surfaces and fluids (no contact resistance).

**Step 1 — Write heat flux between hot fluid and wall surface:**

$$Q_1 = h_1 A (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1})$$

Rearranging for temperature drop:

$$T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1} = \frac{Q}{h_1 A} = Q \cdot R_{conv,1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier's Law through the wall (conduction):**

$$Q_2 = \frac{kA(T_{s,1} - T_{s,2})}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{s,1} - T_{s,2} = \frac{QL}{kA} = Q \cdot R_{cond}$$

**Step 3 — Newton's law of cooling on the cold side:**

$$Q_3 = h_2 A (T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2} = \frac{Q}{h_2 A} = Q \cdot R_{conv,2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state energy balance:** $Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = Q$

Add the three temperature drops:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}) &= (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1}) + (T_{s,1} - T_{s,2}) + (T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2}) \\[4pt]
(T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}) &= Q\left[\frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}\right] \\[4pt]
Q &= \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{\dfrac{1}{h_1 A} + \dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify overall thermal resistance:**

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$

Comparing with $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{tot}}$, the result is consistent. The individual terms $1/(h_1A)$, $L/(kA)$, $1/(h_2A)$ are the **series thermal resistances**.

**Step 6 — Define Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):**

$$\frac{1}{UA} = R_{tot} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$

Cancelling the area $A$ on both sides:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_1} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical Geometry)

**Physical Phenomenon:** Adding insulation to a pipe *does not always* reduce heat loss. For small pipe radii, adding insulation can actually **increase** heat transfer until a certain critical radius is reached.

**Setup:** Cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$, insulated to outer radius $r_2$. Outside convection coefficient $h_o$. Ambient temperature $T_\infty$. Pipe outer surface temperature $T_s$ (assumed constant).

**Step 1 — Total thermal resistance from pipe surface to ambient:**

$$R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_2 L)}$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{tot}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

**Step 3 — Find the value of $r_2$ that maximizes heat loss** (i.e., minimizes $R_{tot}$):

Differentiate $R_{tot}$ with respect to $r_2$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr_2} &= \frac{d}{dr_2}\left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{2\pi L h_o r_2}\right] = 0 \\[6pt]
0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} L}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi L h_o}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for critical radius $r_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{k_{ins} r_c} &= \frac{1}{h_o r_c^2} \\[4pt]
h_o r_c &= k_{ins} \\[4pt]
\boxed{r_c = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation for KTU Exams:**
> - If $r_1 < r_c$: Adding insulation **increases** heat loss (counterintuitive!). Heat loss peaks at $r_2 = r_c$.
> - If $r_1 > r_c$: Adding insulation **decreases** heat loss, as expected intuitively.
> - This is why electrical wires (small $r_1$) often use insulation with **low $k_{ins}$** — it shifts $r_c$ to a smaller value, ensuring $r_1 > r_c$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem Type)

**Problem:** A furnace wall is composed of **150 mm of fireclay brick** ($k = 1.05$ W/m·K) followed by **100 mm of insulating brick** ($k = 0.21$ W/m·K) followed by **75 mm of red brick** ($k = 0.70$ W/m·K). The inside and outside heat transfer coefficients are $h_i = 28$ W/m²·K and $h_o = 11$ W/m²·K. Inside air temperature = 1370 K, outside air temperature = 305 K. Calculate:
(a) Rate of heat loss per m² of wall surface.
(b) Temperature at the interface between fireclay and insulating brick.

**Solution — Per m² of wall area (A = 1 m²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{28} + \frac{0.150}{1.05} + \frac{0.100}{0.21} + \frac{0.075}{0.70} + \frac{1}{11} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0357 + 0.1429 + 0.4762 + 0.1071 + 0.0909 \\[4pt]
&= 0.8528 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$U = 1.173 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**(a) Rate of heat loss per m²:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q/A &= U \cdot \Delta T = U(T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}) \\[4pt]
&= 1.173 \times (1370 - 305) \\[4pt]
&= 1.173 \times 1065 \\[4pt]
\boxed{Q/A &= 1249 \text{ W/m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Interface temperature between fireclay and insulating brick ($T_{s,1}$):**

Starting from the hot side and subtracting temperature drops progressively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{s,1} &= T_{\infty,i} - Q\cdot R_{conv,i} \\[4pt]
&= 1370 - 1249 \times 0.0357 \\[4pt]
&= 1370 - 44.59 \\[4pt]
\boxed{T_{s,1} &\approx 1325.4 \text{ K}}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electrical–Thermal Analogy (Block Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the analogy between an electrical circuit and a thermal resistance network. Each block represents a resistance element, and the arrows indicate the flow direction (current ↔ heat flow).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vsrc[Voltage Source V] --> R1[Electrical Resistor R1]
    R1 --> R2[Electrical Resistor R2]
    R2 --> R3[Electrical Resistor R3]
    R3 --> Gnd[Reference Node Ground]

    Tsrc[Temperature Source T infinity 1] --> Rc1[Convection Resistance 1 slash h1 A]
    Rc1 --> Rk[Conduction Resistance L slash kA]
    Rk --> Rc2[Convection Resistance 1 slash h2 A]
    Rc2 --> Tref[Temperature Sink T infinity 2]

    Vsrc -.analogy.-> Tsrc
    R1 -.analogy.-> Rc1
    R2 -.analogy.-> Rk
    R3 -.analogy.-> Rc2
    Gnd -.analogy.-> Tref
```

### 4.2 Thermal Resistance Network for a Composite Wall (Series–Parallel Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOT_SIDE[Hot Fluid Side]
        direction TB
        Th1[Hot Fluid T infinity 1] -->|Q| Rh1[Convection Resistance 1 slash h1 A]
    end

    subgraph WALL[Composite Wall Layers]
        direction TB
        Rh1 --> Rw1[Layer 1 L1 slash k1 A]
        Rw1 --> Rw2[Layer 2 L2 slash k2 A]
        Rw2 --> Rw3[Layer 3 L3 slash k3 A]
    end

    subgraph COLD_SIDE[Cold Fluid Side]
        direction TB
        Rw3 --> Rh2[Convection Resistance 1 slash h2 A]
        Rh2 --> Th2[Cold Fluid T infinity 2]
    end

    HOT_SIDE ~~~ WALL ~~~ COLD_SIDE
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Solving a Thermal Resistance Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Identify Geometry and Modes] --> B[Step 2 List All Resistances]
    B --> C[Step 3 Determine Series or Parallel Configuration]
    C --> D{Configuration Type}
    D -->|Series| E[Step 4a Sum Resistances Rtot equals Sum Ri]
    D -->|Parallel| F[Step 4b Recip Sum 1 slash Rtot equals Sum 1 slash Ri]
    E --> G[Step 5 Compute Heat Rate Q equals Delta T slash Rtot]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 6 Compute Interface Temperatures using Q times Ri]
    H --> I[Final Answer Heat Transfer Rate and Temperature Profile]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams instead of physical drawings?** A precise 3-D rendering of a composite wall with multiple layers and fluid films is impractical in text format. The block-level functional architecture above preserves the **logical flow of information** that examiners test, and adheres to KTU's emphasis on **network-based problem solving**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define thermal resistance. List the thermal resistances for conduction, convection, and radiation, and state their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
