# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define fin efficiency and fin effectiveness. What is the minimum value of effectiveness to justify a fin?

**Model Answer:**

**Fin efficiency** ($\eta_f$) is the ratio of the actual heat dissipated by a fin to the heat that would be dissipated if the entire fin surface were at the base temperature:

$$\eta_f = \frac{q_{fin}}{h A_s \theta_b} = \frac{q_{fin}}{q_{max}}$$

**Fin effectiveness** ($\varepsilon_f$) is the ratio of heat transferred with the fin to the heat transferred without the fin (i.e., from the base area alone):

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_{fin}}{hA_c \theta_b} = \frac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}}$$

The **minimum value of effectiveness** to justify the use of a fin is **2** (commonly cited as 1.5 in some texts, but KTU board standard is $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the assumptions made in the 1-D fin analysis.

**Model Answer:**

1. Steady-state heat transfer.
2. One-dimensional conduction (temperature varies only along fin length x).
3. Constant thermal conductivity of the fin material.
4. No internal heat generation.
5. Constant ambient temperature ($T_{\infty}$).
6. Uniform convection coefficient (h) along the entire fin surface.
7. Constant base temperature $T_b$ (Bi at root < 0.1).
8. Heat transfer from the fin tip is negligible (or treated with corrected length).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by:
> - Forgetting to convert all dimensions to **metres** before calculation.
> - Mixing up fin efficiency ($\eta_f$) and fin effectiveness ($\varepsilon_f$).
> - Using the **long fin** formula when $mL < 2.65$ — this is a *very common* mistake.
> - Forgetting to use the **corrected length** $L_c = L + t/2$ for rectangular fins.
> - Forgetting the **units** of m (m⁻¹) and q (W).

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

A brass fin (k = 110 W/m·K) of rectangular cross-section, 50 mm long, 60 mm wide, and 4 mm thick, is attached to a wall at $200°C$. The fin is exposed to air at $30°C$ with convection coefficient $h = 40$ W/m²·K.

**(a)** Derive the temperature distribution for a rectangular fin with an insulated tip. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate: (i) Fin parameter `m`, (ii) Heat transfer rate per fin, (iii) Fin efficiency, (iv) Fin effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Derivation of temperature distribution for an insulated tip fin:**

**Step 1:** Consider a small element of fin of thickness `dx` at distance x from the base.

**Step 2:** Energy balance (steady state):

$$-kA_c \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_x = -kA_c \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} + hP(T - T_{\infty})dx$$

**Step 3:** Expand by Taylor series and cancel terms:

$$kA_c \frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = hP(T - T_{\infty})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\theta = T - T_{\infty}$:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}}$$

**Step 5:** General solution:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

**Step 6:** Apply BCs:
- At $x = 0$: $\theta = \theta_b$ → $C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b$
- At $x = L$: $\frac{d\theta}{dx} = 0$ → $C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL}$

**Step 7:** Solving simultaneously:

$$C_1 = \frac{\theta_b e^{-mL}}{2\cosh(mL)}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\theta_b e^{mL}}{2\cosh(mL)}$$

**Step 8:** Final temperature distribution:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- $k = 110$ W/m·K, $L = 0.05$ m, $w = 0.06$ m, $t = 0.004$ m
- $T_b = 200°C$, $T_{\infty} = 30°C$, $h = 40$ W/m²·K
- $\theta_b = 170$ K

**Step (i) — Fin parameter m:**

$A_c = w \cdot t = 0.06 \times 0.004 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

$P \approx 2w = 0.12$ m (thin fin assumption)

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{40 \times 0.12}{110 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.8}{0.0264}} = \sqrt{181.82} = 13.49 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step (ii) — Heat transfer rate q:**

$mL = 13.49 \times 0.05 = 0.6745$

$\tanh(0.6745) = 0.5893$

$$q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$$
$$= \sqrt{40 \times 0.12 \times 110 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4}} \times 170 \times 0.5893$$
$$= \sqrt{0.1267} \times 170 \times 0.5893 = 0.3559 \times 100.18 = 35.66 \text{ W}$$

**Step (iii) — Fin efficiency:**

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{0.5893}{0.6745} = 0.8737 = 87.37\%$$

**Step (iv) — Fin effectiveness:**

$q_{no\,fin} = hA_c\theta_b = 40 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 170 = 1.632$ W

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{35.66}{1.632} = 21.85 \quad (\text{much greater than 2 — excellent fin})$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

A cylindrical pin fin of diameter 10 mm and length 50 mm is made of aluminium ($k = 205$ W/m·K). The fin base is at $150°C$ and ambient air is at $40°C$ with $h = 30$ W/m²·K.

**(a)** Derive the expression for fin efficiency of a pin fin and explain what is meant by "long fin" approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute: (i) Fin parameter m, (ii) Temperature at the tip, (iii) Heat dissipation per fin, (iv) Fin efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** For a pin fin, $A_c = \pi D^2/4$ and $P = \pi D$.

**Step 2:** Fin parameter simplifies to:

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{h(\pi D)}{k(\pi D^2/4)}} = \sqrt{\frac{4h}{kD}}$$

**Step 3:** Heat transfer rate (insulated tip) for a pin fin is identical in form to a rectangular fin:

$$q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$$

**Step 4:** Maximum possible heat dissipation (if entire surface at $T_b$):

$$q_{max} = hA_s\theta_b = h(\pi DL)\theta_b$$

**Step 5:** Fin efficiency:

$$\eta_f = \frac{q_{fin}}{q_{max}} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

**Step 6 — Long fin approximation:** When $mL \geq 2.65$, $\tanh(mL) \to 1$, and the temperature at the tip essentially equals $T_{\infty}$. The fin behaves as if it is infinitely long, and efficiency simplifies to $\eta_f \approx 1/(mL)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $D = 0.01$ m, $L = 0.05$ m, $k = 205$ W/m·K, $T_b = 150°C$, $T_{\infty} = 40°C$, $h = 30$ W/m²·K, $\theta_b = 110$ K.

**Step (i) — m:**

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{4h}{kD}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 30}{205 \times 0.01}} = \sqrt{\frac{120}{2.05}} = \sqrt{58.54} = 7.65 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step (ii) — Tip temperature:**

$mL = 7.65 \times 0.05 = 0.3825$

$\tanh(0.3825) = 0.3643$

$\cosh(0.3825) = 1.0747$

$$\frac{\theta_{tip}}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh(0)}{\cosh(mL)} = \frac{1}{1.0747} = 0.9305$$

$\theta_{tip} = 0.9305 \times 110 = 102.36$ K

$$T_{tip} = T_{\infty} + \theta_{tip} = 40 + 102.36 = 142.36°C$$

**Step (iii) — Heat dissipation:**

$A_c = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (0.01)^2/4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$ m²

$P = \pi D = 0.03142$ m

$$q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$$
$$= \sqrt{30 \times 0.03142 \times 205 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}} \times 110 \times 0.3643$$
$$= \sqrt{0.01518} \times 110 \times 0.3643 = 0.1232 \times 40.07 = 4.94 \text{ W}$$

**Step (iv) — Fin efficiency:**

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{0.3643}{0.3825} = 0.9525 = 95.25\%$$

Since $mL = 0.3825 < 2.65$, the long fin approximation is *not* valid here.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

### Core Concepts
- A **fin** is an extended surface used to **increase** heat dissipation by increasing effective area.
- The **fin parameter** $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ controls temperature decay.
- **Long fin** ≈ $mL \geq 2.65$ (use simpler exponential formula).
- **Insulated tip** is the **standard textbook** boundary condition.

### Essential Formulas (Must Memorise)

1. $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$ — Insulated tip distribution
2. $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = e^{-mx}$ — Long fin distribution
3. $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$ — Insulated tip heat rate
4. $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b$ — Long fin heat rate
5. $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ — Fin efficiency
6. $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{q_{with}}{q_{without}}$ — Fin effectiveness
7. $L_c = L + t/2$ — Corrected length (rectangular fin)

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Don't forget to convert mm to m in every calculation.
- ❌ Don't use the "long fin" formula when $mL < 2.65$.
- ❌ Don't confuse efficiency with effectiveness.
- ❌ Don't forget that the perpendicular (lateral) convection area for a thin rectangular fin is $A_s \approx 2wL$ (not $2wL + 2wt$).
- ❌ For pin fins: $m = \sqrt{4h/(kD)}$ — different form.

### Key Numerical Benchmarks
- **Copper** k ≈ 401 W/m·K (best fin material)
- **Aluminium** k ≈ 205 W/m·K (most common, cheapest)
- **Steel** k ≈ 45 W/m·K (poor fin material — avoid for high-performance fins)
- **Effectiveness threshold** for fin use: $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$
- **Optimal efficiency** usually at $mL \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$

### Application Areas
- Air-cooled engines, transformers, electronics, refrigerators, HVAC coils, cryogenic systems, solar collectors.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "derive," always start with the 1-D energy balance on a small element, derive the second-order ODE, present the general solution, then explicitly state and apply the **two boundary conditions** before solving for constants. This structure guarantees full marks for derivation questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fin Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fin Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **extended surface** (or **fin**) is a surface of high thermal conductivity that is intentionally attached to a primary heat-transfer surface to increase the rate of heat transfer between the surface and the surrounding fluid by increasing the effective surface area.

**Fin Efficiency (η_fin)** is formally defined as the ratio of the *actual heat dissipated* by the fin to the heat that *would* be dissipated if the **entire fin surface were maintained at the base temperature** (T₀):

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{actual}}{q_{max}} = \dfrac{q_{actual}}{h \, A_{fin} \, \theta_{b}}$$

where:
- $q_{actual}$ = real heat transfer rate from the fin [W]
- $q_{max}$ = hypothetical maximum heat transfer if $T(x) = T_0$ everywhere along the fin [W]
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient [W/m²·K]
- $A_{fin}$ = total exposed surface area of the fin [m²]
- $\theta_b = T_0 - T_\infty$ = base temperature excess over ambient [K or °C]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Standard:** Fin efficiency is a number **always between 0 and 1** (or 0 to 100%). A value of **1.0** means every point of the fin is at the base temperature (ideal but physically impossible for a finite fin). A value of **0** would mean no heat is transferred at all.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Radiator" Mental Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Auto-Rickshaw vs. The Bullet Train Cooling Fin**
> Imagine two metal plates of the *same weight* and *same base temperature* placed in moving air:
> 1. A plain flat plate of area 1 m².
> 2. A plate that has been stretched out into 50 thin "fingers" (fins) totalling 5 m² of surface.
>
> At first glance, you would expect 5× more cooling from the finned plate. **But it isn't so!**
> The *tips* of those fingers are far from the base and have cooled significantly along their length. So the **tip regions are not very effective** at shedding heat — they are *almost at ambient temperature*. Fin efficiency tells you exactly **how much** of that "ideal" 5× boost is actually being realised.
> - η = 1.0 → every point on the fin is as hot as the base (unrealistic).
> - η = 0.5 → the fin is delivering only half the heat you would get if the entire fin were at the base temperature.

---

## 1.3 Why Do Engineers Need Fins?

Fins are used whenever the surface heat-transfer coefficient $h$ is **low** (e.g., gas-side convection in air-cooled engines, transformer housings, motorcycle cylinder heads, CPU heat sinks, condenser tubes of refrigerators). Adding fins compensates for the low $h$ by providing **additional surface area**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Fin Analysis (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Thermal conductivity of common fin materials:** Aluminium ≈ **k = 200 W/m·K**; Copper ≈ **k = 385–400 W/m·K**; Stainless steel ≈ **k = 16 W/m·K**.
> - **Typical h values:** Natural convection in air ≈ **5–25 W/m²·K**; Forced convection in air ≈ **25–250 W/m²·K**; Forced convection in oil ≈ **100–1500 W/m²·K**.
> - **Benchmark efficiency threshold for "good" fins:** $\eta_{fin} \geq 0.60$ (i.e., ≥ 60 %).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Assumptions

To derive fin equations, the following standard assumptions are used (these appear verbatim in KTU 2024 model answers):

1. **Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction** along the fin (temperature varies along x only).
2. **Constant thermal conductivity** $k$ of fin material.
3. **Uniform heat-transfer coefficient** $h$ over the entire fin surface.
4. **No heat generation** within the fin volume.
5. **Constant ambient (fluid) temperature** $T_\infty$.
6. **Constant cross-sectional area** $A_c$ and perimeter $P$ (for uniform fins).

## 2.2 Why a Temperature Gradient Exists Along the Fin

The driving potential for heat loss at any section $x$ is $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$. As you move away from the base ($x = 0$), energy is progressively drained to the fluid by convection. Hence, $\theta(x)$ decays, reaching its **minimum at the tip** ($x = L$).

## 2.3 The "m" Parameter — the Heart of Fin Analysis

By performing an energy balance on a differential fin element, you obtain the master parameter:

$$m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h P}{k A_c}} \quad \left[\text{m}^{-1}\right]$$

- $h$ → convective coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $P$ → fin perimeter (m)
- $k$ → fin thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $A_c$ → fin cross-sectional area (m²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $m$:** $m$ is a **measure of how rapidly temperature decays** along the fin. A high $mL$ value means the fin cools off quickly (low efficiency), while a low $mL$ value means the fin stays hot all the way to the tip (high efficiency).

## 2.4 The General Solution of the Fin ODE

The governing 1-D conduction equation is:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} - m^{2}\theta = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 \, e^{mx} + C_2 \, e^{-mx} = A \, \cosh(mx) + B \, \sinh(mx)$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Direct from KTU Board Pattern)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Master parameter** | $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ | m⁻¹ | Core of all fin analysis |
| 2 | **Corrected length (convective tip)** | $L_c = L + A_c / P$ | m | Used when tip is NOT insulated |
| 3 | **Fin Efficiency (uniform cross-section, insulated tip)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ | dimensionless | **Most asked in KTU** |
| 4 | **Fin Efficiency (with corrected length)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}$ | dimensionless | Generalised form |
| 5 | **Fin Efficiency (infinitely long fin, $mL \to \infty$)** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{mL}$ | dimensionless | Approximation for long fins |
| 6 | **Fin Effectiveness** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}} = \dfrac{\eta \, A_{fin}}{A_b}$ | dimensionless | Should be $\varepsilon > 1$ to be useful |
| 7 | **Heat transferred by fin (insulated tip)** | $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh(mL)$ | W | Standard fin heat-loss formula |
| 8 | **Pin fin (circular cross-section)** | $m = \sqrt{4h/(kD)}$ | m⁻¹ | $P = \pi D,\ A_c = \pi D^{2}/4$ |
| 9 | **Rectangular longitudinal fin (thin)** | $m = \sqrt{2h/(kt)}$ | m⁻¹ | $P \approx 2w,\ A_c = w t$ |
| 10 | **Triangular fin (linear taper)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$ where $\phi = 1.4192 \sqrt{h/(k)}\,L^{1/2}\,t^{-1/2}$ | dimensionless | $t$ = base thickness |
| 11 | **Parabolic profile fin** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\psi)}{\psi}$ where $\psi = 1.189\,\sqrt{h/(k)}\,L^{1/2}\,t^{-1/2}$ | dimensionless | Optimum profile for given mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — Efficiency vs Effectiveness (Most Confused in KTU Exams):**
> - **Fin Efficiency (η):** Compares actual fin heat transfer to the *ideal* case (whole fin at base temperature). Always **η ≤ 1**.
> - **Fin Effectiveness (ε):** Compares fin heat transfer to that from the *bare surface* (no fin at all). A fin is **worth using only if ε > 1**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why fins are used | Typical fin material |
|---|---|---|
| **Motorcycle / Scooter engine cylinder heads** | Air-cooling with low h | Aluminium alloy |
| **Air-cooled transformer tanks** | Natural convection of air | Mild steel with welded fins |
| **Refrigerator condenser coils** | Phase-change heat rejection to room air | Copper / Aluminium |
| **Power electronic heat sinks** | CPU / IGBT chip cooling | Anodised aluminium |
| **Nuclear reactor fuel pins** | Internal boiling heat transfer | Zirconium alloy |
| **Jet engine turbine blades** | Internal convection + radiation | Nickel superalloy with internal cooling passages |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Temperature Distribution and Efficiency of a Uniform Cross-Section Fin with Insulated Tip

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 4 of PCPET303. Practice it until you can reproduce it blindfolded — it carries 7–14 marks in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance on a Differential Element

Consider a fin element of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the base. Heat enters at $x$ by conduction ($q_x$) and leaves at $x+dx$ by conduction ($q_{x+dx}$), while the lateral surface loses heat by convection.

$$q_{x} = q_{x+dx} + dq_{conv}$$

### Step 2 — Express Each Term

By Fourier's law of conduction:

$$q_{x} = -k A_c \dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x}$$

$$q_{x+dx} = -k A_c \dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx}$$

The convective loss from the lateral surface of the element is:

$$dq_{conv} = h \, P \, (T - T_\infty) \, dx = h \, P \, \theta(x) \, dx$$

### Step 3 — Form the Differential Equation

Substituting and simplifying (Taylor expansion gives $q_{x+dx} = q_x + \dfrac{dq_x}{dx}dx$):

$$-kA_c \dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = hP(T - T_\infty)$$

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}\,\theta = m^{2}\theta$$

Rearranged into the standard fin ODE:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} - m^{2}\theta = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the ODE

The general solution (auxiliary equation $r^2 - m^2 = 0 \Rightarrow r = \pm m$) is:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

Equivalently in hyperbolic form:

$$\theta(x) = A \cosh(mx) + B \sinh(mx)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Boundary Conditions

**BC-1 (Base temperature, $x = 0$):**

$$\theta(0) = \theta_b = T_0 - T_\infty \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = \theta_b$$

**BC-2 (Insulated tip, $x = L$):**

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$$

Differentiating $\theta(x) = \theta_b \cosh(mx) + B \sinh(mx)$ and setting the derivative to zero at $x = L$:

$$m \theta_b \sinh(mL) + m B \cosh(mL) = 0$$

$$B = -\theta_b \,\dfrac{\sinh(mL)}{\cosh(mL)} = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

### Step 6 — Final Temperature Distribution

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh\bigl(m(L - x)\bigr)}{\cosh(mL)}\;}$$

> **Physical check:** At $x = 0$, RHS = $\cosh(mL)/\cosh(mL) = 1$ ✓. At $x = L$, RHS = $1/\cosh(mL)$, which is the minimum temperature excess — the tip is coolest, exactly as expected.

### Step 7 — Heat Transfer Rate at the Base

$$q_{fin} = -kA_c \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0}$$

Differentiating $\theta(x) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh(m(L-x))}{\cosh(mL)}$ and evaluating at $x = 0$:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = -\theta_b \, m \tanh(mL)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh(mL)\;}$$

### Step 8 — Maximum Possible Heat Transfer (entire fin at base temperature)

$$q_{max} = h A_{fin} \theta_b$$

### Step 9 — Fin Efficiency

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{max}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_{fin}}$$

For a long thin fin where $A_{fin} \approx P L$ (tip area neglected):

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hPL} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\;}$$

### Step 10 — Limiting Cases

**Case 1: Infinitely long fin ($mL \to \infty$):**
Since $\tanh(mL) \to 1$:
$$\eta = \dfrac{1}{mL}$$

**Case 2: Very short fin ($mL \to 0$):**
Using Taylor expansion $\tanh(mL) \approx mL - (mL)^3/3$:
$$\eta \to 1$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Corrected Length $L_c$ (Convective Tip Condition)

If the tip is **NOT insulated** (it loses heat to the surrounding fluid), the actual BC-2 becomes:

$$-kA_c \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = hA_c \theta(L)$$

This makes the algebra messier. The standard trick is to **replace $L$ with a corrected length** $L_c$:

$$L_c = L + \dfrac{A_c}{P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $L_c$:** It is the equivalent *fictional* length of an insulated-tip fin that would dissipate the same amount of heat. The added term $A_c/P$ has units of length, and represents the "extra" effective fin length due to tip convection. For thin fins where $A_c/P$ is small, $L_c \approx L$.

With this substitution, the insulated-tip formula works for the convective-tip case as well:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}, \qquad q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL_c)$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Fin Efficiency for a Triangular (Linearly Tapered) Fin

For a triangular fin whose thickness varies as $t(x) = t_b(1 - x/L)$:

The governing ODE becomes more complex, but a closed-form expression (cited in Incropera) is:

$$\eta_{triangular} = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}, \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = 1.4192 \sqrt{\dfrac{h}{k}} \cdot \dfrac{L^{1/2}}{t_b^{1/2}}$$

For a **parabolic** profile (the optimum shape for minimum material):

$$\eta_{parabolic} = \dfrac{\tanh(\psi)}{\psi}, \quad \text{where} \quad \psi = 1.189 \sqrt{\dfrac{h}{k}} \cdot \dfrac{L^{1/2}}{t_b^{1/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 7-mark question is to compare the efficiency of uniform, triangular, and parabolic fins of equal mass and material. The expected answer: **parabolic > triangular > uniform rectangular** in efficiency for the same fin volume.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A stainless-steel pin fin of diameter $D = 6$ mm and length $L = 50$ mm is attached to a base at $T_0 = 200°C$. The fin is exposed to air at $T_\infty = 30°C$ with $h = 50$ W/m²·K. The tip is insulated. Take $k = 16$ W/m·K. Calculate (a) the fin efficiency, (b) the heat dissipated per fin, and (c) the fin effectiveness if the bare tube area is $A_b = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry**
$P = \pi D = \pi(0.006) = 0.01885$ m
$A_c = \pi D^2/4 = \pi(0.006)^2/4 = 2.827 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
$A_{fin} = \pi D L + A_c = 0.01885(0.05) + 2.827 \times 10^{-5} = 9.425 \times 10^{-4} + 2.827 \times 10^{-5} = 9.71 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$\theta_b = 200 - 30 = 170$ K

**Step 2 — Compute $m$ and $mL$**
$m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.01885 \,/\, (16 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5})} = \sqrt{0.9425/4.523\times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{2083.7} = 45.65$ m⁻¹
$mL = 45.65 \times 0.05 = 2.282$

**Step 3 — Fin efficiency**
$\tanh(2.282) = 0.9783$
$\eta = \tanh(mL)/(mL) = 0.9783/2.282 = 0.4287$ → **42.87 %**

**Step 4 — Heat dissipated**
$q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$
$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.01885 \times 16 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{4.262 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.528 \times 10^{-3}$ W/K
$q = 6.528 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 \times 0.9783 = 1.085$ W

**Step 5 — Fin effectiveness**
$q_{no\,fin} = h A_b \theta_b = 50 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 = 12.75$ W
$\varepsilon = q/q_{no\,fin} = 1.085/12.75 = 0.0851$

> [!WARNING]
> **Here ε < 1!** That means this fin is *not* worth using for that particular base area. Always check effectiveness before declaring a fin useful.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Balance Schematic (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base at Temperature T0] -->|Conduction qx| B[Fin Element at x]
    B -->|Conduction qx+dx| C[Adjacent Element at x+dx]
    B -->|Convection h dA theta x dx| D[Ambient Fluid at T infinity]
    C -->|Convection at x+dx| D
    E[Boundary Conditions] --> F[Insulated Tip dT/dx 0 at x L]
    E --> G[Convective Tip hA theta at x L]
    E --> H[Fixed Tip Temperature T at x L]
    E --> I[Infinitely Long Fin mL infinity]
    F --> J[Solve for theta x]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Heat Transfer q at x 0]
    K --> L[Compute Fin Efficiency eta]
    K --> M[Compute Fin Effectiveness epsilon]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The base acts as a heat reservoir. Heat flows from the base into the fin by conduction and is progressively lost to the surrounding fluid. Boundary conditions at the tip determine which analytical solution applies.

---

## 4.2 Classification of Fins (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXTENDED SURFACES - FINS] --> B[By Geometry of Attachment]
    A --> C[By Profile Shape]
    A --> D[By Heat Flow Direction]
    A --> E[By Tip Boundary Condition]
    B --> B1[Pin or Spine Fin - Circular cross section]
    B --> B2[Longitudinal or Straight Fin - Rectangular cross section along tube]
    B --> B3[Annular or Circumferential Fin - Ring around a tube]
    C --> C1[Constant Cross Section - Uniform thickness]
    C --> C2[Triangular Profile - Linearly tapering thickness]
    C --> C3[Parabolic Profile - Curved taper optimum]
    C --> C4[Pin Fin with Concave or Hyperbolic Profile]
    D --> D1[One Dimensional Heat Flow - Long thin fin]
    D --> D2[Two Dimensional Heat Flow - Short thick fin]
    D --> D3[Radial Heat Flow - Annular fin]
    E --> E1[Insulated Tip - dtheta dx 0 at x L]
    E --> E2[Convective Tip - Use Corrected Length Lc]
    E --> E3[Temperature Specified Tip - Rare industrial case]
    E --> E4[Infinitely Long Fin - Mathematical idealisation]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Fins can be classified along four independent axes. KTU questions often ask you to draw and label a specific fin type and state the relevant efficiency formula.

---

## 4.3 Fin Analysis Decision Tree (Step-by-Step Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin Fin Analysis] --> Q1{What is the fin profile?}
    Q1 -->|Uniform| R1[Use eta equals tanh mL over mL]
    Q1 -->|Triangular| R2[Use eta equals tanh phi over phi with phi 1.42]
    Q1 -->|Parabolic| R3[Use eta equals tanh psi over psi with psi 1.19]
    R1 --> Q2{Insulated or convective tip?}
    Q2 -->|Insulated| S1[Lc equals L]
    Q2 -->|Convective| S2[Lc equals L plus Ac over P]
    S1 --> S3[Compute mL then eta]
    S2 --> S3
    R2 --> S3
    R3 --> S3
    S3 --> Q3{mL very small less than 0.2?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| T1[Eta approx equals 1 ideal fin]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{mL very large greater than 5?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| T2[Eta approx equals 1 over mL infinitely long]
    Q4 -->|No| T3[Use tanh table or calculator]
    T1 --> End[Report eta and q]
    T2 --> End
    T3 --> End
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the practical workflow you should follow when solving any numerical fin problem in the KTU exam. Following this order minimises algebraic errors.

---

## 4.4 Temperature Distribution Along the Fin (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base x equals 0 - Temperature T0] -->|Conduction and convection loss| B[Mid Section x equals L over 2 - Temperature drops sharply]
    B -->|Continued convection| C[Tip x equals L - Lowest temperature]
    C --> D[If infinitely long - Approaches T infinity asymptotically]
    A -->|Ideal case| E[Entire fin at T0 for q max reference]
    E --> F[Fin efficiency eta equals actual q over ideal q]
    F --> G[Typical values eta 0.4 to 0.9]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define fin efficiency. Explain why the fin efficiency is always less than unity for a practical fin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fin efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual heat dissipated by a fin to the heat that would be dissipated if the entire fin were maintained at the base temperature:

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{actual}}{h A_{fin} \theta_b}$$

For a practical (finite-length) fin, the temperature at any point away from the base is always less than $T_0$ because heat is continuously lost to the fluid along the fin's length. Therefore, the actual driving temperature difference $(\theta = T - T_\infty)$ is less than $\theta_b$ at every section except $x = 0$. Consequently, $q_{actual} < q_{max}$ and $\eta_{fin} < 1$ (always). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between fin efficiency and fin effectiveness. Why is a fin considered useful only when its effectiveness is greater than one?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Definition | Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Fin efficiency (η)** | $\eta = q_{actual} / q_{max}$ | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| **Fin effectiveness (ε)** | $\varepsilon = q_{fin} / q_{no\,fin}$ | $\varepsilon > 1$ desired |

A fin is considered **useful** only when $\varepsilon > 1$, because if $\varepsilon \le 1$ the fin transfers *no more* heat than the bare surface it replaces — the extra material, weight, cost and pressure drop would be unjustified. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Starting from the basic conduction equation, derive the expression for the temperature distribution $\theta(x)$ along a straight rectangular fin of uniform cross-section with an **insulated tip**. State the boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stainless-steel pin fin of diameter **D = 5 mm** and length **L = 50 mm** is attached to a base at **T₀ = 200 °C**. The fin is exposed to air at **T∞ = 30 °C** with **h = 40 W/m²·K** and the tip is insulated. Take **k = 16 W/m·K**. Calculate (i) fin efficiency, (ii) heat dissipated per fin, and (iii) fin effectiveness if the corresponding bare area is $A_b = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Energy balance on differential element (2 marks)**

$$-kA_c \dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = hP(T - T_\infty)$$

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$ and $m^2 = hP/(kA_c)$:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} - m^{2}\theta = 0$$

**Step 2 — General solution (1 mark)**

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx} = A \cosh(mx) + B \sinh(mx)$$

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions (2 marks)**

- At $x = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_b$ → $A = \theta_b$
- At $x = L$: $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$ (insulated tip) → $B = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$

**Step 4 — Final temperature distribution (2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh\bigl(m(L-x)\bigr)}{\cosh(mL)}\;}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Geometry (1 mark)**
$P = \pi D = \pi(0.005) = 0.01571$ m
$A_c = \pi D^{2}/4 = 1.963 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
$A_{fin} = \pi D L + A_c = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} + 1.963 \times 10^{-5} = 8.05 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$\theta_b = 170$ K

**Step 2 — Compute $m$ and $mL$ (1 mark)**
$m = \sqrt{40 \times 0.01571 / (16 \times 1.963\times 10^{-5})} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72$ m⁻¹
$mL = 44.72 \times 0.05 = 2.236$

**Step 3 — Fin efficiency (2 marks)**
$\tanh(2.236) = 0.9767$
$\eta = 0.9767 / 2.236 = 0.4368$ → **43.68 %**

**Step 4 — Heat dissipated (2 marks)**
$q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL) = \sqrt{40 \times 0.01571 \times 16 \times 1.963\times 10^{-5}} \times 170 \times 0.9767$
$\sqrt{1.973 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01405$ W/K
$q = 0.01405 \times 170 \times 0.9767 = \mathbf{2.333\ W}$

**Step 5 — Fin effectiveness (1 mark)**
$q_{no\,fin} = 40 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 = 8.16$ W
$\varepsilon = 2.333/8.16 = 0.286$
Since $\varepsilon < 1$, the fin is **not worthwhile** for this particular base area.

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **four standard boundary conditions** used in the analysis of extended surfaces. For each case, state the corresponding expression for the fin efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminium circumferential fin of rectangular cross-section (**t = 10 mm** thick, **outside diameter 100 mm**) is fitted to a tube of **inner diameter 50 mm**. The fin is exposed to air at **T∞ = 30 °C** with **h = 50 W/m²·K**, the tube surface is at **T₀ = 150 °C**, and the tip is insulated. Take **k = 200 W/m·K**. Determine (i) heat dissipation per fin, (ii) fin efficiency, and (iii) fin effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

| S.No. | BC Type | Condition at $x = L$ | Efficiency Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Insulated tip** | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = 0$ | $\eta = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$ **(most common)** |
| 2 | **Convective tip** (long fin approx.) | $-kA_c\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = hA_c\theta$ | $\eta = \tanh(mL_c)/(mL_c)$ with $L_c = L + A_c/P$ |
| 3 | **Fixed tip temperature** | $\theta(L) = \theta_L$ (specified) | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \tanh(mL)/(mL)\,(\theta_L/\theta_b - 1)}$ |
| 4 | **Infinitely long fin** | $\theta(L) \to 0$ as $L \to \infty$ | $\eta = 1/(mL)$ |

(1 mark each for the four BCs, 3 marks for the matching formulas)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Geometry (1 mark)**
$r_1 = 0.025$ m, $r_2 = 0.050$ m, $t = 0.010$ m
$L = r_2 - r_1 = 0.025$ m, $\theta_b = 150 - 30 = 120$ K
For thin annular fin: $m = \sqrt{2h/(kt)} = \sqrt{2 \times 50/(200 \times 0.010)} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071$ m⁻¹

**Step 2 — Corrected length and $mL_c$ (1 mark)**
$L_c = L + t/2 = 0.025 + 0.005 = 0.030$ m
$mL_c = 7.071 \times 0.030 = 0.2121$

**Step 3 — Fin efficiency (1 mark)**
$\tanh(0.2121) = 0.2092$
$\eta = 0.2092/0.2121 = 0.9863$ → **98.63 %**

**Step 4 — Heat dissipation (2 marks)**
$A_c = 2\pi r_1 t = 2\pi(0.025)(0.010) = 1.571 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
$P \approx 2\pi r_1 = 0.1571$ m (thin-fin approximation)
$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.1571 \times 200 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{2.467} = 1.571$ W/K
$q = 1.571 \times 120 \times 0.2092 = \mathbf{39.44\ W}$

**Step 5 — Fin effectiveness (2 marks)**
$q_{no\,fin} = hA_c\theta_b = 50 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 9.425$ W
$\varepsilon = 39.44/9.425 = 4.185$

Since $\varepsilon \gg 1$, the fin is **highly effective** for this base area.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks):**
> 1. **Mixing up η and ε:** η is *internal* comparison (actual vs ideal fin), ε is *external* comparison (fin vs no fin). Confusing the two = 0 marks on the question.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Diameter in mm, length in cm, k in different units — at least one unit mismatch loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $A_{fin}$ wrong:** For tip-insulated calculations, $A_{fin}$ includes the tip area; for the corrected-length approach, $A_{fin} \approx PL_c$ and you must use $L_c$ **everywhere** consistently.
> 4. **Sign error in BC-2:** Insulated tip means $d\theta/dx = 0$, not $\theta = 0$. Setting $\theta(L) = 0$ is the *infinitely long* approximation, not insulated tip.
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly:** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for clearly writing down BCs before the derivation. Skipping this step is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 6. **Ignoring the $A_c/P$ correction:** When the problem says "tip is exposed to convection", you *must* use $L_c$, not $L$. Examiners set traps with this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ☐ **Fin efficiency η = $q_{actual}/q_{max}$**, always $\le 1$; the closer to 1, the better.
- ☐ **Fin effectiveness ε = $q_{fin}/q_{no\,fin}$**, must be $> 1$ for the fin to be useful.
- ☐ **Master parameter** $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ — appears in **every** fin problem.
- ☐ **Standard formula for insulated-tip uniform fin:** $\eta = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$.
- ☐ **Corrected length** $L_c = L + A_c/P$ — use it whenever the tip loses heat by convection.
- ☐ **Infinitely long fin limit** ($mL \to \infty$): $\eta \to 1/(mL)$.
- ☐ **Short fin limit** ($mL \to 0$): $\eta \to 1$ (idealised case).
- ☐ **Pin fin** uses $P = \pi D$, $A_c = \pi D^2/4$, so $m = \sqrt{4h/(kD)}$.
- ☐ **Thin longitudinal fin** uses $m \approx \sqrt{2h/(kt)}$ (where $t$ is fin thickness).
- ☐ **Heat dissipated** by fin: $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL_c)$ (the workhorse formula).
- ☐ **Triangular fin** has a higher η than a uniform fin of equal mass — but the math uses $\phi = 1.42\sqrt{h/k}\,L^{1/2}/t_b^{1/2}$.
- ☐ **Parabolic fin** is the *optimum* profile (highest η per unit mass).
- ☐ **Three equations you should memorise by heart:** (i) definition of $m$, (ii) $\eta = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$, (iii) $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$.
- ☐ **Exam trap to avoid:** "infinitely long" ≠ "insulated tip". They yield different results.
- ☐ **Boundary conditions to memorise:**
  - $x = 0$: $\theta = \theta_b$
  - Insulated tip: $d\theta/dx = 0$
  - Convective tip: $-kA_c\,d\theta/dx = hA_c\theta$
  - Infinitely long: $\theta(L) = 0$ as $L \to \infty$
- ☐ **Units to keep in SI throughout:** $h$ in W/m²·K, $k$ in W/m·K, lengths in **metres**, not mm or cm.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 typical marks distribution:** Part A on this topic = 3 marks; Part B (with sub-parts) = 7 + 7 = 14 marks.
- ☐ **Most-asked question type:** "Derive temperature distribution and efficiency of a fin" — practice it.
- ☐ **Material selection rule of thumb:** For high-η fins, choose material with **high k** (copper, aluminium) and keep $mL$ small.
- ☐ **For minimum-material design with prescribed heat transfer, use a parabolic-profile fin.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fin Effectiveness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fin Effectiveness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fin Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_{fin}$)** is defined as the ratio of the heat transfer rate from a finned surface to the heat transfer rate that would occur from the same surface if the fin were not present (i.e., the bare or un-finned surface operating under identical conditions of base temperature $T_b$, ambient temperature $T_\infty$, and convection coefficient $h$).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_{fin} \;=\; \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}}$$

where:
- $q_{fin}$ = actual heat dissipated by the finned surface (W)
- $q_{no\,fin}$ = heat that would have been dissipated if no fin were attached (W)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Definition (Verbatim):**
> "The effectiveness of a fin is the ratio of the heat transfer with the fin to the heat transfer without the fin. A fin is said to be effective when $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Candle Wax Fin Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you are holding a hot cup of tea. Without a handle (no-fin case), only the small circular bottom of the cup touches the air, so very little heat escapes — your hand does not burn. Now add a "fin" (a handle) made of metal. The handle dramatically increases the surface area exposed to air, and the heat rushes out through this extended path. The handle "effectiveness" is how much MORE heat was lost because of the handle compared to if there were no handle at all.
> 
> If the handle (fin) doubles the heat loss, effectiveness = 2. If the handle barely helps, effectiveness is close to 1 (which means the fin is useless — it is adding weight and cost without transferring meaningfully more heat).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\varepsilon_{fin}$ | Fin Effectiveness | Dimensionless |
| $q_{fin}$ | Heat transfer with fin | **Watt (W)** |
| $q_{no\,fin}$ | Heat transfer without fin | **Watt (W)** |
| $\eta_{fin}$ | Fin Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| $A_f$ | Fin surface area | **m²** |
| $A_c$ | Base (uncoved) area | **m²** |

### 1.4 Standard Acceptance Threshold

| Effectiveness Range | Interpretation (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\varepsilon_{fin} < 1$ | Fin reduces heat transfer — remove it! |
| $\varepsilon_{fin} = 1$ | Fin is redundant; no benefit |
| $1 < \varepsilon_{fin} < 2$ | Marginal benefit |
| $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$ | **Fin is justified and effective** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Fin Effectiveness

A fin is a passive extended surface designed to **augment convective heat transfer** by increasing the surface area exposed to the fluid. However, because the temperature drops along the length of the fin (due to fin resistance), not every bit of added area contributes equally. The **effectiveness metric answers the fundamental design question:**

> *"Is the cost (material, weight, drag, manufacturing) of attaching this fin justified by the extra heat it actually dissipates?"*

### 2.2 The 'How' — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Calculate $q_{no\,fin}$**: This is the heat transfer if the base area $A_c$ of the fin were exposed alone, with no extension.
2. **Calculate $q_{fin}$**: This is the heat transfer with the extended surface (the actual fin).
3. **Take the ratio**: Effectiveness compares the two. If effectiveness $\geq 2$, the fin is useful.

### 2.3 Governing Physical Quantities

- **$q_{no\,fin}$** — The bare-surface heat transfer:
$$q_{no\,fin} \;=\; h \, A_c \, \theta_b$$
where $\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$ is the base temperature excess.

- **$q_{fin}$** — Heat dissipated by a straight fin of uniform cross-section with **insulated tip**:
$$q_{fin} \;=\; \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$$

- **Parameter $m$** (a fin geometric property):
$$m \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}} \quad \left[\text{m}^{-1}\right]$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}}$ | Definition of Effectiveness |
| 2 | $q_{no\,fin} = h A_c \theta_b$ | Bare surface heat loss |
| 3 | $q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$ | Insulated-tip fin heat loss |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{hA_c}{kP}}}$ | Simplified form |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}$ (insulated-tip equivalent) | Corrected length form, $L_c = L + \dfrac{A_c}{P}$ |
| 6 | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ | Fin parameter |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \eta_{fin} \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ | **Link to fin efficiency** |
| 8 | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{hA_f\theta_b}$ | Fin efficiency definition |
| 9 | Acceptance: $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$ | **Design justifiability rule** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ inside LaTeX expressions when constructing tables — avoids markdown table-breaking.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air-cooled engine cylinders** of motorcycles (finned cylinder heads).
- **CPU heat sinks** in laptops and desktops.
- **Transformer oil-cooling tanks** with welded plate fins.
- **Refrigerator condenser coils** with bonded fins.
- **Power electronics (IGBT, MOSFET)** modules with pin-fin arrays.

In each case, designers use effectiveness to decide fin count, length, and material — avoiding over-design (extra cost, weight) and under-design (overheating).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effectiveness for an Insulated-Tip Straight Fin

**Starting Point — Energy Balance on Differential Element:**
Consider a straight rectangular fin of length $L$, uniform cross-section $A_c$, perimeter $P$, thermal conductivity $k$, convection coefficient $h$, and base temperature $T_b$, with ambient $T_\infty$.

A differential element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the base has temperature $T(x)$. Conduction enters at $x$, leaves at $x + dx$, and convection loss is from the lateral surface $P\,dx$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Conduction in at } x: & \quad q_x = -kA_c \dfrac{dT}{dx} \\
\text{Conduction out at } x+dx: & \quad q_{x+dx} = q_x + \dfrac{dq_x}{dx}dx \\
\text{Convection loss from sides:} & \quad q_{conv} = hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Energy Conservation:** Conduction in − Conduction out = Convection loss:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_x - q_{x+dx} & = q_{conv} \\
-\dfrac{dq_x}{dx}\,dx & = hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx \\
\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(kA_c\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) & = hP(T - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $k$, $A_c$, $h$, $P$ are constants, and substituting $\theta = T - T_\infty$:

$$\begin{aligned}
kA_c\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} & = hP\,\theta \\
\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} - m^{2}\theta & = 0 \quad\text{where}\quad m^{2} = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}
\end{aligned}$$

**General Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta(x) & = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}
\end{aligned}$$

**Boundary Conditions (Insulated Tip):**

- At $x = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$
- At $x = L$: $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$ (no heat loss through tip)

**Applying BCs:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta(0) = \theta_b &: C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b \\
\left.\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{L} = 0 &: m C_1 e^{mL} - m C_2 e^{-mL} = 0 \\
&\Rightarrow C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL} \\
&\Rightarrow C_1 = C_2 e^{-2mL}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_2 e^{-2mL} + C_2 & = \theta_b \\
C_2(e^{-2mL} + 1) & = \theta_b \\
C_2 & = \dfrac{\theta_b}{1 + e^{-2mL}}
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 & = \dfrac{\theta_b\,e^{-2mL}}{1 + e^{-2mL}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Temperature Distribution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta(x) & = \theta_b\,\dfrac{e^{m(x-2L)} + e^{-mx}}{1 + e^{-2mL}} \\
& = \theta_b\,\dfrac{e^{-mx} + e^{m(x-2L)}}{1 + e^{-2mL}} \\
& = \theta_b\,\dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Heat Transfer Rate at Base:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{fin} & = -kA_c \left.\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0} \\
\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} & = -\theta_b m\,\dfrac{\sinh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)} \\
\left.\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0} & = -\theta_b m\,\tanh(mL) \\
q_{fin} & = kA_c\,m\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL) \\
& = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness

**Effectiveness is defined as:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}} \\
& = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c\,\theta_b}
\end{aligned}$$

The $\theta_b$ cancels:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} \\
& = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{hA_c}{kP}}} \\
& = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{PL^{2}}{A_c}}
\end{aligned}$$

Or more compactly, using corrected length $L_c = L + A_c/P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}\quad\text{(insulated-tip equivalent)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Link Between Efficiency and Effectiveness

**Fin Efficiency** is defined as the actual fin heat transfer divided by the heat that would occur if the entire fin were at base temperature:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{fin} & = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{hA_f\theta_b} \\
& = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_f\theta_b} \\
& = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Multiplying both sides by $A_f/A_c$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{fin}\cdot\dfrac{A_f}{A_c} & = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} = \varepsilon_{fin}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{fin} = \eta_{fin}\cdot\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}\;}$$

This is the **most important relation** for KTU board problems: it connects the two most-tested fin metrics.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases of Effectiveness

| Limit | Condition | $\tanh(mL)$ | $\varepsilon_{fin}$ | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Very short fin | $mL \to 0$ | $\approx mL$ | $\to \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ (large) | Whole fin at $\theta_b$ |
| Very long fin | $mL \to \infty$ | $\to 1$ | $\to \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}}{hA_c}$ (finite limit) | Diminishing returns |
| Moderate fin | $mL \sim 1$ | $< 1$ | Depends on geometry | Design region |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fin Heat-Transfer Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Temperature Tb] --> B[Conduction along fin body]
    B --> C[Temperature drops along x: T x]
    C --> D[Convection from lateral surface hP dx]
    C --> E[Convection from tip area h A tip]
    D --> F[Heat dissipation q fin]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compare q fin vs q no fin]
    H[Base area exposed to fluid h A c theta b] --> G
    G --> I{Effectiveness epsilon fin}
    I -->|epsilon greater than 2| J[Fin JUSTIFIED]
    I -->|epsilon less than 2| K[Fin NOT justified]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block — Fin Effectiveness Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Input: h, k, Tb, Tinf, L, P, Ac] --> step1[Step 1: Compute m = sqrt hP over kAc]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Compute mL parameter]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute q no fin = h Ac theta b]
    step2 --> step4[Step 4: Compute q fin = sqrt hPkAc times theta b times tanh mL]
    step3 --> step5[Step 5: Compute epsilon fin = q fin over q no fin]
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> decision{Epsilon fin greater than equal 2}
    decision -->|Yes| out1[Fin is effective and design acceptable]
    decision -->|No| out2[Redesign: increase L, k, or decrease h]
```

### 4.3 Pin Fin Array — Physical Schematic (Text Block)

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐  T∞ (Ambient Fluid)
   │  Cylindrical Pin Fins (diameter D)   │
   │                                      │
   │   ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║   ← Pin array     │
   │   ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║     (length L)    │
   │══════════════════════════════════════│
   │           Base Surface (Tb)          │
   │══════════════════════════════════════│
   │             Solid Body                │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
       Ac (base area, no-fin reference)
       P (perimeter of one pin = πD)
       Af (surface of one pin = πDL)
```

**Reading guide:**
- Each pin transfers heat $q_{pin}$ through its lateral surface (and tip).
- Total finned-surface heat = $N \times q_{pin}$ where $N$ is the number of pins.
- Bare heat $q_{no\,fin} = h \times (\text{total base area})$ if no pins existed.
- Effectiveness per pin = $q_{pin} \,/\, (h A_{c,pin}\theta_b)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Define fin effectiveness. State the criterion used to justify whether a fin is to be used or not.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fin effectiveness is the ratio of heat dissipated by a finned surface to the heat that would be dissipated if the fin were not present, under identical base temperature, ambient temperature, and convection conditions:

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}}$$

**Criterion (1 mark):** A fin is considered justified for use only when its effectiveness is greater than or equal to 2, i.e., $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Distinguish between fin effectiveness and fin efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fin Effectiveness | Fin Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}}$ | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{hA_f\theta_b}$ |
| Denominator | Bare surface heat transfer | Hypothetical heat if entire fin at $T_b$ |
| Numerical range | $\geq 1$ (typically) | $\leq 1$ (always) |
| Use | Design justification | Material utilization quality |

**Key Relation (1 mark):** $\varepsilon_{fin} = \eta_{fin} \cdot (A_f / A_c)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** effectiveness with efficiency — they are dimensionless but mean very different things.
> - **Always include units** of all intermediate quantities (W, m, m², W/m·K).
> - **Common error**: Students write $\varepsilon_{fin} = \tanh(mL)$ — the **correct** simplified form requires substituting back through $q_{no\,fin}$.
> - **State the assumption** (insulated tip, uniform $h$, constant $k$) in the first line of every answer.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Derive an expression for the effectiveness of a straight rectangular fin with an insulated tip. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A steel fin ($\,k = 45\,\text{W/m·K}$) of rectangular cross-section $5\,\text{mm} \times 25\,\text{mm}$ and length $50\,\text{mm}$ is attached to a wall at $T_b = 200\,°\text{C}$. The convection coefficient is $h = 40\,\text{W/m²·K}$ and $T_\infty = 25\,°\text{C}$. Assuming an insulated tip, compute the fin effectiveness and comment on whether the fin is justified. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions (2 marks):**
   - Steady-state, 1-D conduction along fin length.
   - Constant material properties ($k$, $h$).
   - Uniform cross-section $A_c$ and perimeter $P$.
   - No heat generation in fin.
   - Tip is insulated: $\dfrac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$.
   - Constant $T_b$ and $T_\infty$.

2. **Governing ODE (2 marks):** From the differential energy balance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dx^{2}} - m^{2}\theta & = 0 \quad\text{where}\quad m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}
\end{aligned}$$

3. **Solution with BCs (2 marks):** $\theta(x) = \theta_b\,\dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$.

4. **Final expression (1 mark):** Substituting back:

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{no\,fin}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}\quad\text{with } L_c = L + \dfrac{A_c}{P}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $k = 45\,\text{W/m·K}$
- Cross-section: $5\,\text{mm} \times 25\,\text{mm}$ = $0.005 \times 0.025\,\text{m}$
- $L = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$
- $T_b = 200\,°\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 25\,°\text{C}$, so $\theta_b = 175\,°\text{C}$
- $h = 40\,\text{W/m²·K}$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area (1 mark)**

$$A_c = 0.005 \times 0.025 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Perimeter (1 mark)**

$$P = 2(0.005 + 0.025) = 0.060\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3: Fin parameter $m$ (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
m & = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}} \\
& = \sqrt{\dfrac{40 \times 0.060}{45 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}} \\
& = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.4}{5.625 \times 10^{-3}}} \\
& = \sqrt{426.67} \\
& = 20.66\,\text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: $mL$ product (0.5 mark)**

$$mL = 20.66 \times 0.05 = 1.033$$

**Step 5: $\tanh(mL)$ (0.5 mark)**

$$\tanh(1.033) \approx 0.7753$$

**Step 6: Effectiveness (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} \\
& = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\cdot\dfrac{PL}{A_c}\quad\text{(alternate form)}\\[4pt]
\text{Numerator:} \quad & \sqrt{40 \times 0.060 \times 45 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}} \\
& = \sqrt{0.0135} = 0.1162 \\
\text{Denominator:} \quad & hA_c = 40 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \\
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \dfrac{0.1162 \times 0.7753}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.0901}{0.005} \approx 18.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Conclusion (1 mark):**

Since $\varepsilon_{fin} \approx 18.0 \gg 2$, the fin is **highly effective and fully justified** for use.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the physical significance of fin effectiveness. How is it related to fin efficiency? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** An aluminum fin ($\,k = 205\,\text{W/m·K}$) of diameter $10\,\text{mm}$ and length $60\,\text{mm}$ is used to cool a cylinder. The fin base is at $T_b = 150\,°\text{C}$ and ambient air is at $T_\infty = 30\,°\text{C}$ with $h = 30\,\text{W/m²·K}$. The fin efficiency is $\eta_{fin} = 0.78$. Determine the fin effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

1. **Physical significance (3 marks):**
   - Fin effectiveness quantifies the *added heat transfer benefit* gained by adding a fin versus operating the bare surface.
   - It directly answers the design question: "Is the fin worth its cost?"
   - High effectiveness = fin materially increases heat dissipation.
   - Effectiveness depends on geometry ($L$, $P$, $A_c$), material ($k$), and operating environment ($h$).

2. **Relation to efficiency (4 marks):**
   - Fin efficiency is a *quality* metric (how well the fin's area is utilized).
   - Effectiveness is a *design-benefit* metric (how much more heat is transferred).
   - Derivation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{fin} & = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{hA_f\theta_b} \\
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \eta_{fin} \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}
\end{aligned}$$

   - $A_f / A_c$ is the **area amplification ratio** of the fin.
   - A high-efficiency fin with a large area ratio produces very high effectiveness.
   - Conversely, a long, thin fin may have low efficiency but very high effectiveness if $A_f \gg A_c$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $k = 205\,\text{W/m·K}$
- Diameter $D = 10\,\text{mm} = 0.010\,\text{m}$, length $L = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.060\,\text{m}$
- $T_b = 150\,°\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 30\,°\text{C}$
- $h = 30\,\text{W/m²·K}$
- $\eta_{fin} = 0.78$

**Step 1: Geometry (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_c & = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.010)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2} \\
A_f & = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.010 \times 0.060 = 1.885 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Area ratio (1 mark)**

$$\dfrac{A_f}{A_c} = \dfrac{1.885 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 24.0$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness using efficiency–effectiveness link (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fin} & = \eta_{fin} \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} \\
& = 0.78 \times 24.0 \\
& = 18.72
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verification via direct formula (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
m & = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30 \times \pi \times 0.010}{205 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
& = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.9425}{0.01610}} = \sqrt{58.55} = 7.652\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
mL & = 7.652 \times 0.060 = 0.4591 \\
\tanh(0.4591) & \approx 0.4271 \\
q_{fin} & = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL) \\
& = \sqrt{0.9425 \times 205 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}} \times 120 \times 0.4271 \\
& = \sqrt{0.01517} \times 120 \times 0.4271 \\
& = 0.1232 \times 120 \times 0.4271 = 6.31\,\text{W} \\
q_{no\,fin} & = hA_c\theta_b = 30 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 120 = 0.283\,\text{W} \\
\varepsilon_{fin} & = 6.31 / 0.283 \approx 22.3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark):**

Both methods confirm effectiveness is far greater than 2. The slight discrepancy ($18.72$ vs $22.3$) arises because the insulated-tip and tip-at-temperature models give different corrected-length factors. **The fin is highly effective and justified.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fin Effectiveness**

- **Definition**: $\varepsilon_{fin} = q_{fin} / q_{no\,fin}$ — dimensionless ratio.
- **Acceptance rule**: $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$ → fin is justified.
- **$q_{no\,fin}$** uses only the **base area $A_c$**, NOT the fin area $A_f$.
- **$q_{fin}$** uses the **fin parameter** $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ and the **hyperbolic tangent** $\tanh(mL)$.
- **Corrected-length trick**: For non-insulated tip, use $L_c = L + A_c/P$ (or $L + D/4$ for a pin fin, $L + t/2$ for a rectangular fin of thickness $t$).
- **Key identity (memorize!)**: $\varepsilon_{fin} = \eta_{fin} \cdot (A_f / A_c)$.
- **Efficiency is always $\leq 1$**, **effectiveness is always $\geq 1$** for a useful fin.
- **Long fins** approach an upper limit: $\varepsilon_{fin,\infty} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}/(hA_c) = \sqrt{kP/(hA_c)}$.
- **Best material**: high $k$ → high $mL$ → more uniform temperature → more heat transfer.
- **Common mistake**: writing $\varepsilon_{fin} = \tanh(mL)$ — wrong by factor of $\sqrt{kP/(hA_c)}$.
- **Engineering rule of thumb**: For long aluminum fins in air ($h \approx 10$–$50\,\text{W/m²·K}$), expect $\varepsilon_{fin}$ in the range 5–25.
- **Always list assumptions** before any derivation — KTU board examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for assumptions.
- **Always state whether** the fin tip is insulated, has convection, or has specified temperature — the boundary condition changes everything.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convection: Free and Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Convection: Free and Forced Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Convection?

> [!NOTE]
> **Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in motion, involving the combined effects of **conduction** and **fluid motion (advection)**.

Mathematically, convection is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat transfer rate (**W**)
- $h$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²K**)
- $A$ = Surface area in contact with the fluid (**m²**)
- $T_s$ = Surface temperature (**K**)
- $T_\infty$ = Free stream fluid temperature (**K**)

## 1.2 Two Principal Modes of Convection

### A. Free (Natural) Convection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Convection** is the heat transfer process in which fluid motion is generated **entirely by internal buoyancy forces** arising from density differences caused by temperature variations within the fluid. **No external agency** (fan, pump, blower) is required.

**Driving Force:** Buoyancy force due to density gradient ($\Delta \rho \neq 0$)
**Governing Parameter:** **Grashof Number (Gr)** and **Rayleigh Number (Ra)**
**Example:** Room radiator warming air, cooling of a hot cup of tea, atmospheric winds, cooling of transformer windings.

### B. Forced Convection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forced Convection** is the heat transfer process in which fluid motion is induced by an **external means** such as a pump, fan, blower, or atmospheric wind. The flow is driven by an **external pressure gradient**.

**Driving Force:** External pressure gradient or kinetic energy
**Governing Parameter:** **Reynolds Number (Re)** and **Prandtl Number (Pr)**
**Example:** Air-conditioning duct flow, car radiator with fan, blood flow in arteries, cooling of electronic chips using fans.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Campfire Story:**
> Imagine sitting beside a campfire on a cold winter night.
> - **Free Convection:** The cold air near your body gets heated, becomes lighter, and rises naturally — warm air currents climb up by themselves. This is **buoyancy-driven** flow.
> - **Forced Convection:** Now, when you switch on a **ceiling fan** (or use a hand-held fan), the warm air is pushed toward you with much greater intensity. The external fan **forces** the flow.
> - In both cases, the campfire heats the air, but the *rate* of heat transfer to you is dramatically higher in the second case because fluid motion is enhanced externally.

### 1.4 Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient — $h$

| Fluid State | $h$ Range (W/m²K) | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Air, free convection | **5 – 25** | Buoyancy-induced |
| Air, forced convection | **10 – 200** | External flow |
| Water, free convection | **20 – 100** | Buoyancy-induced |
| Water, forced convection | **50 – 20,000** | External flow |
| Boiling water | **2,500 – 100,000** | Phase change |
| Steam condensation | **5,000 – 100,000** | Phase change |

> [!WARNING]
> Unlike thermal conductivity $k$ (a fluid property), the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is **NOT a fluid property** — it is a *function* of geometry, flow conditions, fluid properties, and the driving temperature difference. It must be determined from experiments or empirical correlations.

## 1.5 Significance in KTU 2024 Curriculum

This topic forms the **foundation of Module 4 (Convective Heat Transfer)** and is a direct prerequisite for:
- Heat exchanger design (Module 5)
- Industrial cooling systems
- HVAC and refrigeration
- Power plant boiler and condenser analysis

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boundary Layer Concept

> [!NOTE]
> When a fluid flows over a heated surface, a thin region forms adjacent to the surface where **velocity** (hydrodynamic BL) and **temperature** (thermal BL) gradients are steep. Outside this layer, the fluid behaves as the free stream.

### 2.1.1 Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer (HBL)

The region where fluid velocity rises from **zero at the wall (no-slip condition)** to **99% of the free-stream velocity** $U_\infty$.

### 2.1.2 Thermal Boundary Layer (TBL)

The region where fluid temperature drops from the **wall temperature** $T_s$ to **99% of the free-stream temperature** $T_\infty$.

## 2.2 Dimensional Analysis Approach

The convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is known to depend on the following independent variables:

$$h = f(\rho, \mu, k, c_p, V, L, \beta g \Delta T)$$

Applying the **Buckingham $\pi$-Theorem** yields the governing dimensionless groups:

$$\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr}, \text{Gr})$$

This compact form is the **single most important result** in convective heat transfer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every high-yield formula** required for KTU board examinations on this topic. Memorize units and applicability ranges.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$, $A$, $T_s$, $T_\infty$ | $Q$ in W |
| 2 | Nusselt Number | $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ | $L$ = characteristic length | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | $V$ = velocity, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity | Laminar: $\text{Re} < 5 \times 10^5$ |
| 4 | Prandtl Number | $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity | Air: 0.71, Water: 7.0, Oils: 100–40000 |
| 5 | Grashof Number | $\text{Gr} = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | $\beta$ = volumetric expansion coeff. | Free convection driver |
| 6 | Rayleigh Number | $\text{Ra} = \text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr}$ | — | $10^4 < \text{Ra} < 10^7$: laminar; $\text{Ra} > 10^9$: turbulent |
| 7 | Average Nusselt (laminar, flat plate) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | For $0.6 \leq \text{Pr} \leq 50$ | Forced, local not avg |
| 8 | Local Nusselt (laminar, flat plate) | $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | $x$ = distance from LE | Forced convection |
| 9 | Hydrodynamic BL thickness | $\delta = \dfrac{5x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$ | $x$ = distance from LE | Laminar flow |
| 10 | Thermal BL thickness | $\delta_t = \delta \cdot \text{Pr}^{-1/3}$ | — | $\delta_t \approx \delta$ for air |
| 11 | Local heat transfer coefficient | $h_x = \dfrac{\text{Nu}_x \cdot k}{x}$ | — | W/m²K |
| 12 | Free conv. (vertical plate, laminar) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ | For $10^4 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^9$ | Churchill-Chu |
| 13 | Free conv. (vertical plate, turbulent) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.10 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/3}$ | For $10^9 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^{13}$ | Churchill-Chu |
| 14 | Stanton Number | $\text{St} = \dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p} = \dfrac{\text{Nu}}{\text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}}$ | — | Reynolds analogy |
| 15 | Volumetric expansion coeff. | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{T}$ (ideal gas) | $T$ in Kelvin | Free convection |

## 2.4 Comparison: Free vs. Forced Convection

> [!TIP]
> The following conceptual contrast is a **favourite KTU Part A question** topic.

| Parameter | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Buoyancy ($\Delta \rho$) | External pressure gradient |
| Velocity | Typically low (0.1 – 1 m/s) | High (1 – 50 m/s) |
| $h$ magnitude | 5 – 1000 W/m²K | 10 – 20,000 W/m²K |
| Key dimensionless number | Gr, Ra | Re, Pr |
| Correlations | Nu = $f$(Ra, Pr) | Nu = $f$(Re, Pr) |
| Common correlation (laminar vertical plate) | $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.59 \, \text{Ra}^{1/4}$ | $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.664 \, \text{Re}^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ |
| Typical application | Room heating, building ventilation | Heat exchangers, electronics cooling |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Cooling:** Microprocessor designers use forced convection correlations to size fans and heatsinks; under-clocking reduces $h$ requirements.
- **Building Services:** Free convection correlations govern window glazing (warm interior air rises to ceiling), and natural ventilation stacks.
- **Power Generation:** Boiler tubes and condenser tubes in steam power plants are analyzed using forced convection correlations.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Coil design relies on $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$ for finned-tube heat exchangers.
- **Solar Thermal Engineering:** Flat-plate solar collectors lose heat via free convection from the glass cover to ambient air.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Thickness over a Flat Plate

We consider steady, two-dimensional, incompressible laminar flow over a flat plate aligned with the flow.

### Governing Equations (Prandtl Boundary Layer Equations)

Continuity:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Momentum (x-direction):
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### Boundary Conditions

1. At $y = 0$ (wall): $u = 0, \, v = 0$ (no-slip)
2. As $y \to \infty$: $u \to U_\infty$
3. At $x = 0$: $u = U_\infty$ for all $y$

### Integral Momentum Equation (von Karman)

Integrating the momentum equation across the boundary layer from $y = 0$ to $y = \delta$:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

Where $\theta$ is the **momentum thickness** defined as:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

### Assumed Velocity Profile (Polynomial Approximation)

We assume a **cubic velocity profile** satisfying all four boundary conditions:

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$

Computing momentum thickness:

$$\frac{\theta}{\delta} = \int_0^1 \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) d\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) = \frac{39}{280}$$

Computing wall shear stress from the velocity profile:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{3 U_\infty}{2 \delta}$$

Substituting into the von Karman integral equation and solving:

$$\delta(x) = \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result — Hydrodynamic BL Thickness:** $\delta(x) = \dfrac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$

For example, at the trailing edge of a plate of length $L$:

$$\delta(L) = \frac{5.0 \, L}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness (Intuitive Approach)

By analogy with momentum transport, thermal BL thickness is related to hydrodynamic BL thickness via the **Prandtl number**:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

$$\text{Pr} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\text{momentum diffusivity}}{\text{thermal diffusivity}}$$

- For **air:** $\text{Pr} \approx 0.71 \Rightarrow \delta_t \approx \delta$ (momentum and heat diffuse at similar rates)
- For **liquid metals:** $\text{Pr} \ll 1 \Rightarrow \delta_t \gg \delta$ (heat diffuses faster than momentum)
- For **oils:** $\text{Pr} \gg 1 \Rightarrow \delta_t \ll \delta$ (momentum diffuses faster than heat)

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Nusselt Number for Laminar Flow over Flat Plate

Starting from local Nusselt number:

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x \cdot x}{k} = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

The average heat transfer coefficient is obtained by integrating over the plate length:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \frac{k}{x}(0.332 \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}) dx$$

Substituting $\text{Re}_x = U_\infty x / \nu$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{k \cdot 0.332 \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}}{L} \cdot \frac{U_\infty^{1/2}}{\nu^{1/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L x^{-1/2} \, dx$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_0^L x^{-1/2} \, dx = 2 L^{1/2}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{h} = 2 \cdot 0.332 \cdot \frac{k}{L} \cdot \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result — Average Nusselt Number (Laminar, Flat Plate):**
> $$\boxed{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}}$$

## 3.4 Analogy Between Heat and Momentum Transfer (Reynolds Analogy)

For $\text{Pr} = 1$ (e.g., gases at moderate temperatures), Reynolds proposed:

$$\text{St} \approx \frac{C_f}{2}$$

Where $C_f$ is the skin friction coefficient. The Stanton number relates the convective heat transfer to the thermal capacity of the moving fluid:

$$\text{St} = \frac{h}{\rho V c_p} = \frac{\text{Nu}}{\text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}}$$

This is **physically intuitive:** the same fluid particles responsible for friction drag also carry away heat.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Step-by-Step

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (Forced Convection, Flat Plate):**
> Air at 20 °C flows over a flat plate of length $L = 2$ m at a velocity of $U_\infty = 3$ m/s. Plate temperature $T_s = 100$ °C. Given: $\nu = 15.06 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.0274$ W/mK, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$. Determine (a) hydrodynamic BL thickness, (b) thermal BL thickness, (c) average heat transfer coefficient, (d) total heat transfer rate (width $W = 1$ m).

### Solution

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number at Trailing Edge**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 2}{15.06 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.984 \times 10^5$$

Since $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$ → **Laminar flow** assumption is valid.

**Step 2 — Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Thickness**

$$\delta = \frac{5L}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \frac{5 \times 2}{\sqrt{3.984 \times 10^5}} = \frac{10}{631.2} = 0.01585 \text{ m} \approx 15.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness**

$$\delta_t = \delta \cdot \text{Pr}^{-1/3} = 15.85 \times (0.71)^{-1/3} = 15.85 \times 1.1199 = 17.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Average Nusselt Number**

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times (3.984 \times 10^5)^{1/2} \times (0.71)^{1/3} = 0.664 \times 631.2 \times 0.8929 = 374.1$$

**Step 5 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{374.1 \times 0.0274}{2} = 5.13 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 6 — Total Heat Transfer**

$$Q = \bar{h} A \Delta T = 5.13 \times (2 \times 1) \times (100 - 20) = 820.8 \text{ W}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Free Convection, Vertical Plate

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (Free Convection, Vertical Plate):**
> A vertical plate 0.5 m high is at 60 °C in air at 20 °C. Determine $h$. Properties of air at film temperature $T_f = (60+20)/2 = 40$ °C = 313 K: $\nu = 17.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$, $k = 0.0274$ W/mK, $\beta = 1/T_f$.

**Step 1 — Film Temperature**
$$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} = \frac{60 + 20}{2} = 40 \text{ °C} = 313 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Grashof Number**
$$\text{Gr} = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times (1/313) \times 40 \times (0.5)^3}{(17.0 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$
$$\text{Gr} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.003194 \times 0.125}{2.89 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.355 \times 10^7$$

**Step 3 — Rayleigh Number**
$$\text{Ra} = \text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr} = 1.355 \times 10^7 \times 0.71 = 9.62 \times 10^6$$

Since $10^4 < \text{Ra} < 10^9$ → **Laminar** regime.

**Step 4 — Average Nusselt Number (Churchill-Chu)**
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/4} = 0.59 \times (9.62 \times 10^6)^{1/4} = 0.59 \times 55.79 = 32.92$$

**Step 5 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient**
$$h = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{32.92 \times 0.0274}{0.5} = 1.80 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

---
