# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Convection Classification

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[CONVECTION HEAT TRANSFER] --> B[Free Convection]
    A --> C[Forced Convection]
    A --> D[Mixed Convection]

    B --> B1[Driving Force Buoyancy]
    B1 --> B2[Density Difference delta rho]
    B1 --> B3[Governed by Grashof Number Gr]

    C --> C1[Driving Force External Pressure Gradient]
    C1 --> C2[Mechanical Devices Pump Fan Blower]
    C1 --> C3[Governed by Reynolds Number Re]

    D --> D1[Both Buoyancy and External Flow Compete]
    D1 --> D2[Transition Criterion Gr by Re Squared]

    B2 --> B4[Governing Equation Nu equals f of Ra Pr]
    B3 --> B4
    C2 --> C5[Governing Equation Nu equals f of Re Pr]
    C3 --> C5
    D1 --> D6[Governing Equation Nu equals f of Re Gr Pr]
    D2 --> D6
```

## 4.2 Boundary Layer Schematic: Hydrodynamic and Thermal

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Free Stream U infinity T infinity] --> B[Leading Edge x equals 0]
    B --> C[Flat Plate Surface T equals T sub s]
    C --> D[Wall y equals 0]
    B --> E[Position x along plate]
    E --> F[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer delta]
    E --> G[Thermal Boundary Layer delta sub t]
    F --> F1[Velocity rises from 0 to 0.99 U infinity]
    G --> G1[Temperature drops from T sub s to 0.99 T infinity]
    F1 --> H[Outside BL Fluid behaves as free stream]
    G1 --> H
    H --> A
```

## 4.3 Free Convection Flow Pattern over a Vertical Hot Plate

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[Vertical Hot Plate T sub s high] --> P2[Heated air layer adjacent to plate]
    P2 --> P3[Air becomes less dense]
    P3 --> P4[Buoyancy force pushes air upward]
    P4 --> P5[Upward flow entrains cooler air from below]
    P5 --> P6[Circulation cell forms in room]
    P6 --> P7[Cold air from sides replaces rising hot air]
    P7 --> P2
    P4 --> P8[Boundary layer grows with height x]
    P8 --> P9[Transition to turbulent free convection at critical Ra]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Solving a Convection Problem (Engineering Methodology)

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[Start Given problem data] --> S2[Identify Free or Forced convection]
    S2 --> S3[Compute characteristic length L]
    S3 --> S4[Evaluate fluid properties at film temperature]
    S4 --> S5[Compute dimensionless numbers Re Pr Gr Ra]
    S5 --> S6{Determine flow regime}
    S6 -->|Laminar| S7[Select laminar correlation]
    S6 -->|Turbulent| S8[Select turbulent correlation]
    S6 -->|Mixed| S9[Compare Gr by Re squared with 1]
    S7 --> S10[Compute Nu]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Compute h from Nu]
    S11 --> S12[Apply Newton law Q equals h A delta T]
    S12 --> S13[End Final answer]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix: Free vs. Forced Convection at a Glance

```mermaid
graph LR
    FC[Free Convection] --> FC1[Driver Buoyancy]
    FC --> FC2[Key Parameter Ra]
    FC --> FC3[Typical h 5 to 500 W per m squared K]
    FC --> FC4[Example Hot chimney rising plume]
    FC1 --> FC1A[Delta rho g]
    FC2 --> FC2A[Gr times Pr]

    FF[Forced Convection] --> FF1[Driver External Fan or Pump]
    FF --> FF2[Key Parameter Re]
    FF --> FF3[Typical h 10 to 20000 W per m squared K]
    FF --> FF4[Example Car radiator cooling system]
    FF1 --> FF1A[Delta P]
    FF2 --> FF2A[rho V L by mu]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Define the following terms with their physical significance:**
> (i) Nusselt Number
> (ii) Reynolds Number
> (iii) Prandtl Number

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **(i) Nusselt Number (Nu):** It is the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer at the boundary in a fluid. $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ — Dimensionless. It represents the **enhancement of heat transfer** through a fluid layer due to convection over pure conduction.
>
> **(ii) Reynolds Number (Re):** It is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flowing fluid. $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ — Dimensionless. It indicates whether the flow is **laminar** ($\text{Re} < 5 \times 10^5$ for flat plate) or **turbulent**.
>
> **(iii) Prandtl Number (Pr):** It is the ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity. $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ — Dimensionless. It is **a fluid property only** (e.g., $\text{Pr} \approx 0.71$ for air, $\approx 7$ for water) and indicates the relative thickness of hydrodynamic vs. thermal boundary layers.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Differentiate between free convection and forced convection.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Basis | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driving force | Buoyancy (density difference) | External pressure gradient |
> | Key dimensionless number | Grashof (Gr) or Rayleigh (Ra) | Reynolds (Re) |
> | Typical $h$ | 5 – 500 W/m²K | 10 – 20,000 W/m²K |
> | Flow velocity | Low, self-induced | High, externally controlled |
> | Example | Room radiator | Forced-air heat exchanger |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question (with both sub-parts)** out of the two choices provided. **Internal choice is NOT allowed within sub-parts.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the average Nusselt number for **laminar flow over a flat plate**, starting from the local Nusselt number correlation. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Air at 27 °C flows over a flat plate of length 1.5 m at a velocity of 2 m/s. The plate is maintained at 77 °C. Calculate:
> (i) Hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layer thicknesses at the trailing edge
> (ii) Average heat transfer coefficient
> (iii) Total heat transfer rate per unit width of plate
>
> Given: Properties of air at film temperature: $\nu = 18.41 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.0263$ W/mK, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, two-dimensional, incompressible laminar flow.
2. Constant fluid properties.
3. Negligible viscous dissipation and body forces.
4. No-slip condition at the wall.
5. Boundary layer approximations of Prandtl are valid.

**Local Nusselt Number (given by Pohlhausen solution):**
$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x \cdot x}{k} = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

**Average heat transfer coefficient:**
$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x \, dx$$

Substituting $h_x$ and $\text{Re}_x = U_\infty x / \nu$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{0.332 \, k \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \, U_\infty^{1/2}}{L \, \nu^{1/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L x^{-1/2} \, dx$$

Evaluating $\int_0^L x^{-1/2} \, dx = 2 L^{1/2}$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.332 \, k}{L} \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Film Temperature**
$$T_f = \frac{77 + 27}{2} = 52 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number at Trailing Edge**
$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 1.5}{18.41 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.629 \times 10^5$$

Since $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$ → **Laminar flow** ✓

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Thickness**
$$\delta = \frac{5L}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \frac{5 \times 1.5}{\sqrt{1.629 \times 10^5}} = \frac{7.5}{403.6} = 0.01858 \text{ m} \approx 18.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness**
$$\delta_t = \delta \cdot \text{Pr}^{-1/3} = 18.6 \times (0.71)^{-1/3} = 18.6 \times 1.1199 = 20.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Average Nusselt Number**
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times (1.629 \times 10^5)^{1/2} \times (0.71)^{1/3} = 0.664 \times 403.6 \times 0.8929 = 239.2$$

**Step 6 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient**
$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{239.2 \times 0.0263}{1.5} = 4.19 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 7 — Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Width**
$$Q' = \frac{Q}{W} = \bar{h} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = 4.19 \times 1.5 \times (77 - 27) = 314.4 \text{ W/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Properties at film temperature** (not at free stream) is mandatory. Using properties at $T_\infty = 27$ °C instead of $T_f = 52$ °C will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Check the laminar assumption:** Always verify $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$. If $\text{Re}_L > 5 \times 10^5$, a different correlation applies.
> 3. **Units consistency:** Use SI units throughout — $L$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $k$ in W/mK.
> 4. **Final answer unit** must be **W (or W/m if per unit width is asked).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **(a)** Define free convection. Derive the Grashof number using dimensional analysis and explain its physical significance. List the various correlations used for free convection over a vertical plate in different flow regimes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vertical plate 0.4 m high is maintained at 80 °C in air at 20 °C. Calculate:
> (i) Grashof number
> (ii) Rayleigh number
> (iii) Average Nusselt number
> (iv) Convective heat transfer coefficient
>
> Properties of air at film temperature 50 °C = 323 K: $\nu = 17.95 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.0274$ W/mK, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$, $\beta = 1/323$ K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Theory and Derivation

**Definition:** Free convection is the heat transfer process in which fluid motion is caused **entirely by buoyancy forces** arising from density differences due to temperature gradients. No external agency is involved.

**Dimensional Analysis of $h$:**

The dependent variable $h$ depends on:
$$\rho, \, \mu, \, k, \, c_p, \, L, \, g \beta \Delta T$$

Number of variables $n = 7$, Number of fundamental dimensions $m = 4$ (M, L, T, $\Theta$).
Number of $\pi$-groups = $n - m = 3$.

Choosing $\rho$, $V$, $L$, $\Delta T$ as repeating variables, the resulting dimensionless groups are:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{hL}{k} = \text{Nusselt Number, Nu}$$
$$\pi_2 = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \text{Reynolds Number, Re}$$
$$\pi_3 = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = \text{Grashof Number, Gr}$$

**Physical Significance of Gr:** It is the ratio of **buoyancy force to viscous force** acting on the fluid. It plays the same role in free convection that Re plays in forced convection.

**Correlations for Free Convection over Vertical Plate:**

| Regime | Range of Ra | Correlation |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $10^4 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^9$ | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ (Churchill-Chu) |
| Turbulent | $10^9 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^{13}$ | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.10 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/3}$ (Churchill-Chu) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Grashof Number**
$$\text{Gr} = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times (1/323) \times (80 - 20) \times (0.4)^3}{(17.95 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$\text{Gr} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.003096 \times 60 \times 0.064}{3.222 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{0.1166}{3.222 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.62 \times 10^8$$

**Step 2 — Rayleigh Number**
$$\text{Ra} = \text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr} = 3.62 \times 10^8 \times 0.71 = 2.57 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3 — Flow Regime**
Since $10^4 < \text{Ra} = 2.57 \times 10^8 < 10^9$ → **Laminar**

**Step 4 — Average Nusselt Number (Laminar Correlation)**
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \, \text{Ra}_L^{1/4} = 0.59 \times (2.57 \times 10^8)^{1/4}$$

$(2.57 \times 10^8)^{1/4} = 126.86$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \times 126.86 = 74.85$$

**Step 5 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient**
$$h = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{74.85 \times 0.0274}{0.4} = 5.13 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **$\beta$ for ideal gas** = $1/T_f$ (in Kelvin). Forgetting to convert °C to K will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Always state the regime** before using a correlation — shows you have understood the physics, not just memorized the formula.
> 3. **Grashof number exponent** in laminar correlation is $1/4$ (not $1/3$). $1/3$ is for turbulent regime only. **Common mistake** that costs 2 marks.
> 4. Final $h$ for free convection in air is typically **2 – 25 W/m²K**. If your answer is 200 W/m²K, you have made an error — go back and recheck.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 4: Convection**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Definitions
- **Convection** = combined conduction + advection; $Q = hA\Delta T$
- **Free convection** = buoyancy-driven; no external agency
- **Forced convection** = pressure-gradient-driven; requires fan/pump
- **Newton's Law of Cooling** = $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ — foundational

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Dimensionless Numbers
- $\text{Nu} = hL/k$ — convective vs. conductive heat transfer
- $\text{Re} = \rho V L / \mu$ — inertia vs. viscosity (forced convection)
- $\text{Pr} = \mu c_p / k = \nu / \alpha$ — fluid property only
- $\text{Gr} = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ — buoyancy vs. viscosity (free convection)
- $\text{Ra} = \text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr}$ — combined free convection parameter
- $\text{St} = h / (\rho V c_p) = \text{Nu} / (\text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr})$ — Stanton number

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Correlations
- **Laminar flat plate (forced):** $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.664 \text{Re}^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
- **Laminar vertical plate (free):** $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.59 \text{Ra}^{1/4}$
- **Turbulent vertical plate (free):** $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.10 \text{Ra}^{1/3}$
- **HBL thickness:** $\delta = 5x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$
- **TBL thickness:** $\delta_t = \delta \cdot \text{Pr}^{-1/3}$

### 🔑 Critical Exam Hints
1. **Properties at film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ for air.
2. **Laminar limit** for flat plate: $\text{Re} < 5 \times 10^5$.
3. **Free convection laminar limit:** $\text{Ra} < 10^9$.
4. **$\beta = 1/T$** for ideal gases (use $T$ in Kelvin).
5. **$h$ depends on geometry, fluid, and flow** — it is NOT a fluid property.
6. **Nusselt number = 1** implies pure conduction with no convection.

### 🔑 Real-World Applications
- **Electronics cooling** → forced convection via fans
- **Room heating** → free convection from radiators
- **Heat exchangers** → forced convection (correlations in Module 5)
- **Boilers & condensers** → high $h$ due to phase change
- **Building ventilation** → mixed convection stack effect

### 🔑 Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Using $T_\infty$ instead of $T_f$ for property evaluation
- ❌ Confusing $\text{Re}$ and $\text{Gr}$ — Re for forced, Gr for free
- ❌ Using turbulent correlation when flow is laminar (and vice versa)
- ❌ Forgetting to convert °C to K when computing $\beta$ for gases
- ❌ Treating $h$ as a constant fluid property

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Mechanism of Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Physical Mechanism of Forced Convection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Forced Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the bulk fluid motion is induced by an **external agency** such as a pump, blower, compressor, fan, or atmospheric wind. Unlike natural (free) convection, the fluid motion here is *imposed* and is therefore independent of the temperature-induced density gradients within the fluid. When a fluid is forced over a heated surface, a thin region of fluid adjacent to the surface experiences a steep velocity gradient (because of the no-slip condition) and a steep temperature gradient (because of thermal conduction from the surface). This region is known as the **convective boundary layer**, and understanding its physical mechanism is the gateway to solving all forced convection problems. ### Key Physical Quantities Introduced - **Boundary Layer ($\delta$)** — The thin fluid layer adjacent to the surface where velocity (and/or temperature) changes from the wall value to the free-stream value. - **Hydrodynamic (Velocity) Boundary Layer ($\delta$)** — Region where viscous effects are significant. - **Thermal Boundary Layer ($\delta_t$)** — Region where temperature gradients are significant. - **Free-stream velocity ($U_\infty$)** — Velocity of the undisturbed fluid far from the surface. - **Free-stream temperature ($T_\infty$)** — Temperature of the undisturbed fluid far from the surface. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of forced convection like blowing on hot coffee to cool it.** When you blow across the cup, you are providing an external flow of air. The thin layer of air *sticking* to the hot coffee surface (because air molecules "stick" to the wall — this is the **no-slip condition**) heats up rapidly. The breeze you create continuously *sweeps this hot air layer away* and replaces it with cool fresh air. The faster you blow (higher $U_\infty$), the thinner this hot layer gets, and the faster the coffee cools. This is precisely the mechanism of forced convection. **A more formal analogy — the Conveyor Belt Model:** Imagine the heated surface as a stationary hot plate. The forced fluid stream is a conveyor belt moving over it. The bottom layer of fluid on the belt (touching the plate) gets heated. The moving belt carries this hot fluid away downstream. New, cool fluid constantly arrives from upstream to replace it. The "traffic" of heat is therefore proportional to (i) how fast the belt moves (velocity) and (ii) how steep the temperature drop is across the thin hot layer (thermal gradient). ### Real-World Engineering Examples - Air-cooled condenser fins on a refrigerator compressor (fan-forced air). - Coolant flow inside the jackets of an IC engine cylinder. - Heat exchangers in HVAC and refrigeration systems. - Turbine blade cooling passages in jet engines. - Electronics cooling using forced air from a CPU fan. --- ### Important Dimensionless Groups Used Throughout Forced Convection | Group | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Reynolds number | $Re$ | Ratio of inertia to viscous forces | | Prandtl number | $Pr$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity | | Nusselt number | $Nu$ | Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer | | Péclet number | $Pe$ | Ratio of advection to diffusion (Pe = Re·Pr) | | Stanton number | $St$ | Ratio of heat transfer to thermal capacity of fluid | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners in PCPET303 frequently test the student's ability to (i) identify the regime (laminar/turbulent) using $Re$, (ii) select the correct dimensionless correlation for $Nu$, and (iii) physically interpret the relative thickness of $\delta_t$ vs. $\delta$ using $Pr$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Co-existing Boundary Layers When a cool fluid at $T_\infty$ flows with velocity $U_\infty$ over a flat plate maintained at $T_s > T_\infty$: **(A) Hydrodynamic (Velocity) Boundary Layer** - Originates at the leading edge ($x = 0$). - Defined as the region where $0 \le u \le 0.99\, U_\infty$ (conventionally). - Outer edge thickness $\delta(x)$ grows with distance $x$. - Approximately, $\delta(x) \propto x \, Re_x^{-1/2}$ for laminar flow. **(B) Thermal Boundary Layer** - Also originates at $x = 0$. - Defined as the region where $(T_s - T)/(T_s - T_\infty) \le 0.99$. - Outer edge thickness $\delta_t(x)$ grows with distance $x$. - For laminar flow, $\delta_t(x) \propto x \, Pe_x^{-1/2}$. --- ### 2.2 How the Two Layers Grow — The "Why" Behind It 1. **No-slip condition forces $u = 0$ at the wall.** By viscous diffusion (momentum diffusion), the slow-moving fluid drags neighbouring fluid layers, gradually transferring momentum into the bulk. Hence $\delta$ grows. 2. **Thermal diffusion (conduction) at the wall.** Heat conducted from the wall raises the temperature of adjacent fluid. As this hot fluid is swept downstream by advection, the thermal disturbance propagates further. Hence $\delta_t$ grows. 3. **The relative growth is dictated by Pr.** - For liquids (e.g., oils, $Pr \gg 1$): momentum diffuses much faster than heat → $\delta_t \ll \delta$. - For gases (e.g., air, $Pr \approx 0.7$): both diffuse comparably → $\delta_t \approx \delta$. - For liquid metals ($Pr \ll 1$): heat diffuses much faster than momentum → $\delta_t \gg \delta$. This is captured beautifully by the relation: $$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx Pr^{1/3}$$ --- ### 2.3 Local vs. Average Heat Transfer Coefficient - **Local heat transfer coefficient** $h_x$ varies along the plate — it is **highest near the leading edge** (thin boundary layer, large temperature gradient) and **decreases downstream** (thickening boundary layer, shallower gradient). - **Average heat transfer coefficient** $\bar{h}$ is the mean value integrated over the entire plate length $L$ and is the one used in Newton's law of cooling for total heat transfer: $$q = \bar{h} \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$ --- ### 2.4 Critical Reynolds Number & Transition For a flat plate, transition from **laminar to turbulent** flow occurs at: $$Re_{x,c} = \frac{U_\infty \, x_c}{\nu} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$$ - For $x < x_c$: **Laminar** flow throughout. - For $x > x_c$: **Turbulent** flow (after a short transition region). - For $L < x_c$: Plate is **entirely laminar**. - For $L > x_c$: Plate has a **mixed (laminar + turbulent)** regime, often analysed using an average transition location at $Re_{x,c} = 5 \times 10^5$. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the examiner's gold-standard reference for Module 4. Memorize the dimensionless forms — not the dimensional forms.** | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reynolds number (local) | $Re_x = \dfrac{U_\infty \, x}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Reynolds number (plate) | $Re_L = \dfrac{U_\infty \, L}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Prandtl number | $Pr = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Nusselt number (local) | $Nu_x = \dfrac{h_x \, x}{k}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Nusselt number (avg) | $\bar{Nu}_L = \dfrac{\bar{h}\, L}{k}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Hydrodynamic BL thickness (laminar) | $\dfrac{\delta(x)}{x} \approx \dfrac{5.0}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Ratio | | 7 | Thermal BL thickness (laminar) | $\dfrac{\delta_t(x)}{x} \approx \dfrac{5.0}{\sqrt{Re_x \, Pr}}$ | Ratio | | 8 | Ratio $\delta / \delta_t$ | $\dfrac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx Pr^{1/3}$ | Valid for $Pr \gtrsim 0.6$ | | 9 | Local $Nu$ — laminar, $Pr \gtrsim 0.6$ | $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | Exact for $Pr \to \infty$ | | 10 | Average $Nu$ — laminar, $Pr \gtrsim 0.6$ | $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | Integrated over $L$ | | 11 | Local skin-friction (laminar) | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Cf = 2τ/(ρU²) | | 12 | Critical $Re$ (transition) | $Re_{x,c} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ | Flat plate | | 13 | Newton's law of cooling | $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $[q] = \text{W}$ | | 14 | Heat flux | $q''_s = -k \left.\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0}$ | $[q''_s] = \text{W/m}^2$ | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** The vertical bar inside $\left.\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0}$ denotes a derivative *evaluated at $y = 0$*, **not** absolute value. In your answer scripts, write it cleanly as $\left.\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility This entire framework (BL theory + dimensionless correlations) is the **backbone of compact heat exchanger design**, where engineers: - Choose tube/baffle geometry to keep $Re$ in the turbulent regime (higher $h$). - Select working fluids with favourable $Pr$ (e.g., water has $Pr \approx 7$, giving high $h$ for the same $Re$). - Use the average $Nu$ correlation to size the heat exchanger area for a given heat duty. In jet-engine turbine blades, the gas-side $Pr$ is low ($\approx 0.7$) and $Re$ is very high; designers correlate experimental data into empirical $Nu$ vs. $Re$ curves to ensure blade temperatures stay below metallurgical limits. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Scope:** The KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus does not require full PDE solutions. The expected rigour is (i) the integral momentum (von Kármán) analysis for $\delta(x)$, and (ii) the integral energy analysis for $\delta_t(x)$, both of which we now present in full. --- ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Thickness $\delta(x)$ via Momentum Integral Equation **Starting Equation (von Kármán Momentum Integral):** $$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho} = \frac{d}{dx} \int_{0}^{\delta(x)} u \, (U_\infty - u) \, dy$$ **Step 1 — Choose a velocity profile shape (cubic polynomial, valid for laminar flat-plate BL):** We assume the velocity profile has the form: $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = a \eta + b \eta^2 + c \eta^3 \quad \text{where } \eta = \frac{y}{\delta}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the four boundary conditions:** | # | Condition | Physical meaning | |---|---|---| | (i) | $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ | No-slip at wall | | (ii) | $u = U_\infty$ at $y = \delta$ | Velocity matches free-stream | | (iii) | $\partial u / \partial y = 0$ at $y = \delta$ | Smooth merging with free-stream | | (iv) | $\mu \partial u / \partial y = \tau_w$ at $y = 0$ | Wall shear stress condition | **Step 3 — Solve the boundary conditions for the constants.** Condition (i): $0 = 0$ → no constraint on coefficients. Condition (ii): $a + b + c = 1$. Condition (iii): $a + 2b + 3c = 0$. Condition (iv): differentiates to $\partial u/\partial y = (U_\infty/\delta)(a + 2b\eta + 3c\eta^2)$; at $\eta = 0$ this gives $a \, U_\infty/\delta$, so $\mu a U_\infty / \delta = \tau_w$, hence $a = \tau_w \delta / (\mu U_\infty)$. Solving the system yields $a = \tau_w \delta / (\mu U_\infty)$, and ultimately the cubic profile simplifies to the standard Pohlhausen form. For our purposes we adopt the equivalent **polynomial profile** (sinusoidal is equally valid): $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3$$ **Step 4 — Compute the momentum thickness integral.** The momentum thickness is defined as: $$\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$ Substituting the profile $\eta = y/\delta$: $$\theta = \delta \int_{0}^{1} \left(\frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3\right)\left(1 - \frac{3}{2}\eta + \frac{1}{2}\eta^3\right) d\eta$$ Expanding the integrand (term by term): $$\left(\frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3\right)\left(1 - \frac{3}{2}\eta + \frac{1}{2}\eta^3\right) = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{9}{4}\eta^2 + \frac{3}{4}\eta^4 - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3 + \frac{3}{4}\eta^4 - \frac{1}{4}\eta^6$$ Combining like powers: $$= \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{9}{4}\eta^2 - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3 + \frac{3}{2}\eta^4 - \frac{1}{4}\eta^6$$ Now integrating term by term from $0$ to $1$: $$\int_0^1 \frac{3}{2}\eta \, d\eta = \frac{3}{4}$$ $$\int_0^1 -\frac{9}{4}\eta^2 \, d\eta = -\frac{9}{12} = -\frac{3}{4}$$ $$\int_0^1 -\frac{1}{2}\eta^3 \, d\eta = -\frac{1}{8}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{3}{2}\eta^4 \, d\eta = \frac{3}{10}$$ $$\int_0^1 -\frac{1}{4}\eta^6 \, d\eta = -\frac{1}{28}$$ Adding all five: $$\frac{3}{4} - \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{10} - \frac{1}{28}$$ Finding a common denominator of $280$: $$\frac{210}{280} - \frac{210}{280} - \frac{35}{280} + \frac{84}{280} - \frac{10}{280} = \frac{210 - 210 - 35 + 84 - 10}{280} = \frac{39}{280}$$ Therefore: $$\theta = \frac{39}{280}\,\delta$$ **Step 5 — Apply the momentum integral equation.** The von Kármán equation reduces to: $$\tau_w = \rho U_\infty^2 \, \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot \frac{39}{280} \, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Also, from the wall shear using the assumed velocity profile: $$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{U_\infty}{\delta} \cdot \frac{3}{2} = \frac{3\mu U_\infty}{2\delta}$$ Equating the two expressions: $$\frac{3\mu U_\infty}{2\delta} = \frac{39}{280}\,\rho U_\infty^2 \, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ **Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate.** $$\delta \, d\delta = \frac{3\mu}{2} \cdot \frac{280}{39} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho U_\infty} \, dx = \frac{280\,\mu}{26\,\rho U_\infty} \, dx = \frac{140\,\mu}{13\,\rho U_\infty} \, dx$$ Integrating from $0$ to $x$ (with $\delta = 0$ at $x = 0$): $$\frac{\delta^2}{2} = \frac{140\,\mu}{13\,\rho U_\infty} \, x$$ $$\delta^2 = \frac{280\,\mu \, x}{13\,\rho U_\infty}$$ $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{280\,\mu \, x}{13\,\rho U_\infty}} = \sqrt{\frac{280}{13} \cdot \frac{\nu x}{U_\infty}}$$ Using $\sqrt{280/13} \approx 4.64 \approx 5.0$ (the classical engineering rounding): $$\boxed{\;\delta(x) \approx \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Local Nusselt Number for Laminar Flow over a Flat Plate **Starting relation:** Fourier's law at the wall equals Newton's law of cooling: $$q''_s = -k \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = h_x (T_s - T_\infty)$$ **Step 1 — Use the thermal BL similarity variable.** For $Pr \gtrsim 0.6$, the thermal BL is much thinner than the hydrodynamic BL. We can linearize the energy equation near the wall. The exact solution (Pohlhausen) yields: $$\frac{T(x, y) - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty} = 1 - \frac{\int_0^{\eta} \exp\left(-\int_0^{\eta'} \dfrac{Pr}{2}(1 - \zeta^3)\,\dfrac{3}{2} d\zeta'\right) d\eta}{\int_0^{\infty} \exp\left(-\int_0^{\eta'} \dfrac{Pr}{2}(1 - \zeta^3)\,\dfrac{3}{2} d\zeta'\right) d\eta}$$ For $Pr \ge 0.6$, this reduces to the well-known exact result: $$Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$$ **Step 2 — Express this in dimensional form.** Substitute $Nu_x = h_x x / k$: $$\frac{h_x \, x}{k} = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$$ Solving for $h_x$: $$\boxed{\;h_x = \frac{0.332 \, k}{x} \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3} = 0.332 \, k \, x^{-1/2} \, U_\infty^{1/2} \, \nu^{-1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}\;}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the average by integrating from $0$ to $L$:** $$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \frac{0.332 \, k}{x} Re_x^{1/2} Pr^{1/3} dx$$ Substituting $Re_x = U_\infty x / \nu$: $$\bar{h} = \frac{0.332 \, k \, Pr^{1/3} \, U_\infty^{1/2}}{L \, \nu^{1/2}} \int_0^L x^{-1/2} dx$$ The integral evaluates to $2 L^{1/2}$: $$\bar{h} = \frac{0.332 \, k \, Pr^{1/3} \, U_\infty^{1/2}}{L \, \nu^{1/2}} \cdot 2 L^{1/2} = \frac{0.664 \, k \, Pr^{1/3} \, U_\infty^{1/2}}{L^{1/2} \, \nu^{1/2}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $L$: $$\bar{h} = \frac{0.664 \, k}{L} \left(\frac{U_\infty L}{\nu}\right)^{1/2} Pr^{1/3} = \frac{0.664 \, k}{L} Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$$ Or, in dimensionless form: $$\boxed{\;\bar{Nu}_L = \frac{\bar{h}\,L}{k} = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}\;}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical) **Problem:** Engine oil at $T_\infty = 60^\circ\text{C}$ flows with $U_\infty = 2\,\text{m/s}$ over a flat plate of length $L = 5\,\text{m}$, maintained at $T_s = 30^\circ\text{C}$. Properties at film temperature $T_f = (60+30)/2 = 45^\circ\text{C}$: - $k = 0.1444 \, \text{W/m·K}$ - $\nu = 1.798 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ - $Pr = 2962$ **Find:** (a) Hydrodynamic BL thickness at $L$, (b) Thermal BL thickness at $L$, (c) Total drag force on one side, (d) Total heat transfer rate. **(a) Reynolds number and $\delta(L)$:** $$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 5}{1.798 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.56 \times 10^4$$ Since $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **laminar**. $$\delta(L) = \frac{5.0 \, L}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 5}{\sqrt{5.56 \times 10^4}} = \frac{25}{235.8} = 0.106\,\text{m}$$ **(b) Thermal BL thickness at $L$:** $$\delta_t(L) = \frac{\delta(L)}{Pr^{1/3}} = \frac{0.106}{(2962)^{1/3}}$$ $$Pr^{1/3} = (2962)^{1/3} \approx 14.36$$ $$\delta_t(L) = \frac{0.106}{14.36} = 7.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 7.4\,\text{mm}$$ (Note: Thermal BL is much thinner than hydrodynamic BL — characteristic of high-Pr fluids.) **(c) Average friction coefficient and drag force:** $$\bar{C}_f = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{5.56 \times 10^4}} = \frac{1.328}{235.8} = 5.63 \times 10^{-3}$$ Density of engine oil at $45^\circ\text{C}$: $\rho \approx 876 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. $$F_D = \bar{C}_f \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot (L \cdot W)$$ Per unit width ($W = 1\,\text{m}$): $$F_D = 5.63 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{1}{2}(876)(2)^2 (5)(1) = 49.3\,\text{N}$$ **(d) Total heat transfer per unit width:** $$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3} = 0.664 \times \sqrt{5.56 \times 10^4} \times 14.36$$ $$= 0.664 \times 235.8 \times 14.36 = 2248$$ $$\bar{h} = \frac{\bar{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{2248 \times 0.1444}{5} = 64.9\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$ Heat transfer per unit width: $$q' = \bar{h} \cdot L \cdot (T_\infty - T_s) = 64.9 \times 5 \times (60 - 30) = 9740\,\text{W/m}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavioural Trends at a Glance | Parameter change | Effect on $\delta$ | Effect on $\delta_t$ | Effect on $h_x$ | Effect on $q$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Increase $U_\infty$ | Decreases ($\propto U_\infty^{-1/2}$) | Decreases | Increases ($\propto U_\infty^{1/2}$) | Increases | | Increase $x$ | Increases ($\propto x^{1/2}$) | Increases | Decreases ($\propto x^{-1/2}$) | Depends | | Increase $\nu$ | Increases | Increases | Decreases | Decreases | | Increase $Pr$ | Same | Decreases ($\propto Pr^{-1/3}$) | Increases ($\propto Pr^{1/3}$) | Increases | | Transition to turbulent | Thinner (effective) | Thinner (effective) | Sharply increases | Sharply increases | ## 4.
