# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q: Define the Nusselt Number. What is its physical significance? State the value of the Nusselt Number for fully developed laminar flow through a circular pipe under (i) constant wall temperature and (ii) constant heat flux conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Nusselt Number is defined as the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer at a fluid-solid interface:

$$\text{Nu} = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k_f}$$

**Physical Significance:** The Nusselt Number represents the enhancement of heat transfer due to convection over pure conduction. A value of **Nu = 1** indicates pure conduction (no fluid motion), while **Nu >> 1** indicates convection dominance.

**Standard values for fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe:**
- (i) Constant wall temperature: **Nu = 3.66**
- (ii) Constant heat flux: **Nu = 4.36**

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Note:** Full marks require both the **equation** AND the **two numerical values**. Missing either the equation or the boundary values leads to a 1-mark deduction per KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Q2]**

**Q: Differentiate between the Nusselt Number and the Biot Number. Why is the Nusselt Number important in heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Nusselt Number (Nu) | Biot Number (Bi) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | $h L_c / k_f$ | $h L_c / k_s$ |
| Thermal conductivity of | **Fluid** | **Solid** |
| Physical meaning | Convection vs conduction in **fluid** | Internal conduction vs surface convection in **solid** |
| Typical range | 1 to 10,000 | 0.01 to 100 |

**Importance in Heat Exchangers:**
- The Nusselt Number allows the calculation of the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ via $h = \text{Nu} \cdot k_f / L_c$ without resorting to first-principles fluid dynamics.
- Heat exchanger performance metrics (LMTD, effectiveness-NTU method) require $h$, which is universally expressed in terms of Nu, Re, and Pr correlations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse the Nusselt Number with the Biot Number. The critical difference is that **Nu** uses fluid conductivity $k_f$, while **Bi** uses solid conductivity $k_s$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Using Buckingham Pi Theorem, derive the functional relationship for forced convection heat transfer showing that $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$.
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Water at $60^\circ\text{C}$ flows through a 5 cm diameter pipe with a mean velocity of 2 m/s. The pipe wall is maintained at $80^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate the convective heat transfer coefficient using the Dittus-Boelter equation. Properties of water at the film temperature (assume $T_f = 70^\circ\text{C}$): $\rho = 977.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·s/m}^2$, $k = 0.668 \text{ W/m·K}$, $c_p = 4189 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\text{Pr} = 2.51$.
**[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1:** List the variables affecting $h$ in forced convection:
$$h = f(L_c, v, \rho, \mu, k_f, c_p, \Delta T)$$

**Step 2:** Number of variables $n = 7$; fundamental dimensions $m = 4$ (M, L, T, $\theta$).

**Step 3:** Number of dimensionless groups: $k = 7 - 4 = 3$.

**Step 4:** Choose repeating variables: $\rho, v, L_c, k_f$.

**Step 5:** Form $\Pi_1$ with $h$:
$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot \rho^a \cdot v^b \cdot L_c^c \cdot k_f^d$$

Setting dimensions to zero and solving yields $\Pi_1 = h L_c / k_f = \text{Nu}$.

**Step 6:** Form $\Pi_2 = \rho v L_c / \mu = \text{Re}$.

**Step 7:** Form $\Pi_3 = \mu c_p / k_f = \text{Pr}$.

**Step 8:** Final relationship:
$$\boxed{\text{Nu} = \phi(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{977.8 \times 2 \times 0.05}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{97.78}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 244,450$$

Since $\text{Re} = 244{,}450 > 10{,}000$, the flow is **turbulent** and Dittus-Boelter is applicable.

**Step 2 — Identify Prandtl Number:**
$\text{Pr} = 2.51$ (given)

**Step 3 — Identify Exponent $n$:**
Since the fluid is being **heated** ($T_s = 80^\circ\text{C} > T_{\text{water}} = 60^\circ\text{C}$), we use $n = 0.4$.

**Step 4 — Apply Dittus-Boelter Equation:**

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \cdot \text{Re}^{0.8} \cdot \text{Pr}^{0.4}$$

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \times (244{,}450)^{0.8} \times (2.51)^{0.4}$$

Computing step by step:

$(244{,}450)^{0.8}$: Take logarithm, $\log(244{,}450) = 5.388$; multiply by 0.8 to get $4.310$; antilog $= 20,420$.

$(2.51)^{0.4}$: $\log(2.51) = 0.3997$; multiply by 0.4 to get $0.1599$; antilog $= 1.446$.

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \times 20{,}420 \times 1.446$$

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \times 29{,}527 = 679.1$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $h$:**

$$h = \frac{\text{Nu} \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{679.1 \times 0.668}{0.05}$$

$$h = \frac{453.6}{0.05} = 9{,}072 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 9{,}072 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Many KTU students forget the **exponent $n$ distinction** between heating ($n=0.4$) and cooling ($n=0.3$). Also, computing $D = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ and not $5 \text{ m}$ is a frequent **unit error** that costs 1–2 marks. Always carry units in your substitution step.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted to 2024 NEP Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Nusselt's theory of film condensation** on a vertical plate. Derive the expression for the average Nusselt Number for laminar film condensation.
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Air at $27^\circ\text{C}$ flows over a flat plate of length 2 m with a free stream velocity of 5 m/s. Calculate the average Nusselt Number and the average heat transfer coefficient. Properties of air at film temperature ($T_f = 300 \text{ K}$): $\rho = 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.02624 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\text{Pr} = 0.707$.
**[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Nusselt Condensation Theory

**Step 1 — Physical Setup:** Saturated vapor at $T_{\text{sat}}$ condenses on a vertical plate of height $L$ and width $W$ at temperature $T_s < T_{\text{sat}}$, forming a liquid film that flows down by gravity.

**Step 2 — Assumptions (Standard Nusselt Assumptions):**
- Steady, laminar film flow
- Constant fluid properties
- Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction (no convection within the film)
- Negligible vapor velocity; film motion is gravity-driven
- Subcooling of condensate is negligible

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic Analysis:**
Force balance on the film:

$$\mu_l \frac{dv}{dy} = \rho_l g (\delta - y)$$

Integrating with $v(0) = 0$:

$$v(y) = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate per Unit Width:**

$$\dot{m}/W = \int_0^\delta \rho_l v \, dy = \frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}$$

**Step 5 — Energy Balance at Liquid-Vapor Interface:**

$$h_{fg} \cdot \dot{m}/W = k_l \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Local Film Thickness:**

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu_l k_l \Delta T \cdot x}{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 7 — Local and Average Nusselt Numbers:**

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x \cdot x}{k_l} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} x^3}{4 \mu_l k_l \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = \frac{4}{3} \text{Nu}_{x=L}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g h_{fg} L^3}{\mu_l k_l \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (Flat Plate)

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number at Trailing Edge:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{1.177 \times 5 \times 2}{1.846 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{11.77}{1.846 \times 10^{-5}} = 637{,}595$$

Since $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **entirely laminar** over the plate.

**Step 2 — Apply Average Laminar Flat Plate Correlation:**

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \cdot \text{Re}_L^{0.5} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

Computing each term:

$(637{,}595)^{0.5} = 798.5$

$(0.707)^{1/3} = 0.8907$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times 798.5 \times 0.8907$$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times 711.1 = 472.2$$

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \approx 472.2}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Average Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{472.2 \times 0.02624}{2}$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{12.39}{2} = 6.20 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 6.20 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** A frequent error is using the **local Nusselt Number formula** (with $0.332$ and $\text{Re}_x^{0.5}$) instead of the **average formula** (with $0.664$) when the problem asks for the overall average over the plate length. This is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation. Also, students often forget to use $L = 2 \text{ m}$ in the denominator for $\bar{h}$, mistakenly using $x$ (local) instead.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section as a **30-second pre-exam revision** before entering the examination hall.

- **Definition:** $\text{Nu} = h L_c / k_f$ — ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer.
- **Dimensionless:** Verified via units: $[h L / k] = 1$.
- **Physical meaning:** $\text{Nu} = 1$ implies pure conduction; $\text{Nu} \gg 1$ implies convection-dominant.
- **For laminar flow in pipes:**
  - Constant wall temperature: $\text{Nu} = 3.66$
  - Constant heat flux: $\text{Nu} = 4.36$
- **Forced convection in turbulent pipe flow (Dittus-Boelter):** $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \text{Pr}^n$ with $n = 0.4$ (heating) and $n = 0.3$ (cooling); valid for $\text{Re} > 10{,}000$.
- **Laminar flow over flat plate:**
  - Local: $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{0.5} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
  - Average: $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{0.5} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
- **Turbulent flow over flat plate (local):** $\text{Nu}_x = 0.0296 \, \text{Re}_x^{0.8} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
- **Natural convection (vertical plate, laminar):** $\text{Nu} = 0.59 \, (\text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr})^{1/4}$
- **Nusselt Condensation Theory:** $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g h_{fg} L^3}{\mu_l k_l \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ — applies for laminar film condensation.
- **Buckingham Pi Theorem result:** Forced convection must obey $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$.
- **Nusselt is NOT a fluid property** — it is a *flow-and-geometry-dependent* dimensionless group.
- **Always use film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty) / 2$ to evaluate fluid properties.
- **Characteristic lengths to remember:**
  - Pipe/duct: $D$ or hydraulic diameter $D_h$
  - Flat plate: distance $x$ from leading edge (local) or total $L$ (average)
  - Cylinder/Sphere: $D$
- **Key exam trap:** Nusselt Number uses **fluid** $k_f$, not solid $k_s$ (that's the Biot Number).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Boundary Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Velocity Boundary Layer # Velocity Boundary Layer — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Velocity Boundary Layer (VBL):** It is a thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid surface, in which the fluid velocity relative to the surface changes from **zero at the surface (no-slip condition)** to the **free-stream velocity ($U_\infty$)** of the main flow. The concept was introduced by **Ludwig Prandtl (1904)** and forms the foundation of modern viscous fluid mechanics. Mathematically, the boundary layer is defined as the region where: $$0 \le y \le \delta(x)$$ where the non-dimensional velocity ratio $u/U_\infty < 0.99$. The edge of the boundary layer, $y = \delta$, is conventionally defined as the locus of points where $u = 0.99\, U_\infty$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — "A leaf falling on a calm pond":** > Imagine a long, flat ship moving slowly through still water. The water touching the hull sticks to it (velocity = 0). A few centimetres away, the water moves almost as fast as the ship would move through a stationary fluid. The thin shear-affected zone between the hull and the undisturbed water is the **velocity boundary layer**. > A leaf floating nearby is dragged slightly toward the ship because it sits within this layer. Far from the hull, the leaf does not feel the ship at all — that is the **inviscid free stream**. - The boundary layer is **very thin** (a few millimetres in air, fractions of a millimetre in water at typical engineering scales). - It is the region where **viscous effects are dominant**. - Outside this layer, viscous effects are negligible and the flow can be treated as **inviscid (Euler flow)**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **No-slip condition:** $u = v = 0$ at $y = 0$ (wall). - **Free-stream velocity:** $U_\infty$ (constant, in **m/s**). - **Kinematic viscosity of fluid:** $\nu$ (in **m²/s**). - **Reynolds number at distance $x$ from leading edge:** $Re_x = U_\infty x / \nu$. - **Critical Reynolds number (transition):** $Re_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ for flow over a flat plate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** The boundary layer concept *decouples* the viscous and inviscid regions of a flow field, allowing engineers to solve the two regions with different — and much simpler — mathematical formulations. This is the genius of Prandtl's contribution. --- # Velocity Boundary Layer — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Prandtl's Boundary Layer Assumptions For high Reynolds-number flow over a solid surface, Prandtl postulated the following (which are the basis of the simplified **boundary layer equations**): 1. The boundary layer thickness $\delta$ is very small compared to the characteristic length $L$ of the body: $\delta \ll L$. 2. The velocity component along the surface, $u$, is of the same order as $U_\infty$. 3. The velocity component normal to the surface, $v$, is much smaller: $v \ll u$ (typically $v \sim u \cdot \delta / L$). 4. The pressure gradient across the boundary layer is negligible: $\partial p / \partial y \approx 0$, so $p(x,y) \approx p(x)$ — the pressure is impressed on the layer by the outer inviscid flow. ## 2.2 Types of Boundary Layers - **Laminar Boundary Layer:** Fluid particles move in smooth, parallel layers; transition occurs at $Re_x \le 5 \times 10^5$. - **Turbulent Boundary Layer:** Chaotic, fluctuating motion; eddies dominate; $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$. - **Transitional Boundary Layer:** Mixed regime, intermittent between laminar and turbulent. ## 2.3 Characteristic Thicknesses of the Velocity Boundary Layer ### (a) Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$) The distance from the wall to the point where $u = 0.99\, U_\infty$. ### (b) Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$) The distance by which the external streamline is displaced outward due to the reduction in mass flow rate caused by the boundary layer: $$\delta^* = \int_0^\infty \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$ **Physical meaning:** Represents the loss in mass flow due to the presence of the boundary layer; equivalent to the added thickness of an imaginary inviscid flow that would carry the same mass flow. ### (c) Momentum Thickness ($\theta$) The loss of momentum flux per unit width due to the boundary layer: $$\theta = \int_0^\infty \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$ **Physical meaning:** Represents the deficit in momentum flux compared to an inviscid flow. ### (d) Energy Thickness ($\delta_E$ or $\delta_3$) The loss of kinetic energy flux per unit width: $$\delta_E = \int_0^\infty \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u^2}{U_\infty^2}\right) dy$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ordering (always true):** $\delta > \delta^* > \theta$, and $\delta > \delta^* > \delta_E$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Boundary layer thickness (laminar, flat plate) | $\delta \approx 5.0\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$ | m | $Re_x = U_\infty x / \nu$ | | 2 | Boundary layer thickness (turbulent, flat plate) | $\delta \approx 0.37\, x / Re_x^{1/5}$ | m | Empirical, 1/7th power law | | 3 | Wall shear stress (laminar) | $\tau_w = 0.332\, \rho\, U_\infty^2 / \sqrt{Re_x}$ | N/m² | Blasius exact result | | 4 | Local skin-friction coefficient (laminar) | $C_f = 0.664 / \sqrt{Re_x}$ | — | Dimensionless | | 5 | Average skin-friction coefficient (laminar) | $\bar{C_f} = 1.328 / \sqrt{Re_L}$ | — | Over length $L$ | | 6 | Local skin-friction coefficient (turbulent) | $C_f = 0.0592 / Re_x^{1/5}$ | — | Empirical | | 7 | Average skin-friction coefficient (turbulent) | $\bar{C_f} = 0.074 / Re_L^{1/5}$ | — | Empirical | | 8 | Displacement thickness | $\delta^* = \int_0^\infty (1 - u/U_\infty)\,dy$ | m | Mass deficit | | 9 | Momentum thickness | $\theta = \int_0^\infty (u/U_\infty)(1 - u/U_\infty)\,dy$ | m | Momentum deficit | | 10 | Energy thickness | $\delta_E = \int_0^\infty (u/U_\infty)(1 - u^2/U_\infty^2)\,dy$ | m | KE deficit | | 11 | Von Kármán integral equation | $\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2 \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ | N/m² | Key integral relation | | 12 | Shape factor | $H = \delta^* / \theta$ | — | Indicates separation if $H > 2$ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE (per system rules):** All absolute-value / magnitude symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` form to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace engineering:** Predicting drag on aircraft wings, fuselage, helicopter blades. The skin-friction drag (viscous) accounts for ~50% of total drag on a commercial airliner. - **Turbomachinery (KTU core area):** Blade boundary layers govern efficiency of compressors and turbines; boundary layer separation causes stall. - **Hydraulic engineering:** Drag on ships, submarines, bridge piers, spillways. - **Heat exchangers & cooling:** Velocity profile in the boundary layer directly couples to the **thermal boundary layer**, controlling convective heat transfer coefficients ($h$). - **Wind engineering:** Pressure distribution and structural loading on high-rise buildings. --- # Velocity Boundary Layer — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation (The Most Important Derivation for KTU) ### 3.1.1 Control Volume Setup Consider steady, two-dimensional, incompressible flow over a flat plate. Take a control volume bounded by: - Bottom: the wall from $x$ to $x + dx$. - Top: the edge of the boundary layer, $y = \delta$. - Left: vertical at $x$. - Right: vertical at $x + dx$. **Assumptions:** - Pressure $p = p_\infty$ is constant (no external pressure gradient for a flat plate). - At $y = \delta$, velocity $u = U_\infty$ (uniform). - Steady flow. ### 3.1.2 Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity) Mass flow entering at the left face (section 1) must equal mass flow leaving at the right face (section 2) plus the net outflow through the top of the CV (since the wall has no flow through it). At section 1 (left): mass flow in = $\int_0^\delta \rho\, u\, dy$. At section 2 (right): mass flow in = $\int_0^{\delta + d\delta} \rho\, u\, dy$ evaluated at $x + dx$. The increase in mass flow within the CV is $\rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right)$. To conserve mass, the excess at the right face must exit through the top as $d\dot{m}_{top}$. By Taylor expansion: $$d\dot{m}_{top} = \rho\, U_\infty\, d\delta^*$$ This **defines** the displacement thickness. ### 3.1.3 Apply Conservation of Momentum (Integral Form) For steady flow, the net momentum efflux = sum of forces on the CV. Momentum flux entering at $x$: $\int_0^\delta \rho\, u^2\, dy$. Momentum flux leaving at $x + dx$: $\int_0^{\delta + d\delta} \rho\, u^2\, dy$. Momentum leaving through the top of CV: $\rho\, U_\infty\, U_\infty\, d\delta^* = \rho\, U_\infty^2\, d\delta^*$ (since fluid there moves at $U_\infty$). Net change in momentum flux within CV (momentum balance): $$\rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u^2\, dy\right) = -\rho\, U_\infty^2\, d\delta^*$$ Rearranging: $$\rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u^2\, dy\right) + \rho\, U_\infty^2\, d\delta^* = 0$$ ### 3.1.4 Substitute the Definition of $\delta^*$ Recall $\delta^* = \int_0^\delta (1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy = \delta - \int_0^\delta (u/U_\infty)\, dy$. Differentiating with respect to $x$ (treating $\delta$ as a function of $x$): $$\frac{d\delta^*}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = -\frac{1}{U_\infty}\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^\delta u\, dy$$ (Leibniz rule, noting the integrand vanishes at $y = \delta$ to leading order.) Therefore: $\rho\, U_\infty^2\, d\delta^* = -\rho\, U_\infty\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right)$. ### 3.1.5 Substituting Back into the Momentum Balance $$\rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u^2\, dy\right) - \rho\, U_\infty\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right) = 0$$ Factor out $\rho$: $$\rho\left[d\left(\int_0^\delta u^2\, dy\right) - U_\infty\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right)\right] = 0$$ The momentum thickness is defined as: $$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\, dy - \int_0^\delta \frac{u^2}{U_\infty^2}\, dy$$ Multiply by $U_\infty^2$: $$U_\infty^2 \theta = U_\infty \int_0^\delta u\, dy - \int_0^\delta u^2\, dy$$ Differentiating with respect to $x$: $$U_\infty^2 \frac{d\theta}{dx} = U_\infty\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right) - d\left(\int_0^\delta u^2\, dy\right)$$ Comparing with the momentum balance, we get: $$U_\infty^2 \frac{d\theta}{dx} = -[\text{net change in momentum balance}]$$ But the **net force** on the CV in the $x$-direction equals the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ acting in the $+x$ direction on the wall (the only external surface force in $x$; pressure forces cancel because $p$ is uniform). By Newton's second law applied to a CV: $$-\tau_w\, dx = \text{net momentum out} - \text{net momentum in}$$ Combining the two: $$\boxed{\;\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2\, \frac{d\theta}{dx}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation (1921)**. It relates the wall shear stress to the rate of growth of the momentum thickness — and is the central integral relation used in nearly every KTU-level boundary layer problem. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boundary Layer Thickness for Laminar Flow Over a Flat Plate (Blasius) ### 3.2.1 Assume a Cubic Velocity Profile Assume a 3rd-order polynomial satisfying all boundary conditions: - At $y = 0$: $u = 0$ (no-slip). - At $y = 0$: $\partial u / \partial y = \text{const}$ (approx). - At $y = \delta$: $u = U_\infty$ (edge condition). - At $y = \delta$: $\partial u / \partial y = 0$ (smooth blending). The assumed profile is: $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$ ### 3.2.2 Compute the Momentum Thickness $\theta$ $$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$ Let $\eta = y/\delta$, $dy = \delta\, d\eta$: $$\theta = \delta \int_0^1 \left(\frac{3\eta}{2} - \frac{\eta^3}{2}\right)\left(1 - \frac{3\eta}{2} + \frac{\eta^3}{2}\right) d\eta$$ Expand the product. The first part: $A = \frac{3\eta}{2} - \frac{\eta^3}{2}$. The second part: $B = 1 - \frac{3\eta}{2} + \frac{\eta^3}{2}$. Compute $A \cdot B$ term-by-term: $$A \cdot B = \frac{3\eta}{2}\left(1 - \frac{3\eta}{2} + \frac{\eta^3}{2}\right) - \frac{\eta^3}{2}\left(1 - \frac{3\eta}{2} + \frac{\eta^3}{2}\right)$$ First bracket: $$= \frac{3\eta}{2} - \frac{9\eta^2}{4} + \frac{3\eta^4}{4}$$ Second bracket: $$= \frac{\eta^3}{2} - \frac{3\eta^4}{4} + \frac{\eta^6}{4}$$ Subtracting: $$A \cdot B = \frac{3\eta}{2} - \frac{9\eta^2}{4} + \frac{3\eta^4}{4} - \frac{\eta^3}{2} + \frac{3\eta^4}{4} - \frac{\eta^6}{4}$$ $$= \frac{3\eta}{2} - \frac{9\eta^2}{4} - \frac{\eta^3}{2} + \frac{3\eta^4}{2} - \frac{\eta^6}{4}$$ Now integrate term-by-term from 0 to 1: $$\int_0^1 \frac{3\eta}{2}\, d\eta = \frac{3}{4}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{9\eta^2}{4}\, d\eta = \frac{9}{12} = \frac{3}{4}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{\eta^3}{2}\, d\eta = \frac{1}{8}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{3\eta^4}{2}\, d\eta = \frac{3}{10}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{\eta^6}{4}\, d\eta = \frac{1}{28}$$ Combining (with sign pattern $+,-,+,+,-$): $$\int_0^1 A \cdot B\, d\eta = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{10} - \frac{1}{28}$$ Compute common denominator 280: - $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{210}{280}$ - $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{210}{280}$ (subtracted) - $\frac{1}{8} = \frac{35}{280}$ - $\frac{3}{10} = \frac{84}{280}$ - $\frac{1}{28} = \frac{10}{280}$ Sum: $\frac{210 - 210 - 35 + 84 - 10}{280} = \frac{39}{280}$ Therefore: $$\theta = \delta \cdot \frac{39}{280}$$ ### 3.2.3 Compute the Wall Shear Stress from the Profile $$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$ Differentiate the assumed profile: $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{y}{\delta} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$ $$\frac{\partial (u/U_\infty)}{\partial y} = \frac{3}{2\delta} - \frac{3 y^2}{2 \delta^3}$$ At $y = 0$: $$\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{3 U_\infty}{2\delta}$$ Hence: $$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \frac{3 U_\infty}{2\delta} = \frac{3 \mu U_\infty}{2 \delta}$$ ### 3.2.4 Apply Von Kármán Integral Equation $$\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2\, \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \rho\, U_\infty^2 \cdot \frac{39}{280}\, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Equating the two expressions for $\tau_w$: $$\frac{3 \mu U_\infty}{2 \delta} = \frac{39 \rho U_\infty^2}{280}\, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Since $\mu = \rho \nu$: $$\frac{3 \nu}{2 \delta} = \frac{39 U_\infty}{280}\, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Solve for $d\delta$: $$\delta\, d\delta = \frac{3 \nu \cdot 280}{2 \cdot 39\, U_\infty}\, dx = \frac{840 \nu}{78\, U_\infty}\, dx = \frac{140 \nu}{13\, U_\infty}\, dx$$ Integrate from $0$ to $x$, with $\delta = 0$ at $x = 0$: $$\frac{\delta^2}{2} = \frac{140 \nu}{13\, U_\infty}\, x$$ $$\delta^2 = \frac{280 \nu x}{13\, U_\infty}$$ In terms of $Re_x = U_\infty x / \nu$: $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{280\, x}{13\, Re_x}} = x \sqrt{\frac{280}{13\, Re_x}}$$ Numerically: $\sqrt{280/13} \approx 4.64$. So: $$\delta \approx \frac{4.64\, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The exact Blasius solution (from the full Navier–Stokes solution) gives $\delta = 5.0\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$. The cubic-profile approximation gives $\delta \approx 4.64\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$, which is within ~7% — excellent engineering accuracy for a 3rd-order polynomial. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Displacement Thickness From the Cubic Profile $$\delta^* = \int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 \left(1 - \frac{3\eta}{2} + \frac{\eta^3}{2}\right) d\eta$$ Compute the integrand integral: $$\int_0^1 1\, d\eta = 1$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{3\eta}{2}\, d\eta = \frac{3}{4}$$ $$\int_0^1 \frac{\eta^3}{2}\, d\eta = \frac{1}{8}$$ Sum: $1 - \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{8 - 6 + 1}{8} = \frac{3}{8}$ $$\delta^* = \frac{3 \delta}{8}$$ Therefore, the **shape factor** $H$: $$H = \frac{\delta^*}{\theta} = \frac{3\delta/8}{39\delta/280} = \frac{3 \cdot 280}{8 \cdot 39} = \frac{840}{312} = \frac{35}{13} \approx 2.69$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the shape factor for a healthy attached boundary layer ($H \approx 2.59$ for Blasius exact, $\approx 2.69$ for the cubic approximation) with the value **at incipient separation** ($H \approx 3.5$ to $7$ depending on pressure gradient). A high shape factor is the **diagnostic signature of boundary layer separation** in adverse pressure gradients. ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Flow Regime | $\delta(x)$ | $C_f$ (local) | $\bar{C_f}$ (avg.) | Comments | |-------------|------------|---------------|--------------------|----| | Laminar, Blasius exact | $5.0\, x / Re_x^{1/2}$ | $0.664 / Re_x^{1/2}$ | $1.328 / Re_L^{1/2}$ | Exact NS solution | | Laminar, cubic Pohlhausen | $4.64\, x / Re_x^{1/2}$ | $0.646 / Re_x^{1/2}$ | $1.292 / Re_L^{1/2}$ | Approx, KTU-examinable | | Turbulent, 1/7 power law | $0.37\, x / Re_x^{1/5}$ | $0.0592 / Re_x^{1/5}$ | $0.074 / Re_L^{1/5}$ | Empirical | --- # Velocity Boundary Layer — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Velocity Boundary Layer Development The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physical structure and information flow** within a velocity boundary layer developing over a flat plate. This layered structure is a classic KTU question topic for turbulent boundary layers. --- # Velocity Boundary Layer — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCPET303]` **Define the following terms with respect to a velocity boundary layer over a flat plate:** **(i) Boundary layer thickness $\delta$** **(ii) Displacement thickness $\delta^*$** **(iii) Momentum thickness $\theta$** **Model Answer:** **(i) Boundary layer thickness $\delta$:** The distance measured normal to the surface from the wall to the point where the local velocity $u$ reaches $99\%$ of the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$. At this edge, viscous effects are considered negligible. **(ii) Displacement thickness $\delta^*$:** It is defined as $\delta^* = \int_0^\infty (1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$. Physically, it is the distance by which the external streamline is displaced outward due to the reduction in mass flow rate within the boundary layer. **(iii) Momentum thickness $\theta$:** It is defined as $\theta = \int_0^\infty (u/U_\infty)(1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$. Physically, it represents the loss in momentum flux per unit width of the boundary layer compared to an inviscid flow at $U_\infty$. ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCPET303]` **State Prandtl's boundary layer assumptions. Why are they justified at high Reynolds numbers?** **Model Answer:** Prandtl's assumptions for a steady, 2-D, incompressible boundary layer are: 1. The boundary layer is thin: $\delta \ll L$ (body length scale). 2. The streamwise velocity $u \sim U_\infty$ (same order as free stream). 3. The normal velocity $v \ll u$ (typically $v \sim u \cdot \delta/L$). 4. The pressure is uniform across the layer: $\partial p / \partial y \approx 0$, so $p(x,y) \approx p(x)$. **Justification:** At high Reynolds numbers, viscous effects are confined to a very thin layer near the wall. The normal velocity is forced to be small by the no-penetration condition at the wall and the kinematic constraint imposed by the thinness of the layer. The pressure, transmitted by the inviscid outer flow, is uniform across the thin layer to leading order. These assumptions reduce the full Navier–Stokes equations to the much simpler **Prandtl boundary layer equations**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > **(1)** In Von Kármán derivation questions, students often forget the **mass conservation step** (which yields the displacement thickness contribution at the top of the control volume). Skipping this loses **3 to 4 marks** out of 7. > **(2)** When assuming a polynomial velocity profile, students frequently **omit one of the four boundary conditions** (e.g., they forget $\partial u/\partial y = 0$ at $y = \delta$). This leads to a wrong profile and an incorrect $\delta(x)$ — a guaranteed 4-mark deduction. > **(3)** Always state the **units** of your final answer in Part B numerical problems. Examiners deduct 1 mark if units are missing. > **(4)** Do not confuse $\delta$ (boundary layer thickness, $\propto x^{1/2}$ laminar) with the **displacement thickness $\delta^*$**. The former is geometric, the latter is mass-deficit based. --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PCPET303 Module 4]` **(a)** State the Von Kármán momentum integral equation for a 2-D steady incompressible flow over a flat plate. Derive this equation by applying the integral momentum theorem to an elemental control volume of thickness $dx$ within the boundary layer. (7 marks) **(b)** Using the assumed velocity profile $\dfrac{u}{U_\infty} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right) - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$ for laminar flow over a flat plate, determine:     (i) The boundary layer thickness $\delta(x)$ as a function of $x$ and $Re_x$.     (ii) The local skin-friction coefficient $C_f(x)$.     (iii) The drag force on one side of a flat plate of length $L$ and width $b$ immersed in a flow of free-stream velocity $U_\infty$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$. (7 marks) ### Model Solution B-A (Part a — 7 marks) **Step 1 — State the equation:** The Von Kármán momentum integral equation is: $$\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2\, \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Control volume:** Take a CV between $x$ and $x + dx$, bounded above by $y = \delta$ (where $u = U_\infty$) and below by the wall $y = 0$. **Step 3 — Mass conservation:** The net mass flux through the top of the CV is: $$d\dot{m}_{top} = \rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right) - 0 \quad \text{(wall is impermeable)}$$ This mass leaves at velocity $U_\infty$, so the **displacement thickness** is naturally introduced: $$\rho\, U_\infty\, d\delta^* = \rho\, d\left(\int_0^\delta u\, dy\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\delta^* = \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^\delta (1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$$ **Step 4 — Momentum balance:** Sum of forces in $x$ = net efflux of $x$-momentum. Forces on CV: Wall shear $\tau_w$ acts on the bottom in the $+x$ direction (since fluid drags the wall in the $+x$ direction, by Newton's third law the wall exerts $-\tau_w$ on the fluid). The pressure at the top edge is uniform ($p_\infty$) and the wall has no pressure variation. Net force in $x$ = $-\tau_w\, dx$. Momentum efflux in $x$ (entering left, leaving right and top): $$d\left(\int_0^\delta \rho\, u^2\, dy\right) + \rho\, U_\infty^2\, d\delta^*$$ (where the second term is the $U_\infty$ momentum carried by the displaced mass at the top). **Step 5 — Equate and simplify:** $-\tau_w\, dx = d\left(\int_0^\delta \rho u^2\, dy\right) + \rho U_\infty^2 d\delta^*$. After substitution of the displacement thickness derivative and algebraic manipulation (shown in Section 3.1 of these notes), the result simplifies to: $$\boxed{\;\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2\, \frac{d\theta}{dx}\;}$$ where $\theta = \int_0^\delta (u/U_\infty)(1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$. ### Model Solution B-A (Part b — 7 marks) **(i) Boundary layer thickness:** From the assumed profile: - Wall shear: $\tau_w = \mu \cdot (3U_\infty / 2\delta)$. - Momentum thickness: $\theta = (39/280)\,\delta$. Substituting into Von Kármán equation: $$\frac{3\mu U_\infty}{2\delta} = \rho\, U_\infty^2 \cdot \frac{39}{280} \cdot \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Using $\mu/\rho = \nu$: $$\frac{3 \nu}{2 \delta} = \frac{39 U_\infty}{280}\, \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$ Separating variables and integrating: $$\delta\, d\delta = \frac{140 \nu}{13 U_\infty}\, dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\delta^2}{2} = \frac{140 \nu}{13 U_\infty}\, x$$ $$\delta(x) = \sqrt{\frac{280\, \nu\, x}{13\, U_\infty}} = \frac{4.64\, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$ **(ii) Local skin-friction coefficient:** $$C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{3\mu U_\infty / 2\delta}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{3\mu}{\rho U_\infty \delta} = \frac{3\nu}{U_\infty \delta}$$ Substituting $\delta = 4.64\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$: $$C_f = \frac{3\nu}{U_\infty} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{Re_x}}{4.64\, x} = \frac{3}{4.64} \cdot \frac{\nu \sqrt{U_\infty x / \nu}}{U_\infty x} = \frac{3}{4.64} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{Re_x}} = \frac{0.646}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$ **(iii) Drag force:** Total drag on one side = $\int_0^L \tau_w\, b\, dx$. $$F_D = \int_0^L \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot C_f(x) \cdot b\, dx = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 b \int_0^L \frac{0.646}{\sqrt{U_\infty x / \nu}}\, dx$$ $$= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 b \cdot 0.646 \sqrt{\nu / U_\infty} \int_0^L x^{-1/2}\, dx$$ $$= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 b \cdot 0.646 \sqrt{\nu / U_\infty} \cdot 2\sqrt{L}$$ $$\boxed{\;F_D = 0.646\, \rho\, b\, U_\infty^{3/2}\, \sqrt{\nu\, L}\;}$$ Equivalently in $Re_L = U_\infty L / \nu$ form: $$F_D = 1.292\, \rho\, b\, U_\infty^2\, L / \sqrt{Re_L}$$ (The factor $1.328$ comes from the exact Blasius solution; $1.292$ from the cubic-profile approximation.) --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper]` **(a)** For laminar boundary layer flow over a flat plate, the velocity distribution is given by the **Pohlhausen cubic profile:** $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = 2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4, \quad \text{where } \eta = y/\delta$$ **Show that this profile satisfies all four boundary conditions** at $y = 0$ and $y = \delta$. Hence, compute the displacement thickness $\delta^*$ and momentum thickness $\theta$ in terms of $\delta$. (7 marks) **(b)** Air at $27\,^\circ$C ($\rho = 1.177\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg/(m·s)}$) flows over a flat plate of length $L = 2\,\text{m}$ with $U_\infty = 5\,\text{m/s}$. Determine:     (i) The boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge.     (ii) The total drag force on one side of the plate (width $b = 1\,\text{m}$).     (iii) The critical transition location, given $Re_{x,crit} = 5 \times 10^5$. (7 marks) ### Model Solution B-B (Part a — 7 marks) **Check boundary conditions on the given profile $u/U_\infty = 2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4$:** **(i) At $\eta = 0$ (i.e., $y = 0$):** $u/U_\infty = 0$. ✓ (No-slip condition.) **(ii) At $\eta = 0$:** Compute $\partial(u/U_\infty)/\partial y = (1/\delta)(2 - 6\eta^2 + 4\eta^3)$. At $\eta = 0$, this equals $2/\delta \neq 0$. *(Note: the Pohlhausen 4th-order profile does NOT satisfy the wall-gradient condition trivially — that is by design, it allows a non-zero wall gradient to model $\tau_w$.)* **(iii) At $\eta = 1$ (i.e., $y = \delta$):** $u/U_\infty = 2 - 2 + 1 = 1$ → $u = U_\infty$. ✓ (Edge condition.) **(iv) At $\eta = 1$:** $\partial(u/U_\infty)/\partial y = (1/\delta)(2 - 6 + 4) = 0$. ✓ (Smooth matching to free stream.) **Compute $\delta^*$:** $$\delta^* = \int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 (1 - 2\eta + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4)\, d\eta$$ $$= \delta \left[\eta - \eta^2 + \tfrac{\eta^4}{2} - \tfrac{\eta^5}{5}\right]_0^1 = \delta \left(1 - 1 + \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{5}\right) = \delta \cdot \frac{3}{10}$$ $$\boxed{\;\delta^* = 0.30\, \delta\;}$$ **Compute $\theta$:** $$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 (2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4)(1 - 2\eta + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4)\, d\eta$$ Expanding (lengthy but mechanical; the result is standard): $$\int_0^1 (2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4)(1 - 2\eta + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4)\, d\eta = \frac{37}{315}$$ $$\boxed{\;\theta = \frac{37}{315}\, \delta \approx 0.1175\, \delta\;}$$ Therefore the **shape factor** $H = \delta^* / \theta = (3/10) / (37/315) = (3 \cdot 315)/(10 \cdot 37) = 945 / 370 \approx 2.554$. (Close to the Blasius exact value of 2.59.) ### Model Solution B-B (Part b — 7 marks) **Given:** $\rho = 1.177\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg/(m·s)}$, $U_\infty = 5\,\text{m/s}$, $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $b = 1\,\text{m}$. **Kinematic viscosity:** $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{1.846 \times 10^{-5}}{1.177} = 1.569 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$ **(i) Boundary layer thickness at trailing edge** (using Blasius exact, since the Pohlhausen profile in part (a) was for theory): $$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 2}{1.569 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.373 \times 10^5$$ $$\delta(L) = \frac{5.0\, L}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2}{\sqrt{6.373 \times 10^5}} = \frac{10}{798.3} = 0.01253\,\text{m} \approx 12.53\,\text{mm}$$ **(ii) Drag force on one side:** $$\bar{C_f} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{6.373 \times 10^5}} = \frac{1.328}{798.3} = 1.663 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$F_D = \bar{C_f} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot b \cdot L = 1.663 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}(1.177)(5)^2(1)(2)$$ $$= 1.663 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 29.425 = 0.0489\,\text{N}$$ **(iii) Critical transition location:** $$x_{crit} = \frac{Re_{x,crit} \cdot \nu}{U_\infty} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 1.569 \times 10^{-5}}{5} = 1.569\,\text{m}$$ Since $x_{crit} = 1.569\,\text{m} < L = 2\,\text{m}$, the flow becomes turbulent **before** reaching the trailing edge. A complete analysis would require splitting the plate into a laminar region ($0 \le x \le 1.569\,\text{m}$) and a turbulent region ($1.569 \le x \le 2\,\text{m}$). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Velocity Boundary Layer** - **Definition:** Thin region near a wall where velocity varies from $0$ (no-slip) to $0.99\, U_\infty$ (edge). Introduced by **Prandtl (1904)**. - **Order of magnitudes:** $\delta \ll L$ at high $Re$. Normal velocity $v \sim u \cdot (\delta/L)$. - **Three characteristic thicknesses (in order $\delta > \delta^* > \theta$):** - $\delta = y$-location where $u = 0.99\, U_\infty$. - $\delta^* = \int_0^\infty (1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$ (mass deficit). - $\theta = \int_0^\infty (u/U_\infty)(1 - u/U_\infty)\, dy$ (momentum deficit). - **Shape factor $H = \delta^*/\theta$:** - Attached laminar (Blasius): $H \approx 2.59$. - Incipient separation: $H \approx 3.5$–$7$. - **Von Kármán momentum integral equation:** $\tau_w = \rho\, U_\infty^2 \cdot d\theta/dx$ — the **single most important equation** in boundary layer theory. - **Cubic velocity profile result:** $\delta \approx 4.64\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$ (laminar, flat plate). - **Blasius exact result:** $\delta = 5.0\, x / \sqrt{Re_x}$. - **Local skin friction (laminar):** $C_f = 0.664 / \sqrt{Re_x}$ (Blasius exact). - **Average skin friction (laminar):** $\bar{C_f} = 1.328 / \sqrt{Re_L}$. - **Turbulent empirical (1/7 power law):** - $\delta \approx 0.37\, x / Re_x^{1/5}$. - $C_f \approx 0.0592 / Re_x^{1/5}$. - **Critical Reynolds number (transition):** $Re_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (flat plate, in standard conditions). - **Pressure gradient effects:** - **Favourable (accelerating):** Thins the boundary layer; delays separation. - **Adverse (decelerating):** Thickens the layer; promotes **separation** (when $\partial p/\partial x > 0$ is too strong). - **Separation:** Wall shear $\tau_w \to 0$ then reverses; flow can no longer remain attached. Caused by strong adverse pressure gradients (e.g., rear of cylinder, diffuser). - **Procedure for a KTU integral-method problem:** (1) Assume a polynomial profile satisfying 4 boundary conditions. (2) Compute $\tau_w$ from wall gradient and $\theta$ from definition. (3) Substitute into Von Kármán equation; separate variables and integrate. (4) Obtain $\delta(x)$, then $C_f$, then $F_D$. - **Common exam mistakes to AVOID:** - Forgetting the **mass conservation step** in the Von Kármán derivation. - Using $U_\infty$ in place of $u$ in the momentum thickness integral. - Mixing up **displacement thickness** with **boundary layer thickness** numerically. - Omitting units in the final numerical answer. - Forgetting to switch from laminar to turbulent correlations at $x_{crit}$ when the plate is long. ---

topics.title: Laminar and Turbulent Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laminar and Turbulent Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Flow Regimes

In fluid mechanics, the nature of fluid motion is classified into two principal regimes based on the **orderly/random behaviour of fluid particles** and the **dominance of viscous forces over inertial forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Laminar Flow (Streamline Flow):** A flow regime in which fluid particles move along well-defined, smooth, parallel paths (called *streamlines*) without any macroscopic intermixing between adjacent fluid layers. The fluid motion is highly **orderly**, and momentum transfer occurs purely through molecular (viscous) action.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Turbulent Flow:** A flow regime in which fluid particles undergo **irregular, random fluctuations** (eddies, vortices) in both magnitude and direction. There is intense macroscopic mixing between fluid layers, and momentum transfer is dominated by **inertial/eddy diffusion** rather than viscosity alone.

## 1.2 The Deciding Parameter: Reynolds Number

The transition between these two regimes is governed by a **dimensionless parameter** introduced by **Osborne Reynolds (1883)**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

where $\rho$ = **density**, $V$ = **mean velocity**, $D$ = **characteristic length (pipe diameter)**, $\mu$ = **dynamic viscosity**, and $\nu = \mu/\rho$ = **kinematic viscosity**.

- **Low Re** → viscous forces dominate → **Laminar**
- **High Re** → inertial forces dominate → **Turbulent**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_c$):** For flow through a circular pipe, the laminar-to-turbulent transition occurs at $Re_c \approx \mathbf{2300}$ (engineering convention). Reynolds' original experiment showed transition between 12,000 – 14,000, but this was due to inlet disturbances. The universally accepted **lower critical** value is **2000**, and **upper critical** is **4000**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two extreme traffic situations:

- **Laminar Flow → Highway Convoy at Dawn:** Imagine cars driving in a perfectly straight, single-file convoy at a steady speed. Each car follows the one ahead along a clean, predictable line. There is no overtaking, no zigzagging, no random lane changes. This is *laminar flow* — orderly, layered, predictable.

- **Turbulent Flow → School Pickup Time at the Gate:** Now picture cars arriving at a narrow school gate. Vehicles jostle, accelerate, brake, swerve, and cross each other's paths randomly. The motion is chaotic, with sudden swirls and eddies. The average forward progress is much slower than the chaos suggests. This is *turbulent flow* — chaotic mixing with energy dissipation.

The **Reynolds number** is essentially a *traffic intensity index* — when it is low, the "road" is wide enough and "vehicles" (fluid particles) move cooperatively; when it is high, the "road" is congested and the motion breaks into chaos.

## 1.4 Classification of Flow Regimes (Summary Box)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Regime Classification by Reynolds Number (Pipe Flow):**
> - $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar Flow** (definitely laminar)
> - $2000 < Re < 4000$ → **Transitional Flow** (unstable, may revert)
> - $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent Flow** (definitely turbulent)

Explicit constants used in this module:
- $Re_c$ (lower) = **2000**
- $Re_c$ (upper) = **4000**
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
- Density of water at 20°C: $\rho_w = \mathbf{998\ kg/m^3}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Characteristics of Each Regime

### A. Laminar Flow Characteristics
1. Fluid particles move in **smooth, parallel streamlines** with no macroscopic mixing.
2. **Viscous shear stresses dominate**; the flow is highly predictable via exact Navier-Stokes solutions.
3. **Velocity profile is parabolic** (Poiseuille's Law) for circular pipes:

$$u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

4. **Pressure drop is linear** with flow velocity: $\Delta P \propto V$.
5. **Friction factor** ($f$) follows the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**: $f = 64/Re$.
6. No eddies, no pulsations, no audible noise.

### B. Turbulent Flow Characteristics
1. Fluid particles exhibit **irregular fluctuations** (eddies) superimposed on a mean motion.
2. **Inertial forces dominate** viscous forces; viscous effects are confined to a thin *viscous sublayer* near walls.
3. **Velocity profile is "flatter"** (logarithmic / power-law), with a thin high-velocity core.
4. **Pressure drop is approximately quadratic** with velocity: $\Delta P \propto V^{1.75\ to\ 2.0}$.
5. **Friction factor** is governed by empirical formulas (e.g., Blasius: $f = 0.316\,Re^{-0.25}$ for smooth pipes, $4000 < Re < 10^5$).
6. Mixing, noise, and energy dissipation are all high.

## 2.2 Reynolds Experiment (1883)

Osborne Reynolds performed the classic experiment using:
- A **horizontal glass pipe** with a steady water flow.
- A **thin dye filament** (ink) introduced at the inlet via a needle.
- A **flow control valve** to vary mean velocity.

**Observations:**
- At **low velocity**: dye filament travels as a **straight, unbroken line** → **Laminar**.
- At **high velocity**: dye filament **breaks up, diffuses, and mixes** across the cross-section → **Turbulent**.
- At an **intermediate velocity**: the filament **wavers** (transitional) — sensitive to disturbances.

The **critical velocity** at which this transition occurs is:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$$

## 2.3 Why Does the Transition Occur? — The 'Why' and 'How'

| Mechanism | Laminar Regime | Turbulent Regime |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant force | **Viscous** (molecular diffusion) | **Inertial** (eddy diffusion) |
| Mixing mechanism | Molecular only | Macroscopic eddy transport |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High (up to 100× higher) |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic | Flat with steep wall gradient |
| Pressure drop vs. V | Linear ($\Delta P \propto V$) | Quadratic ($\Delta P \propto V^2$) |
| Stability | Stable to small disturbances | Unstable; disturbances amplify |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR BOARD EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re_c = \dfrac{V_c D}{\nu}$ | Critical Reynolds number (pipe) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_c = \dfrac{Re_c \, \nu}{D}$ | Critical (mean) velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $u_{max} = 2 \bar{u}$ | Centreline velocity in laminar pipe flow | m/s |
| 5 | $u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$ | Laminar velocity distribution | m/s |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu V L}{D^2}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop | Pa |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Laminar volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | Blasius (turbulent, smooth pipe) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)_{wall}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 11 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 12 | $Re_x = \dfrac{\rho V x}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number on flat plate (x = distance) | Dimensionless |

> **Engineering utility:** Reynolds number is the single most-used dimensionless parameter in chemical, mechanical, civil, and aerospace engineering. It is the foundation for **dynamic similarity** in scale models (e.g., wind tunnels, ship tanks, bioreactors).

## 2.5 Oscillatory Disturbance & Stability

A laminar flow, while mathematically stable, can be triggered into turbulence by:
- Surface roughness of the pipe wall
- Vibrations / pulsations
- Inlet disturbances
- Bends, valves, fittings

Once $Re > Re_{c,upper}$, **turbulence is self-sustaining** — the eddies generate more eddies via the inertial cascade. The transition is **hysteretic**: a flow that has become turbulent does not return to laminar until $Re$ drops back to a lower value (around 2000).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Velocity Profile in Laminar Pipe Flow is Parabolic

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille derivation** — a board-exam favourite.

**Step 1: Set up the control volume.** Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$, inside a horizontal pipe of radius $R$. The pressure at the inlet face is $P_1$ and at the outlet face is $P_2$.

**Step 2: Apply force balance in the axial (x) direction.** The net pressure force must equal the shear force on the cylindrical surface (steady, fully-developed flow → no acceleration).

$$\text{Pressure force} = (P_1 - P_2) \pi r^2$$

$$\text{Shear force} = \tau \cdot (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 3: Equate forces (Newton's second law, steady state):**

$$(P_1 - P_2) \pi r^2 = \tau (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 4: Substitute Newton's law of viscosity** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$ (note: shear acts opposite to motion, so the magnitude is used with appropriate sign convention):

$$(P_1 - P_2) \pi r^2 = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 5: Separate variables** (let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$):

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r$$

**Step 6: Integrate with respect to r:**

$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^2 + C$$

**Step 7: Apply the no-slip boundary condition** $u(R) = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R^2 + C \implies C = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 8: Final velocity distribution:**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Step 9: Centreline velocity** (at $r = 0$):

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 10: Express in dimensionless form** (divide by $u_{max}$):

$$u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 11: Compute mean velocity** by integrating across the cross-section:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R u(r) (2\pi r) \, dr = \frac{2}{R^2}\int_0^R u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right] r\, dr$$

$$\bar{u} = \frac{2 u_{max}}{R^2}\left[\int_0^R r\, dr - \frac{1}{R^2}\int_0^R r^3\, dr\right] = \frac{2 u_{max}}{R^2}\left[\frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{R^2}{4}\right] = \frac{u_{max}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{u} = \frac{u_{max}}{2} \quad \text{(Laminar)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key contrast:** For turbulent flow, the ratio is $\bar{u}/u_{max} \approx 0.8$ to $0.85$ — the profile is much **flatter**, indicating more uniform velocity across the core.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Flow Rate

Starting from the velocity profile derived in Step 8:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

Volume flow rate through an annular ring of width $dr$:

$$dQ = u(r) \cdot 2\pi r\, dr$$

Integrate from $0$ to $R$:

$$Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)(2\pi r)\, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3)\, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}}$$

**Physical insight:** Flow rate depends on the **fourth power of radius** — halving the pipe diameter reduces flow by a factor of **16** for the same pressure drop. This is why small blockages in pipelines have dramatic effects.

## 3.3 Derivation: Friction Factor Relationship for Laminar Flow

**Definition of Darcy friction factor:**

$$f_D = \frac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V^2} = \frac{8 \tau_w}{\rho \bar{u}^2}$$

**Wall shear stress** from Step 3: $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$

Substitute $u_{max} = 2\bar{u}$ and $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu \bar{u} L}{R^2}$ (rearranging Poiseuille):

$$\tau_w = \frac{R}{2L} \cdot \frac{8\mu \bar{u} L}{R^2} = \frac{4\mu \bar{u}}{R} = \frac{8\mu \bar{u}}{D}$$

Now substitute into friction factor:

$$f_D = \frac{8 \cdot \frac{8\mu \bar{u}}{D}}{\rho \bar{u}^2} = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho \bar{u} D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

$$\boxed{f_D = \frac{64}{Re} \quad \text{(valid only for laminar flow)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Laminar vs. Turbulent

| Property | Laminar | Turbulent |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Logarithmic / Power-law (1/7th power) |
| $u_{max}/\bar{u}$ | 2.0 | $\approx 1.16$ to $1.25$ |
| Shear stress | Purely viscous: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous + Reynolds: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$ |
| $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ | $\Delta P \propto V$ | $\Delta P \propto V^{1.75}$ (Blasius) |
| Friction factor | $f = 64/Re$ | $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius) |
| Boundary layer | Full viscous layer | Thin viscous sublayer + turbulent core |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High (turbulent eddies dissipate) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Reynolds Experiment Schematic

The following Mermaid block diagrams the classic Reynolds experiment setup, mapping the functional flow of fluid and dye observation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constant Head Tank] --> B[Smooth Nozzle Inlet]
    B --> C[Horizontal Glass Test Pipe]
    C --> D[Flow Control Valve]
    D --> E[Measuring Tank]
    F[Dye Reservoir] --> G[Thin Injector Needle]
    G --> C
    H[Observation: Straight Line] -.-> I{Re < 2000}
    I --> J[Laminar Flow]
    K[Observation: Wavy Filament] -.-> L{2000 < Re < 4000}
    L --> M[Transitional Flow]
    N[Observation: Chaotic Mixing] -.-> O{Re > 4000}
    O --> P[Turbulent Flow]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style J fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style M fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
    style P fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

## 4.2 Velocity Profile Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Flow in Circular Pipe] --> Q{Determine Re}
    Q -->|Re < 2000| L[Laminar Regime]
    Q -->|2000 to 4000| T[Transitional Regime]
    Q -->|Re > 4000| Turb[Turbulent Regime]
    L --> L1[Velocity Profile: Parabolic]
    L1 --> L2[Max velocity at centre = 2 x mean]
    L1 --> L3[Shear: viscous only]
    L1 --> L4[Friction: f = 64 / Re]
    L1 --> L5[Pressure drop linear in V]
    Turb --> Tu1[Velocity Profile: Flat with steep wall gradient]
    Tu1 --> Tu2[Max velocity approx 1.16 to 1.25 x mean]
    Tu1 --> Tu3[Shear: viscous plus Reynolds stresses]
    Tu1 --> Tu4[Friction: Blasius 0.316 / Re 0.25]
    Tu1 --> Tu5[Pressure drop nearly quadratic in V]
    T --> T1[Unstable: sensitive to disturbances]
    T1 --> T2[May revert to laminar OR flip to turbulent]
    style L fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style Turb fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style T fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Engineering Decision Flow Using Re

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Given: Pipe Diameter D, Mean Velocity V, Fluid Properties rho and mu] --> Calc[Compute Re = rho V D / mu]
    Calc --> Decision{Re versus 2000}
    Decision -->|Re < 2000| Lam[Use Hagen Poiseuille Equations]
    Decision -->|2000 to 4000| Trans[Avoid Design: Unstable Zone]
    Decision -->|Re > 4000| Turb[Use Blasius or Colebrook Equations]
    Lam --> LamOut[Output: delta P linear, f = 64 / Re]
    Turb --> TurbOut[Output: delta P quadratic, f = 0.316 / Re 0.25 for smooth pipe]
    Trans --> TransOut[Output: Conservative Design, add safety factor]
    style Lam fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style Turb fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style Trans fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
    style Calc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (For Future Sketch Reference)

**Reynolds Apparatus — Physical Layout (text description for student sketches):**

```
   [Dye Reservoir]  [Constant Head Tank]
          |                |
          | (dye stream)   | (water flow)
          v                v
       (needle)  --->  [Glass Test Pipe] ---> [Valve] ---> [Collection]
                            |
                            |  (Observer views dye behaviour)
                            v
                    [Three possible observations]
                  1) Straight line  - LAMINAR
                  2) Wavy filament  - TRANSITIONAL  
                  3) Diffuse cloud  - TURBULENT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Reynolds number. What is its physical significance in distinguishing laminar and turbulent flow?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reynolds number is a **dimensionless quantity** that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** acting on a fluid element.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:** It quantifies the relative dominance of these two forces.
- **Low Re** → viscous forces dominate → fluid particles move in **orderly streamlines** → **Laminar flow**.
- **High Re** → inertial forces dominate → fluid particles undergo **chaotic random motion** with eddies → **Turbulent flow**.

It is the **sole criterion** for predicting flow regime in pipe flow. **(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for force interpretation, 1 mark for regime correlation).**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series-1]`
**State and explain the significance of the critical Reynolds number for flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical Reynolds number** is the value of $Re$ at which the flow regime transitions from laminar to turbulent. For a circular pipe:

- **Lower critical** $Re_c = \mathbf{2000}$ — below this, flow is always laminar.
- **Upper critical** $Re_c = \mathbf{4000}$ — above this, flow is always turbulent.
- Between 2000 and 4000, flow is **transitional** and unstable.

The critical velocity is:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Significance:** It is the basis for **designing pipelines, chemical reactors, and hydraulic systems** — engineers must operate at a known $Re$ to predict friction losses correctly. **(1 mark definition, 1 mark values, 1 mark formula and significance).**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Students often confuse **mean velocity $\bar{u}$** with **maximum velocity $u_{max}$** — for laminar flow the ratio is **2.0**, not 1.0.
> 2. Many students use the Blasius formula ($f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$) for laminar flow — this is **WRONG**. Blasius is strictly for turbulent flow. For laminar, always use $f = 64/Re$.
> 3. The Reynolds number formula uses the **pipe diameter $D$** as the characteristic length for closed conduits, but **distance $x$** from the leading edge for flow over flat plates.
> 4. In Hagen-Poiseuille problems, students forget that the formula gives the **pressure drop**, not the gauge pressure at the outlet.
> 5. Marks are lost when students fail to **state the no-slip boundary condition** explicitly in derivations.
> 6. **Do not mix SI and CGS units** mid-problem — this invites negative marking.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**An oil of viscosity 0.1 N·s/m² and relative density 0.9 is flowing through a horizontal pipe of diameter 50 mm. If the pressure drop over a 20 m length is 200 kPa, determine:**
**(a)** The Reynolds number and the nature of the flow. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The maximum velocity, the mean velocity, and the discharge through the pipe. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.1\ \text{N·s/m}^2$
- $\rho = 0.9 \times 1000 = 900\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $D = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $R = 0.025\ \text{m}$
- $L = 20\ \text{m}$
- $\Delta P = 200\ \text{kPa} = 200 \times 10^3\ \text{Pa}$

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Reynolds Number and Flow Regime

**Step 1: Compute mean velocity from Hagen-Poiseuille equation:**

$$\bar{u} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D^2}{32 \mu L} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times (0.05)^2}{32 \times 0.1 \times 20}$$

$$\bar{u} = \frac{200000 \times 0.0025}{64} = \frac{500}{64} = 7.8125\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{u} D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 7.8125 \times 0.05}{0.1}$$

$$Re = \frac{351.5625}{0.1} = 3515.6$$

**Step 3: Classify the flow regime:**

Since $Re = 3515.6$ lies between **2000 and 4000**, the flow is **TRANSITIONAL**.

> **[7 marks: 3 for calculation of mean velocity, 2 for Re, 2 for classification with justification.]**

#### Part (b) — Maximum Velocity, Mean Velocity, Discharge

**Step 1: Maximum velocity** (laminar relation, used as a reference for transitional flow):
In the transitional regime, we still use the parabolic profile assumption as a first approximation:

$$u_{max} = 2 \bar{u} = 2 \times 7.8125 = 15.625\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Mean velocity** (already computed):

$$\bar{u} = 7.8125\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Volumetric discharge:**

$$Q = \bar{u} \times A = \bar{u} \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 7.8125 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2$$

$$Q = 7.8125 \times 0.0019635 = 0.01534\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 15.34\ \text{L/s}$$

> **[7 marks: 2 for u_max, 1 for mean velocity, 4 for discharge with area calculation.]**

**Final Answers:**
- $Re = 3515.6$ → **Transitional flow**
- $u_{max} = 15.625\ \text{m/s}$
- $\bar{u} = 7.8125\ \text{m/s}$
- $Q = 0.01534\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4, Internal Choice]`

**Water at 20°C flows through a smooth horizontal pipe of diameter 100 mm at an average velocity of 3 m/s. Determine:**
**(a)** The Reynolds number, friction factor, and classify the flow. Use the appropriate formula. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The pressure drop over a 50 m length and the wall shear stress. **(7 marks)**

**Given (water at 20°C):**
- $\rho = 998\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N·s/m}^2$
- $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $D = 0.1\ \text{m}$, $L = 50\ \text{m}$, $\bar{u} = 3\ \text{m/s}$

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Reynolds Number, Friction Factor, Flow Regime

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\bar{u} D}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.1}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.3}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re \approx 2.988 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2: Classify flow:**

Since $Re = 2.988 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully TURBULENT**.

**Step 3: Compute friction factor using Blasius equation** (valid for $4000 < Re < 10^5$ — *but our Re exceeds $10^5$, so we will use it as a near-boundary estimate and indicate the limitation*):

$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(2.988 \times 10^5)^{0.25}}$$

$$(2.988 \times 10^5)^{0.25} = (298800)^{0.25} \approx 23.36$$

$$f = \frac{0.316}{23.36} \approx 0.01353$$

> **Examiner's note:** For Re > $10^5$, the **Colebrook equation** is preferred, but Blasius is acceptable for a first-order KTU exam estimate. State the limitation explicitly to earn full marks.

> **[7 marks: 2 for Re, 2 for classification, 3 for friction factor with formula and validity range.]**

#### Part (b) — Pressure Drop and Wall Shear Stress

**Step 1: Darcy-Weisbach equation for pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho \bar{u}^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.01353 \times \frac{50}{0.1} \times \frac{998 \times 3^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.01353 \times 500 \times \frac{998 \times 9}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.01353 \times 500 \times 4491 = 30379\ \text{Pa} \approx 30.38\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2: Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \cdot \rho \bar{u}^2}{8} = \frac{0.01353 \times 998 \times 9}{8}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{121.5}{8} \approx 15.19\ \text{Pa}$$

**Verification using direct wall-shear formula:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L} = \frac{30379 \times 0.05}{2 \times 50} = \frac{1518.95}{100} = 15.19\ \text{Pa} \quad \checkmark$$

> **[7 marks: 4 for pressure drop, 3 for wall shear stress with both formulas.]**

**Final Answers:**
- $Re \approx 2.99 \times 10^5$ → **Turbulent flow**
- $f \approx 0.0135$ (Blasius)
- $\Delta P \approx 30.38\ \text{kPa}$
- $\tau_w \approx 15.19\ \text{Pa}$

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Reynolds number formula:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ — *always state both forms*.
- [ ] **Critical Re values for pipe flow:** **2000** (lower) and **4000** (upper).
- [ ] **Laminar flow:** $Re < 2000$, parabolic profile, $f = 64/Re$, $\Delta P \propto V$.
- [ ] **Turbulent flow:** $Re > 4000$, flat profile, Blasius $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$, $\Delta P \propto V^{1.75 \text{ to } 2}$.
- [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille equation:** $Q = \pi \Delta P D^4 / (128 \mu L)$ — *the $D^4$ dependence is a favourite viva question*.
- [ ] **Mean vs. Max velocity (Laminar):** $\bar{u} = u_{max}/2$.
- [ ] **Mean vs. Max velocity (Turbulent):** $\bar{u} \approx u_{max}/1.16$ to $u_{max}/1.25$.
- [ ] **Darcy-Weisbach equation:** $\Delta P = f (L/D)(\rho V^2/2)$ — *universal, valid for both regimes*.
- [ ] **Reynolds experiment:** Glass pipe + dye filament + variable velocity; transition observed visually.
- [ ] **Wall shear stress formula:** $\tau_w = \Delta P \cdot R / (2L)$ (universal).
- [ ] **No-slip condition:** $u(R) = 0$ at pipe wall — *mandatory in any derivation*.
- [ ] **Stability:** Laminar flow is stable to small disturbances until $Re_c$; turbulent flow is **hysteretic** — does not revert until $Re$ falls well below the upper critical.
- [ ] **Characteristic length:** Use **D** (diameter) for closed pipes, **x** (distance from leading edge) for flat plates and boundary layers.
- [ ] **SI Units to remember:** $\mu$ in Pa·s (= kg/m·s), $\nu$ in m²/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\Delta P$ in Pa (= N/m²).

**Mnemonic to remember regime limits:** **"LTT" = Laminar < 2000, Transitional 2000–4000, Turbulent > 4000.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a **dimensionless quantity** used in fluid mechanics to predict flow patterns by measuring the relative importance of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a flowing fluid. It is mathematically expressed as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces acting on a fluid element.

The Reynolds number is a fundamental similarity parameter that governs the dynamic similarity between two flow situations. If two geometrically similar flows have the same Reynolds number, they will exhibit dynamically similar flow behavior — this is the **Reynolds Model Law** used heavily in scale-model testing of aircraft, submarines, pipelines, and hydraulic structures.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement

The Reynolds number is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

Where the parameters are defined as follows:

- **$\rho$** = Mass density of the fluid **[kg/m³]**
- **$V$** = Average or characteristic velocity of flow **[m/s]**
- **$L$** = Characteristic length of the flow geometry (typically pipe diameter $D$ for internal flow) **[m]**
- **$\mu$** = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid **[N·s/m² or Pa·s]**
- **$\nu = \mu / \rho$** = Kinematic viscosity of the fluid **[m²/s]**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a large crowd of people rushing through a narrow corridor.
> - If the corridor is **wide** and the people are moving **slowly** (low velocity, high viscosity like honey), the crowd moves in an **organized, layered fashion** — this is **laminar flow** (low Reynolds number).
> - If the corridor is **narrow** and the people are moving **fast** with low stickiness (low viscosity like air), the crowd becomes **chaotic, swirling, and mixing** — this is **turbulent flow** (high Reynolds number).
> - **Reynolds number is essentially a "turbulence meter"** — it tells you whether the flow will behave in an orderly, smooth manner (laminar) or chaotically, with eddies and mixing (turbulent).

The number itself has no units — it is a **pure number** — which is what makes it so powerful: it lets engineers compare flows in fluids as different as water, air, oil, and mercury on a single common scale.

### 1.4 Physical Significance Summary

| Physical Interpretation | Mathematical Implication |
|:------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Ratio of Inertial to Viscous Forces | $Re = \dfrac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}}$ |
| Ratio of Momentum Diffusivity to Viscous Diffusivity | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ |
| Determines Flow Regime | Laminar, Transitional, or Turbulent |
| Governs Dynamic Similarity | Equal Re → Dynamically Similar Flows |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Reynolds Number — Physical Reasoning

To truly understand Reynolds number, we must dissect the two competing forces inside any flowing fluid:

**(A) Inertial Force ($F_i$) — The "Persistence" Force**
Inertia is the property by which a moving fluid wants to keep moving in its current direction and magnitude. It is the force associated with the fluid's **momentum flux**. For a fluid element of characteristic size $L$ moving at velocity $V$:

$$F_i \propto \rho \, V^2 \, L^2$$

This force is dominant when the fluid is **dense**, **fast**, or moving through a **large** passage.

**(B) Viscous Force ($F_v$) — The "Stickiness" Force**
Viscosity is the internal friction of a fluid that **resists** relative motion between fluid layers. It tries to keep the flow orderly and laminar. For the same fluid element:

$$F_v \propto \mu \, V \, L$$

This force is dominant when the fluid is **thick** (high viscosity) or moving through a **small** passage at low speed.

**(C) The Ratio — Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $Re$ is **small** ($Re \ll 1$), viscous forces dominate → flow is **smooth and laminar**. When $Re$ is **large** ($Re \gg 1$), inertial forces dominate → flow is **chaotic and turbulent**. The **critical Reynolds number** marks the transition.

### 2.2 Critical Reynolds Number & Flow Regimes

Osborne Reynolds (1883) experimentally established the following benchmark values for **flow in a circular pipe**:

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Character |
|:------------|:----------------------|:-------------------|
| **Laminar Flow** | $Re < 2300$ | Smooth, parallel streamlines; no mixing between layers |
| **Transitional Flow** | $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Unstable; oscillates between laminar and turbulent |
| **Turbulent Flow** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, eddies, vigorous mixing, random fluctuations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The value $Re_{critical} = 2300$ is often quoted in KTU exam problems. The transitional zone is **NOT** a third "type" of flow — it is a **range of instability** where the flow can be either laminar or turbulent depending on disturbances.

For **flow over a flat plate**, the critical Reynolds number is approximately:

$$Re_{x,critical} \approx 5 \times 10^5$$

where $x$ is the distance from the leading edge. This is the boundary layer transition criterion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in terms of dynamic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number in terms of kinematic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | For pipe flow (L = D, pipe diameter) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D \nu}$ | In terms of volumetric flow rate $Q$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Equivalent form using $D$ as length scale | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_i = \rho V^2 L^2$ | Inertial force on fluid element | N |
| 7 | $F_v = \mu V L$ | Viscous force on fluid element | N |
| 8 | $Re_{cr} = 2300$ | Critical Re for pipe flow (laminar–transitional) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re_{cr} = 4000$ | Re for fully turbulent flow in pipe | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $Re_x = \dfrac{\rho V x}{\mu}$ | Local Re on flat plate at distance $x$ | Dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reynolds number is not just a textbook number — it is the **workhorse parameter** of fluid engineering:

- **Aerospace:** Predicting whether airflow over a wing is laminar (low drag) or turbulent (high drag). Wing design aims to delay transition to as high an $Re$ as possible using **laminar flow airfoils**.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Designing heat exchangers and reactors. Heat transfer coefficients depend strongly on flow regime — turbulent flow gives 5–10× higher heat transfer.
- **Civil/Hydraulic Engineering:** Sizing water supply pipes, designing spillways, and predicting sediment transport in rivers. Open-channel flow uses a modified Reynolds number.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow in arteries is characterized by Reynolds number. Stenosis (narrowing) can locally raise $Re$ and trigger turbulence, producing audible murmurs.
- **Scale Model Testing:** The Reynolds Model Law allows engineers to test small-scale models (e.g., ship hulls, submarines, aircraft) in wind tunnels or towing tanks and extrapolate results to full-scale prototypes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reynolds Number from Ratio of Inertial to Viscous Forces

This is the **most fundamental derivation** and the one most often asked in KTU exams.

**Step 1: Set up the fluid element**

Consider a small fluid element of characteristic length $L$ moving with characteristic velocity $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 2: Express the inertial force**

The inertial force arises from the fluid's momentum. For a fluid element of cross-sectional area $\sim L^2$, the mass of the element is $\rho L^3$, and the momentum flux through the element scales as $\rho V^2 L^2$:

$$F_{inertial} = \text{mass} \times \text{acceleration} = (\rho L^3) \times \frac{V^2}{L} = \rho V^2 L^2$$

**Step 3: Express the viscous force**

The viscous force is governed by Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dV}{dy}$$

For a fluid element with velocity gradient of order $V/L$, the shear stress scales as $\mu V / L$. Multiplying by the surface area $\sim L^2$:

$$F_{viscous} = \tau \times A = \left(\mu \frac{V}{L}\right) \times L^2 = \mu V L$$

**Step 4: Form the dimensionless ratio**

By definition, Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L}$$

**Step 5: Simplify algebraically**

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}}$$

**Step 6: Verify dimensionless nature**

$$[Re] = \frac{[\rho][V][L]}{[\mu]} = \frac{(\text{kg/m}^3)(\text{m/s})(\text{m})}{\text{N·s/m}^2} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/(m·s)}} = 1$$

Thus, Reynolds number is **dimensionless** (a pure number). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number from Buckingham Pi Theorem (Dimensional Analysis)

This derivation is the KTU-favorite for full-mark questions, as it rigorously shows the dimensionless grouping using systematic dimensional analysis.

**Step 1: List the variables affecting pressure drop**

Consider steady, viscous, incompressible flow through a smooth circular pipe. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is assumed to depend on:

$$\Delta P = f(\rho, V, D, \mu)$$

Where:
