**(a) [7 marks] Define absorptivity, reflectivity, transmissivity, and emissivity. Show that for an opaque body, $\alpha + \rho = 1$. Explain Kirchhoff's law and its engineering significance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed: $\alpha = G_{abs}/G$.
- **Reflectivity ($\rho$):** Fraction reflected: $\rho = G_{ref}/G$.
- **Transmissivity ($\tau$):** Fraction transmitted: $\tau = G_{trans}/G$.
- **Emissivity ($\epsilon$):** Ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that of a black body at the same temperature: $\epsilon = E/E_b$.

**Energy balance:**

$$G_{abs} + G_{ref} + G_{trans} = G$$

Dividing by $G$:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For an **opaque** body ($\tau = 0$):

$$\boxed{\alpha + \rho = 1}$$

**Kirchhoff's Law:** At thermal equilibrium, $\epsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$.

**Engineering significance:** *Good absorbers are good emitters.*
- Polished aluminum foil has $\alpha \approx 0.05$; hence $\epsilon \approx 0.05$ — used in thermos flasks and spacecraft thermal blankets.
- A surface painted matte black has high $\alpha$, high $\epsilon$ — used as a heat dissipator in solar absorbers and radiators.

*(7 marks: 4 definitions 2, derivation 2, Kirchhoff statement + significance 3)*

---

**(b) [7 marks] A furnace cavity at 1800 K has a small opening of area $A = 0.005 \; m^{2}$ acting as a black body. Calculate (i) total emissive power, (ii) total radiation emitted per second through the opening, (iii) wavelength of maximum emission.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total emissive power:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1800)^{4}$$

$1800^{4} = 1.04976 \times 10^{13} \; K^{4}$

$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.04976 \times 10^{13} = 5.952 \times 10^{5} \; W/m^{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_b \approx \mathbf{5.95 \times 10^{5} \; W/m^{2} \; = \; 595.2 \; kW/m^{2}}}$$

**(ii) Total radiation emitted per second through the opening:**

$$Q = E_b \cdot A = 5.952 \times 10^{5} \times 0.005$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx \mathbf{2976 \; W \approx 3.0 \; kW}}$$

**(iii) Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's Law):**

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1800}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx \mathbf{1.61 \; \mu m}}$$

This lies in the **near-infrared** region — consistent with hot furnace radiation.

*(7 marks: part (i) 2, (ii) 2, (iii) 2, units/sig. fig. 1)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Units of $\lambda$ mismatch:** Planck's law demands $\lambda$ in **metres**, not micrometres. Students often plug $\lambda$ in $\mu m$ directly and get a wrong answer worth **2–3 marks lost**.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_2/\lambda T$ in the exponent:** Many students write only the numerator $\dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5}$ — that gives $\mathbf{0}$ marks on full derivation.
> 3. **Stefan–Boltzmann requires Kelvin (NOT °C):** Using $(800 - 273)$ instead of $800$ collapses $T^4$ drastically — marks deducted.
> 4. **In Kirchhoff derivation:** Forgetting to cancel $E_b$ after writing the equilibrium equation — partial credit only.
> 5. **Direction confusion in Lambert's law:** $\theta$ is measured from the **normal**, not from the surface. Marks lost on view-factor formulations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Thermal radiation** requires no medium; energy transfer $\propto T^{4}$.
- ✅ **Black body**: $\alpha_\lambda = \epsilon_\lambda = 1$ for **all** $\lambda$; reference standard.
- ✅ **Gray body**: $\alpha = \epsilon = $ constant $< 1$ over all $\lambda$.
- ✅ **Energy balance**: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; **opaque** ⇒ $\tau = 0$ ⇒ $\alpha + \rho = 1$.
- ✅ **Planck's law** is the master equation; all other radiation laws derive from it.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann**: $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$, $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$.
- ✅ **Wien's law**: $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; m \cdot K$.
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's law**: $\epsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ at equilibrium.
- ✅ **Lambert's law**: $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ for diffuse surfaces.
- ✅ **Constants to memorize:**
  - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \; J \cdot s$
  - $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; m/s$
  - $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; J/K$
  - $C_1 = 3.742 \times 10^{-16} \; W \cdot m^{2}$
  - $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \; m \cdot K$
  - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; W/m^{2} \cdot K^{4}$
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Furnace design, solar collectors, radiation shields, pyrometry, spacecraft thermal control.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) + 1 sub-part of a Part-B (7 marks) from this topic in every ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stefan-Boltzmann Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stefan-Boltzmann Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** is a fundamental law of thermal radiation that quantifies the total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area by a **blackbody** (a perfect emitter and absorber of radiation) in thermal equilibrium. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Total emissive power of the blackbody (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- $T$ = Absolute surface temperature of the body (K)

The law is named after **Austrian physicist Josef Stefan**, who derived the empirical relationship in 1879, and **Ludwig Boltzmann**, who provided the thermodynamic theoretical proof in 1884.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Stefan-Boltzmann Law forms the foundation for *all radiation heat transfer calculations* in PCPET303. It is universally tested in both Part A (2–3 marks, direct formula application) and Part B (12–14 marks, integrated problems involving view factors and emissivity).

---

## 1.2 Generalization for Real (Gray) Surfaces

A **blackbody** is an idealized surface that emits the maximum possible radiation at a given temperature. **Real engineering surfaces** emit less radiation and are characterized by a property called **emissivity ($\varepsilon$)**, defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Emissive power of the real surface (W/m²)
- $E_b$ = Emissive power of a blackbody at the same temperature (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity (dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1)

Therefore, for any **real/gray surface**, the Stefan-Boltzmann law is generalized as:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap:**
> - **Blackbody:** $\varepsilon = 1$, $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (maximum possible emission)
> - **Gray Body:** $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ (constant with respect to wavelength), $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$
> - **Perfect Reflector:** $\varepsilon = 0$, $E = 0$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Hot Stove Analogy":**
> Imagine you turn the burner of an electric stove to "low" (say, 400 K). The element glows a dull red, and you can feel warmth if you hover your hand nearby. Now, turn it to "high" (say, 1000 K). The element now glows a bright orange-yellow and releases intense heat. **Even though the temperature only increased by a factor of 2.5, the heat radiated jumps by a factor of $2.5^4 \approx 39$.** This explosive, fourth-power growth is the essence of the Stefan-Boltzmann Law. **A small rise in temperature produces a disproportionately massive increase in radiant heat output.** This is why spacecraft re-entry tiles must withstand such extreme thermal loads, and why filament bulbs need very high temperatures to glow brightly.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| Absolute Zero | $0\,\text{K}$ | $-273.15$ | °C |
| Sun's Surface Temperature | $T_{\odot}$ | $\approx 5778$ | K |
| Sun's Emissive Power | $E_b^{\odot}$ | $\approx 6.3 \times 10^7$ | W/m² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law is rooted in three fundamental pillars of physics:

1. **Maxwell-Boltzmann Statistical Mechanics:** Describes the kinetic energy distribution of particles. Boltzmann's constant $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K appears in Planck's law.

2. **Thermodynamics:** Boltzmann applied the second law of thermodynamics to radiation pressure, proving that total radiation intensity must be proportional to $T^4$.

3. **Electromagnetic Theory:** Radiation is electromagnetic waves. The Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ is related to other fundamental constants:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$$

Where:
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant = $1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $c$ = Speed of light in vacuum = $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $h$ = Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

---

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Validity

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law applies under the following conditions:

- ✅ The emitting surface is in **thermal equilibrium**.
- ✅ The surrounding medium is a **vacuum** or a **dielectric (non-participating) medium** like air.
- ✅ The surface is **diffuse** (emission is uniform in all directions) and follows **Lambert's Cosine Law**.
- ✅ For gray bodies, $\varepsilon$ is assumed **independent of wavelength and temperature**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Blackbody total emissive power | W/m² |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Gray body total emissive power | W/m² |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = E / E_b$ | Definition of emissivity | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance at a surface (opaque: $\tau=0$, so $\alpha + \rho = 1$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ | Kirchhoff's Law (at thermal equilibrium) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q = A \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Total heat radiated by a gray surface | W |
| 7 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Net radiative heat flux from surface to surroundings | W/m² |
| 8 | $Q = A \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net radiation between two large parallel surfaces | W |
| 9 | $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant in terms of fundamental constants | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to find the ratio of emissive powers at two temperatures, use $E_{b1}/E_{b2} = (T_1/T_2)^4$. This is the **most common 3-mark Part A question** on this topic.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law is indispensable in:

- **🔥 Solar Energy Engineering:** Calculating solar irradiance reaching Earth's surface (using $G = \sigma T_{\odot}^4 \cdot (R_{\odot}/R_{SE})^2$).
- **🏭 Furnace & Boiler Design:** Determining heat loss from furnace walls at high operating temperatures (1100–1800 K).
- **🛰️ Spacecraft Thermal Control:** Designing radiators that dissipate heat via radiation in vacuum.
- **💡 Incandescent Lamp Design:** Filaments operate near 2500–3000 K to maximize visible light emission.
- **🌡️ Infrared Thermography & Pyrometers:** Non-contact temperature measurement using emitted radiation intensity.
- **❄️ Cryogenic Insulation:** Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI) blankets rely on low-emissivity surfaces to suppress $T^4$ radiation losses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan-Boltzmann Constant ($\sigma$) from Planck's Law

The **spectral emissive power** of a blackbody, given by **Planck's Law**, is:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

To obtain the **total emissive power**, we integrate $E_{b\lambda}$ over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]} d\lambda$$

**Step 1: Non-dimensional substitution.**
Let $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$. Then:
- $dx = -\dfrac{h c}{\lambda^2 k_B T} d\lambda \implies d\lambda = -\dfrac{\lambda^2 k_B T}{h c} dx$
- $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{x k_B T}$

**Step 2: Express $\lambda^5$ in terms of $x$.**

$$\lambda^5 = \left( \frac{h c}{x k_B T} \right)^5 = \frac{h^5 c^5}{x^5 k_B^5 T^5}$$

**Step 3: Rewrite the integral in terms of $x$.**

$$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\dfrac{h^5 c^5}{x^5 k_B^5 T^5} \cdot \left[ e^{x} - 1 \right]} \cdot \left( -\frac{h c}{x^2 k_B T} \cdot \frac{1}{x k_B T} \right) dx$$

Reversing the limits to remove the negative sign:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^2 \cdot x^5 k_B^5 T^5}{h^5 c^5 (e^x - 1)} \cdot \frac{h c}{x^3 k_B^2 T^2} dx$$

**Step 4: Simplify the integrand.**

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the integral using the Riemann zeta function.**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

(This result is a classical integral: $\int_0^\infty \frac{x^{n-1}}{e^x - 1} dx = \Gamma(n) \zeta(n)$, and for $n=4$, $\Gamma(4)\zeta(4) = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$.)

**Step 6: Substitute back to obtain the Stefan-Boltzmann Law.**

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4$$

By definition:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A furnace has a refractory brick inner wall at 1500 K with an emissivity of 0.85. The wall area is 2 m². Calculate (a) the total rate of radiation heat emission, and (b) the ratio of emissive power if the temperature is doubled.

### Part (a): Total Radiation Heat Emission

Given:
- $T = 1500$ K
- $\varepsilon = 0.85$
- $A = 2$ m²
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)

**Step 1: Apply the generalized Stefan-Boltzmann Law.**

$$Q = A \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$Q = 2 \cdot 0.85 \cdot (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (1500)^4$$

**Step 3: Compute $T^4$.**

$$(1500)^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 4: Compute $Q$.**

$$Q = 2 \cdot 0.85 \cdot (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (5.0625 \times 10^{12})$$

$$Q = 1.7 \cdot 5.67 \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{4}$$

$$Q \approx 48.78 \times 10^{4} \approx 4.88 \times 10^{5} \text{ W} \approx 488 \text{ kW}$$

### Part (b): Ratio of Emissive Power on Doubling Temperature

When temperature is doubled, $T \rightarrow 2T$, so:

$$\frac{E_{new}}{E_{old}} = \frac{\varepsilon \sigma (2T)^4}{\varepsilon \sigma T^4} = 2^4 = 16$$

**Answer:** The radiation emission increases by a factor of **16**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Radiation Emission Process

The following block diagram maps the logical flow of how thermal energy is converted to radiative energy by a gray surface:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Energy Input Q_in] --> B[Surface Heating at Temperature T]
    B --> C{Is Surface a Blackbody?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Emissivity epsilon equals 1]
    C -->|No| E[Determine Emissivity epsilon from 0 to 1]
    D --> F[Apply Stefan-Boltzmann Law E_b equals sigma T^4]
    E --> G[Apply Gray Body Law E equals epsilon sigma T^4]
    F --> H[Compute Total Radiation Q equals A sigma T^4]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Radiative Heat Flux q in W per m squared]
    H --> J[Output Total Power Q in W]
    H --> K[Spectral Distribution via Planck Law]
    I --> L[Engineering Application: Heat Loss / Solar Gain]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Comparison of Emission from Blackbody vs. Gray Body

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BB[Blackbody Reference]
        B1[epsilon equals 1]
        B2[E_b equals sigma T^4]
        B3[Reference Emitter]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph GB[Gray Real Surface]
        G1[epsilon less than 1]
        G2[E equals epsilon sigma T^4]
        G3[Reduced Emitter]
        G1 --> G2 --> G3
    end

    BB -- Compare via epsilon --> GB
    G3 -- Radiative Loss to Surroundings --> S[Surroundings at T_sur]
```

## 4.3 Temperature Dependence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Temperature T_1] --> B[Compute E_1 equals epsilon sigma T_1^4]
    C[New Temperature T_2] --> D[Compute E_2 equals epsilon sigma T_2^4]
    B --> E{Ratio R equals T_2/T_1}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Final Ratio E_2 by E_1 equals R^4]
    F --> G[Insight: Fourth Power Growth]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Insight:** Notice how the **fourth-power temperature dependence** acts as an amplifier. Doubling temperature multiplies radiation by 16; tripling it multiplies by 81. This nonlinear behavior is why even small temperature miscalculations in furnaces or re-entry vehicles lead to catastrophically large errors in predicted heat loads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State and explain the Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Mention its significance in radiation heat transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area of a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

Where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**Significance:**
1. It provides the **fundamental equation** for computing total heat transfer by radiation, the third mode of heat transfer (alongside conduction and convection).
2. It establishes the **fourth-power temperature dependence**, which is crucial for high-temperature applications such as furnaces, combustion chambers, solar collectors, and spacecraft thermal protection.
3. For real (gray) surfaces, the law is extended using emissivity: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, making it directly applicable to engineering materials. *(1 mark each point)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A blackbody surface is at 2000 K. Calculate its emissive power. If the temperature is raised to 2400 K, by what factor does the emissive power increase?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: $T_1 = 2000$ K, $T_2 = 2400$ K, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).

**Step 1: Compute $E_{b1}$ at 2000 K.**

$$E_{b1} = \sigma T_1^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (2000)^4$$

$$E_{b1} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{13}) = 9.07 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the ratio of emissive powers.**

$$\frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^4 = \left( \frac{2400}{2000} \right)^4 = (1.2)^4 = 2.0736$$

**Answer:** The emissive power at 2000 K is **907 kW/m²**, and on heating to 2400 K, it increases by a factor of approximately **2.07**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's spectral distribution law. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small metal sphere of diameter 5 cm at 800 K is suspended in a large evacuated room whose walls are at 300 K. If the emissivity of the sphere is 0.4, calculate:
1. The total rate of heat loss by radiation from the sphere.
2. The wavelength at which maximum emission occurs.
3. If the sphere's surface is polished ($\varepsilon = 0.1$), by what percentage is the heat loss reduced? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from Planck's spectral distribution:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

The total emissive power is:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

By substituting $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$, the integral reduces to:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

Using $\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

Hence proved. (Each of the 4 main steps carries ~1.5–2 marks; full marks require stating the substitution, the zeta-function integral identity, and the final identification of $\sigma$.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Diameter $d = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, so radius $r = 0.025$ m
- Sphere temperature $T_s = 800$ K
- Surroundings $T_{sur} = 300$ K
- Emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.4$ (initial)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)

**Step 1: Surface area of sphere.**

$$A = 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \pi (0.025)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Net heat loss using Stefan-Boltzmann Law for gray surface.**

$$Q = A \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

Compute the temperatures:

$$T_s^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$$

$$T_{sur}^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}$$

Difference:

$$T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 0.081 \times 10^{11} = 4.015 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

Compute Q:

$$Q = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.4 \cdot (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (4.015 \times 10^{11})$$

$$Q = 7.854 \times 0.4 \cdot 5.67 \cdot 4.015 \times 10^{0}$$

$$Q \approx 71.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's Displacement Law).**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T_s} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{800} = 3.62 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 3.62 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 4: Percentage reduction in heat loss when $\varepsilon$ changes from 0.4 to 0.1.**

Since $Q \propto \varepsilon$, percentage reduction is:

$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{\varepsilon_{old} - \varepsilon_{new}}{\varepsilon_{old}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.4 - 0.1}{0.4} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $Q \approx 71.5$ W
2. $\lambda_{\max} \approx 3.62$ μm
3. Heat loss reduced by **75%**

---

### ➤ Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Supplementary Style]

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. Show that it can be expressed in terms of Boltzmann's constant, Planck's constant, and the speed of light. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two large parallel plates of size 1 m × 1 m are separated by a large distance. Plate 1 is at 1000 K with emissivity 0.6, and Plate 2 is at 500 K with emissivity 0.8. The surroundings are at 0 K. Determine the net radiation heat exchange between the plates using:
1. Stefan-Boltzmann Law.
2. The equivalent emissivity formula for parallel plates. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 Marks):**

The Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ represents the **proportionality constant** between the total emissive power of a blackbody and the fourth power of its absolute temperature. Numerically:

$$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$$

**Physical meaning:** $\sigma$ quantifies how efficiently a unit area of an ideal black surface converts its absolute temperature into radiant energy flux. *(1 mark)*

**Derivation from Planck's law (as in Section 3.1):** The result of the integration yields:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$

This expression links $\sigma$ to:
- $k_B$ (Boltzmann's constant): thermodynamic/statistical behavior
- $h$ (Planck's constant): quantum nature of radiation
- $c$ (speed of light): relativistic wave propagation *(3 marks for derivation)*

**Numerical verification:**

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 (1.3806 \times 10^{-23})^4}{15 (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^3 (2.998 \times 10^8)^2}$$

$$= \frac{2 \cdot 306.02 \cdot 3.636 \times 10^{-91}}{15 \cdot 2.910 \times 10^{-100} \cdot 8.988 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$\approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4) \quad \checkmark \quad (3 \text{ marks for verification})$$

---

**Part (b) — Parallel Plates Problem (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Area $A = 1$ m²
- $T_1 = 1000$ K, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$
- $T_2 = 500$ K, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.8$
- $T_{sur} = 0$ K (no surrounding radiation)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$

**Step 1: Equivalent emissivity for parallel plates.**

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{eq}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$$

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{eq}} = \frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{0.8} - 1 = 1.667 + 1.25 - 1 = 1.917$$

$$\varepsilon_{eq} = 0.5217$$

**Step 2: Apply the Stefan-Boltzmann net radiation formula.**

$$Q_{12} = A \cdot \varepsilon_{eq} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

Compute:

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 10^{12}$$

$$T_2^4 = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 10^{12} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 9.375 \times 10^{11}$$

Substitute:

$$Q_{12} = 1 \cdot 0.5217 \cdot (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (9.375 \times 10^{11})$$

$$Q_{12} = 0.5217 \cdot 5.67 \cdot 9.375 \times 10^{4}$$

$$Q_{12} \approx 27.72 \times 10^{3} \approx 2.77 \times 10^{4} \text{ W} \approx 27.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** Net radiation heat exchange $\approx \mathbf{27.7 \text{ kW}}$ (from Plate 1 to Plate 2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Stefan-Boltzmann Problems:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the Absolute Temperature:** Students often plug in temperatures in °C instead of K. **Always convert to Kelvin first** — losing 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **❌ Confusing Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) with Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** Although Kirchhoff's Law states $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at equilibrium, in **solar contexts** (where source ≠ ambient), these values differ. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **❌ Skipping Units in the Final Answer:** Always state $Q$ in **W or kW**, and $E$ in **W/m²**. Examiners often deduct a mark for missing units.
> 4. **❌ Misapplying the Equivalent Emissivity Formula:** For two **infinite parallel plates**, the formula is $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$. For **concentric cylinders/spheres**, the formula is different — note the geometry!
> 5. **❌ Mixing Stefan-Boltzmann Law with Newton's Cooling Law:** The latter is $Q = hA\Delta T$ (linear, convection), while the former is $Q = A\varepsilon\sigma T^4$ (fourth-power, radiation). Don't interchange them.
> 6. **❌ Omitting Intermediate Steps:** In derivation problems, write every substitution clearly. **Step-skipping** can cost up to 3 marks even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stefan-Boltzmann Law**

- ✅ **Primary Equation (Blackbody):** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- ✅ **Gray Body Extension:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ with $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Net Heat Transfer:** $Q = A\varepsilon\sigma(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$.
- ✅ **Fourth-Power Law:** Doubling $T$ → radiation **increases 16×**.
- ✅ **Emissivity Definitions:**
   - Blackbody: $\varepsilon = 1$
   - Gray Body: $\varepsilon = $ constant (0 to 1)
   - Polished metal: $\varepsilon \approx 0.02$–$0.1$
   - Painted/anodized: $\varepsilon \approx 0.8$–$0.95$
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (at thermal equilibrium).
- ✅ **Fundamental Constant Relation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$.
- ✅ **Common Exam Conversions:** °C + 273.15 = K; **never** use °C in Stefan-Boltzmann calculations.
- ✅ **Total Radiant Power:** $Q = A\varepsilon\sigma T^4$ (W).
- ✅ **Radiative Heat Flux:** $q = \varepsilon\sigma T^4$ (W/m²).
- ✅ **Most Tested Formulas in KTU:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, $(T_1/T_2)^4$ ratio problems, and gray-body numerical problems.
- ✅ **Common Engineering Materials:** Tungsten ($\varepsilon \approx 0.25$), Brick ($\varepsilon \approx 0.93$), Aluminum foil ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$).
- ✅ **Real-World Anchor:** Solar surface ≈ 5778 K → emissive power ≈ $6.3 \times 10^7$ W/m².

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wein's Displacement Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wein's Displacement Law # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Wein's Displacement Law** is a fundamental result in thermal radiation physics that establishes an inverse relationship between the **absolute thermodynamic temperature** of a black body and the **wavelength at which its spectral emissive power attains its maximum value**. Mathematically, the product of the peak wavelength ($\lambda_{max}$) and the absolute temperature ($T$) is a universal constant, independent of the physical nature of the radiating body. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Radiation):** > Wein's Displacement Law is a **direct consequence of Planck's Law of Black Body Radiation** and is treated as a high-weightage topic in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for derivations, numericals, and short-answer conceptual questions. $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$$ where the constant $b$ is known as **Wein's Displacement Constant**, with a universally accepted numerical value of: $$b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Black Body Reminder:** A black body is an idealized physical object that absorbs **100%** of the incident electromagnetic radiation across all wavelengths ($\alpha = 1$) and emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at every wavelength for a given temperature. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are heating a piece of iron in a furnace. As it begins to glow, the colour you first observe is a dull **red**. As the temperature continues to climb, the colour shifts toward **orange**, then **yellow-white**, and eventually a bluish-white at extremely high temperatures. The metal is not changing its chemical composition — the **peak of its emitted radiation is simply migrating toward shorter wavelengths** as it gets hotter. This everyday observation is the physical manifestation of Wein's Displacement Law. The hotter the object, the smaller (shorter) the wavelength at which it radiates the most energy. ### Practical Engineering Examples | Phenomenon | Approx. Temperature (K) | Approx. $\lambda_{max}$ | Observed Colour | |---|---|---|---| | Human Body Radiation | 310 | 9.35 µm | Far-Infrared (invisible) | | Red-Hot Iron | 800 | 3.62 µm | Deep Red | | Tungsten Filament Bulb | 2800 | 1.03 µm | Yellowish-White | | Surface of the Sun | 5778 | 0.50 µm | Yellow-Green (visible) | | Lightning Strike | 30000 | 96.6 nm | Ultraviolet | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Displacement"?** The word *displacement* refers to the shift (displacement) of the peak of the spectral distribution curve along the wavelength axis as temperature changes. As $T$ increases, the peak is "displaced" toward smaller $\lambda$. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant to Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - **Wein's Displacement Constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K = **2898 µm·K** > - **Speed of Light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s > - **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s > - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Where Does Wein's Law Come From? Wein's Displacement Law is **not** an independent empirical observation. It is rigorously derived by applying the condition of extremum (maxima) to **Planck's Spectral Distribution Law**, which itself arises from quantum mechanical considerations of photon energy quantization. ### The Logical Chain of Reasoning 1. **Step 1 — Quantum Foundation:** Max Planck postulated that electromagnetic energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta of energy $E = h\nu$. 2. **Step 2 — Planck's Law Emerges:** Using statistical mechanics (Bose-Einstein statistics) for photons in a cavity, Planck derived the spectral emissive power as a function of wavelength. 3. **Step 3 — Mathematical Maximization:** The spectral curve $E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$ has a unique peak. By setting $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$, the wavelength of peak emission $\lambda_{max}$ is found. 4. **Step 4 — Wein's Law Emerges:** The result of the optimization naturally yields the inverse proportionality $\lambda_{max} T = \text{constant}$. ### The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why quantum?** Classical Rayleigh-Jeans law predicted infinite energy at short wavelengths (the *Ultraviolet Catastrophe*). Quantization resolved this. - **Why maximize?** Engineers and physicists care about **peak emission** because that is where a body radiates the most energy per unit wavelength interval. - **Why inverse proportionality?** Higher temperature means more energetic photons — shorter wavelength carriers. Energy and wavelength are linked through $E = hc/\lambda$. ## 2.2 The Governing Equation — Planck's Spectral Distribution The starting point for the derivation is Planck's law of black body spectral emissive power: $$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ where the first and second radiation constants are: $$C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.742 \times 10^{8} \text{ W·µm}^4/\text{m}^2$$ $$C_2 = \frac{hc}{k_B} = 1.439 \times 10^{4} \text{ µm·K}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every formula you need to answer KTU University Exam questions on Wein's Displacement Law. All values use SI-consistent units (metres, kelvin, watts). | # | Formula / Identity | Physical Meaning | Units / Constants | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$ | Core statement of Wein's Displacement Law | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | | 2 | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | Peak wavelength for a given temperature | $\lambda_{max}$ in metres | | 3 | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$ | Planck's spectral emissive power (starting point for derivation) | $C_1 = 3.742 \times 10^{8}$ W·µm⁴/m²; $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{4}$ µm·K | | 4 | $5 \left(1 - e^{-x}\right) = x$ | Transcendental equation for Wein's displacement, where $x = C_2/(\lambda_{max} T)$ | Solution: $x = 4.9651$ | | 5 | $\nu_{max} / T = 2.8214 \text{ k}_B / h$ | Frequency-domain form (less commonly used) | $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | | 6 | $E_{b,\text{total}} = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (total emitted power) | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ | | 7 | $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^5$ | Peak spectral emissive power scales as T⁵ | Proportionality constant derivable from Planck's law | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Students:** > - **Wein's Displacement Law** → relates $\lambda_{max}$ to $T$ (inverse, linear-in-reciprocal) > - **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** → relates *total* emitted power to $T^4$ > - These are **NOT** the same. Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Wein's Displacement Law is not merely academic — it is foundational to numerous modern technologies: - **Pyrometers & Infrared Thermometers:** Non-contact temperature measurement in steel plants, furnaces, and medical diagnostics relies on detecting peak radiation wavelength. - **Astronomy:** The colour (and thus peak wavelength) of a star directly reveals its surface temperature. The Sun at ~5778 K peaks in the visible green-yellow range, while cooler red giants peak in the infrared. - **Thermal Imaging & Night Vision:** Sensors are designed around the infrared peak of human body radiation (~9–10 µm). - **Incandescent Lamp Design:** Filament temperature is chosen to shift emission into the visible spectrum (~0.4–0.7 µm) for maximum luminous efficiency. - **Climate Science & Remote Sensing:** Satellite-borne infrared radiometers use peak-wavelength detection to determine cloud-top and sea-surface temperatures. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Wein's Displacement Law > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) under Module 4. You are expected to reproduce it with full mathematical rigour, including substitution, differentiation, and the transcendental solution. ### Step 1 — State Planck's Spectral Distribution Law The spectral emissive power of a black body at wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$ is given by Planck's quantum-derived formula: $$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ **Logical interpretation:** This expresses the energy emitted per unit area, per unit time, per unit wavelength interval. The denominator arises from the Bose-Einstein distribution of photon occupation numbers in the cavity modes. ### Step 2 — Condition for Maximum Emission The spectral curve $E_{b\lambda}$ vs $\lambda$ has a single maximum for a given $T$. To locate this maximum, we set the first derivative to zero: $$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad \lambda = \lambda_{max}$$ ### Step 3 — Simplification Using a Non-Dimensional Substitution To reduce algebraic complexity, we introduce a dimensionless variable $x$ defined as: $$x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T}$$ This substitution encodes the desired combination $\lambda_{max} T$ in a single parameter. We can equivalently express Planck's law as a function of $x$: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$$ **Logical interpretation:** Since $C_1 T^5 / C_2^5$ is a positive constant for fixed $T$, the maximum of $E_{b\lambda}$ is identical to the maximum of the function $f(x) = \dfrac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$. ### Step 4 — Differentiation of the Reduced Function Differentiate $f(x) = \dfrac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$ with respect to $x$ using the quotient rule: $$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{5x^4 (e^x - 1) - x^5 (e^x)}{(e^x - 1)^2}$$ ### Step 5 — Setting the Derivative to Zero Apply the extremum condition $\dfrac{df}{dx} = 0$. The numerator must vanish: $$5x^4 (e^x - 1) - x^5 e^x = 0$$ Divide through by $x^4$ (since $x \neq 0$): $$5(e^x - 1) - x e^x = 0$$ ### Step 6 — Algebraic Rearrangement Expand and collect terms: $$5e^x - 5 - x e^x = 0$$ Factor $e^x$: $$e^x (5 - x) = 5$$ Rearrange to isolate the exponential: $$e^x = \frac{5}{5 - x}$$ Take natural logarithm of both sides: $$x = \ln\!\left(\frac{5}{5 - x}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{5 - x}{5}\right) = -\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{x}{5}\right)$$ This is the **Wein's Displacement Equation** in implicit form. ### Step 7 — The Elegant Final Form Cross-multiply and rewrite to expose the structure clearly: $$5 \left(1 - e^{-x}\right) = x$$ This transcendental equation has no closed-form algebraic solution and **must be solved numerically** (e.g., by Newton-Raphson iteration). ### Step 8 — Numerical Solution Applying iterative numerical methods yields: $$x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} = 4.9651$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Substituting back, $5(1 - e^{-4.9651}) = 5(1 - 0.00697) = 5 \times 0.99303 = 4.9651$ ✓ ### Step 9 — Back-Substitution to Obtain Wein's Displacement Law Rearranging the numerical value: $$\lambda_{max} T = \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^{4} \text{ µm·K}}{4.9651}$$ $$\lambda_{max} T = 2898 \text{ µm·K} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K} = b$$ ### Step 10 — Final Result $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$$ This is the celebrated **Wein's Displacement Law**. ## 3.2 Compact Multi-Line Derivation Block (For Exam Reproduction) For rapid reproduction in a KTU answer script, the derivation can be compressed into the following aligned chain: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{b\lambda} &= \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1 \right]} \\[6pt] \text{Let } x &= \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} \implies E_{b\lambda} \propto \frac{x^5}{e^x - 1} \\[6pt] \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}\right) &= 0 \implies 5(e^x - 1) - x e^x = 0 \\[6pt] \text{Therefore, } 5(1 - e^{-x}) &= x \implies x = 4.9651 \\[6pt] \lambda_{max} T &= \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K} = b \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples > [!NOTE] > These worked examples reflect the **typical 7-mark calculation sub-parts** seen in KTU ESE questions. ### Numerical 1: Peak Wavelength from Temperature **Problem:** A furnace operates at 1500 K. Determine the wavelength at which it emits maximum radiation. **Solution:** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{1500 \text{ K}} = 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} = 1.932 \text{ µm}}$$ This lies in the **near-infrared** region, consistent with the dull red glow of a furnace at this temperature. ### Numerical 2: Temperature from Peak Wavelength (Pyrometry) **Problem:** A pyrometer detects peak radiation from a molten metal at $\lambda_{max} = 0.65$ µm. Find the metal's surface temperature. **Solution:** $$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{0.65 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}$$ $$T = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{0.65 \times 10^{-6}} = 4458.5 \text{ K}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 4458.5 \text{ K}}$$ ### Numerical 3: Shift of Peak Wavelength **Problem:** A black body's peak shifts from 5.0 µm to 2.0 µm. Find the ratio of final to initial temperature. **Solution:** $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{\lambda_{max,1}}{\lambda_{max,2}} = \frac{5.0}{2.0} = 2.5$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 2.5}$$ ### Numerical 4: Peak Spectral Emissive Power Scaling **Problem:** Show that $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^5$. **Solution:** Substituting $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ into Planck's law: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{b\lambda_{max}} &= \frac{C_1}{\lambda_{max}^5 \left[ \exp(C_2/\lambda_{max} T) - 1 \right]} \\[6pt] &= \frac{C_1 T^5}{b^5 \left[ \exp(4.9651) - 1 \right]} \\[6pt] &= \frac{C_1 T^5}{b^5 \left( e^{4.9651} - 1 \right)} \propto T^5 \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the **T⁵ scaling** of peak spectral emissive power. ## 3.4 Limiting Case Analysis — Wein's Approximation For short wavelengths or low temperatures (i.e., $x = C_2/\lambda T \gg 1$), the term $\exp(C_2/\lambda T) \gg 1$, so: $$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)$$ This is **Wein's Displacement Law in its original (pre-Planck) form**, which historically was the first to predict the peak shift. Planck's law generalizes it across the entire spectrum. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Derivation The following Mermaid block maps the logical architecture linking Planck's quantum hypothesis to Wein's Displacement Law: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Quantum Hypothesis
E equals h nu"] --> B["Planck Spectral Law
Eblambda in terms of lambda and T"] B --> C["Substitution
x equals C2 over lambda max T"] C --> D["Reduced Function
f of x equals x to the 5 over e to the x minus 1"] D --> E["Differentiation
df over dx equals 0"] E --> F["Transcendental Equation
5 times 1 minus e to the minus x equals x"] F --> G["Numerical Solution
x equals 4.9651"] G --> H["Wein Displacement Law
lambda max times T equals b"] H --> I["Constant Value
b equals 2.898 times 10 to the minus 3 m K"] style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style B fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#3b6ba5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D fill:#4a83c8,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style E fill:#6ba3d6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style F fill:#8dc3e3,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#b0d9ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 style H fill:#d44a4a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style I fill:#a83232,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Spectral Distribution Peak Shift Visualization The following diagram illustrates how the peak of Planck's distribution **shifts toward shorter wavelengths** as temperature rises, embodying the physical essence of Wein's Displacement Law: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW_T["Low Temperature T1 = 1000 K"] A1["Peak at lambda max 1 equals 2.898 micrometre
Far Infrared region"] end subgraph MED_T["Medium Temperature T2 = 3000 K"] A2["Peak at lambda max 2 equals 0.966 micrometre
Near Infrared region"] end subgraph HIGH_T["High Temperature T3 = 6000 K"] A3["Peak at lambda max 3 equals 0.483 micrometre
Visible Blue region"] end A1 -- Temperature increases
Peak shifts left --> A2 A2 -- Temperature increases
Peak shifts left --> A3 style A1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style A2 fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style A3 fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wein's Law in Engineering Systems This Mermaid block diagrams how Wein's Displacement Law integrates into a real-world non-contact temperature measurement (pyrometer) system: ```mermaid flowchart TD SOURCE["Hot Black Body Source
Temperature T"] --> EMIT["Emits Continuous
Spectrum of Radiation"] EMIT --> FILTER["Optical Filter
Selects narrow band
around lambda max"] FILTER --> DETECT["Photodetector or
Thermal Sensor"] DETECT --> SIGNAL["Signal Conditioning
and Amplification"] SIGNAL --> MICROCONTROLLER["Microcontroller
Applies Wein Law
T equals b over lambda max"] MICROCONTROLLER --> DISPLAY["Digital Display
Shows Temperature in K or C"] PEAKDETECT["Peak Wavelength
Detector Circuit"] --> MICROCONTROLLER style SOURCE fill:#c92a2a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style EMIT fill:#e8590c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style FILTER fill:#f08c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style DETECT fill:#5c940d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style SIGNAL fill:#2b8a3e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style PEAKDETECT fill:#1971c2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style MICROCONTROLLER fill:#0c8599,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style DISPLAY fill:#5f3dc4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part A carries **3 marks per question** and expects a crisp, well-labelled answer. Aim for a 4–5 sentence response with the key formula. ### Question 1: Define Wein's Displacement Law. State its significance in radiation heat transfer. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Wein's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a black body is maximum is **inversely proportional** to its absolute temperature. Mathematically, $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$, where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. **Significance:** It establishes the **inverse relationship** between peak emission wavelength and temperature, enabling engineers to determine the temperature of a radiating body by measuring the wavelength at which it emits maximum radiation. It is the working principle behind **optical pyrometers** used for non-contact temperature measurement of furnaces, stars, and molten metals. It also explains the **colour-temperature correlation** observed in heated solids (red-hot to white-hot progression). ### Question 2: State Planck's law of black body radiation. How is Wein's displacement law derived from it? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Planck's Law** gives the spectral emissive power of a black body: $$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$ where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the first and second radiation constants respectively. **Derivation outline:** By substituting $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$ and applying the condition $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$, the problem reduces to solving the transcendental equation $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$. Its numerical solution is $x = 4.9651$, which upon back-substitution yields $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = C_2 / 4.9651 = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — this is Wein's Displacement Law. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries **14 marks** with a choice between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks]. You must answer **only one** of the two alternatives. ### Question A — Full Derivation + Numerical Application **A(a) [7 Marks]:** Derive Wein's Displacement Law starting from Planck's law of black body radiation. State clearly all assumptions and the numerical value of the displacement constant. **[Complete Model Solution]** **Step 1 — State Planck's Law:** $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Maximum Condition:** The peak occurs where $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$. **Step 3 — Substitute** $x = C_2/(\lambda_{max} T)$, reducing the problem to maximizing $f(x) = \dfrac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$. **Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero:** $$5(e^x - 1) - x e^x = 0$$ **Step 5 — Simplify** to $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$. **Step 6 — Solve numerically:** $x = 4.9651$. **Step 7 — Back-substitute:** $\lambda_{max} T = C_2 / 4.9651 = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. **Final Result:** $\boxed{\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$ **Assumption:** Black body emitter (emissivity = 1, diffuse, isotropic radiation). --- **A(b) [7 Marks]:** The filament of an incandescent lamp operates at 2500 K. Calculate (i) the wavelength of maximum emission, and (ii) the temperature at which the peak would shift into the visible green region (take $\lambda_{green} = 0.55$ µm). **[Complete Model Solution]** **Part (i):** Using $\lambda_{max} = b/T$: $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{2500 \text{ K}} = 1.159 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} = 1.159 \text{ µm (near-infrared region)}}$$ **Part (ii):** Using $T = b/\lambda_{max}$ with $\lambda_{max} = 0.55 \times 10^{-6}$ m: $$T = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{0.55 \times 10^{-6}} = 5269.1 \text{ K}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 5269 \text{ K}}$$ This explains why incandescent bulbs, with filament temperatures around 2500–3000 K, have poor visible-light efficiency — their peak emission lies in the **infrared**, not the visible spectrum. --- ### Question B — Conceptual + Multi-Numerical Application **B(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the physical significance of Wein's Displacement Law. How does it differ from Stefan-Boltzmann Law? Mention two industrial applications. **[Complete Model Solution]** **Physical Significance:** Wein's Displacement Law reveals that as the temperature of a black body increases, the wavelength at which it radiates maximum energy shifts toward shorter wavelengths. This explains the colour change of heated objects from red → orange → yellow → white → blue with rising temperature. It provides the theoretical basis for determining temperature from spectral observations without physical contact. **Difference from Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** | Aspect | Wein's Displacement Law | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | |---|---|---| | Deals with | Spectral (wavelength-specific) emission | Total (integrated over all wavelengths) emission | | Mathematical form | $\lambda_{max} T = b$ (inverse linear) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | | Origin | Derived from maximizing Planck's law | Derived from integrating Planck's law over all $\lambda$ | | Application | Finding peak wavelength or temperature from colour | Calculating total radiative heat transfer | **Industrial Applications:** 1. **Optical Pyrometry:** Non-contact temperature measurement in steel and glass industries. 2. **Infrared Thermography:** Medical diagnostics and building heat-loss audits using thermal cameras tuned to body-radiation peaks (~9–10 µm). --- **B(b) [7 Marks]:** A black body radiates maximum energy at 2.5 µm. If its temperature is doubled, find the new wavelength of maximum emission and the ratio of new to original peak spectral emissive power. **[Complete Model Solution]** **Given:** $\lambda_{max,1} = 2.5$ µm, $T_2 = 2T_1$. **Original Temperature:** $$T_1 = \frac{b}{\lambda_{max,1}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}} = 1159.2 \text{ K}$$ **New Peak Wavelength:** Since $\lambda_{max} T = b$ and $T_2 = 2T_1$: $$\lambda_{max,2} = \frac{b}{T_2} = \frac{b}{2T_1} = \frac{\lambda_{max,1}}{2} = 1.25 \text{ µm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max,2} = 1.25 \text{ µm}}$$ **Ratio of Peak Spectral Emissive Powers:** Since $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^5$: $$\frac{E_{b\lambda_{max},2}}{E_{b\lambda_{max},1}} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^5 = 2^5 = 32$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{E_{b\lambda_{max},2}}{E_{b\lambda_{max},1}} = 32}$$ Doubling the temperature increases the peak spectral emissive power by a factor of 32, demonstrating the highly nonlinear thermal radiation behaviour. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** If $\lambda_{max}$ is given in µm, then use $b = 2898$ µm·K (not $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K). Many students lose 1–2 marks by not converting units consistently. > > 2. **Confusing Wien with Stefan-Boltzmann:** Examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish $\lambda_{max} T = b$ (Wien) from $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann). Writing one in place of the other costs full marks. > > 3. **Skipping the Numerical Value of x:** In the derivation, students often write $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$ but forget to state the solution $x = 4.9651$. Examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for this numerical value. > > 4. **Forgetting the Constant C₁ and C₂ values:** In numericals involving Planck's law, the values of $C_1$ and $C_2$ are required. State them explicitly in your answer to earn full credit. > > 5. **Misstating the Constant:** The displacement constant is $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (not $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m/K — these are dimensionally different). > > 6. **Not Mentioning Assumptions:** A derivation without stated assumptions (e.g., "black body emitter," "diffuse radiation") typically loses 1 mark in KTU's strict valuation scheme. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam** - **Core Statement:** $\lambda_{max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (Wein's Displacement Law) - **Origin:** Derived by maximizing Planck's spectral emissive power with respect to wavelength - **Transcendental Equation:** $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$, with $x = 4.9651$ - **Planck's Constants:** $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.742 \times 10^{8}$ W·µm⁴/m²; $C_2 = hc/k_B = 1.439 \times 10^{4}$ µm·K - **Peak Spectral Emissive Power Scales as $T^5$:** $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^5$ - **Key Distinction:** Wien's Law gives peak *wavelength*; Stefan-Boltzmann gives total *power* ($T^4$) - **Engineering Applications:** Optical pyrometers, IR thermography, incandescent lamp design, astronomical temperature measurement - **Real-World Temperature Examples:** - Human body (~310 K) → $\lambda_{max} \approx 9.35$ µm (infrared) - Sun surface (~5778 K) → $\lambda_{max} \approx 0.50$ µm (visible green-yellow) - Tungsten bulb (~2800 K) → $\lambda_{max} \approx 1.03$ µm (near-IR) - **Limiting Case:** Wein's original (pre-Planck) approximation valid for short $\lambda$ or low $T$ - **Common Numerical Formulas:** $\lambda_{max} = b/T$; $T = b/\lambda_{max}$; $E_{b\lambda_{max},2}/E_{b\lambda_{max},1} = (T_2/T_1)^5$ - **Trap to Avoid:** The peak shift is in wavelength, not frequency. Frequency-domain form is $\nu_{max}/T = 2.8214 \, k_B/h$ (different constant).

topics.title: Black and Grey Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Black and Grey Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Thermal Radiation Surfaces

Thermal radiation is the energy emitted by a body due to its temperature, transmitted in the form of electromagnetic waves. Every surface above absolute zero radiates energy. The classification of surfaces into **black**, **grey**, and **real** surfaces is fundamental to solving radiation heat transfer problems in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Body (Ideal Radiator):** A **black body** is a perfect absorber and perfect emitter of thermal radiation. It absorbs **100%** of incident radiation at **all wavelengths** and **all directions**, and emits the maximum possible radiation at any given temperature.

A black body is a theoretical idealization. Its defining properties are:
- **Absorptivity:** $\alpha_b = 1$ (for all $\lambda$ and all directions)
- **Emissivity:** $\varepsilon_b = 1$
- **Reflectivity:** $\rho_b = 0$
- **Transmissivity:** $\tau_b = 0$ (assumed opaque)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grey Body:** A **grey body** is a surface whose monochromatic (spectral) emissivity and absorptivity are **constant and independent of wavelength**, but **less than unity**.

For a grey surface:
- $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} < 1$
- $\alpha_\lambda = \alpha = \text{constant} < 1$
- Emission is **diffuse** (uniform in all directions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Surface:** A **real surface** has emissivity/absorptivity that varies with wavelength ($\lambda$) and direction. Real surfaces are the general case; black and grey are idealizations that simplify engineering calculations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are comparing three different surfaces under a hot sun:

- **Pitch-black charcoal (Black Body):** It absorbs every drop of sunlight hitting it, and when heated, it glows brilliantly — emitting the maximum possible energy. A small cavity with a tiny hole (such as a furnace interior) behaves like a black body.

- **A weathered, painted grey wall (Grey Body):** It absorbs a fixed fraction (say **60%**) of sunlight regardless of color of light, and emits proportionally less than a black body at the same temperature. Most engineering materials (oxidized metals, painted surfaces, concrete) approximate grey bodies.

- **A polished mirror (Real Surface):** It absorbs differently for different wavelengths (e.g., infrared vs visible) and reflects directionally — not following the simple grey rule. This is the most complex real-world case.

## 1.4 Key Radiation Properties

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Range |
|----------|--------|------------|-------|
| Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | Fraction of incident energy absorbed | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| Reflectivity | $\rho$ | Fraction of incident energy reflected | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ |
| Transmissivity | $\tau$ | Fraction of incident energy transmitted | $0 \le \tau \le 1$ |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | Ratio of actual emission to black body emission | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |

The energy balance for an **opaque** surface ($\tau = 0$) gives:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

For a **transparent** surface ($\tau > 0$):

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4\text{)}$
- **Black Body Emissive Power:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (W/m²)
- **Typical Emissivity Ranges:** Polished metals $\varepsilon \approx 0.02$–$0.1$; Painted surfaces $\varepsilon \approx 0.8$–$0.95$; Human skin $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Law:** For a surface in **thermal equilibrium** with its surroundings, the monochromatic emissivity equals the monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$.

For a **diffuse, grey surface** (wavelength-independent properties), this simplifies to:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

This identity is the cornerstone of grey-body radiation analysis and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Grey Surface Concept

- **Step 1:** A real surface emits less energy than a black body. The ratio of actual emission to black body emission is the **emissivity** $\varepsilon$.
- **Step 2:** A grey surface assumes this ratio is **constant across all wavelengths** — an idealization, but a powerful one.
- **Step 3:** Because grey surfaces emit less, we model them as having a **radiation resistance** placed in series with the perfect black-body emitter.
- **Step 4:** This resistance is $(1-\varepsilon)/\varepsilon A$, allowing the use of electrical-network analogy for multi-surface radiation problems.

## 2.3 Why Grey Body Approximation Works

- Most engineering surfaces (oxidized metals, non-metals, painted surfaces) have emissivity that is **relatively constant** over the dominant wavelength range of typical engineering temperatures (300 K – 1000 K).
- For wavelengths of interest, Planck's distribution is strongly peaked — small spectral variations get averaged out.
- It enables **closed-form analytical solutions** for radiation networks without requiring spectral integration.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Black body emissive power | W/m² |
| $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Grey body emissive power | W/m² |
| $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Net heat loss from small grey body to surroundings | W/m² |
| $q_{12} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$ | General radiation network (two surfaces) | W |
| $q_{12} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} A$ | Two **infinite parallel plates** ($A_1 = A_2 = A$, $F_{12}=1$) | W |
| $q_{12} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} \left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)}$ | Long concentric cylinders (per unit area $A_1$) | W/m² |
| $q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Small object (1) in large enclosure (2) | W |
| $F_{12} + F_{21} = 1$ (for 2-surface enclosure) | View factor summation rule | dimensionless |
| $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ | Reciprocity relation | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In the radiation network analogy, the surface resistance is $R_s = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$, and the space resistance is $R_{\text{space}} = \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}}$. These two are placed in **series** between the radiosity nodes of the two surfaces.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace design:** Refractory-lined furnace interiors approximate black bodies.
- **Spacecraft thermal control:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) and radiation shields protect electronics by exploiting the series-resistance effect of grey surfaces.
- **Building energy efficiency:** Low-emissivity ("Low-E") window coatings ($\varepsilon \approx 0.1$ for IR) reduce radiative heat transfer.
- **Solar collectors:** Selective absorber surfaces have high $\alpha$ for solar spectrum and low $\varepsilon$ for IR — exploiting the difference between spectral absorptivity and emissivity.

## 2.6 The "Surface Resistance" Concept

For a grey surface with emissivity $\varepsilon$ and area $A$:

$$R_{\text{surface}} = \frac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A} \quad \left(\text{m}^{-2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This represents the **drop in radiative potential** as energy leaves a real surface (where $\varepsilon < 1$) compared to a perfect black body. It is **NOT** a real heat loss — it is a mathematical construct enabling the network analogy.

The **radiosity** $J$ is the total radiation leaving a surface (emitted + reflected):

$$J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + (1-\varepsilon) J_{\text{incident}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kirchhoff's Law for Grey Bodies

**Statement to prove:** For a body in thermal equilibrium with surroundings, $\alpha = \varepsilon$.

**Step 1:** Consider a small body of area $A$ at temperature $T$ enclosed in a much larger black-body cavity also at $T$.

**Step 2:** Energy balance at equilibrium — energy absorbed by the body equals energy emitted by the body.

$$\text{Energy absorbed} = \text{Energy emitted}$$

**Step 3:** Irradiation on the small body from the black cavity is $G = \sigma T^4$ (since the cavity is black).

**Step 4:** Absorbed energy per unit time:
$$E_{\text{abs}} = \alpha G A = \alpha \sigma T^4 A$$

**Step 5:** Emitted energy per unit time (using definition of emissivity):
$$E_{\text{emit}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 A$$

**Step 6:** Setting absorbed = emitted:
$$\alpha \sigma T^4 A = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 A$$

**Step 7:** Cancelling the common terms:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \varepsilon}$$

This is the fundamental identity used throughout grey-body radiation analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radiation Between Two Infinite Parallel Grey Plates

**Geometry:** Two infinite parallel plates (1) and (2), each of area $A$, facing each other, separated by a medium (typically air, which is transparent to thermal IR). Both surfaces are grey, opaque, and diffuse.

**Step 1:** The view factor for infinite parallel plates is $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$ (every ray from one hits the other).

**Step 2:** The general network formula:

$$q_{12} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $A_1 = A_2 = A$ and $F_{12} = 1$:

$$q_{12} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$:

$$q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + 1 + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two surface resistance terms into a common denominator:

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{(1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 + (1-\varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2} = \frac{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

$$= \frac{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - 2\varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 6:** Add the central "1" to the combined term. We want the form $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{\varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 7:** The total denominator becomes:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$$

**Step 8:** Final expression:

$$\boxed{q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$

This is a **very high-yield KTU formula** and must be memorized.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Two large parallel plates, 0.5 m × 0.5 m each, are separated by a small gap. Surface 1 is at 800 K with $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, and Surface 2 is at 500 K with $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$. Find the net heat transfer from plate 1 to plate 2.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify geometry. Infinite parallel plates, $A_1 = A_2 = A = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$.

**Step 2:** Apply the derived formula:

$$q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the temperature terms:

$$T_1^4 = 800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = 500^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^4$$

**Step 4:** Compute the difference:

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 3.471 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

**Step 5:** Compute the resistance term:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.5} - 1 = 1.25 + 2.0 - 1.0 = 2.25$$

**Step 6:** Assemble final answer:

$$q_{12} = \frac{0.25 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11}}{2.25}$$

$$q_{12} = \frac{0.25 \times 19.68}{2.25} = \frac{4.92}{2.25} = 2.187 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Net heat transfer = **2.187 kW** from plate 1 to plate 2.

## 3.4 Small Body in a Large Enclosure

**Geometry:** A small grey object (area $A_1$, emissivity $\varepsilon_1$) inside a large grey enclosure (area $A_2 \gg A_1$, emissivity $\varepsilon_2$).

**Step 1:** The view factor from small body to enclosure is $F_{12} = 1$ (every ray from 1 hits 2).

**Step 2:** The view factor from enclosure to small body is $F_{21} = A_1/A_2 \approx 0$ (very small).

**Step 3:** Net radiation from small body to enclosure:

$$q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) A_1$$

**Why?** The radiation emitted by the small body is almost entirely absorbed by the large enclosure. The "back-radiation" from the large enclosure that is intercepted by the small body is negligible, and the small body's own reflections become insignificant — so the net exchange depends only on $\varepsilon_1$ (and not $\varepsilon_2$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Radiation Network for Two Infinite Parallel Plates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1["T1 BLACKBODY EMITTER\nE_b1 = sigma T1^4"] --> nodeR1["SURFACE RESISTANCE 1\nR_s1 = 1-e1 / e1 A"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeJ1["RADIOSITY NODE 1\nJ1 = radiosity leaving surface 1"]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeRSpace["SPACE RESISTANCE\nR_sp = 1 / A F12 = 1/A"]
    nodeRSpace --> nodeJ2["RADIOSITY NODE 2\nJ2 = radiosity leaving surface 2"]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeR2["SURFACE RESISTANCE 2\nR_s2 = 1-e2 / e2 A"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeT2["T2 BLACKBODY EMITTER\nE_b2 = sigma T2^4"]
    nodeJ1 -- "Heat flow q12" --> nodeJ2
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Grey vs Black Body Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph blackbody["BLACK BODY MODEL"]
        A1["Incident Radiation G"] --> B1["100 percent ABSORBED\nalpha = 1"]
        B1 --> C1["MAXIMUM EMISSION\nE_b = sigma T^4"]
    end
    subgraph greybody["GREY BODY MODEL"]
        A2["Incident Radiation G"] --> B2["alpha FRACTION ABSORBED"]
        A2 --> B2b["1-alpha FRACTION REFLECTED"]
        B2 --> C2["REDUCED EMISSION\nE = epsilon sigma T^4\nwith epsilon less than 1"]
        B2b --> C2
    end
    style blackbody fill:#222,color:#fff
    style greybody fill:#888,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Formula to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify Geometry of Radiation Problem"] --> q1{"Small body in\nlarge enclosure?"}
    q1 -- YES --> f1["Use q = e1 sigma T1^4 - T2^4 A1\nemissivity of small body only"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Infinite parallel\nplates A1=A2?"}
    q2 -- YES --> f2["Use q = A sigma T1^4 - T2^4 divided by\n1/e1 + 1/e2 - 1"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Concentric\ncylinders or spheres?"}
    q3 -- YES --> f3["Use general network formula with\nF12 = 1 and A1 F12 = A2 F21"]
    q3 -- NO --> f4["Use general network with\nview factor F12 and area ratio"]
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture: Radiation Network Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        T1in["T1 absolute K"]
        T2in["T2 absolute K"]
        e1in["e1 emissivity 1"]
        e2in["e2 emissivity 2"]
        A1in["A1 m squared"]
        A2in["A2 m squared"]
        F12in["F12 view factor"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION ENGINE"]
        Rs1["R_s1 = 1-e1 / e1 A1"]
        Rs2["R_s2 = 1-e2 / e2 A2"]
        Rsp["R_sp = 1 / A1 F12"]
        Rtot["R_total = Rs1 + Rsp + Rs2"]
        Net["Net heat = Eb1 - Eb2 / R_total"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT"]
        Qout["q in Watts"]
    end
    T1in --> Net
    T2in --> Net
    e1in --> Rs1
    e2in --> Rs2
    A1in --> Rs1
    A2in --> Rs2
    F12in --> Rsp
    Rs1 --> Rtot
    Rs2 --> Rtot
    Rsp --> Rtot
    Rtot --> Net
    Net --> Qout
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a black body. Why is it considered an ideal radiator?
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A black body is an idealized surface that absorbs all incident radiation at all wavelengths and in all directions, making its absorptivity $\alpha = 1$ and reflectivity $\rho = 0$. As a consequence of Kirchhoff's law, its emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$, meaning it emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at any given temperature — given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law $E_b = \sigma T^4$. Because of this maximum emission capability, it is called the ideal radiator. A small cavity with a tiny aperture (e.g., a furnace interior) closely approximates a black body in practice. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: What is a grey body? State Kirchhoff's law for grey surfaces.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A grey body is a surface whose monochromatic emissivity and absorptivity are constant and independent of wavelength, but less than unity ($\varepsilon = \alpha = \text{constant} < 1$). The emission and absorption are diffuse in nature.

**Kirchhoff's Law for grey surfaces:** Under thermal equilibrium, the total emissivity of a grey surface equals its total absorptivity: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. This identity allows engineers to substitute one property for the other, greatly simplifying radiation network analysis. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose 2–3 marks in Part B problems by (a) confusing $F_{12}$ with $F_{21}$ without using the reciprocity relation, (b) forgetting that the radiation resistance is multiplied by **1/A** (i.e., it depends on area), (c) using $T$ in °C instead of K in the $T^4$ term, and (d) dropping the "$-1$" in the denominator of the parallel-plate formula. Always cross-check the units and the formula before finalizing.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Two large, infinite parallel plates of dimensions 1 m × 1 m are placed facing each other with a small air gap. The first plate is at 1000 K with emissivity $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$, and the second plate is at 400 K with emissivity $\varepsilon_2 = 0.3$. Treating both as grey, diffuse, and opaque surfaces, calculate the net rate of heat transfer from plate 1 to plate 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the electrical network analogy for radiation heat transfer between two grey surfaces, defining radiosity, surface resistance, and space resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify geometry. Infinite parallel plates, $A_1 = A_2 = A = 1 \text{ m}^2$. View factor $F_{12} = 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply the parallel-plate formula:

$$q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (400)^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 9.744 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$\frac{1}{0.7} + \frac{1}{0.3} - 1 = 1.4286 + 3.3333 - 1.0 = 3.7619$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$q_{12} = \frac{1 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11}}{3.7619}$$

$$q_{12} = \frac{55,248}{3.7619} = 14,684 \text{ W} \approx 14.68 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Net heat transfer ≈ **14.68 kW** from plate 1 to plate 2. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Network Analogy Explanation:**

**Concept:** Radiation heat transfer between two grey, diffuse, opaque surfaces can be represented as an **electrical network** where temperature is analogous to voltage and heat flow to current.

**Definitions:**

- **Radiosity ($J$):** Total radiation leaving a surface per unit area per unit time, including both emitted and reflected components.
- **Surface Resistance ($R_s$):** Resistance offered by a real (non-black) surface due to its emissivity being less than 1. For surface 1: $R_{s1} = (1-\varepsilon_1)/(\varepsilon_1 A_1)$.
- **Space Resistance ($R_{sp}$):** Resistance due to the geometric relationship (view factor) between the two surfaces: $R_{sp} = 1/(A_1 F_{12})$.

**Network Sketch (text description for answer script):**

Draw two nodes $E_{b1}$ and $E_{b2}$ (black body emissive powers) connected through three resistances in series:
$E_{b1} \rightarrow R_{s1} \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow R_{sp} \rightarrow J_2 \rightarrow R_{s2} \rightarrow E_{b2}$

The heat flow between the two surfaces is:

$$q_{12} = \frac{E_{b1} - E_{b2}}{R_{s1} + R_{sp} + R_{s2}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

This analogy is powerful for handling complex multi-surface enclosures using standard circuit analysis (Kirchhoff's laws). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for net radiation heat transfer between two **infinite parallel grey plates** starting from the definition of radiosity and surface resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small grey sphere of 5 cm diameter and emissivity 0.4 is placed inside a large furnace whose walls are at 1200 K. If the sphere is at 600 K, find the net rate of radiative heat transfer to the sphere. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For two infinite parallel plates of equal area $A$, $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$.

**Step 2:** Write the general network equation:

$$q_{12} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$:

$$q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + 1 + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$

**Step 4:** Combine the surface and space terms:

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} + 1 = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$$

**Step 5:** Final formula:

$$q_{12} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify geometry. Small body (sphere) in large enclosure.

**Step 2:** Sphere surface area:

$$A_1 = \pi d^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the small-body-in-large-enclosure formula:

$$q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) A_1$$

**Step 4:** Compute temperature terms:

$$T_1^4 = (600)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 - T_1^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 1.296 \times 10^{11} = 1.944 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.944 \times 10^{12} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1.944 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-8+12-3}$$

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1.944 \times 7.854 \times 10^{1}$$

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1526.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10$$

Let me recompute cleanly:

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.944 \times 10^{12} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1.944 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-8+12-3}$$

$$q = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1.944 \times 7.854 \times 10^{1}$$

Computing step by step:
- $5.67 \times 1.944 = 11.022$
- $11.022 \times 7.854 = 86.57$
- $0.4 \times 86.57 = 34.63$
- $34.63 \times 10 = 346.3$ W
