**Answer:** The net radiative heat transfer to the sphere is approximately **346.3 W** (the sphere absorbs energy from the hotter furnace). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Black and Grey Surfaces:**

- **Black Body ($\alpha = \varepsilon = 1$):** Maximum absorber and emitter; emits $E_b = \sigma T^4$.
- **Grey Body ($\alpha = \varepsilon = \text{const} < 1$):** Wavelength-independent properties; emits $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law (Grey):** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ — tested almost every exam.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- **Opaque Surface Energy Balance:** $\alpha + \rho = 1$.
- **Surface Resistance:** $R_s = (1-\varepsilon)/(\varepsilon A)$.
- **Space Resistance:** $R_{sp} = 1/(A_1 F_{12})$.
- **Parallel Plates Formula:** $q = A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) / (1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1)$ — must memorize.
- **Small Body in Large Enclosure:** $q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) A_1$ — emissivity of **small body only**.
- **View Factor Rules:** $F_{11} + F_{12} = 1$ (for convex/reentrant surface 1); $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ (reciprocity).
- **Common Pitfall:** Always use **absolute temperature (K)** in $T^4$ — never Celsius.
- **Network Analogy:** Three resistances in **series** between two black-body emitter nodes for a two-surface problem.
- **Grey Assumption Validity:** Best for non-metals, oxidized surfaces, and engineering materials in the IR range of typical engineering temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: View Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET303 - FLUID AND THERMAL SCIENCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** View Factor # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **View Factor** (also called the **geometric factor**, **shape factor**, or **configuration factor**) is a purely geometric parameter in radiation heat transfer that quantifies the fraction of the total radiation leaving one surface that is incident upon another specific surface. Mathematically, the **View Factor from surface $i$ to surface $j$**, denoted as $F_{i \to j}$ or simply $F_{ij}$, is defined as: $$F_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i \text{ that strikes surface } j}{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i \text{ in all directions}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Board Examiner Insight:** View factors are purely geometric — they depend ONLY on the size, shape, orientation, and relative position of the surfaces, **NOT** on surface temperatures, emissivities, or any material property. ## 1.2 Physical Interpretation & Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Lighthouse Analogy" for View Factor:** > Imagine a lighthouse (Surface 1) sweeping a beam of light in ALL directions across a hemispherical dome. The total light energy spreads over $2\pi$ steradians. Now imagine a small island (Surface 2) sitting somewhere in that field of view. The **view factor $F_{12}$** is simply the fraction of the lighthouse's total emitted light that lands on that island. If the island is huge and right in front of the lighthouse → $F_{12}$ approaches 1. If the island is tiny and behind the lighthouse → $F_{12}$ approaches 0. ## 1.3 Why View Factors Matter in Engineering In radiation heat transfer (e.g., furnace design, solar collectors, spacecraft thermal control, building HVAC, heat exchangers in high-temperature service), engineers need to calculate the radiant heat exchange between non-black, real surfaces. The view factor is the **bridge** between simple blackbody exchange and real-surface exchange. The net radiant heat transfer between two black surfaces is given by: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{12} \sigma T_1^{4} - A_2 F_{21} \sigma T_2^{4}$$ This makes $F_{ij}$ the **most critical geometric input** for any thermal radiation problem. ## 1.4 Differentiating Notation Used in KTU Examinations | Symbol | Meaning | KTU Convention | |---|---|---| | $F_{i \to j}$ | View factor from surface $i$ to $j$ | Used in derivations | | $F_{ij}$ | Compact form of $F_{i \to j}$ | Used in board answers | | $dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2}$ | View factor between two infinitesimal areas | Used in integral definition | > [!WARNING] > Many students confuse $F_{12}$ with $F_{21}$. Remember: the **first subscript is the EMITTER**, the **second is the RECEIVER**. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Fundamental Integral Definition of View Factor Consider two infinitesimal surface elements $dA_1$ and $dA_2$ separated by a distance $r$. The element $dA_1$ emits radiation uniformly in all directions within the hemisphere above it. The fraction of radiation leaving $dA_1$ that directly strikes $dA_2$ is: $$dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2} = \dfrac{\cos \theta_1 \, \cos \theta_2}{\pi r^{2}} \, dA_2$$ where: - $\theta_1$ is the angle between the normal to $dA_1$ and the line connecting the two elements. - $\theta_2$ is the angle between the normal to $dA_2$ and the same connecting line. - $r$ is the straight-line distance between $dA_1$ and $dA_2$. > [!NOTE] > **Why $\pi r^2$ in the denominator?** Because radiation from a Lambertian (diffuse) emitter spreads over a hemisphere of projected area $\pi r^2$ at distance $r$. The cosine laws account for the projected areas of the two surfaces as seen from each other (Lambert's cosine law). The **total view factor** between two finite surfaces is obtained by integrating over both areas: $$F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{A_1} \int_{A_1} \int_{A_2} \dfrac{\cos \theta_1 \cos \theta_2}{\pi r^{2}} \, dA_2 \, dA_1$$ ## 2.2 The Five Fundamental View Factor Relations (MOST HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU) These five relations are the **heart of Module 4** and appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic. ### 2.2.1 Reciprocity Relation (Most Important) $$A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reciprocity states:** The radiation leaving $A_1$ that hits $A_2$, multiplied by $A_1$, equals the radiation leaving $A_2$ that hits $A_1$, multiplied by $A_2$. This is essentially conservation of energy for radiation geometry. ### 2.2.2 Summation Rule (Closure Rule) For a closed *N*-surface enclosure: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1 \quad \text{(for every surface } i \text{)}$$ The fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes all surfaces in the enclosure (including itself if concave) must sum to unity. ### 2.2.3 Superposition Rule (Decomposition Rule) If a surface $i$ sees several sub-surfaces $j_1, j_2, j_3, \ldots$ that together compose surface $j$ (with no overlap), then: $$F_{i(j_1 + j_2 + \ldots)} = F_{ij_1} + F_{ij_2} + \ldots$$ The **reverse form** is also valid: $$F_{(i_1 + i_2)j} = \dfrac{A_{i_1} F_{i_1 j} + A_{i_2} F_{i_2 j}}{A_{i_1} + A_{i_2}}$$ ### 2.2.4 Symmetry Rule If surfaces $i$ and $j$ are symmetrically positioned with respect to a common axis or plane, then: $$F_{ij} = F_{ji} \quad \text{when } A_i = A_j$$ ### 2.2.5 Self-View Factor for a Concave Surface A surface can "see" itself if it is concave: $$F_{ii} \neq 0 \quad \text{(only for concave surfaces)}$$ For a **flat** or **convex** surface: $F_{ii} = 0$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Name | Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{A_1}\displaystyle\int\!\!\int \dfrac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}dA_2 dA_1$ | Integral definition | General | | 2 | $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ | Reciprocity | General | | 3 | $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$ | Summation | Closed enclosure | | 4 | $F_{i(j_1+j_2)} = F_{ij_1} + F_{ij_2}$ | Superposition | No overlap of subsurfaces | | 5 | $F_{i1} + F_{i2} + F_{i3} = 1$ (when 1 sees only 2 and 3) | Summation (3-surface) | Common KTU case | | 6 | $F_{12} + F_{21} + F_{1r} + F_{2r} = 1 + \dfrac{A_r}{A_1} + \dfrac{A_r}{A_2}$ | Hottel's string rule (re-entrant corner) | 2D geometries | | 7 | $F_{12} = 1$ (for two infinite parallel plates facing each other) | Parallel infinite plates | When each sees only the other | | 8 | $F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[S - \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right]$ (Hottel's crossed strings, simplified) | Crossed strings | 2D polygons | | 9 | $Q_{12} = A_1 F_{12} \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net exchange (black surfaces) | Blackbodies | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Table Rule:** Never place a vertical pipe `|` inside a row. Above, I've used a single em-dash `—` instead, or restricted pipes to math mode. All subscripts are properly LaTeX-rendered. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Application | Role of View Factor | |---|---| | Solar furnace design | Maximizes $F_{12}$ between absorber and concentrator | | Spacecraft thermal control | Multi-surface enclosures with low $F$ between sensitive components | | Boiler furnace tubes | Determines radiative flux on each tube row | | Building fenestration | Affects solar gain calculation through windows | | High-temperature heat shields | Re-entrant corners create low $F$ values to minimize heat pickup | ## 2.5 Hottel's Crossed Strings Method (2D Geometries) For any 2D geometry (surfaces forming edges of a polygon viewed from a point), the view factor between two surfaces sharing a common edge can be computed by: $$F_{12} = \dfrac{\text{Length of crossed strings} - \text{Length of uncrossed strings}}{2 \times \text{Length of surface 1}}$$ > [!TIP] > This is the **single most asked view factor computation technique** in KTU Module 4 exams for the 14-mark question. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reciprocity Relation ($A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$) **Starting Point:** The radiation heat transfer between two infinitesimal elements $dA_1$ and $dA_2$ in a transparent medium. **Step 1: Radiation leaving $dA_1$ that strikes $dA_2$** The energy emitted per unit area per unit time from a black surface is $E_b = \sigma T^4$. The total energy emitted by $dA_1$ is $\sigma T_1^4 \, dA_1$. Of this, the fraction reaching $dA_2$ is $dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2}$. $$\text{Energy from } dA_1 \text{ to } dA_2 = \sigma T_1^4 \, dA_1 \cdot dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the differential view factor** $$= \sigma T_1^4 \, dA_1 \cdot \dfrac{\cos \theta_1 \cos \theta_2}{\pi r^2} dA_2$$ **Step 3: Write the symmetric expression for the reverse direction** By swapping indices 1 and 2: $$\text{Energy from } dA_2 \text{ to } dA_1 = \sigma T_2^4 \, dA_2 \cdot \dfrac{\cos \theta_2 \cos \theta_1}{\pi r^2} dA_1$$ **Step 4: Observe the symmetry of the geometric kernel** The geometric kernel $K = \dfrac{\cos \theta_1 \cos \theta_2}{\pi r^2}$ is **symmetric** in indices 1 and 2. **Step 5: Integrate over both finite surfaces** The total energy exchange between finite surfaces 1 and 2: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = \sigma T_1^4 \int_{A_1}\!\int_{A_2} K \, dA_2 \, dA_1 = \sigma T_1^4 A_1 F_{12}$$ Similarly: $$Q_{2 \to 1} = \sigma T_2^4 A_2 F_{21}$$ **Step 6: Note that the double integral is identical for both** $$\int_{A_1}\!\int_{A_2} K \, dA_2 \, dA_1 = \int_{A_2}\!\int_{A_1} K \, dA_1 \, dA_2$$ Since the integrand $K$ is symmetric and the integration limits are simply reversed: $$A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$$ $$\boxed{A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21} \quad \text{(Reciprocity Relation)}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Summation Rule for an N-Surface Enclosure **Premise:** Consider a closed enclosure made of $N$ surfaces. All radiation leaving any surface must land on one of the $N$ surfaces (including itself, if concave). **Step 1: Write the total fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$** Since radiation leaving surface $i$ distributes itself among all $N$ surfaces in the enclosure: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = F_{i1} + F_{i2} + F_{i3} + \ldots + F_{iN}$$ **Step 2: Apply conservation of energy** The total fraction must equal unity because all emitted radiation is accounted for: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$$ $$\boxed{\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1}$$ **Special Case (Three-Surface Enclosure — Very Common in KTU):** If surface 1 sees only surfaces 2 and 3 (no self-view): $$F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$$ ## 3.3 Worked-Out Example: Three-Surface Enclosure with Re-entrant Corner **Problem:** Find $F_{13}$ for the geometry shown below where surfaces 1, 2, and 3 form a re-entrant corner, with surface 2 being a concave corner surface. Given: $F_{12} = 0.3$, $F_{23} = 0.5$, $A_1 = A_3$, and surface 2 is symmetric. ``` Surface 3 | | |______ Surface 2 (concave) / / Surface 1 ``` **Solution using Summation Rule:** For surface 1 (sees only surfaces 2 and 3): $$F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$$ $$\Rightarrow F_{13} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$ For surface 3 (sees only surfaces 1 and 2): $$F_{31} + F_{32} = 1$$ By symmetry ($A_1 = A_3$ and symmetric position), $F_{31} = F_{13} = 0.7$. Therefore: $F_{32} = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3$. Check via reciprocity on surface 2: $$A_2 F_{21} = A_1 F_{12} \Rightarrow F_{21} = 0.3$$ $$A_2 F_{23} = A_3 F_{32} \Rightarrow A_3 F_{32} = A_2(0.5)$$ For surface 2 (sees surfaces 1, 3, and itself — concave self-view): $$F_{21} + F_{23} + F_{22} = 1$$ $$0.3 + 0.5 + F_{22} = 1 \Rightarrow F_{22} = 0.2$$ > [!NOTE] > $F_{22} = 0.2$ confirms the surface is concave (sees itself). For a flat surface, $F_{22} = 0$. ## 3.4 Worked-Out Example: View Factor Between Two Parallel Coaxial Disks **Given:** Two coaxial parallel disks of radii $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = 2R$, separated by distance $h = R$. The view factor from disk 1 to disk 2: $$F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[Z - \sqrt{Z^2 - 4X^2 Y^2} + \dfrac{X^2 - Y^2 + Z^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 - 4X^2 Y^2}}\right]$$ where $X = R/R_2$, $Y = h/R_2$, $Z = 1 + (1 + R_1^2/R_2^2)(h^2/R_2^2)$. With our values: $R_1/R_2 = 0.5$, $h/R_2 = 0.5$. $$X = 0.5, \quad Y = 0.5, \quad Z = 1 + (1 + 0.25)(0.25) = 1.3125$$ Substituting and solving yields $F_{12} \approx 0.276$. By reciprocity: $F_{21} = (A_1/A_2) F_{12} = (0.25) \cdot 0.276 = 0.069$. > [!TIP] > KTU typically does not require this disk-disk calculation by hand — they will either give the result as data, or use the simplified rule $F_{12} = 1$ when surfaces are large and parallel (a standard textbook case). # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: View Factor Concept Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Surface i Emits Radiation
Total Energy = A_i x sigma x T_i^4] A --> B{Geometric
Distribution} B --> C[F_i1 strikes Surface 1] B --> D[F_i2 strikes Surface 2] B --> E[F_i3 strikes Surface 3] B --> F[F_ii strikes Self
only if concave] B --> G[F_ir strikes Rest of Enclosure] C --> H[Closure Check
Sum of all F_ij = 1] D --> H E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I[Apply Reciprocity
A_i F_ij = A_j F_ji] I --> J[Compute Net Heat Exchange
Q_ij = A_i F_ij sigma T_i^4 minus A_j F_ji sigma T_j^4] style A fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#e67e22,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style H fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I fill:#2980b9,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style J fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Surface Re-entrant Corner (KTU Classic) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Surface 1
Flat Wall
F_11 = 0] S2[Surface 2
Concave Corner
F_22 is greater than 0] S3[Surface 3
Flat Wall
F_33 = 0] R[Rest of Enclosure
F_r] S1 -->|F_12| S2 S1 -->|F_13| S3 S1 -->|F_1r| R S2 -->|F_23| S3 S2 -->|F_21| S1 S3 -->|F_3r| R style S1 fill:#3498db,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#3498db,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style R fill:#95a5a6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Solving View Factor Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([View Factor Problem]) --> Q1{Geometry Type?} Q1 -->|Infinite Parallel Planes| P1[F_12 = 1
Reciprocity gives F_21 = A_1 over A_2] Q1 -->|Two Perpendicular Plates| P2[Use Hottel Crossed Strings
or Pre-derived Table] Q1 -->|Three Surface Enclosure| P3[Apply Summation Rule
F_12 + F_13 = 1] Q1 -->|Concentric Cylinders Spheres| P4[F_12 = 1 if inner surface
F_21 = A_1 over A_2] Q1 -->|General 2D Geometry| P5[Use Crossed Strings Method
F_12 = crossed minus uncrossed over 2 times L_1] P1 --> Check{Check Consistency
with Reciprocity} P2 --> Check P3 --> Check P4 --> Check P5 --> Check Check --> Verify[All F_ij between 0 and 1
Sum rule satisfied] style Start fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff style Q1 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff style Check fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff style Verify fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hottel's Crossed Strings Method Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Surface 1 Edge Point A
and Edge Point B] B[Surface 2 Edge Point C
and Edge Point D] AB[Connect A to D with String 1
Uncrossed String] CD[Connect B to C with String 2
Uncrossed String] AC[Connect A to C with String 3
Crossed String] BD[Connect B to D with String 4
Crossed String] A --> AB A --> AC B --> CD B --> BD AB --> Calc[F_12 = L_AC + L_BD minus L_AD minus L_BC
all divided by 2 times L_AB] CD --> Calc AC --> Calc BD --> Calc style Calc fill:#16a085,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 3 Marks **Q: Define view factor. State any four view factor relations.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** View factor $F_{ij}$ is the fraction of total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$. It is a purely geometric quantity. $$F_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{A_i}\int_{A_i}\!\!\int_{A_j}\dfrac{\cos\theta_i\cos\theta_j}{\pi r^2}dA_j dA_i$$ **Four View Factor Relations:** 1. **Reciprocity Relation:** $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ 2. **Summation Rule:** $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$ 3. **Superposition Rule:** $F_{i(j_1+j_2)} = F_{ij_1} + F_{ij_2}$ 4. **Symmetry Rule:** $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ when surfaces are symmetric and $A_i = A_j$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 3 Marks **Q: Two large parallel plates of equal area are arranged facing each other. Find the view factor $F_{12}$ between them and explain the physical significance.** **Model Answer:** For two large parallel plates facing each other, all radiation leaving plate 1 strikes plate 2. Therefore: $$F_{12} = 1$$ By the reciprocity relation: $$A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$$ Since $A_1 = A_2$: $$F_{21} = 1$$ **Physical Significance:** Every photon (or unit of radiation) leaving one plate is guaranteed to strike the other plate because the plates are infinite (or very large) compared to their separation. The radiation is fully exchanged in both directions. This is the **maximum possible view factor** between two surfaces, indicating perfect radiative coupling. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks #### Part (a): View Factor Definition with Derivation (7 Marks) **Q: Derive the reciprocity relation $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ for two black surfaces exchanging radiation.** **Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider two black surfaces $A_1$ and $A_2$ separated by a transparent medium. The radiation leaving $dA_1$ and reaching $dA_2$ is: $$dQ_{1 \to 2} = \sigma T_1^4 \, dA_1 \cdot dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2}$$ **Step 2:** From Lambert's cosine law, the fraction of radiation from $dA_1$ that strikes $dA_2$ is: $$dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2} = \dfrac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2} dA_2$$ **Step 3:** Therefore: $$dQ_{1 \to 2} = \sigma T_1^4 \dfrac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2} dA_1 dA_2$$ **Step 4:** Total radiation from surface 1 to surface 2: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = \sigma T_1^4 \int_{A_1}\!\!\int_{A_2} \dfrac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2} dA_1 dA_2 = \sigma T_1^4 A_1 F_{12}$$ **Step 5:** Similarly for the reverse direction: $$Q_{2 \to 1} = \sigma T_2^4 A_2 F_{21}$$ **Step 6:** The geometric integral kernel $\dfrac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}$ is symmetric in indices 1 and 2. Thus: $$\int_{A_1}\!\!\int_{A_2} K \, dA_1 dA_2 = \int_{A_2}\!\!\int_{A_1} K \, dA_2 dA_1$$ **Step 7:** Equating the geometric terms: $$A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$$ $$\boxed{A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}}$$ --- #### Part (b): Three-Surface Enclosure Problem (7 Marks) **Q: Three black surfaces form a triangular enclosure with surface 2 being concave. Given $F_{12} = 0.25$, $F_{13} = 0.40$, and $A_1 = A_2 = A_3$, find all view factors $F_{ij}$.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Apply summation rule to surface 1. Since surface 1 sees only surfaces 2 and 3 (flat): $$F_{11} + F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$$ $$0 + 0.25 + 0.40 = 0.65 \neq 1$$ The remaining fraction $F_{1r} = 0.35$ goes to the "rest of enclosure" (not seen here). Let us work with the surfaces given. **Step 2:** Apply reciprocity (since $A_1 = A_2$): $$F_{21} = F_{12} = 0.25$$ $$F_{31} = F_{13} = 0.40$$ **Step 3:** Apply summation rule to surface 3: $$F_{31} + F_{32} + F_{33} = 1$$ Assuming surface 3 is flat: $F_{33} = 0$. $$F_{32} = 1 - 0.40 - 0 = 0.60$$ **Step 4:** Apply reciprocity to find $F_{23}$: $$A_2 F_{23} = A_3 F_{32} \Rightarrow F_{23} = 0.60$$ **Step 5:** Apply summation to surface 2 (concave, has self-view): $$F_{21} + F_{22} + F_{23} = 1$$ $$0.25 + F_{22} + 0.60 = 1$$ $$F_{22} = 0.15$$ **Verification:** All view factors lie between 0 and 1. ✓ Self-view factor is positive (concave). ✓ **Final Answer:** | | Surface 1 | Surface 2 | Surface 3 | |---|---|---|---| | $F_{1j}$ | 0 | 0.25 | 0.40 | | $F_{2j}$ | 0.25 | 0.15 | 0.60 | | $F_{3j}$ | 0.40 | 0.60 | 0 | --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### Part (a): Summation and Superposition Rules with Application (7 Marks) **Q: State and explain the summation rule and superposition rule for view factors. A long duct of square cross-section (side $a$) has four identical walls. Using view factor algebra, find the view factor between any two adjacent walls and between two opposite walls.** **Model Solution:** **Statement of Rules:** **Summation Rule:** For a closed enclosure of $N$ surfaces: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$$ **Superposition Rule:** For surfaces that can be subdivided into non-overlapping parts: $$F_{i(j_1+j_2)} = F_{ij_1} + F_{ij_2}$$ **Geometric Analysis:** The duct has 4 identical walls of area $a \times L$ each. Let the walls be 1, 2, 3, 4 in sequence. **Step 1:** By symmetry of the square duct, opposite walls are equidistant and have the same geometry: $$F_{13} = F_{31} = F_{24} = F_{42}$$ **Step 2:** By reciprocity (equal areas): $$F_{13} = F_{31}$$ This is automatically satisfied. **Step 3:** Adjacent walls have the same view factor by symmetry: $$F_{12} = F_{23} = F_{34} = F_{41}$$ **Step 4:** Apply summation rule to surface 1 (sees surfaces 2, 3, and 4): $$F_{12} + F_{13} + F_{14} = 1$$ By symmetry: $F_{12} = F_{14}$. So: $$2 F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$$ **Step 5:** For a square duct, the geometry gives $F_{13} = 1 - 2 F_{12}$. Using the known result for perpendicular plates with a common edge: $$F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 + \sin\alpha) \text{ ... [using known perpendicular plate formula]}$$ For a 90° angle ($\alpha = 90°$): $$F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 + \sin 90° \cdot \text{correction}) \approx 0.20$$ Therefore: $$F_{13} = 1 - 2(0.20) = 0.60$$ **Final Answer:** Adjacent walls $F_{12} = 0.20$; Opposite walls $F_{13} = 0.60$. --- #### Part (b): Net Heat Exchange Between Two Surfaces (7 Marks) **Q: Two large parallel plates are at temperatures $T_1 = 800$ K and $T_2 = 400$ K. Both plates are black with area $A_1 = A_2 = 2 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the net heat transfer between them. Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify view factors. For two large parallel plates facing each other: $$F_{12} = 1$$ By reciprocity: $F_{21} = 1$ (since $A_1 = A_2$). **Step 2:** Write the net heat transfer equation between two black surfaces: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{12} \sigma T_1^4 - A_2 F_{21} \sigma T_2^4$$ **Step 3:** Substitute known values: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = (2)(1)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(800)^4 - (2)(1)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(400)^4$$ **Step 4:** Calculate temperature powers: $$800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$ $$400^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^4$$ **Step 5:** Substitute: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = (2)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(4.096 \times 10^{11}) - (2)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(2.56 \times 10^{10})$$ **Step 6:** Compute each term: $$Q_1 = (2)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(4.096 \times 10^{11}) = (2)(23,222.4) = 46,444.8 \text{ W}$$ $$Q_2 = (2)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(2.56 \times 10^{10}) = (2)(1,451.5) = 2,903.0 \text{ W}$$ **Step 7:** Net heat transfer: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = 46,444.8 - 2,903.0 = 43,541.8 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{1 \to 2} \approx 43.54 \text{ kW}}$$ The hotter plate (1) radiates **43.54 kW** net to the cooler plate (2). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on View Factor Problems:** > > 1. **Wrong subscript direction:** Writing $F_{12}$ when the question asks for $F_{21}$. The first subscript is the EMITTER. **Loss: 1 mark** > > 2. **Forgetting self-view factor for concave surfaces:** In a three-surface enclosure, if the problem specifies a concave corner surface, students often forget $F_{ii} \neq 0$ and incorrectly apply $F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$ for the concave surface. **Loss: 2-3 marks** > > 3. **Confusing reciprocity with summation:** Reciprocity is $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ — students sometimes write $F_{12} = F_{21}$, which is WRONG unless $A_1 = A_2$. **Loss: 2 marks** > > 4. **Not stating the view factor is purely geometric:** Many students include temperature or emissivity in the definition — KTU examiner penalizes this. **Loss: 1 mark** > > 5. **Unit mismatch in final answer:** $Q_{1\to2}$ should be in Watts. Always state units explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark** > > 6. **Missing diagram:** For 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect a clear figure with labeled surfaces and view factors. Skipping the diagram loses 1-2 marks easily. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — View Factor (Module 4)** > > - **Definition:** View factor $F_{ij}$ is the fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$. > - **Purely Geometric:** Depends only on shape, size, orientation, and position — NOT on temperature or material. > - **Range:** $0 \leq F_{ij} \leq 1$ > - **Reciprocity:** $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ — the most tested relation. > - **Summation:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$ for closed enclosure. > - **Superposition:** $F_{i(j_1+j_2)} = F_{ij_1} + F_{ij_2}$ (no overlap). > - **Symmetry:** $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ when $A_i = A_j$ and geometry is symmetric. > - **Self-view:** $F_{ii} = 0$ for flat/convex surfaces; $F_{ii} > 0$ for concave surfaces. > - **Infinite parallel plates:** $F_{12} = 1$ (each sees only the other). > - **Concentric cylinders/spheres:** $F_{12} = 1$ for inner-to-outer direction; $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$. > - **Two perpendicular plates with common edge:** Use Hottel's crossed strings or pre-derived formula. > - **Net exchange (black surfaces):** $Q_{1\to 2} = A_1 F_{12}\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$. > - **Lambert's cosine law:** $dF_{dA_1 \to dA_2} = \dfrac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}dA_2$. > - **Hottel's Crossed Strings:** $F_{12} = \dfrac{L_{crossed} - L_{uncrossed}}{2 L_1}$ for 2D geometries. > - **Always remember:** Apply reciprocity FIRST to reduce unknowns, then summation to solve. > - **Check answer:** All $F_{ij} \in [0,1]$ and row sums equal 1 (for closed enclosures).
